Motivation: The paper challenges the conventional wisdom of confidence-based decoding as the standard inference policy for masked diffusion language models, arguing that it's misaligned with the logical-flow trajectories needed for complex reasoning tasks.

Method: The researchers analyze masked diffusion language models using multi-digit addition as a concrete test case, comparing confidence-based decoding with confidence-aligned training against traditional random masking approaches across five distinct reasoning tasks.

Result: Confidence-aligned training amplifies error rates by an order of magnitude on challenging inputs, while random masking keeps failure rates low. Across five reasoning tasks, confidence-based decoding induces failures on complex inputs, and confidence-aligned training exacerbates them.

Conclusion: Traditional random masking, despite being perceived as inefficient, robustly preserves the reasoning-trajectory conditionals essential for solving challenging problems, suggesting it should be preferred over confidence-aligned approaches for complex reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Masked diffusion language models (MDMs) uniquely support any-order generation, with confidence-based decoding currently serving as the de facto standard inference policy. To optimize for this, recent training schemes attempt to align training mask patterns directly with those observed during generation. However, we argue that confidence-based decoding is inherently misaligned with the logical-flow trajectories required for complex reasoning, and that confidence-aligned training actively entrenches this misalignment. We make this concrete using multi-digit addition, where the decoding strategy prematurely predicts locally easy digits before resolving their long-range dependencies, producing high-confidence errors on challenging inputs. While traditional random masking keeps the failure rate low on this challenging tail, confidence-aligned training amplifies the error rate by an order of magnitude. Across five distinct reasoning tasks, this same pattern emerges with task-dependent severity: confidence-based decoding induces failures on highly complex inputs, and confidence-aligned training exacerbates them. In contrast, random masking -- despite its perceived inefficiency -- robustly preserves the reasoning-trajectory conditionals essential for solving the challenging tail.

</details>


### [27] [Governing Technical Debt in Agentic AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29129)
*Muhammad Zia Hydari,Raja Iqbal,Narayan Ramasubbu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agentic Technical Debt is the accumulated liability from hastily assembled AI agent components, while Stochastic Tax is the ongoing operating cost of managing probabilistic agent behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic AI systems are increasingly deployed as production infrastructure, but they create governance challenges not captured by traditional software or predictive ML technical debt frameworks.

Method: The paper defines two key concepts: Agentic Technical Debt (accumulated liability from patching together prompts, memory, tool schemas, orchestration graphs, etc.) and Stochastic Tax (recurring operating burden of managing probabilistic agent behavior).

Result: The distinction between debt (stock of design/governance liability) and tax (flow of operating cost) is established, with practical implications for management through dashboards and governance controls.

Conclusion: Managers need to make both Agentic Technical Debt and Stochastic Tax visible through lightweight dashboards and governance controls to effectively manage AI agent systems in production environments.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems are increasingly being explored as production infrastructure: they reason over multiple steps, call tools, act through workflows, and adapt through memory and feedback. These systems create governance challenges that are not fully captured by traditional software or predictive ML technical debt. We define Agentic Technical Debt as the accumulated liability created when prompts, memory, tool schemas, orchestration graphs, control policies, and observability routines are patched together faster than they can be validated, standardized, and governed. We define Stochastic Tax as the recurring operating burden of keeping probabilistic agent behavior within acceptable bounds. The distinction matters: debt is a stock of design and governance liability, while the tax is a flow of operating cost that arises because stochastic agents act through tools and workflows. We outline how managers can make both visible through lightweight dashboards and governance controls.

</details>


### [28] [Better Later Than Sooner: Neuro-Symbolic Knowledge Graph Construction via Ontology-grounded Post-extraction Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29168)
*Lorenzo Loconte,Timothy Hospedales,Cristina Cornelio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A neuro-symbolic framework for ontology-grounded knowledge graph construction that improves consistency while reducing LLM usage for complex question answering tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current QA systems struggle with complex queries requiring multi-hop reasoning and symbolic operations. Existing symbolic graph-based RAG methods using LLMs for KG extraction often violate commonsense ontology constraints, leading to consistency issues.

Method: Proposes a neuro-symbolic framework combining: 1) open-domain extraction, 2) embedding-based canonicalization of types and predicates, and 3) targeted LLM-based correction of ontology violations deferred to post-extraction stage.

Result: The method reduces token usage by avoiding repeated LLM calls, improves KG consistency, preserves downstream QA quality, and produces KGs well-suited for symbolic querying (measured by SPARQL graph pattern occurrence).

Conclusion: The proposed neuro-symbolic framework effectively addresses ontology consistency issues in KG construction for QA while optimizing computational efficiency, enabling reliable symbolic querying for complex questions.

Abstract: Question answering (QA) is a core challenge in AI, particularly for complex queries requiring multi-hop reasoning across documents, or symbolic operations like aggregation or exhaustive listing. Retrieval-augmented generation has become the dominant approach to QA, with recent graph-based variants addressing part of these issues by organizing knowledge to better support compositional questions. However, most textual graph-based RAG methods still lack the structure needed for symbolic operations useful to answer complex questions reliably. This motivates symbolic graph-based approaches, which extract knowledge graphs (KGs) whose relations are logic predicates that enable SQL-like querying. Yet these pipelines typically use LLMs for KG extraction, which can introduce consistency issues, where extracted facts may violate commonsense ontology constraints. We propose a neuro-symbolic framework for ontology-grounded KG construction combining open-domain extraction, embedding-based canonicalization of types and predicates, and targeted LLM-based correction of ontology violations. By deferring corrections to a post-extraction stage, our method avoids repeated LLM calls, substantially reducing token usage while improving KG consistency and preserving downstream QA quality. Finally, we show that the extracted KGs are well suited for symbolic querying by measuring the occurrence of SPARQL graph patterns.

</details>


### [29] [Paper Agents, Paper Gains: An Empirical Analysis of DeFi Investment Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29174)
*Jay Yu,Amy Zhao,Danning Sui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TLDR: DeFi investment agents are early-stage and heterogeneous with over $3B in valuations but poor performance - agents retain $30M paper gains while token holders lost $191.7M, with weak connection between valuations and fundamentals, and negative returns for most users.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand the current state and performance of AI-powered DeFi investment agents, which have gained significant market traction with over $3 billion in combined token valuations, but lack systematic analysis of their actual performance, autonomy levels, and stakeholder alignment.

Method: The methodology includes: (1) surveying over 1,900 AI-tagged crypto projects and filtering to investment-focused agents; (2) curating 10 representative projects across strategy and observability dimensions; (3) deep-dive architectural analysis of two prominent agent frameworks (ElizaOS and Virtuals Protocol); (4) quantitative on-chain performance analysis of 11 Solana-based agent treasuries covering 925,323 token holders and publicly attributable trading activity.

Result: Key findings: (1) Many projects lack clear evidence of autonomous trade execution, with visible deployments being basic API integrations; (2) Agent treasuries retain $30M paper gains while token holders lost $191.7M, with top 1% wallets capturing 81.4% of gains ($1.81B); (3) Weak connection between token valuations and fundamentals (market-cap-to-AUM ratios >10,000x vs <1x for established DeFi); (4) Aggregate user gains peaked at $2.4B then declined to net losses, with negative median returns on all platforms and tokens down 93% from highs.

Conclusion: The current state represents a permissionless, first-generation market where open infrastructure enables rapid experimentation but allows naive/speculative agents to launch before robust standards emerge. The authors propose a maturity framework along three dimensions - autonomous execution, risk-adjusted profitability, and stakeholder alignment - to characterize the gap between current deployments and future investment-grade agent systems.

Abstract: DeFi investment agents, systems that use AI for autonomous on-chain trading, have attained over USD 3 billion in combined token valuations since late 2024. We survey over 1,900 AI-tagged crypto projects, filter to investment-focused agents, and curate 10 representative projects spanning strategy and observability dimensions. We then conduct a deep-dive architectural analysis of two prominent agent frameworks, ElizaOS and Virtuals Protocol, and a quantitative on-chain performance analysis of 11 Solana-based agent treasuries with publicly attributable trading activity, covering 925,323 token holders. We find that current deployments remain early and heterogeneous: (1) in our sample, many projects do not yet provide clear evidence of autonomous trade execution, and developer interviews suggest that many visible deployments remain basic API integrations; (2) agent treasuries retain over USD 30M in paper gains while token holders collectively lost USD 191.7M, with the top 1% of wallets capturing 81.4% of all gains (USD 1.81B); (3) token valuations are weakly connected to treasury fundamentals, with market-cap-to-AUM ratios exceeding 10,000x versus below 1x for established DeFi protocols; and (4) aggregate user gains peaked at USD 2.4B before declining to net losses, with median returns negative on every platform and tokens declining 93% on average from all-time highs. We interpret these outcomes as characteristic of a permissionless, first-generation market in which open infrastructure enables rapid experimentation but also allows naive or speculative agents to launch before robust standards for autonomy, performance, and stakeholder alignment emerge. We therefore propose a maturity framework along three dimensions: autonomous execution, risk-adjusted profitability, and stakeholder alignment, to characterize the gap between current deployments and future investment-grade agent systems.

</details>


### [30] [ReasonOps: Operator Segmentation for LLM Reasoning Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29192)
*Daniel Lee,Owen Queen,James Zou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReasonOps is an unsupervised method for analyzing chain-of-thought reasoning traces that discovers 7 universal reasoning operators across LLMs and benchmarks, enabling model identification, correctness prediction, and early quality estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for analyzing chain-of-thought traces (which can span tens of thousands of tokens) are either too rigid or not expressive enough, failing to capture features across different domains and models.

Method: ReasonOps uses unsupervised clustering of sentence-initial 3-token pivots to identify reasoning operators. The method analyzes 44,662 traces from 12 thinking LLMs across 6 families and 8 reasoning benchmarks, validated by three independent LLM judges.

Result: Discovered 7 recurring reasoning operators (discourse-level moves like backtracking, inferring, hypothesizing) that appear across all model families and benchmarks. These operators enable model identification with high accuracy (macro-AUC), predict answer correctness above baselines, and allow early quality estimation (WP-AUC at 50% trace completion).

Conclusion: ReasonOps provides an unsupervised, annotation-free pipeline for deep analysis of LLM reasoning traces, revealing common compositional structure across models and enabling strong downstream applications in model identification and reasoning quality prediction.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought traces from large reasoning models can span tens of thousands of tokens, yet we lack a vocabulary for describing their internal structure. Previous methods developed to analyze chain-of-thought traces are either too rigid or not expressive enough, failing to capture features across domains and models. To remedy this, we develop ReasonOps, an unsupervised, expressive method for annotating chain-of-thought traces, providing succinct universal operators. Using ReasonOps, we analyze 44,662 traces from 12 thinking LLMs spanning 6 families across 8 reasoning benchmarks and discover that they share a common compositional structure: 7 recurring reasoning operators -- discourse-level moves such as backtracking, inferring, and hypothesizing -- that emerge from unsupervised clustering of sentence-initial 3-token pivots. These operators appear across every model family and benchmark domain, confirmed by three independent LLM judges who classify held-out samples at 70 -76% accuracy. We analyze the structure of operators on easy vs. hard problems, revealing that reflective operators are more helpful on hard problems and harm performance on easy problems. Operator sequences are highly model-identifying: a classifier trained on operator distributions alone recovers the source model with macro-AUC, revealing that each model family has a distinctive reasoning fingerprint. Structural operator features predict within-problem answer correctness well above baselines. Classifiers built on these operators reach WP-AUC and on AIME specifically. ReasonOps further enables early quality estimation well before the trace completes: we predict at WP-AUC for only 50% of the trace. The ReasonOps pipeline is unsupervised and annotation-free, enabling deep insights into LLM reasoning traces as well as strong downstream results on model identification and correctness prediction.

</details>


### [31] [GTA: Generating Long-Horizon Tasks for Web Agents at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29218)
*Tenghao Huang,Kung-Hsiang Huang,Prafulla Kumar Choubey,Yilun Zhou,Muhao Chen,Jonathan May,Chien-Sheng Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GTA is a scalable framework that automatically generates realistic multi-hop web-agent tasks with executable trajectories, addressing the lack of process-level supervision in web agent benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing web agent benchmarks lack scalable process-level supervision - they're mostly manually constructed with only start-goal annotations, while recent automatic generation methods are expensive, biased, and shallow, preventing reliable training/evaluation of agents for realistic multi-hop cross-page tasks.

Method: GTA integrates crawling, retrieval-based seeding, in-context generation, and automated quality control to produce realistic tasks paired with executable trajectories. It decouples crawling from generation for efficiency, grounds tasks in site graphs for compositionality, and ensures dense supervision through deterministic replays and systematic validation.

Result: Applied to over 50 websites covering e-commerce, government, forums, and news with multilingual and multi-hop coverage. The benchmark reveals significant human-agent performance gaps and enables detailed diagnostics.

Conclusion: The paper formalizes multi-hop web-agent task generation, proposes an efficient validated pipeline for automatic data creation, and releases a dynamic benchmark with reproducible evaluation to advance web agent development.

Abstract: Web agents, which couple language models with browsing and tool-use capabilities, show promise as open web assistants. Yet progress is increasingly limited by the lack of scalable, process-level supervision. Existing benchmarks are largely manually constructed, providing only coarse start-goal annotations without intermediate trajectories, while recent automatic generation efforts remain expensive, biased, and shallow. These limitations prevent reliable training and evaluation of agents that must generalize to realistic, multi-hop, cross-page tasks. We introduce a scalable framework, GTA, that integrates crawling, retrieval-based seeding, in-context generation, and automated quality control to produce realistic tasks paired with executable trajectories. This design decouples crawling from generation for greater efficiency, grounds tasks in the site graph to enforce compositionality, and ensures dense supervision through deterministic replays and systematic validation. We instantiate the pipeline on over 50 websites covering e-commerce, government, forums, and news, with multilingual and multi-hop coverage. The resulting benchmark reveals a significant human-agent performance gap and enables detailed diagnostics. Our contributions are three-fold: (i) formalizing multi-hop web-agent task generation, (ii) proposing an efficient and validated pipeline for automatic data creation, and (iii) releasing a dynamic benchmark with reproducible evaluation.

</details>


### [32] [BenchTrace: A Benchmark for Testing Reflection Ability and Controlled Evolution in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29225)
*Jiahao Huang,Fei Cheng,Junfeng Jiang,Zefan Yu,Akiko Aizawa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BenchTrace is a benchmark for evaluating self-evolution in LLM agents, featuring reflection evaluation through QA tasks and evolution evaluation via controlled simulations, revealing significant limitations in current models' ability to identify failures and generalize learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation of self-evolving agents is limited because it only measures task scores (leaving reflection quality unknown) and relies on agents' own episode runs without targeting specific failure patterns.

Method: Built a snapshot-reflection dataset of 1,821 annotated episodes across six diverse tasks, with two components: Reflection Evaluation (probing failure identification through QA tasks) and Evolution Evaluation (testing whether past failure experience translates to avoidance behavior in controlled simulations). Also proposed failure avoidance rate (FAR) as a new metric.

Result: Qwen3-32B and GPT-4.1 fell below 30% end-to-end pass rate on reflection evaluation, with diagnosis as the main bottleneck. Self-evolution methods improved FAR over non-evolving baselines, but agents forget early lessons with noise accumulation and fail to generalize beyond specific contexts, showing negative transfer across tasks.

Conclusion: BenchTrace exposes concrete limits of current self-evolution approaches and provides a controlled, model-agnostic framework for targeted evaluation, revealing that only fully correct reflections strongly correlate with higher failure avoidance rates.

Abstract: Self-evolving agents improve over time by reflecting on past failures, but existing evaluation is limited in two ways: it measures only task scores, leaving reflection quality unknown, and it relies on agents' own episode runs, offering no mechanism to target specific failure patterns. We present \textbf{BenchTrace}, a benchmark for evaluating self-evolution ability in LLM agents. BenchTrace is built on a snapshot-reflection dataset of 1,821 annotated episodes spanning six diverse tasks, and comprises a \textbf{Reflection Evaluation} that probes failure identification through targeted QA tasks, and an \textbf{Evolution Evaluation} that tests whether past failure experience translates into avoidance behavior in a controlled self-evolution simulation. Building on BenchTrace, we propose \textbf{failure avoidance rate (FAR)}, a new evaluation metric measuring the fraction of test cases in which the agent successfully avoids the target failure instance. Experiments with Qwen3-32B and GPT-4.1 reveal that both models fall below a 30\% end-to-end pass rate on reflection evaluation, with diagnosis as the primary bottleneck. Evolution evaluation shows that self-evolution methods generally improve FAR over the non-evolving baseline, but agents forget early lessons as noise episodes accumulate, and agents fail to generalize their reflections beyond the specific context, causing negative transfer across task contexts. Our correlation analysis further reveals that only a fully correct reflection is strongly associated with higher FAR. BenchTrace exposes concrete limits of current self-evolution approaches and provides a controlled, model-agnostic framework for targeted evaluation.

</details>


### [33] [Tailoring the Curriculum: Student-Centered Reasoning Distillation via Dynamic Data-Model Compatibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29229)
*Jiahao Huang,Fei Cheng,Junfeng Jiang,Akiko Aizawa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces DMC (Data-Model Compatibility) metric for assessing dataset suitability for reasoning distillation, showing it correlates with performance and guides effective dataset selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current reasoning distillation methods for transferring reasoning abilities from large to smaller language models suffer from inconsistency in success, which depends heavily on how well training data align with the student model's capabilities.

Method: Proposes DMC metric that jointly considers data quality, relative difficulty, and student capability to assess dataset suitability for reasoning distillation. Validates through correlation studies and data selection experiments across multiple models/tasks.

Result: DMC strongly correlates with reasoning distillation performance; using DMC for data selection improves performance; dynamic dataset selection based on DMC during training further enhances performance across multiple student models and tasks.

Conclusion: DMC is an effective metric for assessing dataset compatibility for reasoning distillation, providing practical guidance for data selection that can be dynamically optimized during training to achieve better distillation performance.

Abstract: Reasoning distillation transfers complex reasoning abilities from large language models (LLMs) to smaller ones, yet its success depends on how well the training data align with the student model. This paper introduces the Data-Model Compatibility (DMC) metric, which can be used to assess the suitability of a dataset for reasoning distillation on a student model. DMC provides an assessment by jointly considering data quality, relative difficulty, and student capability. We validated the effectiveness of DMC from two perspectives: (1) DMC exhibits a strong correlation with reasoning distillation performance; and (2) using DMC as the criterion for data selection leads to improved reasoning distillation performance. Both findings are consistently demonstrated across multiple student models and tasks. Moreover, since the DMC of each dataset dynamically changes during training, our experiments demonstrate that dynamically selecting datasets based on DMC can further enhance performance.

</details>


### [34] [Rethinking Literature Search Evaluation: Deep Research Helps, and Human Citation Lists Are Not a Ground Truth](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29234)
*Gaurav Sahu,Laurent Charlin,Christopher Pal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A comprehensive analysis of large-scale literature search that improves retrieval pipelines using Deep Research with breadth-first bibliography expansion (raising recall from <20% to >80%) while critically evaluating human reference lists as ground truth, revealing significant limitations in human citation quality compared to AI re-rankers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve large-scale literature search from two angles: enhancing the retrieval pipeline and critically evaluating the common practice of using human reference lists as evaluation targets, recognizing potential biases and limitations in human citation behavior.

Method: 1) Implemented a Deep Research pipeline that processes full query papers and performs breadth-first retrieval along bibliographies. 2) Used neutral LLM-as-a-judge to assess human citation relevance. 3) Analyzed the OpenAlex co-authorship graph to examine citation patterns and biases.

Result: 1) Deep Research pipeline substantially outperforms vanilla API-only search, raising recall on RollingEval-Jun25 benchmark from below 20% to above 80%. 2) Only 51% of human citations are judged moderately relevant or higher vs. 86-88% for strongest AI re-rankers. 3) Humans are 2.5x more likely than best AI re-rankers to cite direct collaborators.

Conclusion: Literature search evaluation should be multi-dimensional, incorporating recall, topical-relevance scoring, ranked-list diversity, and co-authorship-distance diagnostics as complementary measures of citation quality, rather than relying on single-axis evaluation metrics or uncritically using human reference lists as ground truth.

Abstract: We study large-scale literature search from two complementary angles: improving the retrieval pipeline, and stress-testing the human reference list as an evaluation target. First, we implement a Deep Research pipeline that processes the full query paper and expands the retrieved results breadth-first along their bibliographies, and show that it substantially outperforms vanilla API-only search, raising recall on RollingEval-Jun25 (a 250-paper literature-search benchmark) from below 20% to above 80%. Second, we use a neutral LLM-as-a-judge to determine if human references are sound ground truth for the task. We find significant limitations: only 51% of human citations are judged moderately relevant or higher, against 86--88% for the strongest AI-based re-rankers. We study this gap on the OpenAlex co-authorship graph, finding that humans are 2.5x more likely than the best AI re-rankers to cite a direct collaborator. Together, our results argue against single-axis literature-search evaluation: recall, topical-relevance scoring, ranked-list diversity, and a co-authorship-distance diagnostic each measure complementary properties of citation quality and should be reported jointly.

</details>


### [35] [Surfacing Isolated Learners with Outcome-Independent Mediation of Feedback between Teachers and Students Using AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29240)
*Junsoo Park,Youssef Medhat,Htet Phyo Wai,Ploy Thajchayapong,Ashok K. Goel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An interpretable decision layer ranks course topics needing attention using multiple classroom feedback signals (student difficulty prevalence, learner-self-report disagreement, and teacher concerns) without waiting for grades or outcome labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-augmented classrooms generate rich teacher and student feedback early in the learning process, but these signals are difficult to translate into timely instructional decisions. There's a need for transparent mechanisms that can prioritize course topics requiring attention without relying on grades or post-hoc outcome labels.

Method: Proposes an interpretable decision layer that combines three signals: 1) student learning difficulty prevalence, 2) disagreement between learner self-reports and observed difficulties, and 3) unresolved teacher concerns. The system outputs a ranked set of topic priorities with per-topic decision records explaining each ranking. Tested in a graduate CS course with n=5 instructor interviews and n=279 survey responses.

Result: Prioritized topics aligned well with instructor concerns (top-5 overlap 3/5; Spearman ρ=0.80) and student-reported topic difficulty (ρ=0.46, p=.048). Multi-signal integration identified learners not found through individual signal sources alone (AUC=0.96 vs. 0.91 for gap prevalence alone). Reflective thinking, help-seeking, and self-efficacy signals aligned with learning-related constructs.

Conclusion: Transparent coordination mechanisms using multiple classroom feedback signals can support human-AI co-agency in instructional decision-making when feedback is incomplete, allowing for timely topic prioritization without waiting for graded outcomes.

Abstract: AI-augmented classrooms generate rich teacher and student feedback before graded outcomes become available, yet these signals can be difficult to translate into timely instructional decisions. We propose an interpretable decision layer: a transparent mechanism that ranks course topics requiring attention without using grades or post-hoc outcome labels. The approach combines three signals: student learning difficulty prevalence, disagreement between learner self-reports and observed difficulties, and unresolved teacher concerns. The output is a ranked set of topic priorities with per-topic decision records explaining each ranking. In one graduate CS course offering ($n=5$ instructor interviews; $n=279$ survey responses), prioritized topics aligned with instructor concerns (top-5 overlap 3/5; Spearman $ρ=0.80$) and student-reported topic difficulty ($ρ=0.46$, $p=.048$). Multi-signal integration also surfaced learners not identified through individual signal sources alone (AUC $=0.96$ vs. $0.91$ for gap prevalence alone). Reflective thinking, help-seeking, and self-efficacy provided additional evidence that student behavioral signals align with learning-related constructs. While preliminary, these findings suggest that transparent coordination mechanisms may help support human-AI co-agency when feedback is incomplete.

</details>


### [36] [DenseSteer: Steering Small Language Models towards Dense Math Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29247)
*Yang Ouyang,Shuhang Lin,Jung-Eun Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DenseSteer: A training-free inference-time steering framework that enhances small language model reasoning by modulating internal representations toward dense reasoning patterns observed in larger models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Smaller language models (<= 3B parameters) significantly underperform larger models on multi-step reasoning tasks. Empirical analysis reveals that more proficient reasoning is associated with fewer reasoning steps but higher information density per step (Dense Reasoning).

Method: DenseSteer - a training-free inference-time steering framework that modulates small models' internal representations toward dense reasoning patterns without requiring additional training.

Result: Experiments show consistent accuracy improvements on math reasoning benchmarks without increasing token-level Negative Log-Likelihood, demonstrating effective enhancement of small-model reasoning.

Conclusion: Dense reasoning serves as an effective structural approach to mathematical problem solving, and DenseSteer provides a practical training-free method to improve small model reasoning by steering toward this pattern.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning abilities, while smaller models (<= 3B parameters) significantly underperform on multi-step reasoning tasks. Based on empirical analyses of the Qwen-2.5 model family on math reasoning benchmarks, we find that more proficient reasoning is associated with fewer reasoning steps but higher information density per step, a property we term Dense Reasoning. Motivated by this observation, we propose DenseSteer, a training-free inference-time steering framework that enhances small-model reasoning by modulating internal representations toward dense reasoning patterns. Experiments show that our method yields consistent accuracy improvements without increasing token-level Negative Log-Likelihood, highlighting dense reasoning as an effective structural approach to mathematical problem solving.

</details>


### [37] [Provably Secure Agent Guardrail](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29251)
*Benlong Wu,Weiming Zhang,Kejiang Chen,Han Fang,Nenghai Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes ePCA framework using proof-constrained actions and neural-symbolic isolation to provide deterministic security for AI agents through formal verification instead of empirical semantic guardrails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI agent defenses rely on empirical semantic guardrails and probabilistic adjudicators that fail to provide deterministic security bounds against complex semantic decoupling attacks, creating a crisis as AI gains more execution privileges.

Method: Introduces Proof-Constrained Action (ePCA) framework with neural symbolic isolation architecture that forces agents to formalize intentions into first-order logical mathematical constraints before performing physical operations, abandoning semantic trust in natural language.

Result: Empirical evaluations on 2D dynamic adversarial systems show zero attack success rate and zero false positive rate across scenarios with extremely low computational latency.

Conclusion: Provides a conditional formal foundation under explicit system assumptions and engineering paradigm for constructing underlying defense foundation for future intelligent systems based on logical reasoning limitations.

Abstract: As large language models transition from bounded generative engines to agents with expansive execution privileges, AI going out of control precipitates a fundamental crisis in artificial intelligence security. Existing defense architectures heavily rely on empirical semantic guardrails and probabilistic large model adjudicators, mechanisms that fail to provide deterministic security lower bounds when facing complex semantic symbol decoupling attacks. To overcome this empirical semantic guardrail dilemma, this paper proposes a new security paradigm for agents based on the fundamental limitations of logical reasoning. Based on this paradigm, we further introduce an executable Proof-Constrained Action (ePCA) framework with a neural symbolic isolation architecture. This framework abandons semantic trust in natural language, forcing agents to losslessly formalize their intentions into first-order logical mathematical constraints before performing physical operations. Empirical evaluations of macroscopic and microscopic two-dimensional dynamic adversarial systems demonstrate that our formal verification mechanism achieves zero attack success rate and zero false positive rate across the evaluated scenarios, with extremely low computational latency. This research provides a conditional formal foundation under explicit system assumptions and an engineering paradigm for constructing the underlying defense foundation for future intelligent systems.

</details>


### [38] [OpenClawBench: Benchmarking Process-side Anomalies in Real-world Agent Execution Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29253)
*Yibing Liu,Yangze Liu,Xiaolong Yin,Bin Wang,Chong Zhang,Hao Yin,Zhongyi Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Researchers identify the "Outcome-Process Gap" where agents pass task evaluations but still accumulate process anomalies, and introduce OpenClawBench, a large dataset for detecting these hidden failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent evaluation focuses only on final task success, missing important process-side anomalies like unresolved ambiguity, unsafe external writes, ignored errors, weakly grounded commitments, and capability-boundary overcommitment that can occur even when tasks appear successful.

Method: Created OpenClawBench - a large-scale dataset of 31,264 annotated agent execution trajectories from 6 source models. Developed FullTax system to convert trajectories into structured anomaly supervision with binary labels, evidence, onset/span localization, severity, recoverability, and a 5-class anomaly taxonomy.

Result: Among 31,135 oracle-passing executions, 2,904 (9.3%) were labeled process-anomalous, showing that success-only evaluation misses concrete process failures. A fine-tuned Gemma 3 12B detector achieved binary F1=0.729 on test data.

Conclusion: The Outcome-Process Gap is measurable and significant. OpenClawBench enables turning real agent execution logs into auditable supervision for studying, diagnosing, and monitoring runtime agent reliability beyond simple success/failure metrics.

Abstract: Task success can hide process anomalies in real-world agent executions. An agent may pass the final task oracle while still accumulating unresolved ambiguity, unsafe external writes, ignored errors, weakly grounded commitments, or capability-boundary overcommitment. We study this mismatch as the Outcome-Process Gap and introduce OpenClawBench, a large-scale dataset for measuring and supervising process-side anomalies in real agent execution processes. OpenClawBench is built from BFCL-driven OpenClaw sessions produced by 6 source models and contains 31,264 annotated trajectories. It aligns task-oracle outcomes with structured process evidence. FullTax converts the aligned trajectories into structured anomaly supervision: binary labels, supporting evidence, onset/span localization, severity, recoverability, and a 5-class anomaly taxonomy. Using OpenClawBench, we make the Outcome-Process Gap measurable. Among 31,135 oracle-passing executions, 2,904 are still labeled process-anomalous under FullTax. These results show that success-only evaluation misses a concrete class of process-side failures in real agent executions. A LoRA-fine-tuned Gemma 3 12B detector trained on the high-confidence FullTax supervised pool reaches binary F1=0.729 on the cleaner-labels held-out test split. Together, OpenClawBench turns real agent execution logs into auditable and reusable supervision for studying, diagnosing, and operationally monitoring runtime agent reliability.

</details>


### [39] [Harmonizing Real-Time Constraints and Long-Horizon Reasoning: An Asynchronous Agentic Framework for Dynamic Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29262)
*Shijie Cao,Yuan Yuan,Jing Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RACE-Sched is an asynchronous agent-based framework that combines LLM reasoning with real-time scheduling for Dynamic Flexible Job Shop problems, using dual-stream architecture to separate reactive execution from deliberative rule evolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a fundamental conflict between needing millisecond-level decisions in industrial scheduling and wanting to use LLMs for complex reasoning. Current approaches either lack flexibility (conventional rules), compromise interpretability (learning-based methods), or have latency issues (LLMs) that prevent practical application.

Method: A dual-stream asynchronous architecture: Reactive Stream uses low-latency symbolic heuristics for real-time dispatching, while parallel Deliberative Stream employs LLMs to synthesize, validate, and evolve rules. Features atomic rule updates, sandbox testing, and a semantic rule repository for transferability.

Result: Extensive evaluation on GEN-Bench, MK-Bench, and JMS-Bench shows RACE-Sched outperforms leading Deep Reinforcement Learning and other LLM-based baselines, achieving superior solution quality and robust adaptation to dynamic events.

Conclusion: RACE-Sched successfully harmonizes real-time constraints with long-horizon reasoning, demonstrating that LLMs can be practically integrated into industrial scheduling systems through an asynchronous, safety-focused architecture that maintains both performance and interpretability.

Abstract: The Dynamic Flexible Job Shop Scheduling Problem (DFJSP) necessitates a trade-off between instant reaction to stochastic disturbances and global optimization of production goals. Conventional priority rules are insufficiently flexible to handle complex disruptions, whereas learning-based approaches often compromise interpretability or fail to generalize across problem scales. Although Large Language Models (LLMs) offer advanced reasoning capabilities to bridge this gap, their substantial inference latency is incompatible with the millisecond-level decision cycles of industrial control systems. To resolve this conflict, we introduce RACE-Sched, an asynchronous agent-based framework that decouples policy execution from logical reasoning via a dual-stream architecture. The Reactive Stream executes low-latency symbolic heuristics to enable real-time dispatching, while the parallel Deliberative Stream leverages an LLM to synthesize, validate, and evolve these rules. Candidate rules undergo rigorous testing in a sandbox and are deployed via atomic updates, ensuring safety without blocking the control loop. Additionally, a semantic rule repository indexes validated heuristics for retrieval-based initialization which enhances transferability across problem scales. Extensive evaluations on GEN-Bench, MK-Bench, and JMS-Bench demonstrate that RACE-Sched outperforms leading Deep Reinforcement Learning and other LLM-based baselines. This approach harmonizes real-time constraints with long-horizon reasoning to achieve superior solution quality and robust adaptation to dynamic events.

</details>


### [40] [When and How Human Curation Backfires: Preference Alignment under Multi-Model Self-Consuming Loop](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29267)
*Yang Zhang,Xiukun Wei,Xueru Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multi-model self-consuming training can degrade long-term alignment despite human curation, unlike single-model settings where curation always improves alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current research on self-consuming training (models trained on synthetic data from prior iterations) focuses on isolated single models, but in practice models interact and train on outputs from multiple other models, creating a need to study the multi-model regime.

Method: Developed a formal framework for interacting self-consuming models, analyzed convergence properties of the resulting dynamical system, and examined self-influence and cross-influence effects of human curation on model alignment.

Result: Cross-model interactions can dampen or invert the positive alignment effects of human curation, potentially degrading long-term alignment, unlike isolated settings where human curation always enhances alignment.

Conclusion: The multi-model nature of real-world self-consuming training creates complex interactions that can undermine alignment efforts, requiring new approaches beyond single-model solutions.

Abstract: Foundation models are increasingly trained on synthetic data generated by prior model iterations rather than exclusively on real data. This self-consuming training paradigm can lead to model collapse, divergence, or bias amplification. Recent work (Ferbach et al., 2024) shows that incorporating human curation into the loop can steer a self-consuming model toward human-aligned behavior, but these analyses focus on a single, isolated model that solely consumes its own outputs. In practice, however, models often interact and train on input-output pairs produced by other models. This paper studies self-consuming training in the multi-model regime. We first formalize a framework for interacting self-consuming models and characterize when the resulting dynamical system converges to a stable point. We then examine how human curation of one model affects its own alignment (self-influence) and how such effects propagate to other models (cross-influence). Unlike isolated settings where human curation always enhances model alignment, we show that cross-model interactions can dampen or even invert this effect, ultimately degrading long-term alignment.

</details>


### [41] [Indexing the Unreadable: LLM-Native Recursive Construction and Search of Service Taxonomies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29270)
*Wei Zheng,Yang Yan,Yiyang Shao,Jinyang Li,Zeze Chang,Yukuang Jia,Qiming Mao,Chihyung Wang,Jingbin Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A2X is an LLM-native service discovery system that organizes services into a hierarchical taxonomy and performs progressive disclosure during queries, solving the Lost-in-the-Middle problem and dramatically improving retrieval accuracy with much lower token costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents need to orchestrate growing populations of MCP servers, A2A endpoints, and other services, but face a structural mismatch: context windows don't scale with service numbers. Concatenating thousands of service descriptions overflows context windows and suffers from the Lost-in-the-Middle phenomenon where models under-attend to information in long inputs.

Method: Proposed A2X (Agent-to-Anything service discovery) - an LLM-driven pipeline that automatically organizes registered services into a hierarchical taxonomy and walks it layer by layer at query time. Each LLM call only sees a small, highly relevant candidate set, decoupling effective-context scarcity from registry size.

Result: Compared to full-context dumping: achieves 6.2-point Hit Rate gain at 1/9th the prompt-token cost. Compared to state-of-the-art open-source embedding-based baseline: improves Hit Rate by more than 20 points.

Conclusion: A2X provides an effective solution to the context management problem in service discovery for the Internet of Agents era, significantly reducing token consumption while improving retrieval accuracy through LLM-native progressive disclosure.

Abstract: The era of the Internet of Agents (IoA) is taking shape: LLM agents are expected to fulfill user goals by orchestrating fast-growing populations of Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, Agent-to-Agent (A2A) endpoints, reusable skills, and other LLM-callable services. Yet LLMs face a structural mismatch with this regime: effective context is a scarce resource that does not scale with the number of services. Concatenating thousands of service descriptions into a prompt overflows the context window, and even when the window is large enough, models systematically under-attend to information in the middle of long inputs, the well-documented Lost-in-the-Middle phenomenon. This is fundamentally a question of context management for service discovery. To address this, we propose an LLM-native progressive-disclosure scheme and its concrete instantiation, A2X (Agent-to-Anything service discovery): an LLM-driven pipeline that automatically organizes the registered services into a hierarchical taxonomy and walks it layer by layer at query time, so that every LLM call sees only a small candidate set highly relevant to the user query. This decouples effective-context scarcity from registry size and significantly reduces token consumption while improving retrieval accuracy. Compared to full-context dumping, A2X achieves a 6.2-point Hit Rate gain at one-ninth the prompt-token cost; compared to the state-of-the-art open-source embedding-based baseline, A2X improves Hit Rate by more than 20 points.

</details>


### [42] [CoHyDE: Iterative Co-Training of LLM Rewriter & Dense Encoder for Tool Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29271)
*Vaishali Senthil,Ashutosh Hathidara,Sebastian Schreiber*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoHyDE: A co-training approach that jointly trains a dense encoder and LLM rewriter for tool retrieval, improving performance on both well-formed and vague queries compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool retrieval for LLM agents faces a fundamental mismatch: user queries use colloquial language while API catalogs use technical vocabulary. Existing approaches (contrastive encoder fine-tuning and HyDE-style query expansion) fail in complementary ways - each works well in some scenarios but poorly in others.

Method: CoHyDE is an iterative co-training procedure where: 1) a dense encoder is retrained with InfoNCE on catalog-style hypothetical descriptions produced by the LLM rewriter, and 2) the LLM rewriter is preference-aligned via DPO against the encoder's retrieval scores. Both components are warm-started on the tool catalog before the iterative loop begins.

Result: On a ~10k tool subset of ToolBench catalog, three rounds of CoHyDE improve over the strongest single-component baseline by +2.5 pp NDCG@5 on standard queries and +6.3 pp on held-out vague queries, with gains up to +8 pp on the hardest vague tier. Ablations show co-training is essential - using either component alone fails to match CoHyDE's performance.

Conclusion: Co-training the dense encoder and LLM rewriter as a co-evolving system significantly improves tool retrieval across both well-formed and vague queries, addressing the complementary limitations of existing approaches and providing a more robust solution for real-world LLM agent scenarios.

Abstract: Tool retrieval over large API catalogs is a core bottleneck for LLM agents: user queries arrive in colloquial, often underspecified language, while the catalog uses technical API vocabulary that no fixed encoder can bridge on its own. The two dominant training approaches, contrastive encoder fine-tuning and HyDE-style query expansion with a frozen LLM, address this problem from opposite ends and fail in complementary directions: the fine-tuned encoder excels when the query's surface form already matches the catalog but collapses when it does not, while zero-shot HyDE is more robust to underspecified queries yet generates catalog-unaware hypothetical descriptions that degrade retrieval when queries are well-formed. We introduce CoHyDE, an iterative procedure that trains the dense encoder and the LLM rewriter as a single co-evolving system: the encoder is retrained with InfoNCE on catalog-style hypothetical descriptions produced by the rewriter, and the rewriter is preference-aligned via DPO against the encoder's retrieval scores, with both sides warm-started on the tool catalog before the loop begins. On a ~10k tool subset of the ToolBench catalog, three rounds of CoHyDE improve over the strongest single-component baseline by +2.5 pp NDCG@5 on standard queries and +6.3 pp on held-out vague queries, with gains as large as +8 pp on the hardest vague tier. Ablations confirm that co-training is the key ingredient: using either component in isolation fails to match CoHyDE on both well-formed and vague queries, with losses of up to -8 pp on vague queries.

</details>


### [43] [Diagnosing Harmful Continuation in Answer-Correct Long-CoT Training Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29288)
*Chen He,Yuhao Wu,Lei Wang,Wenxuan Zhang,Fumin Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies "harmful continuation" in long chain-of-thought data - where reasoning continues after the answer is sufficiently supported, which hurts fine-tuning outcomes. Removing these post-conclusion continuations improves SFT performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand why answer-correct long chain-of-thought traces can still lead to different fine-tuning outcomes, specifically examining what happens when reasoning continues after the conclusion has already been sufficiently supported.

Method: Researchers used a delete-only editor to construct answer-preserving suffix removal from long-CoT traces, comparing SFT on original vs processed traces. They characterized removed continuations through uncertainty and hidden-state progress analysis, and developed Harmful Continuation Cut (HCC) as a lightweight boundary proxy.

Result: SFT outcomes improved after removing editor-identified post-conclusion continuations, showing these continuations are harmful to training. Analysis revealed persistent local uncertainty with weakened terminal-directional progress (uncertainty-geometry mismatch). HCC effectively approximates the harmful continuation boundary.

Conclusion: Post-conclusion continuations in long chain-of-thought data are empirically harmful for SFT training. The paper provides both empirical evidence of this harm and a characterization of the phenomenon through uncertainty and progress analysis, offering HCC as a practical solution.

Abstract: Long chain-of-thought (CoT) traces are widely used as supervision for reasoning-oriented LLM SFT, yet answer-correct traces can still lead to markedly different fine-tuning outcomes. We study post-conclusion continuation in answer-correct long-CoT data: a continuation where the answer appears sufficiently supported, but the trace continues with additional reasoning that remains in the supervised target. To test its training effect, we use a delete-only editor to construct answer-preserving suffix removal and compare CoT-based SFT on the original and processed traces. We observe improved SFT outcomes after removing the editor-identified post-conclusion continuation, suggesting that this continuation is harmful to training in our setting. We therefore refer to this empirically supported phenomenon as harmful continuation. Beyond this intervention, we further characterize the removed post-conclusion continuation through uncertainty and hidden-state progress. We observe persistent local uncertainty together with weakened terminal-directional progress, forming an uncertainty--geometry mismatch. Finally, we instantiate Harmful Continuation Cut (HCC), a lightweight boundary proxy that approximates the editor-identified post-conclusion continuation boundary.

</details>


### [44] [Entropy-KL Divergence-based Token Masking: A Novel Approach for Selective Fine-tuning of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29303)
*Qi Liu,Mingdi Sun,Yongyi He,Zhi Zheng,Tong Xu,Yi Zheng,Zhefeng Wang,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EKSFT is a selective fine-tuning method that masks high-uncertainty tokens during supervised fine-tuning to preserve pre-trained model distribution while activating task-relevant capabilities, leading to better RL exploration and overall performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SFT+RL paradigm suffers from distribution shift when using limited SFT data, causing models to overfit to few samples and lose pre-trained knowledge, which impairs RL exploration efficiency.

Method: EKSFT (Entropy-KL Selective Fine-Tuning) selectively masks tokens that show high entropy or high KL divergence from a reference model, focusing imitation only on confident, distribution-preserving tokens to inject task knowledge without disrupting pre-trained distribution.

Result: EKSFT outperforms standard SFT on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, and RL fine-tuning from EKSFT-initialized models yields consistently better post-RL performance, demonstrating improved exploration capabilities.

Conclusion: In low-data SFT regimes, prioritizing activation of task-relevant capabilities over memorization through selective token masking (EKSFT) preserves pre-trained distributions and enables more effective RL exploration, leading to superior overall performance.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by reinforcement learning (RL) has become a standard post-training paradigm for large language models. This paradigm provides a cold-start for RL exploration, avoiding the inefficiency of pure RL where on-policy sampling yields insufficient positive samples. However, in practice, existing approaches often use a small amount of data for SFT initialization compared to the RL phase, which can cause the model to fit the limited samples and shift away from its pre-trained distribution. This distribution shift impedes the model's ability to effectively explore during subsequent RL training. To address this challenge, we propose that in low-data regimes, SFT should prioritize activating task-relevant capabilities rather than memorizing specific content. Along this line, we propose EKSFT (Entropy-KL Selective Fine-Tuning), which selectively masks tokens that exhibit either high entropy or high KL divergence from a reference model. By excluding these high-uncertainty, distribution-shifting tokens from imitation, EKSFT injects task-specific knowledge while preserving the integrity of the model's pre-trained distribution. Empirical evaluations on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that EKSFT consistently outperforms standard SFT. Further RL fine-tuning from the EKSFT model yields consistently better post-RL performance, indicating improved exploration for the RL stage. Our codes and datasets are available at https://github.com/MINE-USTC/EKSFT.

</details>


### [45] [Rubric-Guided Process Reward for Stepwise Model Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29310)
*Shenghao Ye,Yu Guo,Zhengheng Li,Shuangwu Chen,Jian Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RoRo introduces a process reward framework for stepwise model routing that combines outcome-based rewards with process-based evaluation using query-specific rubrics, outperforming previous RL-based routing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current model routing methods use reinforcement learning with only outcome rewards (final answer correctness), which fails to evaluate intermediate routing decisions, leading to weak performance and poor generalization.

Method: RoRo collects diverse routing trajectories and constructs preference pairs based on outcome, cost, and process quality. It trains a Rubricor to generate query-specific evaluation rubrics and a Judge to score trajectories under these rubrics through alternating optimization. The process rewards are combined with outcome rewards to optimize routing policy via GRPO.

Result: Experiments on five reasoning benchmarks under both same-family and cross-family settings show RoRo consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves better accuracy and cost trade-offs.

Conclusion: Process rewards significantly improve stepwise model routing by providing better evaluation of intermediate decisions, leading to more effective and generalizable routing policies than outcome-only reward methods.

Abstract: Stepwise model routing improves the efficiency of Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) by assigning each reasoning step to a suitable model. Recent methods formulate routing as a sequential decision process and train the router with reinforcement learning. However, although they model routing as a process, they still supervise the router with outcome rewards. Such rewards only reflect final answer correctness and fail to evaluate intermediate routing decisions, which can weaken performance and generalization. To address this gap, we propose RoRo, a rubric-guided process reward framework for stepwise model routing. RoRo first collects diverse routing trajectories and constructs preference pairs based on outcome, cost, and process quality. It then trains a Rubricor to generate a query-specific evaluation rubric and a Judge to score routing trajectories under this rubric through alternating optimization. The resulting process rewards are combined with outcome rewards to optimize the routing policy via GRPO. Experiments on five reasoning benchmarks under both same-family and cross-family settings show that RoRo consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves better accuracy and cost trade-offs.

</details>


### [46] [ConMoE: Expert-Pool Consolidation via Prototype Reassignment for MoE Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29350)
*Yilun Yao,Jiaming Pan,Elsie Dai,Peizhuang Cong,Yaoming Li,Tong Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ConMoE is a train-free compression method for Mixture-of-Experts language models that consolidates expert pools via prototype remapping without weight updates, achieving competitive performance at reduced expert counts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE models reduce computation costs but still require storing and serving all experts, making deployment memory-intensive. Existing compression methods rely on pruning experts or merging weights, which may not be optimal.

Method: Formulates MoE compression as expert-pool consolidation: selects a smaller set of pretrained experts as reusable prototypes, deterministically remaps original expert references to selected prototypes without weight updates or fine-tuning. Uses calibration-based contribution and replaceability signals for expert selection.

Result: ConMoE matches or outperforms pruning and merging baselines on three pretrained MoE models. Achieves best average score on deepseek-moe-16b-base at 25% and 50% routed-expert reduction, while remaining competitive on Qwen3-30B-A3B and OLMoE-1B-7B-0125.

Conclusion: Deterministic prototype remapping is an effective train-free compression approach for MoE models, with deterministic reassignment being the most stable component and broader cross-layer sharing/model-dependent benefits requiring further optimization.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models reduce per-token computation but still require storing and serving all experts, making deployment memory-intensive. Existing post-training compression methods mainly shrink this cost by pruning experts or merging their weights. We formulate post-training MoE compression as expert-pool consolidation: retaining a smaller set of pretrained experts as reusable prototypes and deterministically remapping each original expert reference to one selected prototype. This view separates the reduced expert pool from the reuse structure that represents the original expert slots, and allows prototype sharing within local layer scopes while preserving the original router interface. We propose ConMoE, a train-free prototype remapping framework that selects retained experts using calibration-based contribution and replaceability signals, then redirects original expert calls to the selected prototypes without weight updates or post-compression fine-tuning. Experiments on three pretrained MoE language models show that ConMoE matches or outperforms strong pruning and merging baselines in several settings, achieving the best average score on deepseek-moe-16b-base at both 25% and 50% routed-expert reduction, while remaining competitive on Qwen3-30B-A3B and OLMoE-1B-7B-0125. Ablations indicate that deterministic reassignment is the most stable component, whereas broader cross-layer sharing and post-hoc weight fusion are model-dependent.

</details>


### [47] [PassNet: Scaling Large Language Models for Graph Compiler Pass Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29357)
*Yiqun Liu,Yingsheng Wu,Ruqi Yang,Enrong Zheng,Honglei Qiu,Sijun He,Tai Liang,Jingjing Wu,Yuhan Zhou,Yiwei Zhang,Dongyan Chen,Weihan Yi,Xinqi Li,Siqi Bao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PassNet is a system for LLM-based compiler pass generation that addresses performance issues in long-tail workloads by having LLMs author structured graph transformations that integrate into compiler pipelines, demonstrating significant speedups on individual subgraphs while identifying consistency as the main bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern tensor compilers like TorchInductor face systematic performance ceilings on long-tail workloads, with 43% of real-world subgraphs experiencing end-to-end slowdowns under default compilation. While LLMs could help automate optimization, existing approaches focus only on standalone kernel generation rather than the more appropriate abstraction of compiler pass generation.

Method: PassNet creates a large-scale ecosystem for LLM-based compiler pass generation consisting of: (1) PassNet-Dataset with over 18K unique computational graphs from 100K real-world models, and (2) PassBench with 200 curated long-tail fusible tasks (2,060 subgraphs) evaluated using Error-aware Speedup Score (ES_t) that unifies correctness, stability, and performance, with layered integrity defenses against LLM exploitation.

Result: The best frontier model trails TorchInductor by 37% in aggregate, but on individual subgraphs LLMs achieve up to 3x speedup over the same compiler, indicating that consistency rather than capability is the bottleneck. Fine-tuning a small model on ~4K PassNet trajectories yields a 2.67x improvement approaching frontier-model performance.

Conclusion: PassNet demonstrates substantial headroom for LLM-driven compiler optimization and serves as live training infrastructure for advancing this field, with all data, benchmarks, and tooling publicly available to enable further research.

Abstract: Modern tensor compilers such as TorchInductor deliver substantial speedups on mainstream models, yet face a systematic performance ceiling on long-tail workloads -- our profiling shows that 43% of real-world subgraphs experience end-to-end slowdowns under default compilation. While LLMs offer a path toward automated optimization, existing efforts focus on standalone kernel generation. We argue that pass generation -- where LLMs author structured graph transformations that integrate directly into compiler pipelines -- is the more appropriate abstraction. We propose PassNet, the first large-scale ecosystem for LLM-based compiler pass generation, comprising: (1) PassNet-Dataset, over 18K unique computational graphs from 100K real-world models; and (2) PassBench, 200 curated long-tail fusible tasks (comprising 2,060 subgraphs in total) evaluated under the Error-aware Speedup Score (ES_t) -- a metric unifying correctness, stability, and performance -- with layered integrity defenses against systematic LLM exploitation. Experiments reveal that PassBench is both highly discriminative and genuinely unsaturated: the best frontier model trails TorchInductor by 37% in aggregate, yet on individual subgraphs LLMs achieve up to 3x speedup over the same compiler -- indicating that the bottleneck is consistency, not capability. Fine-tuning a small model on merely ~4K PassNet trajectories yields a 2.67x improvement approaching frontier-model performance, demonstrating substantial headroom and validating PassNet as live training infrastructure for advancing LLM-driven compiler optimization. All data, benchmarks, and tooling are publicly available.

</details>


### [48] [Scaling Monosemanticity: Extracting Interpretable Features from Claude 3 Sonnet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29358)
*Adly Templeton,Tom Conerly,Jonathan Marcus,Jack Lindsey,Trenton Bricken,Brian Chen,Adam Pearce,Craig Citro,Emmanuel Ameisen,Andy Jones,Hoagy Cunningham,Nicholas L Turner,Callum McDougall,Monte MacDiarmid,Alex Tamkin,Esin Durmus,Tristan Hume,Francesco Mosconi,C. Daniel Freeman,Theodore R. Sumers,Edward Rees,Joshua Batson,Adam Jermyn,Shan Carter,Chris Olah,Tom Henighan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Sparse autoencoders can extract interpretable features from Claude 3 Sonnet at production scale, discovering features ranging from concrete entities to abstract concepts and harmful behaviors, enabling causal steering of model outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address whether dictionary learning methods like sparse autoencoders can scale beyond small transformers to production-scale language models like Claude 3 Sonnet, and to extract interpretable features that could help understand model computations and identify potentially harmful capabilities.

Method: Trained sparse autoencoders with up to 34 million features on Claude 3 Sonnet's middle layer residual stream, using scaling laws for hyperparameter selection. The approach involves text-only training but demonstrates generalization to multimodal contexts.

Result: Successfully extracted multilingual, multimodal features that generalize to images despite text-only training. Features represent both concrete entities (famous people/locations) and abstract concepts (sarcasm, code errors). Identified harmful features (deception, power-seeking, sycophancy, bias) that causally influence model outputs when manipulated. Features can steer model behavior consistently with their interpretations.

Conclusion: Sparse autoencoders scale to production models and extract meaningful interpretable features, including potentially harmful ones, enabling causal intervention. However, feature sets remain incomplete, and rigorous evaluation methods for faithfully capturing model computations are lacking, representing significant limitations.

Abstract: We demonstrate that sparse autoencoders can extract interpretable features from Claude 3 Sonnet, a production-scale language model, addressing the open question of whether dictionary learning methods scale beyond small transformers. We trained sparse autoencoders with up to 34 million features on the model's middle layer residual stream, using scaling laws to guide hyperparameter selection. The resulting features are multilingual and multimodal (generalizing to images despite text-only training), respond to both concrete instances and abstract discussions of concepts, and can be used to steer model behavior in ways consistent with their interpretations. We find features corresponding to famous entities and locations, as well as more abstract concepts like sarcasm or errors in code. We also identify features relevant to ways in which language models might cause harm--including features representing deception, power-seeking, sycophancy, and bias--and show that these causally influence model outputs when manipulated. Additionally, we conduct analyses of feature interpretability, geometry, and computational function. However, significant limitations remain: our suite of features is incomplete, and we lack rigorous methods for evaluating whether our features faithfully capture model computations.

</details>


### [49] [MiraBench: Evaluating Action-Conditioned Reliability in Robotic World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29360)
*Tianzhuo Yang,Zihan Shen,Zirui Mi,Zhaoyi Zhang,Jiayi Zhou,Jiaming Ji,Juntao Dai,Jiawei Chen,Boyuan Chen,Yaodong Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MiraBench is a new benchmark for evaluating action-conditioned reliability of robotic world models, focusing on physical plausibility, action following, and failure detection rather than just visual fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current world model evaluations emphasize visual fidelity but lack assessment of whether predictions are physically plausible, faithful to commanded actions, and calibrated to failure when actions should not succeed. There's a need for benchmarks that specifically evaluate action-conditioned reliability in robotic simulation.

Method: MiraBench defines three hierarchical levels of evaluation: 1) Physics Adherence (reference-free physical consistency), 2) Action-Following Fidelity (respect for task-relevant actions), and 3) Optimism Bias Detection (tendency to predict successful outcomes under failure-inducing actions). The authors curate a human-annotated corpus with over 16,000 judgments across tasks, failure categories, and leading world models, evaluating 12 representative model configurations.

Result: Key findings: 1) Visual fidelity is a poor proxy for action fidelity, 2) Increasing model scale does not reliably improve action following, and 3) Optimism bias is pervasive across current systems. The benchmark reveals limitations in existing world models' ability to serve as faithful simulators for robot learning.

Conclusion: MiraBench shifts evaluation from appearance to action-conditioned reliability, providing a diagnostic foundation for assessing and improving robotic world models as faithful simulators, addressing critical gaps in current evaluation methodologies.

Abstract: Action-conditioned world models are increasingly used as scalable simulators for robot learning, yet current evaluations provide limited evidence that their predictions are reliable under the actions they condition on. Existing benchmarks largely emphasize visual fidelity, leaving unclear whether predicted futures are physically plausible, faithful to commanded actions, and calibrated to failure when actions should not succeed. We introduce \textsc{MiraBench}, a hierarchical benchmark that defines \emph{action-conditioned reliability} as a core evaluation target for robotic world models. MiraBench decomposes this target into three progressively demanding levels: \emph{Physics Adherence}, which evaluates reference-free physical consistency; \emph{Action-Following Fidelity}, which measures whether predictions respect task-relevant action inputs; and \emph{Optimism Bias Detection}, which probes the tendency to predict successful outcomes under failure-inducing actions. To support this evaluation, we curate a human-annotated corpus with over 16,000 judgments across tasks, failure categories, and leading world models. We evaluate 12 representative model configurations spanning vector-conditioned robotic world models, text-conditioned generative world models, open-weight systems, closed-source systems, and multiple model scales. Across this broad model landscape, MiraBench reveals three central findings: visual fidelity is a poor proxy for action fidelity; increasing model scale does not reliably improve action following; and optimism bias is pervasive across current systems. By shifting evaluation from appearance to action-conditioned reliability, MiraBench provides a diagnostic foundation for assessing and improving robotic world models as faithful simulators.

</details>


### [50] [EvoMD-LLM: Learning the Language of Species Evolution in Reactive Molecular Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29394)
*Zhichen Tang,Zhengzheng Dang,Yulin Chen,Jixin Wu,Haiwen Li,Yanming Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EvoMD-LLM transforms molecular dynamics into a symbolic temporal language modeling problem, enabling LLMs to learn dynamic physical processes through autoregressive fine-tuning with temporal scaffolding, achieving better accuracy than traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models excel at static reasoning but struggle with modeling temporal structure in dynamic physical processes, particularly in molecular dynamics where understanding species evolution over time is crucial.

Method: EvoMD-LLM reformulates reactive molecular dynamics trajectories as sequences of molecular events, where each token represents a chemical species with persistence duration. The approach uses temporal scaffolding that treats event duration as explicit linguistic tokens, serving as structured inductive bias for autoregressive fine-tuning of standard LLMs.

Result: The model achieves up to 66.14% accuracy on temporal prediction tasks, consistently outperforming sequential neural networks and language-based baselines. Qualitatively, it can generate interpretations for its predictions by incorporating chemical knowledge without explicit supervision on trajectory-explanation pairs.

Conclusion: Symbolic temporal language modeling provides an effective framework for grounding LLMs in dynamic physical simulations, enabling them to learn compositional evolution over time and reduce hallucinations in molecular dynamics predictions.

Abstract: While large language models (LLMs) excel at static scientific reasoning, they struggle to model the temporal structure of dynamic physical processes. We present EvoMD-LLM (Evolutionary Molecular Dynamics Large Language Model), a framework that reformulates species-level molecular dynamics as a symbolic temporal language modeling problem. Reactive MD trajectories are discretized into sequences of molecular events, where each token represents a chemical species augmented with its persistence duration, enabling standard autoregressive LLMs to learn compositional evolution over time through efficient fine-tuning. A key component of EvoMD-LLM is temporal scaffolding, which treats event duration as an explicit linguistic token and serves as a structured inductive bias, significantly reducing invalid or hallucinated molecular outputs compared to conventional sequence modeling approaches. We evaluate EvoMD-LLM on multiple temporal prediction tasks, achieving up to 66.14% accuracy and consistently outperforming sequential neural networks and language-based baselines. Beyond quantitative improvements, we qualitatively observe that the model is capable of generating interpretations for its own predictions by incorporating relevant chemical knowledge, even though it was not explicitly supervised with paired trajectory-explanation data. These results demonstrate that symbolic temporal language modeling provides an effective framework for grounding LLMs in dynamic physical simulations.

</details>


### [51] [Aligned but Fragile: Enhancing LLM Safety Robustness via Zeroth-Order Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29396)
*Zhihao Liu,Yifan Wu,Jian Lou,Di Wang,Yuxi Zhou,Yuke Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hybrid optimization framework that combines first-order safety alignment with zeroth-order refinement to improve robustness against lightweight post-alignment manipulations while preserving safety behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current safety alignment for LLMs is fragile and can be easily weakened by lightweight manipulations like parameter noise, activation noise, or quantization. Prior robustness improvements focused on data, objectives, and parameter identification, leaving the optimizer's role unexplored.

Method: Proposes a hybrid framework: 1) Perform standard first-order safety alignment, 2) Apply zeroth-order refinement using perturbation-based evaluations to enhance robustness. Improves efficiency by estimating layer-wise robustness sensitivity to concentrate updates on robustness-critical layers.

Result: Theoretical and empirical evidence shows only a few zeroth-order refinement steps can enhance robustness while preserving safety alignment. The method improves efficiency by focusing on robustness-critical layers with modest training overhead.

Conclusion: Zeroth-order optimization provides a promising direction for improving robustness of safety-aligned LLMs against post-alignment manipulations, offering a new optimizer-centric approach that complements existing robustness enhancement techniques.

Abstract: Safety alignment for large language models (LLMs) aims to reduce harmful or unsafe behavior while preserving general utility. However, recent findings reveal that alignment effects can be fragile: lightweight post-alignment manipulations, such as parameter noise, activation noise, or quantization, can easily weaken the intended safety behavior. Prior efforts to improve robustness have primarily focused on data curation, modified alignment objectives, and safety-critical parameter identification, leaving the role of the optimizer itself largely unexplored.
