  In this paper, we are the first to study the robustness of safety alignment from the perspective of the base optimizer. This optimizer-centric view naturally points to zeroth-order optimization, which provides a robustness-oriented signal by evaluating safety alignment under perturbations. Based on this insight, we propose a hybrid framework that first performs standard first-order safety alignment and then applies zeroth-order refinement to improve robustness. Both theoretically and empirically, we show that only a few zeroth-order refinement steps can enhance robustness while preserving safety alignment. We further improve the efficiency of zeroth-order refinement by exploiting its inherent perturbation-based evaluations to estimate layer-wise robustness sensitivity, enabling the refinement process to concentrate updates on robustness-critical layers with modest training overhead.

</details>


### [52] [Architecture-Sensitive Supervised Fine-Tuning for Screen-Conditioned Action Prediction: A PiSAR Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29400)
*Rahul Bissa,Abhishek Vyas,Yash Jain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Fine-tuning significantly outperforms zero-shot frontier models on PiSAR benchmark, but success depends heavily on model architecture with some models resisting improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether supervised fine-tuning can outperform frontier zero-shot models on screen-anchored behavioral rationale tasks, and understand which models are amenable to fine-tuning improvements.

Method: Benchmarked three supervised fine-tuned models against frontier zero-shot baselines (Claude Opus 4.7 and GPT-5.5) on a 661-row slice of PiSAR dataset, using same scoring pipeline for all models.

Result: 1) Fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct achieved sem_sim 0.783 vs 0.459/0.482 for frontier zero-shot models, with 79% rows clearing 0.7 threshold vs 1-2% for baselines. 2) Same training on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-IT scored only 0.441, similar to zero-shot baselines, showing recipe-vs-model mismatch.

Conclusion: Fine-tuning can dramatically outperform zero-shot frontier models on specific tasks, but success depends on model architecture - some reasoning-tuned models resist displacement and would need more data or stronger fine-tuning methods.

Abstract: We benchmark three supervised fine-tuned models against frontier zero-shot baselines on a 661-row held-out slice of PiSAR (Persona, intent, Screen, Action, Rationale), a 12,929-tuple corpus of screen-anchored behavioural rationales curated from public app-store reviews, Pew American Trends Panel demographics, and the OPeRA shopper traces. Every model, frontier or fine-tuned, is evaluated on the same 661-row slice with the same scoring pipeline. Two findings. First, frontier zero-shot baselines (Claude Opus 4.7 and GPT-5.5) reach sem_sim 0.459 and 0.482 respectively; a fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct reaches 0.783 and clears sem_sim >= 0.7 on 79% of rows, against 1-2% for either frontier baseline, a gap of 0.30 absolute on the same test set. Second, the same training data and recipe on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-IT scores only 0.441, in the same band as the frontier zero-shot baselines rather than the fine-tuned Qwen. We read this as a recipe-vs-model mismatch: the reasoning-tuned high-parameter model resists displacement and would likely need either more data or a stronger fine-tuning method.

</details>


### [53] [When Does Persona Prompting Actually Help? A Retrieval and Metric Analysis of Expert Role Injection in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29420)
*Shuai Xiao,Su Liu,Weikai Zhou,Jialun Wu,Xinjie He,Zhiyuan Lin,Qiyang Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Persona prompting doesn't consistently improve LLM response quality overall, but systematically trades expertise depth for clarity, with effects highly dependent on question type and domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior evaluations of persona prompting use aggregate scores, making it unclear whether expert-role prompting actually improves response quality or just changes responses along different quality dimensions. The practical value of persona prompting remains uncertain.

Method: Controlled comparison of four prompting conditions across 1,140 open-ended questions spanning 38 expert roles and six domains: (1) no role prompt, (2) generic domain-expert prompt, (3) embedding-based role retrieval, and (4) hybrid retrieval combining embedding search with LLM-based role selection.

Result: Aggregate results show only small overall differences, but metric-level analysis reveals a consistent tradeoff: role prompting systematically increases expertise depth while reducing clarity. Effects are highly conditional - works best for advisory questions in medicine/psychology, while baseline performs better for conceptual/explanatory questions in finance, legal, science, technology. Hybrid retrieval improves over embedding-only selection but doesn't eliminate the tradeoff.

Conclusion: Persona prompting primarily reshapes response characteristics rather than broadly improving capability. Multi-metric evaluation is necessary to understand its effects, as different prompting strategies create tradeoffs between expertise depth and clarity depending on domain and question type.

Abstract: Persona prompting is widely used to steer large language models, yet its practical value remains unclear. Prior work often evaluates persona prompting using aggregate scores, making it difficult to determine whether expert-role prompting consistently improves response quality or instead changes responses along different quality dimensions. We study this question through a controlled comparison of four prompting conditions across 1,140 open-ended questions spanning 38 expert roles and six domains: no role prompt, a generic domain-expert prompt, embedding-based role retrieval, and a hybrid retrieval method combining embedding search with LLM-based role selection. Aggregate results show only small overall differences between conditions. However, metric-level analysis reveals a consistent tradeoff that aggregate averages obscure: role prompting systematically increases expertise depth while reducing clarity. These effects are highly conditional rather than universal. Role prompting performs best on advisory questions and in domains such as medicine and psychology, where structured expert framing and risk communication are intrinsically valuable. In contrast, baseline prompting performs better on conceptual and explanatory questions in finance, legal, science, and technology domains, where concise plain-language explanation is more important. We further show that hybrid retrieval significantly improves over embedding-only role selection, although better role retrieval does not eliminate the broader expertise-depth versus clarity tradeoff. Overall, our findings suggest that persona prompting primarily reshapes response characteristics rather than broadly improving capability, and that multi-metric evaluation is necessary for understanding its effects.

</details>


### [54] [ReasonLight: A Multimodal Foundation Model-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning Framework for Zero-Shot Traffic Signal Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29425)
*Aoyu Pang,Maonan Wang,Yuejiao Xie,Chung Shue Chen,Zhiwei Yang,Man-On Pun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReasonLight is a multimodal foundation model-enhanced RL framework for zero-shot traffic signal control that integrates sensor data, multi-view camera observations, and RL decisions to adapt to unseen events without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RL-based traffic signal control fails to respond to observable real-world events not seen in training data, despite IoT-enabled intersections providing rich sensor and camera data that could enable better adaptability.

Method: ReasonLight integrates structured traffic measurements, multi-view camera observations, and candidate phase decisions from a pre-trained RL controller. It extracts visual semantics from cameras, aligns them with sensor-derived scene descriptions, and uses a semantic-guided refinement module to preserve or adjust RL-proposed actions based on traffic rules and event semantics.

Result: ReasonLight achieves zero-shot adaptation without retraining, reducing emergency vehicle waiting time by up to 88.7% compared to RL-only approaches while maintaining comparable routine traffic performance.

Conclusion: Multimodal foundation models can enhance RL-based traffic signal control by enabling zero-shot adaptation to unseen events, combining the strengths of RL optimization with semantic understanding of real-world traffic situations.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in traffic signal control (TSC). However, its reliance on predefined states limits responsiveness to observable open-world events that are absent from training data. IoT-enabled intersections provide heterogeneous observations from roadside sensors and cameras, creating opportunities to improve RL adaptability to such events. To this end, we propose ReasonLight, a multimodal foundation model-enhanced RL framework for zero-shot TSC. ReasonLight integrates three sources of information: structured traffic measurements, multi-view camera observations, and candidate phase decisions from a pre-trained RL controller. Given an RL-proposed phase, ReasonLight extracts visual semantics from multi-view images and aligns them with compact sensor-derived scene descriptions. This alignment enables a semantic-guided refinement module to either preserve or adjust the proposed action according to traffic rules and event semantics. To ensure operational reliability, refined actions are constrained by the set of available phases. Any invalid decision is rejected, and the system falls back to the original RL action. We evaluate ReasonLight on two types of rare events not seen during RL training: emergency vehicle priority and temporary traffic regulation. Experimental results show that ReasonLight achieves zero-shot adaptation without retraining. It reduces emergency vehicle waiting time by up to 88.7% compared with the RL-only backbone while preserving comparable routine traffic performance.

</details>


### [55] [Towards Human-Like Interactive Speech Recognition With Agentic Correction and Semantic Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29430)
*Zixuan Jiang,Yanqiao Zhu,Peng Wang,Qinyuan Chen,Xinjian Zhao,Xipeng Qiu,Wupeng Wang,Zhifu Gao,Xiangang Li,Kai Yu,Xie Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Agentic ASR, a closed-loop interactive ASR framework that uses iterative refinement to reduce semantic errors, along with a new semantic evaluation metric S²ER and interactive simulation system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current single-pass ASR systems are poorly aligned with human communication where misunderstandings are resolved through iterative clarification, and traditional token-level metrics like WER/CER cannot adequately capture semantic errors that affect meaning-critical understanding.

Method: Proposes Agentic ASR, a closed-loop framework combining single-pass ASR front-end with semantic correction, intent routing, and reasoning-based editing. Also introduces Sentence-level Semantic Error Rate (S²ER) as an LLM-based semantic evaluation metric and an Interactive Simulation System for scalable benchmarking.

Result: Experiments on multilingual, named-entity-intensive, and code-switching benchmarks show iterative interaction consistently reduces semantic errors, with much larger gains in S²ER than in conventional token-level metrics. Human-AI alignment and ablation studies validate the reliability of the semantic judge and framework robustness.

Conclusion: Interactive ASR with the proposed Agentic ASR framework and S²ER metric enables more human-aligned speech recognition that reduces semantic errors through iterative refinement, addressing limitations of current single-pass ASR systems.

Abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) is a core component of human--computer interaction and an increasingly important front-end for LLM-based assistants and agents. However, most current ASR systems still follow a single-pass paradigm, which is poorly aligned with human communication, where misunderstandings are resolved through iterative clarification and refinement. This mismatch makes it difficult to correct meaning-critical errors once they occur. Meanwhile, token-level metrics such as WER or CER cannot adequately reflect such a problem. To address these limitations, we formulate \emph{Interactive ASR} as a multi-turn refinement task and propose \textbf{Agentic ASR}, a closed-loop framework that combines a single-pass ASR front-end with semantic correction, intent routing, and reasoning-based editing. We further introduce the \textbf{Sentence-level Semantic Error Rate} ($S^2ER$), an LLM-based semantic evaluation metric, together with an \textbf{Interactive Simulation System} for scalable and reproducible benchmarking. Experiments on multilingual, named-entity-intensive, and code-switching benchmarks show that iterative interaction consistently reduces semantic errors, with much larger gains in $S^2ER$ than in conventional token-level metrics. Human--AI alignment and ablation studies further validate the reliability of the semantic judge and the robustness of the proposed framework. The code is available at: https://interactiveasr.github.io/ and the live demo is available at https://i-asr.sjtuxlance.com/

</details>


### [56] [CrystalXRD-Bench: Benchmarking Vision-Language Models for XRD Peak Indexing Across Diverse Crystalline Materials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29446)
*Chengliang Xu,Xiaogang Li,Peiyao Xiao,Beng Wang,Hu Wei,Bing Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A benchmark called CrystalXRD-Bench evaluates vision-language models on extracting crystallographic information from powder XRD patterns, revealing significant limitations in current models' ability to perform multi-step crystallographic reasoning from scientific figures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal benchmarks don't test the specific capabilities needed for Miller-index identification from powder XRD patterns, which requires both reading narrow peak locations from rendered scientific curves and connecting those observations to multi-step crystallographic reasoning.

Method: Created CrystalXRD-Bench, a 250-sample benchmark built from 10 public crystallographic databases for a single task: recovering the full set of HKLs (Miller indices) contributing to the highest-intensity peak in an XRD pattern. Each sample pairs rendered XRD images with source CIF text and chemical formula, allowing examination of both visual extraction and reasoning errors. Evaluated seven vision-language models on this benchmark.

Result: Best performing model (GPT-5.4) achieved Jaccard score of 0.5888 with exact-match rate of 37.6%, but six of seven models remained below Jaccard 0.50, indicating the task is far from solved. Error patterns showed systematic variations: double-peak cases are brittle, recall-heavy models over-predict HKLs for coverage, and access to CIF text doesn't close the gap in crystallographic calculation.

Conclusion: The benchmark not only provides model rankings but also identifies specific conditions under which current vision-language models fail on quantitative scientific figures. The data and evaluation code will be made publicly available to advance research in this area.

Abstract: Miller-index identification from powder XRD patterns requires capabilities untested by existing multimodal benchmarks: the model must read a narrow peak location from a rendered scientific curve and then connect that observation to multi-step crystallographic reasoning. We introduce CrystalXRD-Bench, a 250-sample benchmark built from 10 public crystallographic databases for a single task: recover the full set of HKLs contributing to the highest-intensity peak in an XRD pattern. Each sample pairs the rendered XRD image with the source CIF text and chemical formula, so visual extraction errors and reasoning errors can be examined side by side. We evaluate seven vision-language models. The best Jaccard score is 0.5888 (GPT-5.4) with an exact-match rate of 37.6%, yet six of seven models remain below Jaccard 0.50; the task is far from solved. Error patterns vary systematically: double-peak cases are especially brittle, recall-heavy models gain coverage by over-predicting HKLs, and access to CIF text does not close the gap in crystallographic calculation. Alongside model rankings, the benchmark identifies the conditions under which current VLMs fail on quantitative scientific figures. All data and evaluation code will be publicly available.

</details>


### [57] [VitalAgent: A Tool-Augmented Agent for Reactive and Proactive Physiological Monitoring over Wearable Health Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29483)
*Di Zhu,Yu Yvonne Wu,Hong Jia,Aaqib Saeed,Vassilis Kostakos,Ting Dang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VitalAgent is a tool-augmented agentic framework for ECG/PPG-based mHealth that supports both reactive QA and proactive monitoring using longitudinal physiological memory and dynamic computation tools, achieving significant improvements over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mHealth systems are limited to task-specific prediction or static QA, lacking temporal reasoning, persistent physiological context, and proactive monitoring capabilities for continuous physiological signals like ECG/PPG.

Method: Proposed VitalAgent framework built on longitudinal physiological memory and tool-augmented reasoning interface for dynamic computation over raw signals. Also introduced VitalBench dataset with 1,862 QA pairs for reactive evaluation and 90.2 hours of ECG/PPG recordings for proactive monitoring.

Result: VitalAgent achieves over 30% improvement over prompt-based and ReAct baselines in reactive evaluation and successfully supports proactive alert monitoring over long-term physiological signals.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates the importance of dynamic tool use and long-term physiological monitoring in mHealth systems, enabling both reactive and proactive healthcare applications.

Abstract: Wearable devices enable continuous monitoring of physiological signals such as ECG and PPG, but existing mHealth systems are largely limited to task-specific prediction pipelines or reactive question answering over static summaries. They lack the ability to support temporal reasoning, persistent physiological context, and proactive monitoring over long-term signal streams. We propose VitalAgent, a tool-augmented agentic framework for ECG/PPG-based mHealth that supports both reactive question answering and proactive monitoring. VitalAgent is built on a longitudinal physiological memory and a tool-augmented reasoning interface that enables dynamic computation over raw signals. We further introduce VitalBench, a longitudinal physiological monitoring benchmark dataset comprising 1,862 QA pairs for reactive question answering and 90.2 hours of continuous ECG/PPG recordings for proactive monitoring, covering cardiac, physical activity, and stress-related tasks. Experiments demonstrate that VitalAgent achieves over 30% improvement over prompt-based and ReAct baselines in reactive evaluation and supports proactive alert monitoring over long-term physiological signals, highlighting the importance of dynamic tool use and long-term physiological monitoring.

</details>


### [58] [The Curse of Helpfulness: Inverse Scaling Law in Robustness to Distractor Instructions via DistractionIF](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29491)
*Zeli Su,Zhankai Xu,Tianlei Chen,Longfei Zheng,Xiaolu Zhang,Jun Zhou,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs deployed in agentic/RAG systems show inverse scaling in robustness to distractor instructions in reference text - larger models perform worse, but reinforcement learning (GRPO) can significantly improve robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs in real-world applications often process unstructured reference text containing benign but instruction-like semantic noise (editorial comments, system traces) that should be treated as data, not instructions. Current models lack robustness to such distractors.

Method: 1) Introduce DistractionIF benchmark to evaluate robustness against distractor instructions in reference text. 2) Conduct experiments across various model sizes showing inverse scaling phenomenon. 3) Use perplexity analysis to understand mechanistic basis. 4) Apply reinforcement learning (Group Relative Policy Optimization - GRPO) to improve robustness.

Result: 1) Inverse scaling observed: larger models less robust, with performance dropping up to 30 points with scale. 2) Perplexity analysis shows scaling erodes probabilistic boundary between robust/distracted behaviors. 3) GRPO improves robustness by up to 15.5% without compromising general instruction-following capability.

Conclusion: There's a critical instruction-following robustness gap in reference-grounded tasks. Reinforcement learning (particularly GRPO) is a promising approach for enforcing strict data-instruction separation at scale, addressing the inverse scaling problem.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in agentic and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, where they must execute user-specified tasks over externally provided reference text. In practice, such context is often unstructured and contaminated with benign but instruction-like semantic noise, such as editorial comments and system traces, which should be treated strictly as data. We introduce DistractionIF, a benchmark designed to evaluate robustness against such distractor instructions in reference text. Across a broad range of models, we observe a consistent inverse scaling phenomenon: larger models are often less robust, with performance dropping by up to 30 points as scale increases. Mechanistically, our perplexity analysis reveals that scaling erodes the probabilistic boundary between robust and distracted behaviors, making models increasingly prone to over-interpreting noise as instructions. To address this, we demonstrate that reinforcement learning, specifically Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), can restore this boundary, improving robustness by up to 15.5% without compromising general instruction-following capability. Our findings highlight a critical instruction-following robustness gap in reference-grounded tasks and establish reinforcement learning as a promising path for enforcing strict data-instruction separation at scale.

</details>


### [59] [Xetrieval: Mechanistically Explaining Dense Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29507)
*Zhixin Cai,Jun Bai,Yang Liu,Jiaqi Li,Yichi Zhang,Taichuan Li,Zhuofan Chen,Zixia Jia,Zilong Zheng,Wenge Rong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Xetrieval is an embedding-level mechanistic framework that explains dense retrieval by enriching embeddings with reasoning information and decomposing them into sparse, interpretable features for explaining individual retrieval decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current explanations for dense retrievers are limited because they focus on surface signals like lexical matches or token alignments, which don't provide insight into the latent factors shaping retrieval behavior at the embedding level. Dense retrievers make decisions through opaque high-dimensional embeddings that are difficult to interpret.

Method: 1) Introduces a lightweight reasoning internalizer that approximates Chain-of-Thought reasoning directly in embedding space with a single forward pass, enriching sentence embeddings with reasoning-oriented information without expensive autoregressive generation. 2) Decomposes these reasoning-enhanced embeddings into sparse, human-interpretable features, each with coherent natural language descriptions. 3) Aggregates sparse feature overlaps across multiple document-side views to provide feature-level explanations of individual retrieval decisions.

Result: Experiments on diverse retrievers and benchmarks show that Xetrieval: 1) uncovers coherent interpretable features, 2) yields stronger pair-level intervention effects, and 3) supports task-level feature steering.

Conclusion: Xetrieval provides a novel embedding-level mechanistic framework for explaining dense retrieval that goes beyond surface-level explanations by directly analyzing the latent reasoning processes in embedding space, offering more meaningful insights into retrieval behavior while maintaining computational efficiency.

Abstract: Explaining why dense retrievers assign high relevance scores remains challenging because retrieval decisions are made through opaque high-dimensional embeddings. Existing explanations often focus on surface signals, such as lexical matches, token alignments, or post-hoc textual rationales, and thus provide limited insight into the latent factors that shape dense retrieval behavior at the embedding level. We propose \textit{Xetrieval}, an embedding-level mechanistic framework for explaining dense retrieval. \textit{Xetrieval} first introduces a lightweight reasoning internalizer that approximates Chain-of-Thought reasoning directly in the embedding space with a single forward pass, enriching sentence embeddings with reasoning-oriented information while avoiding expensive autoregressive generation. It then decomposes these reasoning-enhanced embeddings into sparse, human-interpretable features, each associated with a coherent natural language description. By aggregating sparse feature overlaps across multiple document-side views, \textit{Xetrieval} provides feature-level explanations of individual retrieval decisions. Experiments on diverse retrievers and benchmarks show that \textit{Xetrieval} uncovers coherent interpretable features, yields stronger pair-level intervention effects, and supports task-level feature steering. The project page and source code are available at https://hihiczx.github.io/Xetrieval .

</details>


### [60] [MINDGAMES: A Live Arena for Evaluating Social and Strategic Reasoning in Multi-Agent LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29512)
*Kevin Wang,Anna Thöni,Benjamin Kempinski,Bobby Cheng,Jianzhu Yao,Benjamin Finch,Leon Guertler,Viraj Nadkarni,Yihan Jiang,Aliaksei Korshuk,Alexander Buyantuev,Ilya Makarov,Siyuan Wu,Yu-Chi Cheng,Yan-Ru Ju,Ti-Rong Wu,I-Hsuan Chu,Yu-Yu Yang,I-Chen Wu,Yitian Huang,Qinlu Cao,Yiheng Sun,Yuhong Dai,Hongkun Yao,Jingxuan Fu,Jiwei Zhang,Hao Liao,Mossimo Ebeling,Govind Arun,Sadhvik Bathini,Mihir S Arya,Avinash Anish,Aditya Ranjan,Kirtana Sunil Phatnani,Paval KS,Vrushali Mehta,Aravind S,Nikhil Arora,Tanya Upadhyay,Amol Bandagale,Yuan Lu,ChunEn Hsiao,YuTing Lin,Arvin Chung,Jerry John Thomas,Mathieu Laurière,Leshem Choshen,Yoram Bachrach,Pramod Viswanath,Maria Polukarov,Cheston Tan,Tal Kachman,Atlas Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Mindgames is a multi-game platform for evaluating LLM agents' social and strategic reasoning across four games, revealing limitations in rule adherence, structural scaffolding dependence, and evaluation confounds like error-survival bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM evaluations use static vignettes or single-game benchmarks that don't capture sustained, multi-faceted reasoning needed for real-world multi-agent interactions. There's a lack of comprehensive platforms to assess social and strategic reasoning capabilities like theory of mind, belief attribution, opponent modeling, and deception.

Method: Introduced Mindgames, a multi-game arena built on TextArena with unified interaction interface, TrueSkill-based rating, and full trajectory logging across four game environments: Colonel Blotto, Iterated Prisoner's Dilemma, Codenames, and Secret Mafia. Conducted a 2025 competition with 944 agents from 76 teams. Also developed MG-Ref, a deterministic offline tournament protocol.

Result: Analysis revealed agent-level limitations: brittle rule adherence remains a major bottleneck, top systems rely on explicit structural scaffolding. Evaluation-level limitations: leaderboard validity varies sharply across environments, with failure-heavy environments (especially Secret Mafia) showing error-survival confound where robustness to opponent errors can be rewarded more than strategic ability. Collected dataset of 29,571 multi-agent games.

Conclusion: Mindgames provides a comprehensive framework for evaluating LLM agents' social reasoning, uncovering critical limitations in current systems and evaluation methodologies. The platform and dataset enable better assessment of multi-agent reasoning capabilities, while highlighting the need to address error-survival confounds in competitive evaluations.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as interactive agents, yet their capacity for social and strategic reasoning over extended interaction remains poorly understood. Existing evaluations rely on static vignettes or single-game benchmarks that cannot capture the sustained, multi-faceted reasoning that real-world multi-agent settings demand. We introduce Mindgames, a multi-game arena and evaluation platform for LLM agents that operationalizes complementary reasoning demands relevant to ``theory of mind'': belief attribution under hidden information, opponent modeling through repeated strategic interaction, cooperative inference under knowledge asymmetries, and sustained deception in social deduction. Built on TextArena, Mindgames provides a unified interaction interface, TrueSkill-based rating, and full trajectory logging across four game environments. We instantiate Mindgames through a 2025 competition cycle hosted at a major AI conference, which assessed 944 submitted agents from 76 teams across four games: Colonel Blotto, Iterated Prisoner's Dilemma, Codenames, and Secret Mafia. Our analysis surfaces both agent-level and evaluation-level limitations: brittle rule adherence remains a major bottleneck, top-performing systems repeatedly rely on explicit structural scaffolding, and leaderboard validity differs sharply across environments. In particular, failure-heavy environments can reward robustness to opponent errors as much as strategic ability, with Secret Mafia exhibiting a pronounced error-survival confound in this cycle. We release a dataset of 29,571 multi-agent games with turn-level observations, actions, and rewards, together with MG-Ref, a deterministic offline tournament protocol that scores new agents against a frozen reference pool of top-ranked, low-error Stage~II submissions under the same error-attribution lens used in this analysis.

</details>


### [61] [DeepSurvey: Enhancing Analytical Depth and Citation Reliability in Automated Survey Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29522)
*Ziyue Yang,Da Ma,Hanqi Li,Zijian Wang,Tiancheng Huang,Zijian Hu,Chenrun Wang,Yunzhe Zhang,Xiaobao Wu,Kai Yu,Lu Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DeepSurvey is an agentic system for automated scientific survey generation that addresses limitations of existing approaches by providing deeper analytical depth through full-text analysis and more reliable citations through evidence-constrained methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing automated survey generation systems suffer from limited analytical depth (relying only on abstracts and isolated paper processing) and unreliable citations (due to imprecise retrieval and post-hoc grounding), leading to superficial surveys that may mislead researchers.

Method: DeepSurvey uses a multi-faceted approach: 1) For depth: extracts structured keynotes from full-text papers, models cross-paper relationships through clustering/comparative analysis, and analyzes code repositories for implementation details. 2) For reliability: combines citation-graph expansion with hybrid filtering for topic-focused retrieval, enforces evidence-constrained citation assignment, and uses multi-granularity agentic refinement to validate citation-claim alignment.

Result: Experiments show DeepSurvey achieves the highest content score (8.644/10) and citation quality (12.3% recall and 9.3% precision gains over strongest baseline), generalizes more robustly across domains (0.14 vs 0.22 to 0.69 CS-to-non-CS drop), and is preferred by domain experts over human-written surveys (83.3% overall quality, 100% content depth).

Conclusion: DeepSurvey successfully addresses key limitations in automated survey generation by providing both analytical depth through comprehensive full-text processing and reliability through evidence-based citation methods, outperforming existing systems and even human-written surveys in expert evaluations.

Abstract: As scientific literature grows rapidly, automated survey generation has become a key capability for AI scientists and human researchers. However, existing systems suffer from limited analytical depth due to reliance on abstracts and isolated paper processing, and unreliable citations from imprecise retrieval and post-hoc grounding, producing superficial surveys and may mislead researchers. We present DeepSurvey, an agentic system that addresses both. To enhance depth, DeepSurvey extracts structured keynotes from full-text papers, models cross-paper relationships through clustering and comparative analysis, and integrates code-repository analysis to recover implementation-level details. To fortify reliability, it combines citation-graph expansion with hybrid filtering for topic-focussed retrieval, enforces evidence-constrained citation assignment, and deploys multi-granularity agentic refinement to validate citation-claim alignment. Experiments show that DeepSurvey achieves the highest content score (8.644/10) and citation quality (12.3% and 9.3% recall and precision gains over the strongest baseline), generalizes more robustly across domains (0.14 vs 0.22 to 0.69 CS-to-non-CS drop), and is preferred over human-written surveys by domain experts (83.3% overall quality, 100% content depth).

</details>


### [62] [UI-KOBE: Knowledge-Oriented Behavior Exploration for Lightweight Graph-Guided GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29534)
*Yuxiang Chai,Han Xiao,Xinyu Fu,Jinpeng Chen,Rui Liu,Hongsheng Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: UI-KOBE is a framework that improves lightweight mobile GUI agents by using reusable app-specific knowledge graphs to guide task planning and execution, enabling more reliable on-device automation without large vision-language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While mobile GUI agents have advanced, effective systems rely on large vision-language models that are resource-intensive and privacy-concerning. Lightweight agents deployable on mobile devices are more practical but unreliable for end-to-end task planning from screenshots due to limited model capacity.

Method: UI-KOBE first autonomously explores mobile apps to construct knowledge graphs where nodes represent UI states and edges represent transitions. At runtime, lightweight agents use these graphs as external guidance to identify current UI states and select among self-loop actions, neighboring transitions, task completion, or fallback actions.

Result: The framework reduces the burden of end-to-end GUI planning by providing app-specific graph guidance, helping lightweight models perform mobile GUI tasks more effectively without requiring large vision-language models.

Conclusion: UI-KOBE represents a practical step toward efficient, interpretable, and privacy-conscious on-device GUI agents by combining lightweight models with reusable app knowledge graphs for reliable mobile automation.

Abstract: Recent advances in mobile GUI agents have shown strong potential for automating mobile tasks, but most effective systems still depend on large vision-language models for screenshot understanding and long-horizon planning. Small GUI agents that can be deployed directly on mobile devices are more attractive for practical use, offering lower inference cost and better protection of sensitive on-device information. However, due to limited model capacity, such lightweight agents remain unreliable when planning and executing GUI tasks end-to-end from screenshots alone. We propose Knowledge-Oriented Behavior Exploration (\textbf{UI-KOBE}), a framework that improves lightweight mobile GUI agents with reusable app-specific graph knowledge. UI-KOBE first autonomously explores a mobile application and constructs an app knowledge graph, where nodes represent distinct UI states and edges represent executable transitions. At runtime, a lightweight GUI agent uses the graph as external guidance: given a user task and the current screenshot, it identifies the current graph node and selects among self-loop actions, neighboring transitions, task completion, or fallback free actions associated with that node. By supporting runtime decisions with app-specific graph guidance, UI-KOBE reduces the burden of end-to-end GUI planning and helps lightweight models perform mobile GUI tasks more effectively, offering a practical step toward efficient, interpretable, and privacy-conscious on-device GUI agents.

</details>


### [63] [Opt-Verifier: Unleashing the Power of LLMs for Optimization Modeling via Dual-Side Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29556)
*Haoyang Liu,Jie Wang,Boxuan Niu,Xiongwei Han,Yian Xu,Mingxuan Ye,Zijie Geng,Fangzhou Zhu,Tao Zhong,Mingxuan Yuan,Jianye Hao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Opt-Verifier is an LLM-based framework with dual-side verification (structure and solution perspectives) that improves automated optimization model building accuracy by over 20% through comprehensive correctness checking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based approaches for automating mathematical optimization model building struggle to verify correctness of generated models. They often fail to check rationality of constraints and variables or validate solutions, which hampers verification and correction steps and severely hurts modeling accuracy.

Method: Proposed Opt-Verifier framework with dual-side verification: (1) Structure-side verification ensures generated optimization models' structure aligns with original problem description, accurately capturing constraints and requirements. (2) Solution-side verification interprets and evaluates solutions' validity, confirming models are logically and mathematically sound.

Result: Experiments on popular benchmarks demonstrate over 20% improvement in modeling accuracy compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: The dual-side verification framework (Opt-Verifier) effectively addresses verification challenges in LLM-based optimization model generation, significantly improving modeling accuracy through comprehensive structure and solution verification.

Abstract: Building mathematical optimization models is critical in operations research (OR), while it requires substantial human expertise. Recent advancements have utilized large language models (LLMs) to automate this modeling process. However, existing works often struggle to verify the correctness of the generated optimization models, without checking the rationality of the constraints and variables or the validity of solutions to the generated models. This hampers the subsequent verification and correction steps, and thus it severely hurts the modeling accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose a novel LLM-based framework with Dual-side Verification (Opt-Verifier) from both structure and solution perspectives, thereby improving the modeling accuracy. The structure-side verification ensures that the modeling structure of the generated optimization models aligns with the original problem description, accurately capturing the problem's constraints and requirements. Meanwhile, the solution-side verification interprets and evaluates the solutions' validity, confirming that the optimization models are logically and mathematically sound. Experiments on popular benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves over 20\% improvement in accuracy.

</details>


### [64] [Battery-Sim-Agent: Leveraging LLM-Agent for Inverse Battery Parameter Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29560)
*Jiawei Chen,Xiaofan Gui,Shikai Fang,Shengyu Tao,Shun Zheng,Weiqing Liu,Jiang Bian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel LLM-agent framework that outperforms traditional optimization methods for battery digital twin parameter estimation by mimicking human scientist reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for parameterizing high-fidelity battery "digital twins" rely on sample-inefficient black-box optimization that ignores underlying physics, hindering battery innovation.

Method: Introduces Battery-Sim-Agent, which deploys an LLM agent in a closed loop with a high-fidelity battery simulator. The agent interprets multi-modal simulator feedback, forms physics-grounded hypotheses to explain discrepancies, and proposes structured parameter updates.

Result: Significantly outperforms Bayesian optimization and other black-box optimization baselines across diverse battery chemistries, operating conditions, and difficulty levels. Also successfully handles complex long-horizon degradation fitting and validates on real-world datasets.

Conclusion: LLM agents show promise as reasoning-based optimizers for scientific discovery and battery parameter estimation, offering a new paradigm beyond traditional black-box optimization.

Abstract: Parameterizing high-fidelity "digital twins" of batteries is a critical yet challenging inverse problem that hinders the pace of battery innovation. Prevailing methods formulate this as a black-box optimization (BBO) task, employing algorithms that are sample-inefficient and blind to the underlying physics. In this work, we introduce a new paradigm that reframes the inverse problem as a reasoning task, and present Battery-Sim-Agent, the first framework to deploy a Large Language Model (LLM) agent in a closed loop with a high-fidelity battery simulator. The agent mimics a human scientist's workflow: it interprets rich, multi-modal feedback from the simulator, forms physically-grounded hypotheses to explain discrepancies, and proposes structured parameter updates. On a systematically constructed benchmark suite spanning diverse battery chemistries, operating conditions, and difficulty levels, our agent significantly outperforms strong BBO baselines like Bayesian optimization in identifying accurate parameters. We further demonstrate the framework's capability in complex long-horizon degradation fitting tasks and validate its practical applicability on real-world battery datasets. Our results highlight the promise of LLM-agents as reasoning-based optimizers for scientific discovery and battery parameter estimation.

</details>


### [65] [ParaTool: Shifting Tool Representations from Context to Parameters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29561)
*Zekai Yu,Qi Meng,Qizhi Chu,Yu Hao,Chuan Shi,Cheng Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ParaTool is a framework that parameterizes tools for LLMs, enabling tool calling without in-context documentation, reducing inference overhead while improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current ICL approaches for tool calling require lengthy tool documentation in context, causing substantial inference overhead and hallucination risks. Tuning-based methods improve general capabilities but still depend on in-context documentation and fail to internalize specific tool details.

Method: Three-stage approach: 1) Parametric tool pre-training that encapsulates tool knowledge into independent parameter modules; 2) Soft tool selection using a gating network to dynamically weigh and aggregate relevant tool parameters; 3) Parametric tool fine-tuning to jointly update tool parameters and align training with inference.

Result: Experiments on Stable ToolBench and BFCL show ParaTool significantly outperforms ICL-based baselines, achieving superior performance while reducing computational complexity.

Conclusion: ParaTool successfully addresses limitations of both ICL and tuning-based approaches by parameterizing tools, enabling efficient tool calling without in-context documentation dependency, leading to better performance and lower computational overhead.

Abstract: Tool calling extends large language models (LLMs) by enabling grounded interaction with external executable interfaces, thereby supporting environment-coupled problem solving. However, mainstream in-context learning (ICL) approaches typically incorporate detailed tool documentation and usage examples directly into the context. This results in substantial inference overhead and heightened risks of hallucination as the context length grows. Conversely, while tuning-based methods improve general tool-calling capabilities, they often fail to effectively internalize the specific details of previously seen tools, thereby retaining a dependency on in-context documentation. To address these limitations, we propose ParaTool, a framework that projects each tool into a dedicated, loadable set of parameters. By equipping a dynamic integration of these parameterized tools, the LLM can perform tool calling without relying on in-context documents or examples. Specifically, our approach consists of three stages: (1) parametric tool pre-training encapsulates the knowledge of different tools into independent parameter modules; (2) soft tool selection employs a gating network to dynamically weigh and aggregate relevant tool parameters; and (3) parametric tool fine-tuning jointly updates tool parameters to align the training and inference processes. Experiments on Stable ToolBench and BFCL demonstrate that ParaTool significantly outperforms strong ICL-based baselines, achieving superior performance while reducing computational complexity.

</details>


### [66] [Planning with the Views via Scene Self-Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29563)
*Kangrui Wang,Linjie Li,Zhengyuan Yang,Shiqi Chen,Zihan Wang,Li Fei-Fei,Jiajun Wu,Leonidas Guibas,Lijuan Wang,Manling Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VLMs struggle with multi-step view planning in 3D environments, but an iterative self-exploration framework with view graph distillation dramatically improves performance from 2.5% to 47.8% success rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can understand basic camera movements but fail at complex multi-step planning in 3D environments, creating a critical gap for real-world applications requiring long-horizon view planning.

Method: Proposes ViewSuite for evaluation, identifies the planning gap, then introduces an iterative framework combining self-exploration with view graph distillation - collecting exploration trajectories to create a view graph of scene connectivity, which is distilled into supervised tasks to improve planning policies.

Result: Significant improvement from 2.5% to 47.8% success rate on interactive view planning with Qwen2.5-VL-7B, outperforming GPT-5.4 Pro (18.5%) and Gemini 3.1 Pro (21.4%). Shows the framework effectively addresses sparse reward problems in RL-based approaches.

Conclusion: Self-exploration combined with view graph distillation is a promising approach for developing VLMs capable of active reasoning and multi-step planning in 3D environments, overcoming fundamental limitations in current models.

Abstract: Can VLMs predict how each camera move changes the view, and plan many such moves ahead? We call this capability view planning, requiring (1)understanding how a single action transforms the view, and (2)composing many such transformations across multi-turn plans to identify a target view. We probe both abilities in our proposed ViewSuite, a 3D point-cloud environment on real ScanNet scenes. Across 13 frontier VLMs, a critical planning gap emerges: they possess basic view-action knowledge but fail to compose it across multi-turn plans, with the gap widening as viewpoint distance grows. To close this gap, we propose an iterative framework that alternates self-exploration with view graph distillation. The key insight is that all exploration trajectories, regardless of their outcome, collectively form a view graph that compactly captures how viewpoints connect across a scene. Distilling this graph into diverse supervised tasks reshapes the policy distribution and overcomes the sparse rewards that stall pure RL. This improves Qwen2.5-VL-7B from 2.5% to 47.8% on interactive view planning, surpassing GPT-5.4 Pro (18.5%) and Gemini 3.1 Pro (21.4%). Self-exploration emerges as a promising path toward VLMs that can actively reason and plan in 3D space.

</details>


### [67] [DeepTool: Scaling Interleaved Deliberation in Tool-Integrated Reasoning via Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29568)
*Yang He,Xiao Ding,Bibo Cai,Yufei Zhang,Kai Xiong,Zhouhao Sun,Bing Qin,Ting Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DeepTool is a framework that enhances Tool-Integrated Reasoning in LLMs by introducing interleaved thinking, action, and observation with process-supervised reinforcement learning to improve strategic planning and self-correction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Tool-Integrated Reasoning methods lack deliberation during sequential tool invocation, preventing strategic planning and self-correction. Reinforcement learning approaches suffer from sparse outcome-based rewards that fail to supervise intermediate reasoning steps and tool invocations.

Method: DeepTool uses a two-part approach: 1) A synthesis pipeline that evolves extended thinking into interleaved trajectories with adversarial perturbations for robustness, and 2) Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning based on GRPO with Action-Centric Process Reward to reinforce intermediate thinking and enforce precise tool invocation at every turn.

Result: DeepTool achieves superior performance, boosting Qwen2.5-7B significantly across six benchmarks (e.g., AIME24: 3.2% -> 40.4% and HMMT25: 0.0% -> 28.6%). Token cost-effectiveness analysis confirms optimal balance between performance and token efficiency.

Conclusion: DeepTool effectively addresses the limitations of existing Tool-Integrated Reasoning methods by introducing deliberate interleaved thinking with process-supervised reinforcement learning, demonstrating both performance improvements and token efficiency.

Abstract: Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) extends LLM capabilities by leveraging external environments. However, existing methods lack the deliberation during sequential tool invocation required for strategic planning and self-correction. While RL mitigates this, conventional approaches for Tool-Integrated Reasoning are hindered by sparse outcome-based rewards, failing to supervise intermediate reasoning steps and tool invocations. To address this, we propose DeepTool, a novel framework that scales deliberate thinking within the interleaved process of thinking, action, and observation at each turn. In DeepTool, we first introduce a synthesis pipeline that evolves extended thinking into interleaved trajectories, integrating adversarial perturbations to ensure robustness and self-correction. Secondly, we devise Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning based on GRPO, which utilizes an Action-Centric Process Reward to reinforce intermediate interleaved thinking and enforce precise tool invocation at every turn. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DeepTool achieves superior performance, boosting Qwen2.5-7B significantly across six benchmarks (e.g., AIME24: 3.2% -> 40.4% and HMMT25: 0.0% -> 28.6%). Furthermore, the token cost-effectiveness analysis confirms the utility of interleaved thinking, demonstrating DeepTool's optimal balance between performance and token efficiency.

</details>


### [68] [GPS-Enhanced Tourist Mobility Modeling with Seasonal Spatial Priors and LLM-Based Activity Chain Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29578)
*Yifan Liu,Yanling Sang,Xishun Liao,Morgan Sun,Bo Yang,Zhiyuan Zhang,Chris Stanford,Haoxuan Ma,Jiaqi Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel simulation framework for generating individual tourist mobility schedules that combines GPS data priors, demographic modeling, and LLM-based activity generation while preserving privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tourist mobility differs fundamentally from resident commuting—it's non-routine, attraction-driven, and sensitive to factors like season, purpose, and group composition. Existing approaches either only measure aggregate patterns without individual schedules or lack tourist-specific structures like duration conditioning, seasonal attraction demand, and household travel rules.

Method: A four-stage simulation framework: 1) Month-conditioned spatial priors from aggregated GPS and survey data (privacy-preserving), 2) Trip extent prediction from tourist demographics, 3) Distance-feasible ward sequence assignment, and 4) LLM-based activity chain generation under household and spatial constraints.

Result: The framework successfully recovers spatial visitation patterns consistent with survey data for Tokyo tourism. It produces demographically aligned synthetic schedules whose ward-level visitation shares closely match both survey distributions and monthly visitation patterns derived from staypoints.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides an effective, geographically grounded, and demographically aware approach to tourist mobility modeling that preserves privacy while generating realistic individual schedules with tourist-specific characteristics.

Abstract: Tourist mobility poses a distinct challenge for urban transportation planning. Unlike resident commuting, tourist travel is largely non-routine, attraction driven, and highly sensitive to trip purpose, travel season, and trip member composition. Existing approaches either measure aggregate tourist spatial patterns without generating individual schedules, or synthesize mobility without tourist specific structure such as trip duration conditioning, month varying attraction demand, and household co-travel rules. To address these challenges, we propose a four stage simulation framework combining month conditioned spatial priors derived from GPS and survey data, trip extent prediction from tourist demographics, distance feasible ward sequence assignment, and LLM-based activity chain generation under household and spatial constraints. GPS data are used only in privacy preserving aggregated form as month conditioned spatial priors, with no individual traces retained or exposed. Experiments on tourism in Tokyo demonstrate that the GPS based tourist cohort extraction recovers spatial visitation signatures consistent with survey references, and our framework produces demographically aligned synthetic schedules whose ward-level visitation shares align closely with both survey distributions and staypoint derived monthly visitation patterns. The results demonstrate the framework's effectiveness as a geographically grounded, demographically aware approach to tourist mobility modeling.

</details>


### [69] [FinVerBench: Benchmark Validity and Calibration in Large Language Model Financial Statement Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29586)
*Silu Panda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FinVerBench is a financial statement verification benchmark that tests LLMs' ability to detect numerical inconsistencies in corporate financial statements, revealing high false positive rates and rendering effects on model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLMs need evaluation on financial statement verification tasks to determine if they can identify numerical inconsistencies in corporate financial documents, which requires more than simple arithmetic and involves judgment under incomplete information.

Method: Created FinVerBench from SEC 10-K XBRL filings for 43 S&P 500 companies, defined a four-category error taxonomy (arithmetic, cross-statement linkage, year-over-year, magnitude perturbations), and evaluated 14 LLM runs on 105 observable diagnostic instances.

Result: Nine of fourteen LLM runs showed 95-100% false positives on clean statements; one achieved 0% observed false positives. Recall varied significantly with rendering choices: 79.0% recall with 0% FPR on realistic rounded variant vs 100.0% recall on unrounded variant.

Conclusion: Financial statement verification is not just arithmetic detection but requires calibrated judgment under incomplete observability, prompt-induced assumptions, and realistic numerical rendering. FinVerBench serves as a construct-validity benchmark rather than a simple leaderboard.

Abstract: We introduce FinVerBench, a benchmark and validity study for financial statement verification: determining whether a set of corporate financial statements is numerically consistent from the information shown to the model. FinVerBench is built from SEC 10-K XBRL filings for 43 S&P 500 companies and defines a four-category error taxonomy covering arithmetic, cross-statement linkage, year-over-year, and magnitude perturbations. We attempt fifteen contemporary LLM evaluations and report fourteen complete runs; a Gemini 2.5 Pro run is excluded from the main comparison because 40/108 gateway calls failed. All binary metrics exclude underdetermined positive instances whose perturbed line item is not rendered, leaving a 105-instance observable diagnostic subset (43 clean, 62 error-injected). Under the original guided-checklist prompt on the unrounded diagnostic subset, nine of fourteen complete LLM runs produce 95-100% false positives on clean statements, while one run achieves 0% observed false positives. Benchmark rendering choices materially affect measured recall: on a realistic rounded variant of the same observable subset, the calibrated model's recall is 79.0% with 0% observed FPR, compared with 100.0% recall on the unrounded diagnostic variant. These results support a construct-validity conclusion rather than a final leaderboard: financial statement verification is not merely arithmetic detection, but calibrated judgment under incomplete observability, prompt-induced assumptions, and realistic numerical rendering. FinVerBench and all code are publicly available.

</details>


### [70] [Mind-Omni: A Unified Multi-Task Framework for Brain-Vision-Language Modeling via Discrete Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29591)
*Yizhuo Lu,Changde Du,Qingyu Shi,Hang Chen,Jie Peng,Liuyun Jiang,Shuangchen Zhao,Huiguang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Mind-Omni: first versatile framework unifying 7 brain encoding/decoding tasks via discrete diffusion, using novel Brain Tokenizer to convert continuous signals to discrete tokens in shared semantic space, achieving SOTA with multi-task synergy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior brain-computer interface research uses specialized single-task models, limiting versatility and ignoring potential inter-task synergies. Need unified approach to model external stimuli and internal neural representations.

Method: Propose Mind-Omni framework with: 1) Brain Tokenizer that transforms heterogeneous continuous brain signals into standardized discrete tokens, 2) discrete diffusion paradigm enabling token-level interactions between modalities in shared semantic space, 3) Brain Question Answering instruction-tuning dataset for advanced reasoning.

Result: Establishes new state-of-the-art among multi-task unified frameworks, demonstrates strong multi-task synergy, achieves performance competitive/superior to larger specialized models, offers unified approach for 7 distinct encoding/decoding tasks.

Conclusion: Mind-Omni provides powerful new paradigm for neural modeling, paves way for foundation models of neural activity by unifying brain signal processing through discrete tokenization and shared semantic space, with code publicly available.

Abstract: Modeling the interplay between external stimuli and internal neural representations is a pivotal research area for Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs). A major limitation of prior work is the prevailing paradigm of specialized, single-task models, which curtails versatility and neglects inter-task synergies. To address this, we propose Mind-Omni, the first versatile framework that unifies seven distinct encoding and decoding tasks through a discrete diffusion paradigm. At its core is a novel Brain Tokenizer that transforms heterogeneous, continuous brain signals into standardized, discrete tokens. This enables direct, token-level interactions for mutual understanding and generation between any two or more modalities within a shared semantic space. To unlock advanced reasoning capabilities, we further curate a specialized Brain Question Answering (BQA) instruction-tuning dataset. Our model not only establishes a new state-of-the-art among multi-task unified frameworks but also provides strong evidence for multi-task synergy. By demonstrating performance competitive with, and at times superior to, larger specialized models, our work offers a powerful new paradigm for neural modeling and paves the way for foundation models of neural activity. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/ReedOnePeck/Mind-Omni.

</details>


### [71] [HiKEY: Hierarchical Multimodal Retrieval for Open-Domain Document Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29606)
*Joongmin Shin,Gyuho Shim,Jeongbae Park,Jaehyung Seo,Heuiseok Lim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HiKEY is a hierarchical tree-based multimodal retrieval framework that improves document-based open-domain QA by leveraging document structure for better routing and evidence integration, outperforming chunk- and page-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for document-based open-domain QA faces two critical bottlenecks: routing failure (difficulty locating correct document among thousands) and evidence fragmentation (inability to integrate scattered multimodal evidence like tables and figures within token limits). Existing flat text chunk or page-level approaches struggle with precise document pinpointing and organic multimodal evidence connection.

Method: HiKEY reconstructs a logical heterogeneous graph via Document Hierarchical Parsing (DHP) that explicitly encodes parent-child relationships. It uses a hierarchical coarse-to-fine strategy: (1) global routing to prune search space using hierarchical indexing, (2) fine-grained retrieval to rank sections using multimodal fusion that captures discriminative evidence, and (3) assembles token-efficient evidence subgraph via hybrid structural-semantic packing strategy.

Result: Experiments on ODQA benchmarks show HiKEY significantly outperforms page- and chunk-based baselines, improving retrieval recall by up to 12.9% and end-to-end QA performance by up to 6.8%.

Conclusion: Elevating document hierarchy to a first-class retrieval signal through hierarchical tree-based multimodal retrieval effectively addresses routing and evidence fragmentation challenges in large-scale industrial document QA systems, demonstrating substantial performance improvements over traditional approaches.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for document-based Open-domain Question Answering (ODQA) on large-scale industrial corpora faces two critical bottlenecks: routing failure in locating the correct document and evidence fragmentation in integrating scattered information. Existing approaches relying on flat text chunks or page-level images inherently struggle to (i) precisely pinpoint the target document among thousands of candidates and (ii) organically connect multimodal evidence, such as tables and figures, within a limited token budget. To address these challenges, we propose HiKEY, a hierarchical tree-based multimodal retrieval framework that elevates document hierarchy to a first-class retrieval signal. Instead of simple chunking, HiKEY reconstructs a logical heterogeneous graph via Document Hierarchical Parsing (DHP), explicitly encoding parent-child relationships. Adopting a hierarchical coarse-to-fine strategy, the framework (1) performs global routing to rapidly prune the search space using hierarchical indexing, and (2) conducts fine-grained retrieval to rank sections by employing a multimodal fusion strategy that captures the most discriminative evidence. Finally, HiKEY assembles a token-efficient evidence subgraph via a hybrid structural-semantic packing strategy. Experiments on ODQA benchmarks demonstrate that HiKEY significantly outperforms page- and chunk-based baselines, improving retrieval recall by up to 12.9% and end-to-end QA performance by up to 6.8%.

</details>


### [72] [Improving Collaborative Storytelling with a Multi-Agent Framework Based on Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29625)
*Arturo Valdivia,Paolo Burelli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Children and LLMs co-create stories through a physical board game using an iterative Writer-Editor process, with simulation showing quality improvement through successive refinement loops.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most co-creation research focuses on adult-human interactions in digital settings, leaving a gap in understanding how AI agents can interact with children in physical, playful contexts to create narratives suitable for young players.

Method: A multi-agent framework using an iterative Writer-Editor process where one LLM generates stories and another evaluates them, providing feedback for refinement. The study uses a simulation with multiple LLMs interacting through a physical board game scenario designed for children.

Result: The iterative interaction consistently improves perceived quality of generated stories across successive refinement loops. Results show that a small number of refinement steps may be sufficient to achieve high-quality outputs in interactive storytelling systems for children.

Conclusion: The iterative Writer-Editor approach effectively enhances story quality in ludic co-creation between children and LLMs, suggesting promising applications for AI-assisted creative activities in child-friendly physical game settings with minimal refinement cycles needed.

Abstract: The topic of Co-creation, i.e., AI agents interacting with humans to generate outputs (e.g., art), has gained significant attention recently. However, most studies focus on adult-human interactions in a digital setting. This paper explores a novel ludic co-creation scenario involving children and Large Language Models (LLMs) interacting through a physical board game to create written stories. Our goal is to develop a multi-agent framework capable of producing high-quality narratives suitable for young players. At the core of our approach is an iterative Writer-Editor process in which one LLM generates stories while another evaluates them and provides feedback for refinement. Through a simulation study involving multiple LLMs, we show that this iterative interaction consistently improves the perceived quality of generated stories across successive loops. The results indicate that a small number of refinement steps may be sufficient to achieve high-quality outputs in interactive storytelling systems.

</details>


### [73] [Beyond Attack Success Rate: Temporal Logit Observability for LLM Safety Failures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29629)
*Junyoung Park,Sunghwan Park,Seongyong Ju,Jaewoo Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Temporal Logit Observability (TLO) is a training-free diagnostic that reveals how jailbreak attacks unfold during LLM generation by tracking logits, going beyond simple success/failure rates to show different attack paths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Attack Success Rate (ASR) evaluation only gives binary yes/no labels for jailbreak attacks, failing to reveal how attacks actually unfold. Two attacks with the same ASR may follow completely different paths, but ASR cannot distinguish between them, missing crucial information about failure mechanisms.

Method: Temporal Logit Observability (TLO) monitors compliance-refusal margin during decoding from logits alone, placing each model-attack condition on a calibrated 2D plane. This training-free diagnostic reveals temporal patterns of attack progression without requiring model training or hidden state access.

Result: Across four aligned LLMs and three jailbreak paradigms, attacks with nearly identical ASR landed at clearly different points on the TLO plane, showing models fail through different temporal patterns. TLO geometry matched refusal-direction probes from hidden states for most conditions. A simple early-stop rule derived from TLO cut successful jailbreaks by more than half without false alarms on benign queries.

Conclusion: Safety evaluation should report when and how failures unfold, not just whether they occurred. TLO makes the first two observable from logits alone, providing more informative diagnostics than ASR, especially for attacks that succeed for different reasons.

Abstract: Attack Success Rate (ASR) evaluates each jailbreak with a single yes/no label at the end of generation, telling us whether a failure happened but not how it unfolded. Two attacks that produce equally harmful outputs may have followed completely different paths, and ASR cannot tell them apart. We make those hidden paths observable from logits alone. Temporal Logit Observability (TLO) is a training-free diagnostic that watches a compliance-refusal margin during decoding and places each model-attack condition on a calibrated 2D plane. By design, this plane is most informative exactly where ASR is least informative: among attacks that succeed for genuinely different reasons. Across four aligned LLMs and three jailbreak paradigms, attacks with nearly identical ASR land at clearly different points on the plane: the same model can fail through different temporal patterns. The geometry matches refusal-direction probes from hidden states on most conditions, with one model showing the limit of our fixed-lexicon approach. A simple early-stop rule derived from TLO cuts successful jailbreaks by more than half, without false alarms on plain benign queries. Safety evaluation should report when and how a failure unfolds, not only whether it occurred. TLO makes the first two observable from logits alone.

</details>


### [74] [VikingMem: A Memory Base Management System for Stateful LLM-based Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29640)
*Jiajie Fu,Junwen Chen,Mengzhao Wang,Aoxiang He,Maojia Sheng,Xiangyu Ke,Yifan Zhu,Yunjun Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VikingMem is a Memory Base Management System that solves LLM context window limitations by creating persistent, evolving memories through selective extraction, stateful evolution, and generalizable abstractions, achieving 30% better retrieval performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs have finite context windows that hinder long-term interactions. Existing memory approaches are simplistic, incomplete, and lack generalizability across diverse applications, performing poorly on downstream tasks.

Method: Introduces Memory Base paradigm with three principles: selective extraction, stateful evolution, and generalizable abstraction. Implements VikingMem on VikingDB vector engine with event/entity abstractions, temporal compression, time-weighted recall, and progressive summarization.

Result: VikingMem outperforms baselines by up to 30% in memory retrieval effectiveness while maintaining low latency essential for interactive applications, as demonstrated in extensive evaluations on long-term memory benchmarks.

Conclusion: The Memory Base paradigm and VikingMem implementation provide a robust solution for managing persistent state in long-term LLM interactions, offering improved retrieval effectiveness, generalizability across applications, and practical deployment viability.

Abstract: Large Language Models have revolutionized interactive applications; however, their finite context windows pose a critical data management challenge for maintaining stateful, long-term interactions. Existing memory approaches often rely on simplistic extraction methods that lead to incomplete memories or use rigid, single-purpose memory extraction prompts tailored to a single use case, such as chatbots. Consequently, they lack generalizability and perform poorly across diverse downstream tasks. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Memory Base, a novel data management paradigm for managing the persistent state of long-term interactions. It is characterized by three core principles: selective extraction of high-value memories from raw information streams; inherent statefulness and evolution, where memory content is progressively summarized, corrected, and temporally weighted to prioritize recent interactions; and a generalizable abstraction paradigm designed for robust transferability across diverse applications, including education, recommendation, and agent memory. Building on this foundation, we present VikingMem, an end-to-end Memory Base Management System implemented on the VikingDB vector engine. VikingMem materializes this paradigm through interconnected event and entity abstractions. It features event-centric memory extraction to selectively handle complex information streams, while entities are dynamically updated by events to achieve stateful evolution. Using temporal compression via a topic-wise timeline and time-weighted recall, the system progressively produces high-level summary memories, prioritizes recent items, and compresses and fades older ones. Extensive evaluations on long-term memory benchmarks demonstrate that VikingMem outperformes baselines by up to 30% in memory retrieval effectiveness while maintaining the low latency essential for interactive applications.

</details>


### [75] [LLM-Evolved Domain-Independent Heuristics for Symbolic AI Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29649)
*Elliot Gestrin,Jendrik Seipp*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-generated domain-independent heuristics through evolutionary search outperform hand-engineered state-of-the-art heuristics in AI planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While heuristic search dominates symbolic AI planning and strong hand-engineered heuristics exist, LLMs have only produced domain-specific heuristics so far. The authors aim to create the first LLM-generated domain-independent heuristics that surpass human-designed ones.

Method: Use evolutionary search where an LLM mutates parent heuristics written in C++. Store candidates in a MAP-Elites archive keyed on informedness and speed. Calculate fitness scores by blending coverage with solving time. Also benchmark hand-engineered heuristics on their informedness-speed tradeoff for comparison.

Result: On unseen testing domains, the best evolved heuristic solves more tasks than even the strongest baseline. The full heuristic suite spans the Pareto frontier of the informedness-speed tradeoff. Interestingly, seeding evolution from the trivial blind heuristic outperforms seeding from the strong FF heuristic. The evolved programs are plain C++ and can slot into existing planners as drop-in replacements.

Conclusion: Evolutionary search with LLMs can produce domain-independent heuristics that exceed the hand-engineered state of the art in AI planning. The approach yields practical, efficient C++ heuristics that integrate seamlessly into existing planners while inheriting soundness and completeness guarantees.

Abstract: Heuristic search is the dominant paradigm in symbolic AI planning, and the strongest heuristics are the result of decades of work by planning researchers. Recent work has shown that large language models (LLMs) can design heuristics for individual planning domains, but no LLM-generated heuristic has so far worked on arbitrary planning tasks. In this paper, we use evolutionary search to produce the first LLM-generated domain-independent heuristics that exceed the hand-engineered state of the art. We let an LLM mutate parent heuristics written in C++, store candidates in a MAP-Elites archive keyed on informedness and speed and calculate fitness scores by blending coverage with solving time. To place the evolved programs in context, we additionally benchmark a broad set of hand-engineered heuristics on their informedness-speed tradeoff, which to our knowledge has not been done before. On unseen testing domains, our best evolved heuristic solves more tasks than even the strongest baseline, with our full heuristic suite spanning the Pareto frontier of said tradeoff. We also find that seeding evolution from the trivial blind heuristic outperforms seeding from the strong FF heuristic, even when the resulting program is itself an FF variant, and that LLM reasoning effort affects how often candidates compile much more than the quality of those that do. Because the evolved programs are plain C++, they slot into existing planners as drop-in replacements and inherit the soundness and completeness guarantees of the underlying search.

</details>


### [76] [Think Fast, Talk Smart: Partitioning Deterministic and Neural Computation for Structured Health Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29652)
*Kai-Chen Cheng,Haejun Han,David Q. Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A hybrid approach for health text generation where deterministic code handles recurring analysis and LLMs are used only for bounded expression tasks, resulting in lower errors and costs than pure LLM approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models are increasingly used to generate health text from structured data, but for recurring health outputs, fluency alone is insufficient. Systems need faithfulness to source data, evidence-based explanations, policy compliance, machine-readable outputs, and cost-effectiveness for repeated use.

Method: Introduces "Think Fast, Talk Smart" - a sleep-health insight pipeline where deterministic code performs recurring analysis first, then makes only one bounded LLM call for expression. Evaluated across 280 user-nights and six models, comparing against structured zero-shot and few-shot one-call baselines.

Result: The approach achieves lower numeric error, lower instruction-compliance error, and lower end-to-end cost than baselines. Analysis shows LLM comparison increases numeric error, LLM ranking degrades policy selection, LLM attribution increases unsupported causal claims, and LLM-generated interfaces reintroduce errors.

Conclusion: A design rule emerges: let deterministic code handle recurring analysis, and use LLMs only to express verified facts within bounded interfaces. This hybrid approach balances reliability and expressiveness for recurring health text generation tasks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being used to generate health text from structured records such as wearable time series, biomarkers, vitals, and care-management logs. For recurring health outputs, fluency is not enough: systems must remain faithful to source data, ground explanatory claims in available evidence, follow stated policies, emit machine-readable outputs, and run cheaply enough for repeated use. We ask which responsibilities in structured health generation should be deterministic computation rather than runtime LLM prompting.
