  We introduce Think Fast, Talk Smart, a sleep-health insight pipeline in which deterministic code performs recurring analysis before one bounded LLM writer call. Across 280 user-nights and six models, achieves lower numeric error, lower instruction-compliance error, and lower end-to-end cost than structured zero-shot and few-shot one-call baselines.
  Layer replacement reveals contract-specific failures: LLM comparison raises numeric error, LLM ranking degrades policy selection, LLM attribution increases unsupported causal language, and an LLM-generated writer interface reintroduces errors even after upstream facts are deterministic. The results support a broader design rule: let code own recurring analysis, and let LLMs express verified facts within bounded interfaces.

</details>


### [77] [PTCG-Bench: Can LLM Agents Master Pokémon Trading Card Game?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29653)
*Dongdong Hua,Yifei Sun,Renhong Huang,Feng Gao,Chunping Wang,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PTCG-Bench is a new benchmark built on the Pokémon Trading Card Game that evaluates LLM agents' strategic decision-making and self-evolution capabilities in complex interactive environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent benchmarks often fail to capture strategic and evolving decision-making scenarios that are crucial for autonomous agents operating in realistic interactive environments. Human players can quickly develop strategies in complex games after few rounds, but similar capabilities are needed for AI agents.

Method: The authors developed PTCG-Bench with two complementary evaluation levels: 1) decision-making performance within a single complex environment, and 2) ability to self-evolve through accumulated experience. They included a modular harness ablation to separate agent performance from model capability, allowing better interpretation of results.

Result: Experiments show that LLM agents can achieve non-trivial gameplay performance in the Pokémon Trading Card Game environment. However, sustained and stable self-evolution remains challenging, and performance is sensitive to harness design choices.

Conclusion: The PTCG-Bench benchmark addresses important gaps in current agent evaluation by focusing on strategic decision-making and self-evolution. It aims to facilitate future research on harness-aware and self-evolving agents in realistic interactive environments, highlighting that while LLM agents show promise, significant challenges remain in achieving stable self-evolution.

Abstract: Given a strategically complex board game, human players can quickly learn to devise strategies after playing a few rounds. Autonomous agents require similar capabilities in realistic interactive environments, yet existing agent benchmarks often fail to fully capture such strategic and evolving decision-making scenarios. We present PTCG-Bench, a benchmark built on the Pok'{e}mon Trading Card Game (PTCG) that evaluates LLM agents at two complementary levels: (1) their decision-making performance within a single complex environment, and (2) their ability to self-evolving through accumulated experience. We further include a modular harness ablation to better interpret agent performance without conflating it with model capability. Our experiments show that, although LLM agents can achieve non-trivial gameplay performance, sustained and stable self-evolution remains challenging, and performance is sensitive to harness design. We hope that PTCG-Bench will facilitate future research on harness-aware and self-evolving agents in realistic interactive environments.

</details>


### [78] [TRACE: Toulmin-based Reasoning Assessment through Constructive Elements for LLM CoT Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29656)
*Yundong Kim,Heyoung Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TRACE is a new metric for evaluating Chain-of-Thought reasoning in LLMs by analyzing argument construction using Toulmin's argumentation theory and Flavell's metacognitive framework, showing strong correlation with benchmark accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing metrics for evaluating open-ended LLM outputs focus on final-answer accuracy or surface-level statistics, leaving the reasoning process itself unexamined. There's a need for metrics that assess the quality of reasoning processes rather than just outcomes.

Method: TRACE integrates Toulmin's argumentation theory (which analyzes how arguments are constructed) with Flavell's metacognitive framework to assess reasoning structure. It focuses on analyzing Chain-of-Thought reasoning processes rather than judging outcomes.

Result: Experiments on 26.3K QA samples across 7 reasoning models show strong correlation with benchmark accuracy (r=0.74). TRACE is effective as a reinforcement learning reward signal, outperforming accuracy-only baselines.

Conclusion: Logically sound reasoning leads to higher-quality answers, and TRACE serves as a complementary metric for evaluating open-ended outputs that focuses on reasoning quality rather than just final answers.

Abstract: Evaluating open-ended outputs from large language models (LLMs) remains challenging due to the absence of ground truth. Existing metrics rely on final-answer accuracy or surface-level statistics, leaving the reasoning process itself unexamined. We introduce TRACE (Toulmin-based Reasoning Assessment through Constructive Elements), a metric that analyzes Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning processes. Rather than judging outcomes, TRACE inspects how arguments are constructed by integrating Toulmin's argumentation theory with Flavell's metacognitive framework to assess reasoning structure. Experiments on 26.3K QA samples across 7 reasoning models show strong correlation with benchmark accuracy (r=0.74). Furthermore, TRACE is effective as a reinforcement learning reward signal, outperforming accuracy-only baselines. Together, these results indicate that logically sound reasoning leads to higher-quality answers. TRACE thus serves as a complementary metric for evaluating open-ended outputs. Code is available at https://github.com/hyyangkisti/trace.

</details>


### [79] [GRASP: Gated Regression-Aware Skill Proposer for Self-Improving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29668)
*Johannes Moll,Jean-Philippe Corbeil,Jiazhen Pan,Martin Hadamitzky,Daniel Rueckert,Lisa Adams,Keno Bressem*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GRASP improves LLM agents by using gated skill acquisition with regression testing, achieving significant performance gains in clinical and non-clinical domains while preventing performance degradation from new skills.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents often fail operationally in structured environments due to inadequate procedural knowledge. Existing self-improvement methods add natural-language guidance without verifying that new items don't regress previously correct behavior, causing fixes for one trajectory to silently break others.

Method: GRASP treats agent improvement as editing a bounded skill library, accepting new skills only if they produce net improvement on held-out probes while staying within a hard regression budget. It uses comparative proposal generation, an acceptance gate, and regression testing to validate skills.

Result: On MedAgentBench, GRASP improved gpt-oss-120b from 40.6% to 88.8%, exceeded the strongest baseline by 21.0 points, and improved every other base model by 17.2 to 40.3 points. It generalized beyond clinical domains, improved agents in 3 of 4 non-clinical environments, and showed transfer learning where skills from stronger models improved weaker executors.

Conclusion: GRASP's gated skill acquisition with regression testing significantly improves LLM agent reliability in structured environments. The validation mechanism is crucial—skill writing alone without validation performs no better than using no skills. The approach enables safe knowledge transfer between models while preventing negative transfer.

Abstract: LLM agents acting in structured environments fail in operational rather than conversational ways, and reliability depends on procedural knowledge of the environment. Prior self-improvement methods accumulate natural-language guidance without checking that each new item preserves previously correct behavior, so a note that fixes one trajectory can silently regress another. We introduce GRASP (Gated Regression-Aware Skill Proposer), which treats agent improvement as a sequence of edits to a bounded skill library, admitting each candidate only if it produces a net improvement on a balanced held-out probe under a hard regression budget. We evaluate GRASP across five base models (gpt-oss-120b, DeepSeek V4 Flash, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite, GPT-4.1, GPT-5.4) on two FHIR-based clinical benchmarks. On MedAgentBench, GRASP lifts gpt-oss-120b from 40.6% to 88.8%, exceeds the strongest of five self-improvement baselines by 21.0 points, and improves every other base model by 17.2 to 40.3 points. Ablations attribute the gain to comparative proposal generation, the acceptance gate, and the hard regression budget rather than to skill writing itself, which without validation is no better than using no skills. The mechanism generalizes beyond the clinical domain, improving agents on three of four non-clinical environments and remaining flat only where the action space is open-ended. Frozen libraries transfer across models, where skills from a stronger model improve weaker executors beyond what they learn for themselves while the reverse does not, an asymmetry that no ungated baseline reproduces.

</details>


### [80] [Notation Matters: A Benchmark Study of Token-Optimized Formats in Agentic AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29676)
*Lorenz Kutschka,Bernhard Geiger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Token-optimized alternatives TOON and TRON are evaluated as JSON replacements for Agentic AI systems to reduce token overhead, with TRON achieving up to 27% token reduction and TOON achieving 18% reduction, but both come with accuracy trade-offs in end-to-end agent benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: JSON is inefficient for Agentic AI systems because its structural elements impose substantial token overhead, and while token-optimized alternatives like TOON and TRON exist, their effectiveness in end-to-end agentic loops hasn't been evaluated.

Method: Evaluated TOON and TRON on four agentic benchmarks (BFCL, MCPToolBenchPP, MCP-Universe, StableToolBench) with five open-weight LLMs, decoupling input compression from output compression to measure comprehension and generation independently.

Result: TRON reduces tokens by up to 27% with accuracy within 14 percentage points of JSON baseline; TOON achieves up to 18% reduction at similar 9pp accuracy cost, but suffers from cascading multi-turn parsing failures and collapses parallel tool-call output for most models.

Conclusion: Token-optimized formats like TRON and TOON can substantially reduce token overhead in Agentic AI systems, but come with accuracy trade-offs and implementation challenges that need to be carefully considered based on specific use cases.

Abstract: Large language models in Agentic AI systems consume tool schemas and execution results and emit tool invocations as structured data. The default language for that exchange, JSON, was designed for application-to-application interchange rather than token efficiency, so its structural elements impose substantial token overhead. Recent work proposes token-optimized alternatives such as TOON (Token-Oriented Object Notation) and TRON (Token Reduced Object Notation) as more compact replacements, but these formats have been evaluated only on isolated comprehension or generation tasks. Whether their token reductions hold inside end-to-end agentic loops therefore remains an open question. We evaluate TOON and TRON on four agentic benchmarks (BFCL, MCPToolBenchPP, MCP-Universe, StableToolBench) and five open-weight LLMs, decoupling input compression from output compression to measure comprehension and generation independently. TRON reduces tokens by up to 27% with accuracy within 14pp of the JSON baseline. TOON achieves up to 18% reduction at a similar 9pp accuracy cost, but additionally cascades on multi-turn parsing failures and collapses parallel tool-call output for most models.

</details>


### [81] [NICE: A Theory-Grounded Diagnostic Benchmark for Social Intelligence of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29685)
*Yunjin Qi,Zhaojun Jiang,Xuan Wu,Hanxi Pan,Yixuan Wang,Yanfang Liu,Xiang Ji,Churu Yu,Chunyuan Zheng,Yingze Chen,Jie He,Liuqing Chen,Zaifeng Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NICE is a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating LLM social intelligence with a unified framework (4 categories, 11 dimensions) grounded in social theory, revealing models' weakness in Communication aspects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in social contexts like emotional companionship and customer service, existing social intelligence benchmarks lack a unified framework for fine-grained diagnosis of social abilities, making quality and safety assessment difficult.

Method: 1) Constructed a social intelligence framework through literature review and multi-stage expert validation guided by psychometric principles; 2) Built NICE benchmark with 137 items operationalized through Chinese contexts; 3) Evaluated 5 frontier LLMs and human reference group.

Result: Models achieved higher aggregate accuracy but showed consistent weakness in Communication, specifically in three capability facets: multi-turn communication, nonverbal communication, and synchrony.

Conclusion: NICE reframes social intelligence evaluation toward theory-grounded diagnosis of socially consequential weaknesses in LLMs, enabling more precise assessment of models' social capabilities.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in social contexts such as emotional companionship and customer service, measuring their social intelligence has become critical to the quality and safety of human-AI interaction. However, existing social intelligence benchmarks lack a unified framework that organizes social abilities into a unified structure, and therefore cannot enable fine-grained diagnosis. To build the first holistic diagnostic evaluation grounded in social theory, we first construct a social intelligence framework through a literature review and multi-stage expert validation guided by psychometric principles. The resulting framework includes 4 categories and 11 dimensions, each further specified by fine-grained capability facets. Building on this framework, we introduce NICE (Norm, Interaction, Cognition, Experience), a diagnostic benchmark of 137 items operationalized through representative Chinese contexts. Across 5 frontier LLMs and a human reference group, models score higher in aggregate accuracy yet show a consistent weakness in Communication, which the framework localizes to 3 specific capability facets: multi-turn communication, nonverbal communication, and synchrony. NICE thus reframes social intelligence evaluation toward theory-grounded diagnosis of socially consequential weaknesses in LLMs.

</details>


### [82] [Reliable Reasoning with Large Language Models via Preference-Based Maximum Satisfiability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29687)
*Pedro Orvalho,Marta Kwiatkowska,Guillem Alenyà,Felip Manyà*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs generate Python code to encode user constraints/preferences as MaxSAT problems, which are then solved by exact solvers and independently verified for correctness, achieving much higher feasibility rates than direct approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs excel at natural language understanding but struggle with optimization tasks involving multiple constraints and user-defined preferences, which are common in domains like robotics.

Method: Hybrid reasoning approach where LLMs generate Python code that encodes constraints/preferences as preference-based MaxSAT problems. The generated code is solved by exact MaxSAT solvers, and solutions are independently verified for feasibility and optimality against canonical MaxSAT encodings.

Result: The MaxSAT-based pipeline achieves substantially higher acceptance rates (in some cases exceeding 80%) compared to direct-answer, chain-of-thought, and program-of-thought baselines. The baselines rarely produce feasible solutions while the proposed approach enables solver-verifiable optimization.

Conclusion: LLM-driven code generation combined with preference-based MaxSAT enables solver-verifiable optimization and substantially improves correctness under independently verified reference semantics, demonstrating the effectiveness of this hybrid reasoning approach.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at understanding natural language but struggle with optimisation tasks involving multiple constraints and user-defined preferences, which commonly arise in domains such as robotics. We propose a hybrid reasoning approach in which LLMs externalise reasoning through code generation. Given a natural language problem description, an LLM generates Python code that encodes user-defined constraints and preferences as a preference-based Maximum Satisfiability (MaxSAT) problem, which is then solved by an exact MaxSAT solver. To ensure correctness, solutions returned by the model-generated code are independently verified for feasibility and optimality against a canonical MaxSAT encoding, allowing for different encodings and multiple optimal solutions. We evaluate our approach using both open-source and closed-access LLMs on three families of preference-based reasoning tasks, and compare it against direct-answer, chain-of-thought, and program-of-thought baselines using the same models. While these baselines rarely produce feasible solutions, the MaxSAT-based pipeline achieves substantially higher acceptance rates, in some cases exceeding 80%. Our results demonstrate that LLM-driven code generation combined with preference-based MaxSAT enables solver-verifiable optimisation with respect to generated encodings, and substantially improves correctness under independently verified reference semantics.

</details>


### [83] [FHRFormer: A Self-Supervised Masked Transformer Framework for Fetal Heart Rate Time-Series Inpainting and Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29695)
*Kjersti Engan,Neel Kanwal,Anita Yeconia,Ladislaus Blacy,Yuda Munyaw,Estomih Mduma,Hege Ersdal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI-powered masked transformer autoencoder reconstructs missing fetal heart rate signals from wearable monitors to enable more accurate risk prediction of neonatal breathing assistance needs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Approximately 10% of newborns need breathing assistance at birth, and fetal heart rate monitoring is crucial for detecting risks. However, wearable FHR monitors suffer from signal dropout due to maternal movement, creating missing data that limits AI analysis. Traditional interpolation methods fail to preserve signal characteristics needed for accurate risk prediction.

Method: The authors propose a masked transformer-based autoencoder approach to reconstruct missing FHR signals. This method captures both local temporal patterns and frequency components of the data, making it robust across varying durations of missing data. The approach can be used for both signal inpainting (filling gaps) and forecasting.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates robustness across different durations of missing data and can reconstruct FHR signals while preserving their spectral characteristics. It enables signal inpainting and forecasting, overcoming limitations of traditional interpolation methods.

Conclusion: The masked transformer autoencoder successfully reconstructs missing FHR data, enabling more reliable AI-based risk analysis for predicting neonatal breathing assistance needs. The method can be applied retrospectively to research datasets and potentially integrated into wearable FHR monitoring devices for earlier and more robust risk detection.

Abstract: Approximately 10% of newborns require assistance to initiate breathing at birth, and around 5% need ventilation support. Fetal heart rate (FHR) monitoring plays a crucial role in assessing fetal well-being during prenatal care, enabling the detection of abnormal patterns and supporting timely obstetric interventions to mitigate fetal risks during labor. Applying artificial intelligence (AI) methods to analyze large datasets of continuous FHR monitoring episodes with diverse outcomes may offer novel insights into predicting the risk of needing breathing assistance or interventions. Recent advances in wearable FHR monitors have enabled continuous fetal monitoring without compromising maternal mobility. However, sensor displacement during maternal movement, as well as changes in fetal or maternal position, often lead to signal dropout, resulting in gaps in recorded FHR data. Such missing data limits the extraction of meaningful insights and complicates automated (AI-based) analysis. Traditional approaches to handling missing data, such as simple interpolation techniques, often fail to preserve the spectral characteristics of the signals. In this paper, we propose a masked transformer-based autoencoder approach to reconstruct missing FHR signals by capturing both local temporal and frequency components of the data. The proposed method demonstrates robustness across varying durations of missing data and can be used for signal inpainting and forecasting. The proposed approach can be applied retrospectively to research datasets to support the development of AI-based risk algorithms. In the future, the proposed method could be integrated into wearable FHR monitoring devices to achieve earlier and more robust risk detection.

</details>


### [84] [Beyond Trajectory Rewards: Step-level Credit Assignment for Agentic Search via Graph Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29697)
*Yuchen Liu,Yingjie Feng,Lixiong Qin,Jiasi Chen,Jianing Yu,Sheng Gao,Sheng Yang,Weiran Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Graph-Distance Contribution Reward (GDCR) provides step-level process rewards for agentic search by measuring entity distances to answers in a knowledge graph, combined with Step Advantage Policy Optimization (SAPO) that merges step-level and trajectory-level advantages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agentic search methods have limitations: trajectory-level outcome rewards don't quantify individual step contributions, while existing step-level reward methods rely on costly tree sampling. There's a need for efficient step-level reward mechanisms that can properly attribute behavioral contributions to individual search steps.

Method: Proposes viewing world knowledge as a latent world graph and each information-seeking task as search within a latent task graph. Introduces Graph-Distance Contribution Reward (GDCR) that scores newly-retrieved and newly-cited entities by their distance to the answer node in a training-time Entity-Relation (ER) graph. Also proposes Step Advantage Policy Optimization (SAPO) which converts GDCR into step-level advantages and combines them with trajectory-level outcome advantages.

Result: Experiments on four challenging benchmarks validate the effectiveness of the proposed method, demonstrating improved performance in agentic search tasks.

Conclusion: The GDCR+SAPO framework successfully addresses the limitations of existing reward mechanisms by providing efficient step-level process rewards based on graph distances, enabling more effective attribution of contributions to individual search steps without requiring costly tree sampling.

Abstract: In Agentic Search, trajectory-level outcome rewards fail to quantify the behavioral contributions of individual steps, while existing step-level reward methods typically rely on costly tree sampling. We view world knowledge as a latent world graph and each IS task as search within a latent task graph, where effective steps should make graph progress toward the answer node. Based on this prior, we propose Graph-Distance Contribution Reward (GDCR), a step-level process reward that scores newly-retrieved and newly-cited entities by their distance to the answer node in a training-time Entity-Relation (ER) graph. We further propose Step Advantage Policy Optimization (SAPO), which converts GDCR into step-level advantages and combines them with trajectory-level outcome advantages. Experiments on four challenging benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our method.

</details>


### [85] [BitTP: The Lightweight Trajectory Prediction Model with BitLLM for Edge-Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29705)
*Mincheol Kang,Hyunjin Lim,Bomin Kang,Daehee Park*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BitTP converts LLM-based trajectory predictors to lightweight bitlinear architectures using 1.58-bit weight-only quantization, improving prediction quality while reducing memory usage for edge deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based trajectory predictors provide strong contextual reasoning and interpretability but are extremely memory- and compute-intensive, making them difficult to deploy on resource-constrained edge devices like autonomous robot on-board computers.

Method: Proposes BitTP which converts LLM-based trajectory predictors into lightweight bitlinear architectures using weight-only quantization to 1.58-bit (BitTP-Weight) while keeping activations in full precision to avoid degradation in spatio-temporal reasoning.

Result: BitTP-Weight not only preserves but improves prediction quality over full-precision (BF16) LLM baseline, reducing ADE by 14.29% and FDE by 20.97% on average while reducing memory usage and inference latency relative to other quantization methods.

Conclusion: Carefully designed quantization acts as an effective regularizer, enabling practical deployment of sophisticated LLM-based reasoning on edge devices, bridging the gap between powerful LLM predictors and resource-constrained edge deployment.

Abstract: Trajectory prediction is a fundamental task for autonomous systems, requiring complex reasoning about multi-agent interactions and intents. Large language models (LLMs) have recently been adopted for this task, as they provide strong contextual reasoning and interpretable, language-based trajectory representations. However, these LLM-based predictors are extremely memory- and compute-intensive, making them difficult to deploy on resource-constrained edge devices such as on-board computers in autonomous robots. To bridge this gap, we propose BitTP, which converts an LLM-based trajectory predictor into a lightweight bitlinear architecture. We demonstrate that weight-only quantization to 1.58-bit (BitTP-Weight) is optimal. Crucially, activations must remain in full precision, as quantizing them leads to severe degradation and instability in spatio-temporal reasoning. Empirically, BitTP-Weight not only preserves but improves prediction quality over the full-precision (BF16) LLM baseline, reducing ADE by 14.29% and FDE by 20.97% on average, while simultaneously reducing memory usage and inference latency relative to other quantization methods. These results demonstrate that carefully designed quantization acts as an effective regularizer, enabling the practical deployment of sophisticated LLM-based reasoning on edge devices. Code is available at: https://github.com/MintCat98/BitTP.

</details>


### [86] [NaRA: Noise-Aware LoRA for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Diffusion LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29716)
*Shuaidi Wang,Zhan Zhuang,Ruping Huang,Yu Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NaRA is a noise-aware parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for diffusion large language models that adapts to different noise levels during the denoising process, outperforming standard PEFT methods like LoRA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PEFT methods (e.g., LoRA) were designed for autoregressive models and use static parameters that don't account for the varying noise levels in diffusion processes. This makes them suboptimal for dLLMs since input distributions and generation difficulty change significantly along the denoising trajectory.

Method: Proposes Noise-aware Low-Rank Adaptation (NaRA), which introduces a low-rank core matrix generated by a lightweight hypernetwork conditioned on the noise level. This allows update matrices to vary continuously during the diffusion process while keeping parameter and latency overhead minimal.

Result: NaRA consistently outperforms noise-agnostic baselines across multiple benchmarks including commonsense reasoning, mathematical reasoning, and code generation tasks.

Conclusion: NaRA successfully addresses the limitations of existing PEFT methods for dLLMs by incorporating noise awareness into the fine-tuning process, providing both theoretical justification and empirical improvements across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) have emerged as a promising non-autoregressive generative paradigm. Given the prohibitive computational cost of full fine-tuning, Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) has become the standard approach. However, existing PEFT methods (e.g., LoRA), originally tailored for autoregressive models, rely on static parameters that are agnostic to the noise level. Consequently, they ignore the intrinsic dynamics of the diffusion process, where input distributions and generation difficulty shift significantly along the denoising trajectory, rendering them suboptimal for dLLMs. To address this, we propose Noise-aware Low-Rank Adaptation (NaRA), which introduces a low-rank core matrix generated by a lightweight, globally shared hypernetwork conditioned on the noise level. This design enables the update matrices to vary continuously along the diffusion process while keeping parameter and latency overhead negligible. We provide a theoretical justification for the proposed NaRA framework and empirically demonstrate consistent improvements over noise-agnostic baselines across commonsense reasoning, mathematical reasoning, and code generation benchmarks. Our code is available at https://github.com/generaldi/NaRA.

</details>


### [87] [Uncertainty-Aware Transfer Learning for Cross-Building Energy Forecasting: Toward Robust and Scalable District-Level Energy Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29733)
*Shadmehr Zaregarizi,Khashayar Yavari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An uncertainty-aware transfer learning framework using Temporal Fusion Transformer achieves robust cross-building energy forecasting with minimal target-domain data through probe-only fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling data-driven energy forecasting to district level requires models that can be re-used across buildings with minimal target-domain data while providing reliable uncertainty estimates, addressing the challenge of deploying energy forecasting models in diverse building contexts.

Method: Uses an uncertainty-aware transfer learning framework based on Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT), evaluated on real sub-meter datasets from educational building (source) and multi-typology building (target). Introduces Transfer Robustness Index (TRI) as an architecture-agnostic metric for generalization quality. Applies four-strategy layer-freezing ablation with Monte Carlo Dropout for uncertainty quantification.

Result: Probe-Only fine-tuning (updating only 455 output-layer parameters out of 806K) achieved best transfer quality (TRI = 3,097), outperforming full fine-tuning. Monte Carlo Dropout achieved 93.2% prediction interval coverage probability (close to 95% target). Data-scarcity analysis showed monotonic improvement with increasing target-domain data.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates that TFT encoders learn transferable temporal representations, enabling effective cross-building energy forecasting with minimal target data. Probe-Only fine-tuning is particularly effective, and the approach provides practical guidance for district energy deployment with honest uncertainty estimates.

Abstract: Scaling data-driven energy forecasting to district level requires models that can be re-used across buildings with minimal target-domain data and honest uncertainty estimates. We present an uncertainty-aware transfer learning (TL) framework for cross-building energy forecasting based on the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT), evaluated on a newly released high-resolution real sub-meter dataset: an educational building at Aalborg University, Denmark (source) and the multi-typology NEST building at EMPA, Switzerland (target). We introduce the Transfer Robustness Index (TRI), an architecture-agnostic metric for quantifying generalization quality across domain gaps. A four-strategy layer-freezing ablation shows that Probe-Only fine-tuning, updating only 455 output-layer parameters out of 806K, achieves the best transfer quality (TRI = 3,097), outperforming full fine-tuning and suggesting that TFT encoders learn transferable temporal representations. Monte Carlo Dropout yields a prediction interval coverage probability of 93.2%, close to the nominal 95% target. A data-scarcity analysis further shows monotonic improvement with increasing target-domain data, providing practical guidance for district energy deployment.

</details>


### [88] [Citation-Closure Retrieval and Per-Rule Attribution for Real-World Regulatory Compliance Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29742)
*Yeong-Joon Ju,Seong-Whan Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RefWalk is a novel framework for regulatory compliance QA that addresses limitations of existing RAG systems by using topic-anchored citation traversal and explicit attribution mapping, evaluated on the new RegOps-Bench benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RAG systems struggle with regulatory compliance QA due to flattened citation edges, fragmented retrieval expansions, and fragile post-hoc attribution when dealing with multi-tiered authority structures in regulations.

Method: RefWalk uses a shared topic anchor to traverse cross-document citations, fuses multi-view candidates via max-based aggregation, and enforces per-rule attribution to explicitly map claims to sources. The RegOps-Bench benchmark features an Operational Knowledge Graph from complex national R&D regulations.

Result: Substantial improvements in retrieval recall and citation accuracy were achieved. Contrastive evaluation on HIPAA showed existing systems saturate on flat-structure rules but struggle with complex regulatory structures, highlighting the need for RegOps-Bench.

Conclusion: Regulatory compliance QA requires specialized approaches like RefWalk that handle structured procedural lookups and evidence-set closure, with RegOps-Bench addressing gaps in existing benchmarks for evaluating such systems.

Abstract: Deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) for regulatory compliance demands rigorous traceability via comprehensive citations across multi-tiered authority structures. Unlike traditional multi-hop or legal QA, this task requires structured procedural lookups and evidence-set closure rather than entity resolution or case-law reasoning. Existing RAG systems struggle here due to flattened citation edges, fragmented retrieval expansions, and fragile post-hoc attribution. We formalize Regulatory Compliance QA with RegOps-Bench, a novel benchmark featuring an Operational Knowledge Graph derived from complex national R\&D regulations. To address these bottlenecks, we propose RefWalk, a unified framework driven by a shared topic anchor. RefWalk traverses cross-document citations, fuses multi-view candidates via max-based aggregation, and enforces per-rule attribution to explicitly map claims to sources. We establish a strong baseline with substantial improvements in retrieval recall and citation accuracy. Finally, a contrastive evaluation on a U.S. health compliance dataset (HIPAA) reveals that existing systems exhibit saturation on flat-structure rules, underscoring the need for RegOps-Bench. Our code is available at https://github.com/yeongjoonJu/RefWalk.

</details>


### [89] [Benchmarking Positional Encoding Strategies for Transformer-Based EEG Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29754)
*Ayse Betul Yuce,Sebastian Stober*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EEG foundation models require positional encoding for electrodes, and this study benchmarks five strategies showing task-dependent performance with no universal best approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers used in EEG decoding require positional information since they're permutation-invariant. Unlike text tokens, EEG electrodes have specific spatial scalp positions, raising the question of how best to encode these positions in transformer-based EEG models for effective brain activity analysis.

Method: The study benchmarks five positional encoding strategies within the CBraMod backbone, evaluating them using linear probing and fine-tuning protocols on two EEG tasks: motor imagery classification and emotion recognition.

Result: No single positional encoding strategy consistently outperforms across tasks. Spherical Positional Encoding (SPE) works well for motor imagery but underperforms on emotion recognition, while Asymmetric Conditional Positional Encoding (ACPE) shows more consistent performance across both tasks.

Conclusion: Optimal positional encoding strategy for transformer-based EEG models is task-dependent, with no universal solution across different EEG decoding scenarios, suggesting researchers should consider task-specific requirements when choosing encoding methods.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a widely used non-invasive technique for measuring brain activity in brain-computer interface (BCI) applications. Supervised EEG decoding models often struggle to generalize across tasks, subjects, and datasets, motivating transformer-based EEG foundation models trained with self-supervised learning. Since transformers are permutation-invariant, they require explicit positional information. Unlike textual tokens, EEG electrodes are spatially distributed across the scalp, raising the question of how electrode positions should be encoded in transformer-based EEG models. In this study, we benchmark five positional encoding strategies within the CBraMod backbone and evaluate them under linear probing and fine-tuning protocols on motor imagery classification and emotion recognition. Our results show that no single strategy consistently outperforms across tasks. Spherical Positional Encoding (SPE) yields strong representations for motor imagery but underperforms on emotion recognition, while Asymmetric Conditional Positional Encoding (ACPE) demonstrates more consistent performance across tasks. These findings suggest that the optimal positional encoding strategy is task-dependent, with no universal solution across EEG decoding scenarios.

</details>


### [90] [LFQ: Logit-aware Final-block Quantization for Boosting the Generation Quality of Low-Bit Quantized LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29756)
*Jung Hyun Lee,June Yong Yang,Jungwook Choi,Eunho Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LFQ (Logit-aware Final-block Quantization) improves block-wise PTQ for generative tasks by quantizing the final Transformer block to align logits with the FP model, using cross-entropy instead of MSE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current block-wise post-training quantization (PTQ) performs well on basic language tasks but degrades on generative tasks, especially for longer responses and chains of thought. This is due to two issues: omission of the unembedding layer in optimization and reliance on MSE objective, causing token probability misalignment.

Method: LFQ enhances block-wise PTQ by quantizing the final Transformer block while minimizing cross-entropy between FP and quantized model logits, focusing on aligning token probability distributions at the logit level rather than using MSE.

Result: LFQ consistently improves accuracy on complex generation tasks over state-of-the-art block-wise PTQ across diverse model families while maintaining parity with FP baselines on language modeling and understanding tasks.

Conclusion: By addressing the token probability distribution misalignment in block-wise PTQ through logit-aware final-block quantization, LFQ provides an effective solution for maintaining generative task accuracy in low-bit weight-only quantization of large language models.

Abstract: As large language models continue to scale, low-bit weight-only post-training quantization (PTQ) offers a practical solution to their memory-efficient deployment. Although block-wise PTQ is capable of matching the full-precision (FP) baseline on basic language modeling and understanding, its quality is degraded for generative tasks -- especially at longer responses and extended chains of thought, which is critical in boosting task accuracy. We attribute this shortfall to two factors: (i) the omission of the unembedding layer (the LM head) in block-wise optimization and (ii) the reliance on the mean squared error (MSE) objective. Both factors cause the token probability distribution of the quantized model to misalign with that of the FP model, yielding notable accuracy drops on text generation benchmarks. To rectify the discrepancy, we introduce Logit-aware Final-block Quantization (LFQ), a simple yet effective enhancement to block-wise PTQ that quantizes the final Transformer block by minimizing the cross-entropy between the logits of the FP model and those of its quantized counterpart. By aligning token probabilities at the logit level in the final block, LFQ consistently improves the accuracy of complex generation tasks over state-of-the-art block-wise PTQ across diverse model families, while maintaining parity with FP baselines on language modeling and understanding.

</details>


### [91] [From XXLTraffic to EvoXXLTraffic: Scaling Traffic Forecasting to Sensor-Evolving Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29768)
*Du Yin,Hao Xue,Arian Prabowo,Shuang Ao,Flora Salim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: XXLTraffic dataset family enables traffic forecasting on continuously growing sensor networks, with EvoXXLTraffic specifically modeling sensor evolution over up to 27 years, revealing that many state-of-the-art methods fail under realistic evolving road networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing traffic forecasting benchmarks assume fixed sensor sets, but real-world road-sensor networks continuously grow and evolve as infrastructure changes over years, creating a mismatch between research datasets and real-world conditions.

Method: Introduced XXLTraffic dataset family spanning up to 27 years of California PeMS and Transport for NSW data, with EvoXXLTraffic specifically reorganizing data to expose per-year active sensors, yearly traffic-flow matrices, and yearly graph snapshots. Created a yearly streaming forecasting protocol where each calendar year is a continual task, and benchmarked various baselines including static spatio-temporal GNNs, online schemes, evolving-graph continual methods, and retrieval/test-time methods.

Result: The ultra-large evolutionary dataset better reflects real-world conditions, and many state-of-the-art methods that work on fixed-sensor datasets fail when confronted with evolving sensor networks with growth ratios ranging from +305% to over +10,000% across nine PeMS districts.

Conclusion: The XXLTraffic dataset family complements existing benchmarks by enabling more realistic traffic forecasting under ultra-long evolutionary road networks, addressing the critical gap between static research datasets and dynamically evolving real-world infrastructure.

Abstract: Existing traffic forecasting benchmarks assume a fixed sensor set, but real road-sensor networks grow continuously as the road network changes year by year. We introduce the XXLTraffic dataset family, which spans up to 27 years of California PeMS and Transport for NSW data. The fixed-sensor subsets of XXLTraffic support extremely long forecasting with multi-year gaps and standard hourly / daily long-horizon forecasting. We extend it to EvoXXLTraffic, a sensor-evolving reorganization that exposes per-year active sensors, yearly traffic-flow matrices, and yearly graph snapshots across nine PeMS districts, with growth ratios ranging from +305% to over +10,000%. We define a yearly streaming forecasting protocol on EvoXXLTraffic in which each calendar year is a continual task, and benchmark a wide range of representative baselines drawn from static spatio-temporal GNNs, naïve online schemes, evolving-graph continual methods, and retrieval / test-time methods. We find that our ultra-large evolutionary dataset better reflects the real world, and many state-of-the-art (SOTA) results no longer work. Our dataset complements existing benchmarks by enabling more realistic forecasting under ultra-long evolutionary road networks.

</details>


### [92] [Croissant Tasks: A Metadata Format for Reproducible Machine Learning Evaluations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29786)
*Omar Benjelloun,Leonardo Martins Bianco,Isabelle Guyon,Thanh Gia Hieu Khuong,Jonathan Lebensold,Sebastian Lobentanzer,Luis Oala,Benedictus Kent Rachmat,Ihsan Ullah,Peyman Vahidi,Joaquin Vanschoren*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Croissant Tasks introduces a declarative metadata format for machine learning reproducibility that enables autonomous agents to generate implementations from specifications rather than requiring brittle code replication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reproducibility is a critical challenge in machine learning due to underspecified execution details, brittle software environments, and the limitations of human-centric remedies like checklists and manual verification that require intensive effort and don't scale.

Method: The authors propose: (1) the Croissant Tasks specification - a declarative, machine-actionable metadata format that abstracts low-level implementation details into high-level specifications, formally decoupling task problem from solution; (2) an automated LLM pipeline that retrofits existing benchmarks into this format; and (3) autonomous agents that ingest these specifications to generate functional reproduction pipelines from scratch.

Result: Empirical validation shows that autonomous agents can successfully ingest Croissant Tasks specifications to generate functional, accurate reproduction pipelines from scratch, demonstrating conceptual reproducibility.

Conclusion: Croissant Tasks format provides a new foundation for automated and conceptual reproducibility in machine learning, enabling verification of claims through independent agent-generated implementations rather than brittle source code replication.

Abstract: Reproducibility is fundamental to the scientific method, yet remains a critical challenge in machine learning. Contributing factors include underspecified execution details and brittle software environments. Human-centric remedies, such as checklists and manual verification, help but require intensive effort and fail to scale. To address this, we introduce Croissant Tasks: a declarative, machine-actionable metadata format that abstracts low-level implementation details into high-level specifications. This format enables conceptual reproducibility: verifying claims via independent, agent-generated implementations rather than brittle source code replication. We contribute: (1) the Croissant Tasks specification, formally decoupling task problem from solution; (2) an automated LLM pipeline that retrofits existing benchmarks into this format; and (3) empirical validation showing autonomous agents can ingest these specifications to generate functional, accurate reproduction pipelines from scratch. We envision this format as a new foundation for automated and conceptual reproducibility in machine learning.

</details>


### [93] [Certified Policy Optimisation for Nested Causal Bandits via PAC-Bayes Risk](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29788)
*Tim Woydt,Paul-David Zuercher*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Nested Causal Thompson Sampling (NCTS) addresses hierarchical sequential decision-making with causal coupling between timescales, providing off-policy risk certification for safe deployment via progressive certified handover.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard bandit and reinforcement learning theory fails to capture causal coupling between hierarchical decision timescales (strategic decisions shaping tactical contexts), which is critical for many real-world sequential decision problems.

Method: Formalizes Nested Contextual Causal Bandits (NCCBs) as hierarchical structural causal models where actions set next-level context distributions. Proposes Nested Causal Thompson Sampling (NCTS) with mechanism-factorised belief sampling per episode and recursive action under those beliefs.

Result: Develops causal PAC-Bayesian excess-risk bound for off-policy deployment certification from historical data. Experiments show factorised SCM-mechanism posterior transfers better zero-shot under distribution shifts, recursive meta-to-inner commit dominates joint-commit alternative, and certificates contract with accumulated data.

Conclusion: Enables progressive certified handover - a safe deployment method where each timescale transitions from legacy controller to NCTS independently when gains can be certified, addressing the fundamental "can we trust this agent here?" deployment question.

Abstract: Critical sequential decisions are rarely single-timescale: a strategic decision causally shapes the context in which every subsequent tactical choice is made; standard bandit and reinforcement-learning theory does not capture this causal coupling between timescales. We formalise the problem class as Nested Contextual Causal Bandits (NCCBs), a hierarchical SCM where each level's action sets the next level's context distribution, and propose Nested Causal Thompson Sampling (NCTS), which draws one mechanism-factorised belief per episode and acts recursively under it. Our main theoretical result is a causal PAC-Bayesian excess-risk bound that certifies any candidate deployment policy from historic data alone, off-policy and anytime, answering the deployment question: can we trust this agent here, and at what risk? Experiments on a hierarchical SCM show that, against a matched RFF-GP joint regression on the same function class, the factorised SCM-mechanism posterior transfers significantly better zero-shot under exogenous distribution shifts, the recursive meta-to-inner commit significantly dominates the joint-commit alternative in distribution, and the certificate significantly contracts as offline data accumulates. Combining these results, we establish progressive certified handover, a safe-deployment method: each timescale flips from a legacy controller to NCTS when gains can be certified, independently of the others.

</details>


### [94] [SkillsInjector: Dynamic Skill Context Construction for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29794)
*Yanchao Li,Wanhao Liu,Ben Gao,Jiaqing Xie,Zhehong Ai,Na Zou,Yuqiang Li,Tianfan Fu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Adaptive skill injection framework that dynamically selects skills, adjusts injection budget, and tailors descriptions based on context outperforms static skill libraries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods treat skill injection as static - fixed skill selection, predetermined budget, and unchanged descriptions. This static approach can undermine skill utility since which skills are exposed, how many are included, and how they're presented all affect downstream performance.

Method: SkillsInjector: two-stage adaptive method with (1) context planner that learns execution-grounded skill preferences and admits adaptive number of skills per task, and (2) set-aware renderer that tailors how selected descriptions are presented relative to co-injected neighbors.

Result: Achieves highest scores across tau2-bench (+3.9%), SkillsBench (+6.1%), and ALFWorld (+7.3%) over strongest baselines. Ablations show skill selection, adaptive budgeting, and set-aware rendering each contribute to gains.

Conclusion: Skill-augmented agents benefit from optimizing the injected context itself, showing that adaptive skill injection significantly improves performance over static approaches.

Abstract: LLM agents now draw on growing skill libraries to handle complex tasks. However, injecting more skills does not always improve task completion and can even degrade it. Existing methods still treat skill injection as a static step, selecting skills with fixed criteria, fixing the budget in advance, and leaving descriptions unchanged. We argue that this static treatment can undermine the utility of skills, because which skills are exposed, how many are included, and how they are presented all affect downstream performance. We propose SkillsInjector, a two-stage adaptive method that jointly addresses these decisions. First, a context planner learns execution-grounded skill preferences and admits an adaptive number of skills for each task. A set-aware renderer then tailors how selected descriptions are presented relative to their co-injected neighbors. Across tau2-bench, SkillsBench, and ALFWorld, SkillsInjector achieves the highest score, improving over the strongest baseline by 3.9, 6.1, and 7.3 percentage points, respectively. Ablation studies show that skill selection, adaptive budgeting, and set-aware rendering each contribute to the gain. These results show that skill-augmented agents benefit from optimizing the injected context itself. Code will be released upon publication

</details>


### [95] [MEMENTO: Leveraging Web as a Learning Signal for Low-Data Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29795)
*Ashutosh Ojha,Vinay Aggarwal,Ashutosh Srivastava,Siddharth Yedlapati,Yaman K Singla,Jitendra Ajmera*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MEMENTO is a framework that treats the web as a learning signal rather than just a retrieval interface, enabling AI agents to learn reusable research strategies and domain expertise through iterative web exploration without additional model training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches to low-data learning (few-shot prompting, instruction tuning, synthetic data generation) still treat labeled data as the primary learning signal. The authors note that humans learn through repeated, self-directed web interaction, progressively refining both domain knowledge and search strategies.

Method: MEMENTO operates at two levels: 1) Within each session, it uses an Adaptive Exploration Tree (AET) for iterative web exploration, decomposing tasks into evolving questions and reflecting on intermediate findings. 2) Across sessions, it accumulates experience through dual-channel memory that separates declarative knowledge (facts) from procedural knowledge (search strategies).

Result: Empirical evaluation on sales automation and legal research domains shows consistent improvements over ReAct-based baselines: +25.6% on sales automation and +36.5% on legal research.

Conclusion: The web can serve as a scalable learning source for acquiring task-specific expertise in data-scarce settings, with MEMENTO demonstrating that treating web interaction as a learning signal enables agents to develop reusable research strategies and domain knowledge without additional model training.

Abstract: Real-world tasks often lack large labeled datasets, motivating extensive work on learning in low-data regimes. However, existing approaches such as few-shot prompting, instruction tuning, and synthetic data generation, continue to treat labeled or pseudo-labeled data as the primary learning signal. In contrast, human practitioners acquire expertise through repeated, self-directed interaction with the open web, progressively refining both domain knowledge and search strategies. We propose MEMENTO, a framework that treats the web as a learning signal rather than a stateless retrieval interface. MEMENTO operates at two levels: within each session, it conducts iterative web exploration via an Adaptive Exploration Tree (AET) that decomposes tasks into evolving questions and reflects on intermediate findings; across sessions, it accumulates experience through dual-channel memory, separating declarative knowledge (facts) from procedural knowledge (search strategies). This design enables agents to learn reusable research strategies and domain expertise from trajectories of web interaction without additional model training. We evaluate MEMENTO on two low-data professional domains: sales automation and legal research. Our empirical results show consistent improvements in performance over ReAct based baselines (+25.6% on sales automation and 36.5% on legal research), demonstrating that the web can serve as a scalable learning source for acquiring task-specific expertise in data-scarce settings.

</details>


### [96] [SAAS: Self-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Over-Search Mitigation in Agentic Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29796)
*Yunbo Tang,Chengyi Yang,Shiyu Liu,Zhishang Xiang,Zerui Chen,Qinggang Zhang,Jinsong Su*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SAAS is an RL framework that trains LLM agents to become self-aware of when search is needed, reducing unnecessary searches while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agentic search systems lack self-awareness about knowledge boundaries, causing over-search - unnecessary searches when internal knowledge suffices, and failure to terminate searches when adequate evidence is collected. This results in high inference latency and computational costs.

Method: SAAS uses RL with three components: (1) search boundary modeling that compares search-disabled vs search-enabled rollouts to identify boundaries, (2) boundary-aware reward module that penalizes unnecessary/redundant searches at trajectory level, and (3) stage-wise optimization with sequential curriculum to prioritize reasoning over search regularization and avoid reward hacking.

Result: Extensive experiments show SAAS substantially reduces over-search while maintaining accuracy in agentic search systems.

Conclusion: SAAS successfully addresses the over-search problem in LLM agentic systems by cultivating dynamic self-awareness, balancing search efficiency with accuracy.

Abstract: Agentic search enables LLMs to solve complex multi-hop questions through iterative reasoning and external search. Despite the effectiveness, these systems often suffer from a critical limitation in practice: agents fail to recognize their own knowledge boundaries, blindly triggering searches when internal knowledge suffices and failing to terminate search even when adequate evidence has been collected. The lack of self-awareness leads to severe \textbf{over-search}, incurring substantial inference latency and prohibitive computational cost. To this end, we propose SAAS, a novel RL framework designed to cultivate dynamic self-awareness that precisely regulates search behavior without compromising accuracy. SAAS introduces three key components: (i) a search boundary modeling mechanism, which identifies the search boundary under the evolving policy by contrasting search-disabled and search-enabled rollouts; (ii) a boundary-aware reward module, which translates this boundary awareness into trajectory-level penalties, suppressing unnecessary and redundant searches; and (iii) a stage-wise optimization strategy, which leverages a sequential curriculum to prioritize reasoning over search regularization, thereby avoiding reward hacking. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SAAS substantially reduces over-search, while maintaining accuracy. Our code is anonymously released at https://github.com/XMUDeepLIT/SAAS.

</details>


### [97] [AgentDoG 1.5: A Lightweight and Scalable Alignment Framework for AI Agent Safety and Security](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29801)
*Dongrui Liu,Yu Li,Zhonghao Yang,Peng Wang,Guanxu Chen,Yuejin Xie,Qinghua Mao,Wanying Qu,Yanxu Zhu,Tianyi Zhou,Leitao Yuan,Zhijie Zheng,Qihao Lin,Yimin Wang,Haoyu Luo,Shuai Shao,Chen Qian,Qingyu Liu,Ling Tang,Ruiyang Qin,Qihan Ren,Junxiao Yang,Kun Wang,Zhiheng Xi,Linfeng Zhang,Ranjie Duan,Bo Zhang,Wenjie Wang,Wen Shen,Qiaosheng Zhang,Yan Teng,Chaochao Lu,Rui Mei,Man Li,Jialing Tao,Xi Lin,Tianhang Zheng,Yong Liu,Quanshi Zhang,Lei Zhu,Xingjun Ma,Junhua Liu,Hui Xue,Xiaoxiang Zuo,Xiangnan He,Chao Shen,Xianglong Liu,Minlie Huang,Jing Shao,Xia Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A lightweight agent safety alignment framework (AgentDoG 1.5) that addresses emerging risks from open-world AI agents like OpenClaw, achieving SOTA performance with minimal training data and efficient deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern open-world agents (e.g., OpenClaw) introduce broad new safety risks while advanced AI models lower attack barriers, making current alignment frameworks inadequate for real-world deployment.

Method: Updated agent safety taxonomy for emergent risks; built taxonomy-guided data engine with influence-function purification; trained lightweight AgentDoG 1.5 variants (0.8B-8B) using ~1k samples; constructed efficient SFT/RL training environment reducing deployment overhead by 100x.

Result: AgentDoG 1.5 achieves comparable performance with leading closed-source models (e.g., GPT-5.4) and SOTA performance in diverse interactive agentic scenarios; deployed as training-free online guardrail; models and datasets openly released.

Conclusion: Proposed lightweight, scalable framework effectively addresses emergent safety threats in open-world agents with minimal data and computational overhead, enabling practical real-world deployment.

Abstract: Modern open-world agents such as OpenClaw exhibit powerful cross-environment execution capabilities yet introduce broad new safety risk sources. Meanwhile, advanced frontier AI models drastically lower attack barriers, rendering current agent alignment frameworks inadequate for real-world deployment. To tackle these emerging threats, we propose a lightweight and scalable agent safety alignment framework. Specifically, we update the agent safety taxonomy to accommodate emergent risks from Codex and OpenClaw execution scenarios. We further build a taxonomy-guided data engine with influence-function purification to train lightweight AgentDoG 1.5 variants (0.8B, 2B, 4B, and 8B parameters) using only around 1k samples, achieving comparable performance with leading closed-source models (e.g., GPT-5.4). Based on AgentDoG 1.5, we construct a highly efficient agentic safety SFT and RL training environment, which reduces deployment overhead in Docker-level environments by two orders of magnitude. Finally, we deploy AgentDoG 1.5 as a training-free online guardrail for real-time safety moderation. Extensive experimental results indicate that AgentDoG 1.5 achieves state-of-the-art performance in diverse and complex interactive agentic scenarios. All models and datasets are openly released.

</details>


### [98] [PRAIB: Peer Review AI Benchmark of Behaviour of LLM-Assisted Reviewing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29815)
*Krzysztof Żurawicki,Julia Farganus,Arkadiusz Gaweł,Mateusz Bystroński,Tomasz Jan Kajdanowicz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-generated peer reviews diverge significantly from human reviews: they're less variable, positively biased, overconfident, generate longer/complex reviews but miss atomic weaknesses, with PRAIB framework providing diagnostic tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The growing number of submitted papers motivates using LLMs to support peer review scalability, but it's unknown whether LLMs engage with manuscripts similarly to humans or just produce review-looking text without genuine engagement.

Method: Introduce PRAIB framework with metrics for review specificity, style, and engagement behavior. Conduct large-scale empirical study using 11,000 reviews from 5 proprietary/open-source models for 1,000 ICLR/NeurIPS papers (2021-2025), comparing against human feedback across various prompting strategies.

Result: LLM-generated reviews diverge significantly: ratings are less variable, positively biased, overconfident; cross-reference patterns are model-dependent and distinct from human norms; LLMs generate longer, more complex reviews but frequently overlook atomic weaknesses noted by humans.

Conclusion: PRAIB provides a diagnostic tool for identifying which review aspects LLMs can reliably support and which require further development, characterizing where/how LLM reviewing behavior departs from human norms to guide responsible deployment.

Abstract: The growing number of submitted papers has motivated the exploration of Large Language Models (LLMs) as a means to support and augment the peer review process, particularly in terms of improving its speed and scalability. Yet, it remains unknown whether LLMs engage with scientific manuscripts in the same manner as human reviewers, or whether they merely produce review-looking text. To address this, we introduce the Peer Review AI Benchmark (PRAIB), a novel framework comprising thoroughly defined metrics that measure review specificity, style, and behavior of engagement. To complement the PRAIB framework, we conduct a large-scale empirical study leveraging a dataset of 11,000 reviews generated by five proprietary and open-source models for 1,000 ICLR and NeurIPS papers. Spanning the 2021--2025 period, these machine-generated reviews are compared against original human feedback across diverse prompting strategies to identify systematic behavioral divergences. Our analysis reveals that the generated reviews diverge significantly from feedback provided by human reviewers: LLM ratings are less variable, positively biased, and overconfident, and their cross-reference patterns are model-dependent and distinct from human norms. Furthermore, when evaluated through PRAIB, we observe that LLMs tend to generate longer, more complex reviews, yet frequently overlook the atomic weaknesses noted by human reviewers. By characterizing where and how LLMs reviewing behavior departs from human norms, PRAIB provides the community with a diagnostic tool for identifying which aspects of the review process LLMs can reliably support today and which require further development before deployment.

</details>


### [99] [Harnessing non-adversarial robustness in large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29816)
*Qinghua Zhou,Ellina Aleshina,Andrey Lovyagin,Oleg Somov,Mikhail Seleznyov,Alexander Panchenko,Ivan Oseledets,Elena Tutubalina,Ivan Y. Tyukin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A method called "debiasing for robustness" that fine-tunes LLMs to make them more robust against semantically similar prompt variations without expensive full retraining, with theoretical analysis showing when and why this approach works.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are sensitive to semantically similar but textually different prompts, which can significantly impact performance. The paper addresses whether robustness to these prompt variations can be achieved without expensive full model retraining.

Method: Theoretical analysis identifies systematic expected shift/perturbation-induced bias in neural network outputs as a key factor affecting robustness. Motivated by this, the paper proposes a simple fine-tuning process called "debiasing for robustness" to address this bias.

Result: The debiasing approach enhances LLM robustness against random prompt perturbations, with both theoretical analysis and extensive experiments demonstrating its effectiveness. The paper identifies conditions when debiasing helps versus when it doesn't, and shows it provides certification against prompt perturbations.

Conclusion: Debiasing for robustness is a quick and efficient tool to enhance LLM robustness against semantically-neutral prompt alterations without requiring expensive full model retraining, offering both theoretical grounding and practical effectiveness with certification guarantees.

Abstract: The work presents an approach for addressing the challenge of robustness in Large Language Models (LLMs) to alterations and potential errors caused by semantically similar but textually different prompts. Recent works have shown that these kinds of prompt variations can significantly impact the performance of LLMs on tasks. The central question is: can LLMs' robustness to semantically-neutral prompt alterations be acquired without expensive retraining of the entire model? We address this question both theoretically and through experiments. Our theoretical analysis reveals a crucial factor impacting model robustness - a systematic expected shift or perturbation-induced bias in neural network module outputs. Motivated by this analysis, we show that robustness can be achieved via a simple fine-tuning process: debiasing for robustness. We identify conditions when debiasing helps and when it does not, and demonstrate, through both theory and extensive experiments, that debiasing for robustness may indeed be a quick and efficient tool to enhance robustness and provide certification against random prompt perturbations.

</details>


### [100] [Quantifying and Optimizing Simplicity via Polynomial Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29823)
*Tianren Zhang,Xiangxin Li,Minghao Xiao,Guanyu Chen,Feng Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Deep learning models prefer simple solutions, but measuring simplicity is hard. The paper introduces polynomial representations as a quantitative simplicity metric that predicts generalization better than existing proxies like sharpness, and can be used as a regularizer to improve performance across various tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep neural networks exhibit a simplicity bias that is believed to be crucial for generalization, but there's a lack of broadly applicable, quantitative measures of simplicity. Existing generalization proxies like sharpness have limitations, and a practical way to measure and leverage simplicity would be valuable.

Method: The paper introduces polynomial representations as distribution-aware, low-dimensional surrogates for neural functions. They approximate a network's predictive behavior along data-dependent interpolation paths using orthogonal polynomial bases, creating compact functional representations. From these, they derive an effective degree metric that serves as a simplicity measure.

Result: The effective degree of polynomial representations serves as a practical simplicity metric that consistently outperforms existing generalization proxies like sharpness across various tasks and architectures. Additionally, polynomial representations naturally yield a differentiable simplicity regularizer that improves generalization in image/text classification, fine-tuning contrastive vision-language models, and reinforcement learning.

Conclusion: Polynomial representations provide an effective, quantitative measure of simplicity that both predicts generalization and can be used as a regularizer to improve model performance across diverse applications, offering a practical approach to understand and leverage the simplicity bias in deep learning.

Abstract: Deep networks often exhibit a preference for "simple" solutions, and such a simplicity bias is widely believed to play a key role in generalization. Yet a broadly applicable, quantitative measure of simplicity remains elusive. We introduce polynomial representations as a distribution-aware, low-dimensional surrogate for neural functions: we approximate a network's predictive behavior along data-dependent interpolation paths using orthogonal polynomial bases, yielding a compact functional representation. We show that the effective degree of this representation serves as a practical simplicity metric that is predictive of generalization across tasks and architectures, and consistently outperforms existing generalization proxies such as sharpness. Finally, polynomial representations naturally yield a differentiable simplicity regularizer, which consistently improves generalization in image and text classification, fine-tuning contrastive vision-language models, and reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [101] [OptSkills: Learning Generalizable Optimization Skills from Problem Archetypes via Cluster-Based Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29829)
*Haochen Yang,Ke Zhao,Mengyuan Ma,Xingyu Lu,Xiangfeng Wang,Hong Qian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OptSkills is an archetype-centric system that improves generalization in LLM-based optimization by clustering problems by underlying archetypes rather than surface narratives, distilling workflow-level skills, and adapting to new problem types through skill refinement and expansion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based optimization methods have limited generalization: they're sensitive to narrative variations, reuse experience mainly at case level, and struggle with shifted or emerging problem types. There's a need for more robust and adaptable systems that can handle diverse optimization problems effectively.

Method: The system clusters optimization problems by their underlying archetypes rather than surface narratives. It explores diverse modeling paradigms and solver configurations within each cluster, distills successful trajectories into reusable workflow-level skills, and can refine existing skills or expand the skill library using new trajectories for out-of-distribution generalization.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art micro-averaged accuracy of 68.27% on diverse optimization datasets, outperforms DeepSeek-V3.2-Thinking by 4.53% on MIPLIB-NL benchmark (26.91% accuracy), and reaches 72.79% accuracy on OOD NLCO benchmark after skill learning on Nano-CO.

Conclusion: OptSkills demonstrates effective archetype-centric skill learning and reasoning for optimization problems, significantly improving both in-distribution and out-of-distribution generalization compared to existing methods, with practical performance gains on challenging benchmarks.

Abstract: Leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically formulate and solve optimization problems from natural language has emerged as an efficient paradigm for automated optimization. However, existing methods still exhibit limited generalization: they are sensitive to superficial narrative variations, reuse experience mainly at the case level, and struggle to adapt to shifted or emerging problem types. We propose OptSkills, an archetype-centric skill learning and reasoning agent system for optimization modeling and solving. To improve robust generalization, our system clusters problems by their underlying archetypes rather than surface narratives. To improve in-distribution generalization, it explores diverse modeling paradigms and solver configurations within each cluster, then distills successful trajectories into reusable workflow-level skills. To improve out-of-distribution generalization, it refines existing skills or expands the skill library using newly obtained trajectories. Our system achieves a state-of-the-art micro-averaged accuracy of 68.27% on datasets encompassing diverse problem types and scenarios. In addition, on MIPLIB-NL, a highly challenging large-scale and high-dimensional benchmark, it achieves 26.91% accuracy, outperforming DeepSeek-V3.2-Thinking by 4.53%. After skill learning on Nano-CO, it reaches 72.79% on the OOD NLCO benchmark. Code and skills are available at https://github.com/fujiwaranoM0kou/OptSkills.

</details>


### [102] [OmniMatBench: A Human-Calibrated Multimodal Reasoning Benchmark Across 19 Materials Science Subfields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29833)
*Wanhao Liu,Jiaqing Xie,Qian Tan,Weida Wang,Jue Wang,Ran Sun,Zhuo Yang,Wanli Ouyang,Lei Bai,Tianfan Fu,Lu Chen,Xin Chen,Yuqiang Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OmniMatBench is a comprehensive multimodal reasoning benchmark for materials science containing 3,171 expert-curated problems across 19 subfields, revealing significant gaps in current MLLMs' materials reasoning capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Materials science provides a critical testbed for multimodal language models due to its interdisciplinary, multimodal, and application-driven nature, but existing benchmarks focus narrowly on property prediction, knowledge QA, or characterization understanding, leaving broader reasoning processes from materials knowledge to application underexplored.

Method: Created OmniMatBench - a human-calibrated multimodal reasoning benchmark containing 3,171 expert-curated QA and calculation problems across 19 materials-science subfields, covering fundamental knowledge, structural/engineering materials, processing/manufacturing, and functional/applied materials. Evaluated 13 open-source and closed-source MLLMs on this benchmark.

Result: The best model achieved only a 0.372 overall score, revealing substantial gaps in materials-science reasoning. Analysis showed strong variation across subfields, fixed reasoning heuristics, uneven materials knowledge, and limited high-level knowledge application under formula-, retrieval-, and code-assisted settings.

Conclusion: OmniMatBench provides crucial insights into the capabilities and limitations of current MLLMs and establishes a foundation for developing reliable AI assistants in materials-science research, highlighting the need for improved reasoning capabilities in this domain.

Abstract: As multimodal language models play an increasingly important role in scientific research, materials science offers a critical testbed due to its interdisciplinary, multimodal, and application-driven nature. However, existing materials benchmarks mainly focus on property prediction, knowledge QA, or characterization understanding, leaving the broader reasoning process from materials knowledge to application underexplored. To fill this gap, we present OmniMatBench, a human-calibrated multimodal reasoning benchmark for materials science. OmniMatBench contains 3,171 expert-curated QA and calculation problems across 19 materials-science subfields, spanning fundamental materials knowledge, structural and engineering materials, materials processing and manufacturing, and functional and applied materials. We evaluate 13 open-source and closed-source MLLMs and find that the best model achieves only a 0.372 overall score, revealing a substantial gap in current materials-science reasoning. Further analysis shows strong variation across subfields, fixed reasoning heuristics, uneven materials knowledge, and limited high-level knowledge application under formula-, retrieval-, and code-assisted settings. OmniMatBench provides crucial insights into the capabilities and limitations of current MLLMs and establishes a foundation for reliable AI assistants in materials-science research.

</details>


### [103] [Moment-KV: Momentum-Based Decode-Time KV Cache Compression for Long Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29873)
*Soumyadeep Jana,Sagar Nishad,Sanasam Ranbir Singh*

Main category: cs.AI
