Motivation: Despite widespread adoption of Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for fine-tuning large language models, there's insufficient understanding of the internal representational changes it induces and how these changes relate to pretrained model features.

Method: Used Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) with a delta activation framework to isolate LoRA adapter contributions. Trained adapter-specific SAEs on Gemma-2-9B across ranks (4, 8, 16, 32) and layers, comparing with pretrained SAE dictionaries using cosine similarity, principal-angle analysis, and Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA).

Result: Found weak geometric alignment between LoRA-induced and pretrained features across all layers and ranks. Adapter-specific SAEs reconstruct delta activations better than pretrained SAEs, suggesting distinct representational structures. Feature density increases with rank and depth, while geometric divergence remains stable across ranks.

Conclusion: LoRA fine-tuning induces feature structures not fully captured by pretrained interpretability dictionaries, with implications for mechanistic interpretability, adaptation analysis, and safety auditing of fine-tuned language models.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has emerged as a widely adopted approach for adapting large language models, yet the internal representational changes induced by LoRA fine-tuning remain insufficiently understood. In this work, we investigate the geometry of LoRA-induced representations using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs). We introduce a delta activation framework that isolates the adapter-specific contribution to the residual stream.
  Using Gemma-2-9B with LoRA ranks 4, 8, 16, and 32, we train adapter-specific SAEs across multiple transformer layers and compare their learned feature spaces with pretrained SAE dictionaries. We evaluate representational alignment using cosine similarity between decoder directions, principal-angle analysis of feature subspaces, and Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) between activation representations.
  Across layers and ranks, we consistently observe comparatively weak geometric alignment between LoRA-induced feature dictionaries and pretrained SAE features. Adapter-specific SAEs also reconstruct delta activations more effectively than pretrained SAEs, suggesting that LoRA updates occupy partially distinct representational structure within the residual stream. Additionally, feature density increases with rank and depth, while geometric divergence remains relatively stable across ranks.
  These findings provide empirical evidence that LoRA fine-tuning can induce feature structures that are not fully captured by pretrained interpretability dictionaries, with implications for mechanistic interpretability, adaptation analysis, and safety auditing of fine-tuned language models.

</details>


### [157] [Spectral Guidance for Flexible and Efficient Control of Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28900)
*Gabriel Moreira,Manuel Marques,João Paulo Costeira,Chenyan Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spectral Guidance is a framework for controlling diffusion models by identifying key informative features during the generative process and projecting guidance signals onto them, enabling fast, stable control without retraining or backpropagation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for controlling diffusion models during sampling often require computationally expensive backpropagation through denoisers or model retraining, limiting practical deployment. There's a need for efficient, stable control mechanisms that work with various guidance signals.

Method: The framework leverages the intrinsic geometry of diffusion processes by identifying singular functions of a conditional expectation operator that remain informative during data corruption. These features are learned via self-supervised learning and form a basis for projecting arbitrary guidance signals (labels, CLIP embeddings, masks) directly onto the sampling trajectory.

Result: Improves conditional accuracy on CIFAR-10 by 37 percentage points over strongest training-free baseline with 4× faster sampling. Enables multiple control types (label, CLIP, spatial/mask-based) using the same learned representations without auxiliary models. Reveals a phase transition pinpointing optimal time window for guidance.

Conclusion: Spectral Guidance provides an efficient, unified framework for controlling diffusion models by exploiting intrinsic process geometry, offering significant improvements in accuracy, speed, and flexibility without requiring expensive retraining or backpropagation during sampling.

Abstract: We introduce Spectral Guidance, a framework for controlling diffusion models by leveraging the intrinsic geometry of the generative process. As data is progressively corrupted by noise, only a small number of features remain informative for control. We characterize them as the singular functions of a conditional expectation operator and show that they can be learned via a self-supervised objective. Once recovered, this basis enables the projection of arbitrary guidance signals, such as labels, CLIP embeddings, or masks, directly onto the sampling trajectory. This approach allows for stable, high-fidelity control without retraining or denoiser backpropagation during sampling. Empirically, we improve conditional accuracy on CIFAR-10 by 37 percentage points over the strongest training-free baseline while offering $4\times$ faster sampling. Moreover, the same representations that support label and CLIP guidance also enable spatial control, such as mask-based guidance, without auxiliary models. Finally, our framework reveals a phase transition in the generative process, pinpointing the optimal time window for effective guidance.

</details>


### [158] [Sequential Physics-Constrained Neural Operator Forward Modeling for the $\textit{Norne}$ Reservoir System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28909)
*Clement Etienam,Juntao Yang,Oleg Ovcharenko,Nick Luiken,Tsubasa Onishi,Nefeli Moridis,Issam Said*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive neural operator framework for sequential surrogate modeling of three-phase black-oil reservoir dynamics, featuring theoretical guarantees and empirical validation on the Norne benchmark with ~10^4× speedup over traditional simulators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional reservoir simulation using finite-volume methods is computationally expensive for uncertainty quantification and optimization tasks. There's a need for fast, accurate surrogate models that can handle complex three-phase flow dynamics while maintaining physical consistency.

Method: Theoretical and computational framework using Fourier Neural Operators (FNO) and Physics-Informed Neural Operators (PINO) for sequential surrogate modeling. Applied to the Norne reservoir (46×112×22 grid, 113,344 cells) with 30 timesteps over 3298 days. The method includes functional-analytic formulation, covariate shift analysis, physics-constrained spectral stability, and truncated backpropagation through time gradient analysis.

Result: Autoregressive PINO surrogates achieve R²>0.99 (oil), R²>0.90 (gas), R²≈0.80 (pressure), and monotonically improving R² (water) across full 3298-day horizon. Training on eight NVIDIA B200 GPUs takes under one hour, and a 1000-member ensemble runs in under one minute on a single B200 GPU, providing ~10^4× wall-clock speedup over OPM finite-volume simulator.

Conclusion: The developed neural operator framework provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for surrogate modeling of complex reservoir dynamics with strong theoretical guarantees and exceptional computational efficiency. The approach enables practical uncertainty quantification and optimization tasks that were previously infeasible due to computational constraints.

Abstract: We develop a comprehensive mathematical and computational framework for sequential surrogate modeling of three-phase black-oil reservoir dynamics using neural operators, with particular emphasis on Fourier Neural Operators (FNO) and their physics-informed variant (PINO). The application focus is the Norne benchmark reservoir, defined on a heterogeneous $46\times112\times22$ grid ($N=113,344$ cells), with a production history spanning $T=30$ timesteps covering 3298 days. Our theoretical contributions are organized around four interlocking problems: (1) functional-analytic formulation in a product-Sobolev-space setting, including well-posedness of the implicit timestep map and sharp local Lipschitz estimates; (2) covariate shift quantification, proving that the Wasserstein-2 distance grows as $W_2 \leq \varepsilon(L^n-1)/(L-1)$, with exponential population-risk discrepancy for $L>1$; (3) physics-constrained spectral stability, showing PINO training with $λ_R \geq λ^*_R$ reduces the learned Jacobian spectral radius to $ρ_F + Cλ_R^{-1/2}$, yielding uniform-in-time rollout error $|δ_n| \leq \varepsilon/(1-ρ)$; and (4) $K$-step TBPTT gradient analysis, deriving geometric bias decay $O(ρ^K)$, optimal window $K^ = O(\log(T/σ^2))$, and Adam convergence $O(1/\sqrt{t}) + O(ρ^{K^*})$. Empirical validation confirms all theoretical predictions: autoregressive PINO surrogates sustain $R^2>0.99$ (oil), $R^2>0.90$ (gas), $R^2\approx 0.80$ (pressure), and monotonically improving $R^2$ (water) across the full 3298-day horizon, trained on eight NVIDIA B200 GPUs in under one hour. A 1000-member ensemble runs in under one minute on a single B200 GPU, giving a ${\sim}10^4\times$ wall-clock speedup over the OPM finite-volume simulator.

</details>


### [159] [Cycle-Space Informed Detection of Autoencoded Blind False Data Injection Attacks on Power Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28912)
*Xin Li,Chenhan Xiao,Jonathan Cohen,Aviad Elyashar,Yang Weng,Rami Puzis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A topology-informed Cycle-Space Detector (CSD) that uses network cycle constraints to detect data-driven False Data Injection Attacks (FDIAs) that exploit Jacobian null space, outperforming traditional statistical/data-driven detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-driven data centers and energy storage systems increasingly rely on real-time measurements for power system operation, but existing attack detection methods can fail when attackers exploit the same data structures used by defenders, creating stealthy perturbations that evade both residual-based and time-series anomaly detectors.

Method: Propose a topology-informed Cycle-Space Detector (CSD) that leverages the Cycle-Space of the power network to impose structural constraints that enhance null space estimation for attack detection. Uses Minimum Cycle Basis (MCB) to achieve optimal generalization error for detection, exploiting topology-derived cycle constraints rather than relying solely on numerical null space estimation.

Result: Simulation results on IEEE 14-, 30-, 57-, and 118-bus systems demonstrate that the proposed method effectively detects data-driven FDIAs under realistic measurement noise, improving separation between normal and attacked measurements without requiring precise line parameters.

Conclusion: The topology-informed CSD provides a robust defense against data-driven FDIAs by exploiting physical network constraints rather than just data patterns, achieving better detection performance against stealthy attacks that exploit measurement manifolds and Jacobian null spaces.

Abstract: The rapid growth of AI-driven data centers and large-scale energy storage systems is increasing the reliance of power system operation on real-time measurement data and automated decision-making. However, many existing detection methods rely on statistical or data-driven analysis of measurements and can fail when attackers exploit the same data structure to craft stealthy perturbations. To illustrate this limitation, we demonstrate a blind False Data Injection Attack (FDIA) in which an Autoencoder learns the measurement manifold and generates perturbations aligned with the Jacobian null space, thereby allowing the attack to evade both residual-based baddata detectors and time-series anomaly detectors. To mitigate data-driven FDIAs which exploit the null space, we propose a topology-informed Cycle-Space Detector (CSD) that leverages the Cycle-Space of the network to impose structural constraints that enhance null space estimation. In addition, we prove that by using the Minimum Cycle Basis (MCB), the proposed CSD achieves the optimal generalization error for attack detection. By exploiting topology-derived cycle constraints rather than relying solely on numerical null space estimation, the proposed method does not require precise line parameters and improves the separation between normal and attacked measurements. Simulation results on IEEE 14-, 30-, 57-, and 118-bus systems demonstrate that the proposed method effectively detects data-driven FDIAs under realistic measurement noise.

</details>


### [160] [When LLM Reward Design Fails: Diagnostic-Driven Refinement for Sparse Structured RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28918)
*Youting Wang,Yuan Tang,Bowen Liu,Xuan Liu,Dingyan Shang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-generated reward shaping works better as iterative debugging rather than one-shot generation, with diagnostic-driven refinement improving agent performance significantly on sparse structured tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: One-shot LLM-generated reward functions for reinforcement learning often fail due to issues like reward flooding and semantic misunderstandings, suggesting a need for iterative refinement approaches.

Method: Proposed diagnostic-driven iterative refinement using training diagnostics and failure-mode taxonomy to guide targeted reward-function revisions, tested with PPO-trained agents on MiniGrid and MuJoCo environments.

Result: Refinement dramatically improved performance: DoorKey-8x8 from 2.3% to 97.6%, KeyCorridor from 31.2% to 86.7%. Controls showed gains weren't from retrying or extra training but from the taxonomy-guided approach.

Conclusion: LLM-generated reward shaping is better framed as debugging than one-shot generation, with diagnostic-driven refinement effective for sparse structured tasks but limited for dense-reward scenarios like continuous control.

Abstract: For sparse, structured reinforcement-learning tasks with semantic reward-function interfaces, LLM-generated reward shaping is better framed as debugging than one-shot generation. We study PPO-trained agents using MiniGrid as core evaluation and MuJoCo as boundary stress test. Our audit finds two dominant one-shot failure modes -- reward flooding and semantic/API misunderstanding -- plus a rarer weak-shaping case. We propose diagnostic-driven iterative refinement, where training diagnostics and a failure-mode taxonomy guide targeted reward-function revision. Refinement improves DoorKey-8x8 from 2.3% to 97.6% and KeyCorridor from 31.2% to 86.7% with high seed-to-seed variance. Controls show these gains are not from retrying or extra training: metrics-only re-prompting yields large drops, while a static-vocabulary control recovers much of the gap (87.6%; 70.7%), showing the taxonomy prompt is a major mechanism and dynamic labels provide only partially isolated incremental evidence. Budget-matched and Best-of-3 comparisons separate refinement from selection and training-time effects. Component-removal tests, sensitivity analyses, and an audit against author labels provide converging evidence for the debugging interpretation while revealing calibration limits. Continuous-control results show the boundary: success-based diagnostics can misfire in dense-reward locomotion, and return-trend feedback removes one false-positive mechanism without robust gains. The low-call protocol is a cost contrast with population-based reward search, not a benchmark comparison. In four crossed-variance-design environments, point estimates suggest larger gains when LLM reward-function variance dominates but bootstrap intervals are wide. The method is bounded to sparse structured tasks with reliable interfaces under PPO; fields like event_text may help, hurt, or be neutral.

</details>


### [161] [CosmicFish-HRM: Adaptive Reasoning via Hierarchical Recurrent Mechanisms in Compact Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28919)
*Venkat Akhil Lakkapragada*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CosmicFish-HRM is a compact language model with adaptive reasoning depth that dynamically allocates computational effort based on input complexity, offering an alternative to large parameter counts for reasoning capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models achieve strong reasoning capabilities but at the cost of massive parameters and expensive inference. The paper explores an alternative direction: adaptive reasoning depth in compact models to reduce computational costs while maintaining reasoning ability.

Method: Introduces CosmicFish-HRM with a Hierarchical Reasoning Module (HRM) that dynamically allocates computational effort. The model iterates through high-level and low-level reasoning cycles and learns when to halt based on input complexity. Combines this with modern transformer components including Grouped Query Attention, RoPE, and SwiGLU activations.

Result: The model learns non-uniform reasoning behavior, allocating different numbers of reasoning steps across tasks and inputs. While HRM introduces overhead at small scale, the tradeoff becomes increasingly favorable as model size grows (relative HRM cost diminishes).

Conclusion: Adaptive reasoning depth offers a promising alternative to relying solely on parameter scale for reasoning capability. This approach could enable more efficient reasoning in compact language models.

Abstract: Large language models have achieved strong reasoning capabilities, though often at the cost of massive parameter counts and expensive inference. In this work, we explore a different direction: adaptive reasoning depth in compact language models. We present CosmicFish-HRM, a compact language model built around a Hierarchical Reasoning Module (HRM) that dynamically allocates computational effort during inference. Instead of applying fixed computation to every input, the model iterates through high-level and low-level reasoning cycles and learns when to halt based on input complexity. CosmicFish-HRM combines this adaptive reasoning core with modern transformer components including Grouped Query Attention, RoPE, and SwiGLU activations. While the additional reasoning infrastructure introduces overhead at small scale, we hypothesize that this tradeoff becomes increasingly favorable as model size grows and the relative cost of the HRM core diminishes. Our results show that the model learns non-uniform reasoning behavior, allocating different numbers of reasoning steps across tasks and inputs. These findings suggest that adaptive reasoning depth may offer a promising alternative to relying solely on parameter scale for reasoning capability.

</details>


### [162] [Conf-Gen: Conformal Uncertainty Quantification for Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28920)
*Gabriel Loaiza-Ganem,Kevin Zhang,Wei Cui,Marc T. Law,Kin Kwan Leung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conf-Gen extends conformal prediction to unsupervised generative AI models like LLMs and image generators, providing uncertainty quantification with formal guarantees across novel applications including non-memorized image generation, conversational AI, and AI agent correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional conformal prediction (CP) and conformal risk control (CRC) frameworks are designed for supervised learning, making them incompatible with the recent breakthroughs in unsupervised generative AI models like LLMs and image generators, which lack the necessary supervised structure.

Method: Conf-Gen adapts CRC to generative tasks by relaxing its theoretical assumptions, creating a general framework that unifies and generalizes previous attempts to apply CP to LLMs while extending conformal methodology to new generative domains.

Result: The framework successfully demonstrates flexibility through novel applications including: 1) providing conformal guarantees on image generators producing non-memorized images, 2) ensuring conversational AI systems ask enough clarifying questions, and 3) verifying AI agent output correctness.

Conclusion: Conf-Gen bridges the gap between traditional conformal prediction methods and modern generative AI, enabling formal uncertainty quantification across diverse generative tasks and opening new avenues for reliable AI deployment in unsupervised settings.

Abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) and its extension, conformal risk control (CRC), are established frameworks for quantifying uncertainty in supervised machine learning through formal guarantees. However, recent breakthroughs in artificial intelligence (AI) have been driven by unsupervised generative models, such as large language models (LLMs) and image generators, which are not directly compatible with CP or CRC. In this work we introduce conformal generation (Conf-Gen), a general framework adapting CRC to generative tasks while relaxing its theoretical assumptions. Conf-Gen unifies and generalizes previous attempts to apply CP to LLMs, and extends conformal methodology to entirely new domains. We demonstrate the flexibility of Conf-Gen through some novel applications, including obtaining conformal guarantees on: image generators producing non-memorized images, conversational AI systems having asked enough clarifying questions, and the output of AI agents being correct.

</details>


### [163] [A Training-Time Diagnostic for Generalization via the Log-Alignment Ratio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28975)
*Ali Shehper,Ashish Vaswani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LAR measures parameter-activation alignment, predicts memorization/generalization transitions, and tracks generalization gap in language models without validation data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding the transition between memorization and generalization in neural networks, particularly in phenomena like grokking, and developing metrics that can track this transition without requiring held-out validation data.

Method: Reformulate LAR as overlap between weight spectrum (normalized squared singular values) and activation spectrum (normalized squared projections of inputs). Apply LAR analysis to grokking scenarios and large-scale language model pre-training (3B parameters).

Result: In grokking, LAR predicts effective dimension of learned function: k ≈ n^{2(1-LAR)}. In 3B-parameter LM pre-training, LAR deviation from non-overfitting baseline tracks generalization gap, and its rate of decline increases as overfitting approaches. LAR is computable from forward pass with negligible overhead.

Conclusion: LAR serves as a practical, computationally efficient measure that captures the spread of weight and activation spectra during training, predicting memorization/generalization transitions and generalization gaps without requiring validation data.

Abstract: We study the log-alignment ratio (LAR), a measure of parameter-activation alignment, introduced in parameterization theory. We reformulate it as the overlap between a weight spectrum $p$ of the normalized squared singular values of a matrix and an activation spectrum $q$ of the normalized squared projections of inputs onto its singular directions. We show that unembedding LAR tracks the transition between memorization and generalization in two different settings by capturing the spread of $p$ and $q$ during training. In grokking, LAR predicts the effective dimension of the learned function: $k \approx n^{2(1-\text{LAR})}$, where $n$ is the input dimension of the matrix. In 3B-parameter language model pre-training, its deviation from a non-overfitting baseline tracks the generalization gap, and its rate of decline increases as overfitting approaches. LAR is computable from quantities available during the forward pass with negligible computational overhead, and requires no held-out validation data.

</details>


### [164] [Comparing Post-Hoc Explainable AI Methods for Interpreting Black-Box EEG Models in Depression Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28977)
*Antonia Šarčević,Nikolina Frid*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study evaluates multiple post-hoc explainability methods (DeepSHAP, Integrated Gradients, GradCAM, Occlusion, Permutation Feature Importance) applied to an InceptionTime deep learning model for EEG-based Major Depressive Disorder detection, finding partially convergent attribution patterns but methodological variability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While deep learning enables accurate EEG-based MDD classification, the decision-making processes of high-capacity models remain difficult to interpret, creating a need for explainability methods to understand what features these models rely on for depression detection.

Method: Applied multiple post-hoc explainability methods (Shapley-based, gradient-based, perturbation-based) to an InceptionTime architecture trained for EEG-based MDD detection. Analysis was performed within a subject-level stratified 5-fold cross-validation framework using global attribution aggregation across EEG segments and subjects.

Result: Explainability methods revealed partially convergent attribution patterns emphasizing frontal, temporal, and posterior EEG regions, particularly in the right hemisphere. Quantitative comparison showed substantial agreement between gradient- and perturbation-based approaches, while DeepSHAP produced distinct attribution distributions. Methods captured partially overlapping relevance structures.

Conclusion: Different post-hoc explainability approaches capture partially overlapping relevance structures in EEG-based depression detection models. While attribution patterns are broadly consistent with previous EEG studies of MDD, this should be interpreted as exploratory rather than evidence of definitive biomarkers or clinical applicability. Highlights both usefulness and limitations of post-hoc explainability for psychiatric black-box classifiers.

Abstract: Recent advances in deep learning have enabled increasingly accurate electroencephalography (EEG)-based classification of Major Depressive Disorder (MDD), but the decision-making processes of high-capacity models remain difficult to interpret. This study investigates multiple post-hoc explainability methods applied to an InceptionTime architecture trained for EEG-based MDD detection. The analysis includes Shapley-based, gradient-based, and perturbation-based attribution approaches: DeepSHAP, Integrated Gradients, GradCAM, Occlusion, and Permutation Feature Importance. Explainability analysis was performed within a subject-level stratified 5-fold cross-validation framework using global attribution aggregation across EEG segments and subjects. The evaluated methods revealed partially convergent attribution patterns, with recurring emphasis on frontal, temporal, and posterior EEG regions, particularly in the right hemisphere. Quantitative comparison demonstrated substantial agreement between gradient- and perturbation-based approaches, while DeepSHAP produced comparatively distinct attribution distributions. At the same time, variability between explainability methods highlighted the influence of methodological assumptions on the resulting explanations. Overall, the results suggest that different post-hoc explainability approaches capture partially overlapping relevance structures in EEG-based deep learning models for depression detection. Although the observed attribution patterns are broadly consistent with several previous EEG studies of MDD, the analysis should be interpreted as exploratory rather than evidence of definitive neurophysiological biomarkers or clinical applicability. The study highlights both the usefulness and limitations of post-hoc explainability for interpreting black-box EEG classifiers in psychiatric applications.

</details>


### [165] [The Hamilton-Jacobi Theory of Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28983)
*Jose Marie Antonio Miñoza,Erika Fille T. Legara,Christopher P. Monterola*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Training neural networks is mathematically equivalent to solving Hamilton-Jacobi PDEs, with different architectures (ResNets, Transformers, RNNs) discretizing these equations. A single parameter ε unifies four perspectives: neural networks, tropical algebra, viscous PDEs, and convex optimization, yielding insights into generalization, robustness, and attribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a unified mathematical framework connecting neural network training to classical Hamilton-Jacobi PDE theory, revealing deep structural connections between disparate architectures and providing principled explanations for network behavior.

Method: Exact identification of neural network training as search through Hamilton-Jacobi initial-value problems, with each gradient step selecting initial data for viscous Hamilton-Jacobi equations. Different architectures discretize the same class of PDEs with architecture-dependent Hamiltonian and viscosity terms.

Result: A commutative diagram unifying four perspectives under Lipschitz conditions; theoretical insights include minimax optimal generalization rate O(n^{-1/(d+2)}), adversarial robustness control via ε, backpropagation as Pontryagin Maximum Principle co-state equation, scaling exponents via PDE quadrature, and closed-form influence functions.

Conclusion: Neural network architectures share a fundamental mathematical structure as discretizations of Hamilton-Jacobi PDEs, with parameter ε controlling the deformation between tropical (ε→0) and viscous (ε>0) regimes, providing a unified theory for understanding generalization, robustness, and attribution patterns across diverse architectures.

Abstract: In this paper, training a neural network is identified, exactly, as a search through Hamilton--Jacobi initial-value problems: each gradient step selects the initial data of a viscous Hamilton--Jacobi equation whose Hopf--Cole propagator best fits the observations; at inference, the input is the spatial point at which that solution is evaluated and the initial condition is already encoded in the weights. The correspondence is exact for log-sum-exp layers and structural for broader architectures: residual networks, transformers, and recurrent architectures (RNNs, LSTMs, SSMs) each discretize the same class of Hamilton--Jacobi equations, with architecture-dependent Hamiltonian and viscosity. A single deformation parameter $\varepsilon$ unifies all four perspectives (network, tropical algebra, viscous PDE, convex optimization) in a commutative diagram closed under Lipschitz conditions. Quantitative consequences include: the minimax optimal generalization rate $O(n^{-1/(d+2)})$ for fixed $t$; adversarial robustness controlled by $\varepsilon$; backpropagation as the co-state equation of the Hamiltonian system for residual networks (Pontryagin Maximum Principle); scaling exponents consistent with data intrinsic dimension via PDE quadrature; and a closed-form $O(N)$ influence function (softmax attribution weights $π_j$) whose entropy landscape undergoes fold bifurcations as $\varepsilon$ increases, each merging attribution basins.

</details>


### [166] [Learning Robust and Task-Invariant Functional Representation from fMRI through Siamese Self-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28990)
*Jiyao Wang,Peiyu Duan,Nicha C. Dvornek,Lawrence H. Staib,Denis Sukhodolsky,Pamela Ventola,James S. Duncan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BrainSimSiam is a lightweight self-supervised framework for fMRI data that learns robust features from positive-only data pairs, outperforming supervised baselines and approaching large-scale models while being computationally efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: fMRI data analysis faces challenges due to high acquisition costs, small sample sizes, variable label quality in psychiatric scales, high dimensionality leading to overfitting risk, and prohibitive computational requirements for large-scale foundation models.

Method: Proposed BrainSimSiam - a data-efficient self-supervised representation learning framework that leverages positive-only data pairs to learn robust and generalizable features from fMRI data.

Result: The learned representations achieve strong performance across multiple downstream classification and regression tasks, outperforming fully supervised baselines and approaching the performance of large-scale models with much lower computational requirements.

Conclusion: BrainSimSiam demonstrates significant potential for data-limited neuroimaging applications by providing a computationally efficient alternative to large-scale foundation models while maintaining strong generalization across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) is a powerful tool for investigating human brain function. However, the high cost of data acquisition and the inherent subjectivity of psychiatric rating scales often lead to datasets with small sample sizes and variable label quality, especially when targeting a specific neurological condition. Combined with the inherently high dimensionality of fMRI data, these limitations substantially increase the risk of model overfitting. Recent years have seen growing interest in developing fMRI foundation models by combining multiple datasets; however, the computational resources needed for pretraining and fine-tuning are often prohibitive. We show that a lightweight self-supervised framework yields representations that generalize across diverse downstream tasks, outperforming fully supervised baselines and approaching the performance of large-scale models. We introduce BrainSimSiam, a data-efficient self-supervised representation learning framework that leverages positive-only data pairs to learn robust and generalizable features. We demonstrate that the learned representations achieve strong performance across multiple downstream classification and regression tasks, highlighting the potential of BrainSimSiam for data-limited neuroimaging applications.

</details>


### [167] [FormInv: A Measurement Protocol for Semantic Invariance in Mathematical Reasoning Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29001)
*Nishal Thomas,Noel Thomas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A paper introducing FormInv, a method for auditing semantic consistency in theorem-proving benchmarks, revealing significant errors in existing evaluations and showing that model rankings can be manipulated through paraphrase selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for theorem-proving models have a measurement gap: they don't adequately test semantic consistency across semantically equivalent theorem restatements. Standard accuracy metrics miss important inconsistencies in model behavior.

Method: Proposes FormInv framework with: 1) Paraphrase-quality audit using cross-model unanimity detection, 2) Semantic Consistency Rate (SCR) metric measuring response invariance across paraphrases, 3) Per-theorem Cochran's Q test, and 4) FormInvSelector for regime-aware model selection.

Result: Found 3.1% semantically incorrect paraphrases in MathCheck benchmark; Claude Haiku shows 86% accuracy but only 50% SCR; aggregate model accuracy ranges 86-96% but SCR spans 50-82% (32-point gap); demonstrated No-Free-Benchmark corollary showing rankings can be manipulated via paraphrase selection.

Conclusion: Standard benchmarks are inadequate for measuring semantic consistency in theorem-proving models. The FormInv framework provides essential auditing tools and metrics to properly evaluate model robustness and fairness, exposing hidden ranking vulnerabilities in existing benchmarks.

Abstract: A paraphrase-quality audit of MathCheck (ICLR 2025) detected 4 semantically incorrect paraphrases in 129 groups (3.1%); removing them drops GPT-4o from rank 2 to rank 4 and elevates Claude Haiku and DeepSeek V3 above it; these ranking changes are invisible to any single-model evaluation. Cross-model unanimity found these errors automatically (>= 3/4 models for MathCheck; >= 6/9 for our primary evaluation) for under $10; in our own dataset the same protocol found that 47% of auto-generated connective-variation paraphrases were semantically incorrect. That flaw compounds a deeper measurement gap: Claude Haiku 4.5 achieves 86% accuracy yet SCR=50%, meaning half its theorems are answered differently under semantically equivalent restatements, while aggregate accuracy across 9 models spans only 86-96% yet Semantic Consistency Rates (SCR) span 50-82% -- a 32-point gap invisible to standard benchmarks. Formally, for any target ranking over 9 frontier models there exists a weighting over paraphrase families that realizes it (No-Free-Benchmark corollary), because no model Pareto-dominates all families -- so benchmark designers who select families are implicitly choosing which model wins. FormInv supplies the audit protocol (replicated on external benchmarks at 100% recall), SCR and per-theorem Cochran's Q as primary invariance measures evaluated on 9 models across 366-811 items (on Lean4-verified theorems), and FormInvSelector for regime-aware model selection.

</details>


### [168] [FedQHD: Closed-Form Function-Space Federated Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29002)
*Yuchen Hou,Yongshan Chen,Zhuowen Zou,Calvin Yeung,Mohsen Imani,Tian Lan,Mahdi Imani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedQHD: A federated Q-learning method using hyperdimensional random-feature encoders with linear readout that enables function-space consistent aggregation, addressing the limitations of FedAvg-style parameter averaging in federated reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FedAvg-style parameter averaging is not function-space consistent - when clients use heterogeneous encoders or identical nonlinear networks, averaged parameters don't correspond to weighted averages of client value functions in any common function space, limiting effective federated reinforcement learning.

Method: Proposes FedQHD using hyperdimensional (random-feature) state encoders with linear readout, making Q-functions nonlinear in state but linear in trainable parameters. This enables closed-form aggregation: with shared encoder - weighted averaging of local readout matrices; with heterogeneous encoders - server constructs global teacher by averaging client Q-values on shared anchor-state set, clients compile teacher via single ridge projection.

Result: FedQHD matches or outperforms FedAvg-style baselines and distillation alternatives on four continuous-state, discrete-action control benchmarks while requiring substantially less computation. Theoretical analysis shows federation gap decomposes into subspace misalignment, anchor-set conditioning, and regularization bias, with anchor-to-dimension ratio m ≥ D_i as the well-conditioned regime reducing gap to a multiple of encoder heterogeneity floor.

Conclusion: FedQHD provides a theoretically grounded, computationally efficient approach to federated reinforcement learning that overcomes function-space inconsistency issues in parameter averaging, with empirical results matching theoretical predictions on federation gap behavior.

Abstract: Federated reinforcement learning enables decentralized agents to collaboratively improve policies or value estimates without exchanging raw trajectories. However, FedAvg-style parameter averaging is not function-space consistent: when clients use heterogeneous encoders or even identical nonlinear networks, averaged parameters need not correspond to the weighted average of client value functions in any common function space. We propose FedQHD, a federated Q-learning method using hyperdimensional (random-feature) state encoders with a linear readout, so that Q-functions are nonlinear in state yet linear in trainable parameters. This linear structure enables closed-form aggregation. With a shared encoder, the function-space consensus update coincides exactly with weighted averaging of local readout matrices. With heterogeneous encoders, the server constructs a global teacher by averaging client Q-values on a shared anchor-state set, and each client compiles this teacher into its local representation via a single ridge projection. We formalize the federation gap -- the error incurred when compiling a federated teacher into a heterogeneous client representation -- relative to a client-specific oracle projection. We show that this gap decomposes into subspace misalignment, anchor-set conditioning, and regularization bias. We further identify the anchor-to-dimension ratio $m \geq D_i$ as the well-conditioned regime in which the gap reduces to a multiple of the encoder heterogeneity floor. On four continuous-state, discrete-action control benchmarks, FedQHD matches or outperforms FedAvg-style baselines and distillation-based alternatives while requiring substantially less computation, and the empirical dependence of the federation gap on encoder dimension matches our theoretical analysis.

</details>


### [169] [LoRe: Adaptive Interaction-Evaluation Routing with Per-Step Interaction Budgets for Iterative Graph Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29005)
*Jintao Li,Yong-Yi Wang,Zheng-An Wang,Heng Fan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRe is an inference-time wrapper for diffusion-based combinatorial optimization solvers that dynamically routes computation to high-conflict interactions, achieving significant speedups (8-15×) and memory reductions (12-44×) while preserving solution quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based neural solvers for combinatorial optimization are computationally expensive due to repeatedly evaluating dense edge/factor interactions, making them memory-bound and slow at scale.

Method: LoRe is a training-free, inference-time drop-in wrapper that enforces per-step interaction-evaluation budgeting by dynamically routing computation only to high-conflict or high-uncertainty interactions, rather than using fixed sparsification methods like static kNN graphs.

Result: On Maximum Independent Set problems, LoRe enables 3× larger problem sizes beyond baseline's out-of-memory limit, with ∼8× speedup, ∼12× peak-memory reduction while preserving solution quality. On Traveling Salesperson Problem (n=1000), achieves ∼15× speedup, 44× memory reduction with competitive tour quality.

Conclusion: LoRe provides a scalable, efficient solution for diffusion-based combinatorial optimization solvers by applying principles from many-body physics, offering substantial computational benefits across different problem types with minimal impact on solution quality.

Abstract: Diffusion-based neural solvers for combinatorial optimization repeatedly re-evaluate dense edge/factor interactions, making inference expensive in wall-clock time and often memory-bound at scale. Inspired by the computational methodologies of many-body physics, we introduce LoRe, a training-free, inference-time drop-in wrapper that enforces per-step interaction-evaluation budgeting: at each iteration, it evaluates only a fixed fraction of interactions by dynamically routing computation to high-conflict or high-uncertainty interactions, instead of using a fixed sparsification (e.g., static kNN graphs or static masks). Under fully inclusive end-to-end wall-clock accounting, LoRe substantially improves scalability on the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem, extending feasible inference more than $3\times$ beyond the baseline's out-of-memory limit, delivering a $\sim 8\times$ speedup and a $\sim 12\times$ peak-memory reduction, with solution quality preserved in this regime. Demonstrating cross-task generality on the large-scale Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) and zero-shot robustness to topology shifts, LoRe achieves a $\sim 15\times$ speedup at $n=1000$ with a $44\times$ memory reduction and competitive tour quality.

</details>


### [170] [Causal Intelligence for Constraint-Aware Intervention Design to Induce State Transitions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29008)
*Zixuan Song,Uwe Mueller,Dimitris V. Manatakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: COAST is a causal-intelligence framework that learns causal models from source-target state data to design constrained interventions that achieve desired state transitions while balancing efficacy, complexity, and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most predictive models lack mechanistic insight and principled frameworks for decision-making in state transition problems, limiting their usefulness for designing targeted interventions in scientific applications.

Method: COAST learns context-specific causal graphs and structural causal models from source-target state data, attributes distributional shifts to causal drivers, and uses constraint-aware multi-objective optimization to design interventions balancing transition efficacy, intervention complexity, and target-state stability.

Result: Across synthetic benchmarks and real biological datasets, COAST recovers key causal drivers and identifies robust single- and multi-target intervention strategies that achieve desired state transitions, providing transparent mechanistic rationales for experimental validation.

Conclusion: COAST provides a principled, modular, and domain-agnostic causal-intelligence framework for designing constrained interventions that induce state transitions, offering both predictive capabilities and mechanistic insight to guide experimental validation.

Abstract: Driving a system from one state to another through targeted interventions is a fundamental challenge in science, yet most predictive models offer limited mechanistic insight and no principled framework for decision-making. Here we present COAST (Causally Optimal Actions for State Transitions), a causal-intelligence approach for the in-silico design of constrained interventions that induce user-defined state transitions. Given data characterizing source and target states, COAST learns context-specific causal graphs and structural causal models, attributes observed distributional shifts to mechanism-level causal drivers, and introduces a novel constraint-aware multi-objective optimization formulation that balances transition efficacy, intervention complexity, and target-state stability. The approach is modular and domain-agnostic, integrating feature selection, causal discovery, causal modeling, and intervention identification and evaluation through interchangeable components. Across synthetic benchmarks and real biological datasets, COAST recovers key causal drivers and identifies robust single- and multi-target intervention strategies that achieve desired state transitions, accompanied by transparent mechanistic rationales to guide experimental validation.

</details>


### [171] [Label-Free Reinforcement Learning via Cross-Model Entropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29009)
*Matt Gorbett,Hossein Shirazi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CME (Cross-Model Entropy) uses the mean log-likelihood of a generator's response under a separate verifier model as a label-free reward signal for RL post-training, overcoming limitations of existing methods that require ground-truth verifiers or human preferences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL post-training methods for LLMs are bottlenecked by reward signals: they require either ground-truth verifiable rewards (limited to domains with automatic correctness checks) or expensive human preference labels prone to reward hacking. Recent label-free methods use self-referential signals but risk reinforcing model errors through self-consistency.

Method: Propose Cross-Model Entropy (CME) - the mean log-likelihood of a generator's response under a separate verifier model as a continuous, training-free reward signal. CME is integrated into GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) without other changes to training. The method leverages the principle that responses a verifier finds unsurprising are likely correct/high-quality, and the independent verifier prevents gaming through self-consistency.

Result: CME rewards outperform untrained baselines in head-to-head LLM-as-Judge comparisons across four model families (Qwen, Llama, Gemma, OLMo) and three training regimes (pretrained, SFT, instruction-tuned) on open-ended instruction following (UltraFeedback prompts, AlpacaEval 2.0), achieving tie-adjusted win rates from 52.5% to 71.4%.

Conclusion: CME provides an effective label-free reward signal for RL post-training that overcomes limitations of existing methods, extends label-free RL to open-ended instruction following where self-referential signals are inapplicable, and demonstrates consistent improvements across diverse model families and training regimes.

Abstract: Post-training large language models with reinforcement learning is bottlenecked by the reward signal. Existing approaches require either ground-truth verifiable rewards, restricting training to domains with automatic correctness checks (e.g., mathematics, code execution), or human preference labels, which are expensive to collect and prone to reward hacking. Recent label-free methods replace ground-truth verifiers with self-referential signals like majority voting or token entropy over a model's own outputs, but risk reinforcing a model's own errors. In this work we propose Cross-Model Entropy (CME), the mean log-likelihood of a generator's response under a separate verifier model, as a label-free reward signal for RL post-training. CME is continuous, training-free, and grounded in the principle that responses a verifier finds unsurprising are likely correct or high quality. Because the verifier is independent of the generator, the signal cannot be gamed through self-consistency. We integrate CME into GRPO with no other changes to the training loop, extending label-free RL to open-ended instruction following -- a regime where self-referential signals are inapplicable or poorly suited. On open-ended instruction following (UltraFeedback prompts, evaluated on AlpacaEval 2.0), CME rewards beat the untrained base in head-to-head LLM-as-Judge comparisons across four model families (Qwen, Llama, Gemma, OLMo) and three training regimes (pretrained, SFT, and instruction-tuned), with tie-adjusted win rates ranging from 52.5% to 71.4%. Code will be released upon publication.

</details>


### [172] [Designing Active Tether-Net Systems for Space Debris Capture with Graph-Learning-Aided Mixed-Combinatorial Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29021)
*Feng Liu,Achira Boonrath,Gishnu Madhu,Eleonora M. Botta,Souma Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based optimization approach to solve Mixed Combinatorial Nonlinear Programming (MCNLP) problems in active tether-net system design, reducing the complex combinatorial-continuous optimization to a simpler NLP problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Active tether-net systems for space debris capture present complex MCNLP optimization problems with continuous, integer, and categorical variables. Classical binary encoding methods are ineffective for these highly nonlinear multimodal problems, while integer coding can introduce spurious relations. There's limited systematic exploration of design and control choices for such systems.

Method: The paper adopts and extends a graph-learning-aided optimization approach where a GNN is trained to score and recommend candidate combinations (represented as graph nodes) based on continuous variable inputs. This reduces MCNLP to NLP, which is then solved using Particle Swarm Optimization with gradient-based fine-tuning.

Result: The GNN-based recommender achieves significantly faster convergence to similar optimal solutions compared to directly solving the MCNLP problem, demonstrated on concurrent design of net morphology, MU mass/thruster choices, and controller aiming points.

Conclusion: The GNN-based optimization approach effectively handles complex mixed combinatorial-continuous optimization problems in engineering design, particularly for graph-structured combinatorial spaces like active tether-net systems, offering improved computational efficiency.

Abstract: Active tether-net systems are a promising solution for capturing large non-cooperative targets, such as space debris, by deploying a flexible net manipulated by maneuverable units (MUs). However, concurrent systematic explorations of design and control choices of the tether-net system to understand its full potential remain limited, partly due to the complex, constrained, nonlinear optimization problem that it presents -- one that involves a mixture of continuous, integer and categorical variables, with the latter two arising from net connectivity and component choices, respectively. Classical binary encoding methods are often ineffective for solving highly nonlinear and multimodal Mixed Combinatorial Nonlinear Programmings (MCNLPs) in engineering design, while integer coding approaches can introduce spurious relations among combinations. Given the graph-structured characteristics of the combinatorial space, this paper adopts and extends a new graph-learning-aided optimization approach to solve this MCNLP problem. Here, a Graph Neural Network (GNN) is trained to score (as output) and thereof recommend candidate combinations represented as nodes in a graph, with the continuous variable vector portion of a candidate design given as input. As a result, the MCNLP optimization reduces to an NLP, which can be solved using standard solvers. While this reduction approach is agnostic to the choice of the NLP solver, here a state-of-the-art Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm with gradient-based fine-tuning is used as the solver. Demonstrated on the problem of concurrently designing the morphology of the net, choice of mass and thrusters in the MUs and aiming points used by the controller of the tether-net system, the GNN-based recommender is shown to provide significantly faster convergence to similar optimal solutions, compared to direct solution of the MCNLP problem.

</details>


### [173] [Return-to-Go Is More Than a Number: Q-Guided Alignment for Return-Conditioned Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29028)
*Yuxiao Yang,Weitong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Q-ALIGN DT improves Conditioned Sequence Models by ensuring alignment between input return-to-go values and actual policy performance through Q-value consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CSMs treat return-to-go values as simple numerical inputs without ensuring they align with the actual performance of their policies. This misalignment limits their controllability and effectiveness.

Method: Proposes Q-ALIGN DT framework that enforces alignment by ensuring the Q-value of the output policy is consistent with the input RTG. Uses Q-function for dense guidance and fine-tunes with RTG-perturbation technique with the CSM.

Result: Achieves superior controllability and performance across D4RL benchmark. Learns a structured family of policies that maintains precise alignment and generalizes to tasks like velocity-tracking where prior methods fail.

Conclusion: Q-ALIGN DT provides an effective framework for aligning conditioned sequence models with performance objectives, enabling better control and generalization in reinforcement learning tasks.

Abstract: Conditioned Sequence Models (CSMs) learn policies by treating return-to-go (RTG) as a control signal. However, existing CSMs often treat the RTGs as simple numerical inputs rather than aligning them with the performance of their policies. In this paper, we propose Q-ALIGN DT, a framework that enforces this alignment by ensuring the $Q$-value of the output policy is consistent with the input RTG. By leveraging a $Q$ function to provide dense guidance to CSMs and further fine-tuning it using an RTG-perturbation technique with the CSM, our method ensures that higher RTGs are consistently mapped to trajectories with higher expected returns. Theoretically, we show that Q-ALIGN DT can efficiently learn the desired policy and output a near-optimal one when the RTG is sufficiently high. Empirically, we demonstrate through extensive experiments that Q-ALIGN DT achieves superior controllability and performance across the D4RL benchmark. Remarkably, our model effectively learns a structured family of policies that maintains precise alignment and generalizes to tasks like velocity-tracking where prior methods fail.

</details>


### [174] [Theoretical Foundations and Effective Algorithms for Policy-Aware Simulator Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29032)
*Christoph Dann,Yishay Mansour,Mehryar Mohri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A game-theoretic approach to model-based RL that learns strategically robust simulators by playing a minimax game between a model and adversarial policy, reducing reality gap and improving real-world performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional MBRL focuses on minimizing predictive loss, but powerful RL optimizers exploit minor model inaccuracies, leading to simulator exploitation and the reality gap where policies succeed in simulation but fail in real-world deployment.

Method: Formulate simulator learning as a zero-sum minimax game between a model player and adversarial policy player. Develop three theoretical components: (1) online learning guarantees, (2) critic-based simplification bounding policy-value gap, and (3) Error-MDP duality showing worst-case policy finding is dual to standard RL.

Result: The approach reduces prediction error in strategically important regions by 1.5-2.2× and enables policies trained purely in simulation to match near-optimal real-world performance on continuous control tasks.

Conclusion: Strategic robustness should be prioritized over predictive accuracy for simulator learning in MBRL. The minimax framework provides theoretical guarantees and practical benefits for bridging the reality gap.

Abstract: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) agents typically learn world models by minimizing predictive loss. However, powerful RL optimizers inevitably exploit minor model inaccuracies, leading to simulator exploitation and a reality gap where policies succeed in simulation but fail in the real world. We propose that the objective for learning simulators should be strategic robustness rather than predictive accuracy, and formulate this as a zero-sum minimax game between a model player and an adversarial policy player. We provide a comprehensive theoretical analysis: (1) an online learning guarantee showing the game is learnable with sublinear regret bounds; (2) a tractable critic-based simplification bounding the global policy-value gap by the local critic's loss; and (3) an Error-MDP duality, proving that finding the worst-case policy is formally dual to a standard RL problem where the reward is the one-step critic error. This duality yields a provably convergent active data selection algorithm. Experiments on continuous control tasks demonstrate that our approach reduces prediction error in strategically important regions by $1.5$-$2.2\times$ and enables policies trained purely in simulation to match near-optimal real-world performance.

</details>


### [175] [Moment Matching Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29033)
*Yiyan,Liang,Sifei Liu,Weitong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoMa QL uses maximum mean discrepancy to accelerate flow-based policy inference for more efficient offline-to-online RL with faster action sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Score-based and flow-based generative models have great expressive power but suffer from prolonged inference latency, creating computational bottlenecks in RL with iterative sampling.

Method: Moment Matching Q-Learning (MoMa QL) uses maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) to match all orders of statistics between original and target distributions, providing strong regularization on moment statistics for stable distribution-level convergence.

Result: MoMa QL achieves computational efficiency with competitive performance on D4RL tasks, and demonstrates superior offline-to-online RL performance due to faster flow-based policy action sampling and better adaptability.

Conclusion: MoMa QL effectively addresses inference latency in generative RL models through moment matching, enabling faster and more adaptable offline-to-online reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Score-based and flow-based generative models exhibit remarkable expressive capacity in capturing complex distributions, and have been extensively deployed in tasks ranging from image generation to reinforcement learning. Nevertheless, these models suffer from prolonged inference latency, which imposes a significant computational bottleneck in RL with iterative sampling. To overcome this limitation, we propose a new framework named Moment Matching Q-Learning (MoMa QL), which utilizes a technique from statistical hypothesis testing known as maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) that intend to match all orders of statistics between the original and target distribution. By enforcing strong regularization on all moment statistics, this algorithm guarantees distribution-level convergence for conditional score function and remains stable under various hyperparameters. Empirically, we show that our method MoMa QL is more computationally efficient with a comparable if not competitive performance in various D4RL tasks. Remarkably, by accelerating the action sampling process for flow-based policies, MoMa QL demonstrates superior performance in offline-to-online RL tasks because of faster and stronger adaptability for online interactive finetuning.

</details>


### [176] [Parallel Adaptive Multi-Objective Evolutionary Learning of Discretized Bayesian Network Classifiers for Clinical Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29058)
*Damy M. F. Ha,Tanja Alderliesten,Peter A. N. Bosman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Baymex is a multi-objective evolutionary algorithm for learning Bayesian Networks that has been improved with parallelization and adaptive steering to address scalability issues, and reconfigured for clinical classification tasks, achieving significant speedups and competitive predictive performance while producing interpretable models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayesian Networks are valuable for explainable AI due to their transparent probabilistic models, but existing methods like Baymex have two key limitations: 1) they require substantial computation time, and 2) they have only been evaluated on synthetic data, limiting real-world applicability.

Method: The authors introduce two main improvements to Baymex: 1) a parallelization strategy for better scalability, and 2) an adaptive steering mechanism to reduce overfitting. Additionally, they reconfigure Baymex for classification tasks by using multi-objective optimization of cross-entropy loss and BIC complexity term.

Result: The enhanced Baymex achieved speedups of over 54× on a 16-core CPU. On clinical datasets (RADCURE, SUPPORT, and an in-house dataset), it showed statistically similar or better predictive performance compared to baselines (decision trees, logistic regression, naive Bayes, random forests) while producing compact, inspectable BNs that include established clinical factors.

Conclusion: The improved Baymex algorithm successfully addresses both computational scalability and real-world applicability concerns, demonstrating that multi-objective evolutionary approaches can produce competitive, interpretable Bayesian Network classifiers for clinical decision support while maintaining computational efficiency.

Abstract: Bayesian Networks (BNs) are of interest from an explainable AI viewpoint, offering transparent probabilistic models for decision support. Baymex is a recently introduced multi-objective evolutionary algorithm for learning discretized BNs, enabling experts to trade-off different objectives of interest, such as likelihood, model complexity, and prior beliefs. While Baymex has been shown to outperform state-of-the-art BN learning approaches, Baymex still 1) requires a lot of computation time and 2) has only been evaluated on synthetic data. To improve scalability, we introduce a parallelization strategy as well as a mechanism that enables adaptively steering optimization toward networks that overfit less. We furthermore reconfigure Baymex to train a BN classifier through multi-objective optimization of cross-entropy loss and the BIC complexity term so as to evaluate its performance on real-world clinical classification tasks. Besides observing speedups up to over 54 times on a 16-core CPU, comparisons against clinically familiar baselines (decision trees, logistic regression, naive Bayes, and random forests) on two open-source (RADCURE and SUPPORT) and one in-house dataset, show that Baymex obtains statistically similar or better predictive performance while producing compact, clinically inspectable BNs. Importantly, Baymex finds multiple plausible BN classifiers that contain predictors consistent with established clinical factors.

</details>


### [177] [Ensemble Score Filtering for Real-Data Energy Consumption Forecast Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29072)
*Ruoyu Hu,Dahai Yu,Feng Bao,Guang Wang,Guannan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A data assimilation approach using Ensemble Score Filter (EnSF) with pretrained black-box forecasting models improves energy consumption state estimation by correcting forecasts with partial, noisy observations, outperforming traditional Ensemble Kalman Filter in nonlinear settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In energy consumption forecasting, complete and timely measurements are often unavailable in practice, with data being partial, noisy, or delayed. This necessitates methods that can correct learned forecasting models using available observations through data assimilation techniques.

Method: Uses Ensemble Score Filter (EnSF) which employs score-based diffusion models to approximate filtering distributions. The approach treats a pretrained black-box spatio-temporal forecasting model as the state propagator in the filtering procedure, avoiding retraining neural networks during assimilation through closed-form score representation and Monte Carlo approximation.

Result: Numerical experiments show that open-loop propagation of learned forecasting models becomes unreliable over long horizons, while EnSF-based correction substantially improves state estimation. EnSF provides stronger correction than Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) under the nonlinear observation setting considered.

Conclusion: The EnSF approach successfully integrates learned forecasting models with data assimilation for energy consumption forecasting, demonstrating improved reliability and accuracy over traditional methods and uncorrected learned models, particularly in handling nonlinear observation scenarios with partial/noisy data.

Abstract: Accurate estimation and forecasting of energy consumption are important for power-system operation, planning, and demand-side management. In practice, however, complete and timely measurements may not always be available, and the observed data can be partial, noisy, or delayed. This motivates the use of learned forecasting models for predicting the evolving consumption state, together with data assimilation methods for sequential forecast correction. In this work, we study a high-dimensional data assimilation problem for real energy-consumption data. \modeltext{The forward prediction is supplied by a pretrained black-box spatio-temporal forecasting model, which is treated as the state propagator in the filtering procedure.} We employ the Ensemble Score Filter (EnSF) to assimilate partial and noisy observations and to correct the forecast trajectory over time. The EnSF uses score-based diffusion models to approximate filtering distributions and avoids retraining neural-network score models during assimilation by using a closed-form score representation and Monte Carlo approximation. Numerical experiments demonstrate that open-loop propagation of the learned forecasting model can become unreliable over long horizons, while EnSF-based correction substantially improves state estimation. Comparisons with the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) further show that EnSF provides stronger correction under the nonlinear observation setting considered in this work.

</details>


### [178] [Knowledge Offloading: Decomposing LLMs into Sparse Backbones and Memory Modules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29075)
*Karim Galliamov,Rochelle Choenni,Ivan Titov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Knowledge Offloading (KOFF) decomposes pretrained LLMs into sparse shared backbones for general capabilities and external memory modules for domain-specific knowledge, enabling modular knowledge reorganization without large performance loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs combine both general capabilities and domain-specific knowledge in a single parameter set, which limits flexibility. The paper aims to explore whether this capacity can be reorganized by keeping broadly useful computation in a shared backbone while moving specialized knowledge into external memory modules for better modularity and efficiency.

Method: Proposes Knowledge Offloading (KOFF) framework that starts from a frozen base model and jointly learns a structured pruning mask plus lightweight recovery modules. Uses LoRA adapters and learned key-value caches as external memory modules. Evaluates across Llama and Qwen models from 3B to 8B parameters.

Result: Shows non-trivial capacity can be moved out of the shared backbone without large performance loss. At ~12% global sparsity, KOFF preserves most of the unpruned model's performance, while pruning the same frozen model without memories degrades sharply. LoRA and learned KV memories are complementary, and specialization analyses reveal meaningful decomposition: language-specific neurons are preferentially removed while language-general neurons remain in the backbone.

Conclusion: Knowledge can be successfully reallocated between a shared core and swappable external memories, demonstrating that LLM capacity can be reorganized into modular components. This approach enables more flexible and efficient model architectures.

Abstract: LLMs encode both general capabilities and domain-specific knowledge in a single set of parameters. We ask whether this capacity can be reorganized: keeping broadly useful computation in a shared backbone, while moving specialized knowledge into external memory modules. We propose \emph{knowledge offloading} (KOFF), a framework for decomposing a pretrained LLM into a sparse shared backbone and domain-specific memories. Starting from a frozen base model, we jointly learn a structured pruning mask and lightweight recovery modules, implemented as LoRA adapters and learned key-value caches. Across Llama and Qwen models from 3B to 8B, we find that non-trivial capacity can be moved out of the shared backbone without a large loss in model ability. At around 12\% global sparsity, KOFF preserves much of the unpruned model's performance, while pruning the same frozen model without memories degrades sharply. Ablations show that LoRA and learned KV memories are complementary, and specialization analyses suggest that the learned decomposition is meaningful: language-specific neurons are preferentially removed while language-general neurons largely remain in the backbone. These results suggest that knowledge can be reallocated between a shared core and swappable external memories.

</details>


### [179] [OISD: On-Policy Internal Self-Distillation of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29089)
*Xinyu Liu,Darryl Cherian Jacob,Yang Zhou,Jindong Wang,Pan He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OISD is a new reinforcement learning framework that improves reasoning by using the final layer as an internal teacher to transfer predictive signals to intermediate layers through logit and attention alignment, achieving significant improvements on mathematical reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL post-training approaches mainly optimize final output policies using sparse outcome-level rewards, overlooking valuable predictive signals encoded in intermediate representations. There's a need to leverage these intermediate representations to improve reasoning capabilities.

Method: Introduces On-policy Internal Self-Distillation (OISD) framework that transfers predictive signals from final layer to intermediate layers. Uses final layer as detached internal teacher during GRPO optimization. Employs two alignment mechanisms: logit alignment (transfers reasoning behaviors) and attention alignment (ensures consistent attention patterns). Uses signed advantage-weighted Jensen-Shannon alignment to distill intermediate representations while maintaining policy consistency.

Result: OISD demonstrates substantial and consistent improvements over strong reasoning RL baselines across four mathematical reasoning tasks. The framework effectively leverages intermediate representations without requiring external privileged information.

Conclusion: The OISD framework successfully addresses the limitation of overlooking intermediate representations in RL post-training. By using internal self-distillation with alignment mechanisms, it significantly improves reasoning performance in mathematical tasks, providing a new paradigm for leveraging internal representations in reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Recent reinforcement learning (RL) post-training approaches primarily optimize the final output policy using sparse outcome-level rewards, while largely overlooking predictive signals encoded in intermediate representations. In this paper, we introduce a new paradigm called on-policy internal self-distillation and propose the OISD framework, which improves reasoning by transferring on-policy predictive signals from the final layer to intermediate representations. During rollout and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) optimization, the final layer acts as both the policy and a detached internal teacher for selected intermediate layers, which are guided to align with it through two complementary mechanisms: logit alignment, which transfers high-level reasoning behaviors (how to think), and attention alignment, which enforces consistent attention patterns (where to look) from the final layer to the selected intermediate layer, both without requiring external privileged information. Our OISD, together with GRPO, employs signed advantage-weighted Jensen--Shannon alignment to distill informative intermediate representations while preserving policy consistency under a unified acting policy. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of OISD, with substantial and consistent improvements over strong reasoning RL baselines across four mathematical reasoning tasks. The code will be released at https://github.com/THE-MALT-LAB/OISD

</details>


### [180] [Model Merging by Output-Space Projection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29101)
*Bethan Evans,Benjamin Etheridge,Stephen Roberts,Jared Tanner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QP-Merge: A convex quadratic programming framework for model merging with optimality guarantees, outperforming existing heuristic methods like task arithmetic and TIES.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing model merging methods (task arithmetic, model soups, TIES, DARE) are computationally efficient but rely on heuristic design choices and lack formal optimality guarantees. There's a need for a principled, optimal approach to merging fine-tuned checkpoints into multi-task models.

Method: Formulates model merging as a convex quadratic program (QP) over residual updates, minimizing a squared-output calibration objective using calibration inputs and fine-tuned model outputs. Includes a closed-form diagnostic (fraction of residual energy captured) to predict merge quality. Extends to multi-layer merging via sequential layer-wise algorithm.

Result: QP framework matches or outperforms existing methods in single-layer setting, with the optimal basis sometimes providing significant gains over diagonal QP. Consistent gains demonstrated across language and vision benchmarks with multi-layer extension.

Conclusion: Model merging can be optimally solved via convex quadratic programming, providing theoretical guarantees while subsuming existing heuristic methods as special cases, with practical performance improvements validated empirically.

Abstract: Model merging combines fine-tuned checkpoints into a single multi-task model without retraining. Existing methods - such as task arithmetic, model soups, TIES, and DARE - are computationally efficient and empirically successful, but rely on heuristic design choices and lack formal optimality guarantees. We show that merging can be formulated as a convex quadratic programme over residual updates, yielding weights that minimise a squared-output calibration objective using calibration inputs and fine-tuned model outputs, and subsuming existing methods as special cases. Our framework yields a closed-form diagnostic - the fraction of residual energy captured by a chosen basis - that predicts downstream merge quality using only the calibration set. Empirically, the QP matches or outperforms existing methods in the single-layer setting, and we characterise when the optimal basis provides significant gains over the cheaper diagonal QP. We extend to multi-layer merging via a sequential layer-wise algorithm and demonstrate consistent gains across language and vision benchmarks.

</details>


### [181] [Bridging Chemists and AI: An Expert-Augmented Framework for Interpretable Route Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29108)
*Yujia Guo,Mikhail Kabeshov,Tat Hong Duong Le,Samuel Genheden,Marco V. Mijangos,Varvara Voinarvoska,Giulia Bergonzini,Ola Engkvist,Samuel Kaski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel expert-augmented ML framework that scores multi-step synthetic routes using DeepSets and tree edit distance, achieving significant improvements over baselines with both quantitative and qualitative assessments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current data-driven assessment systems for multi-step synthetic route selection oversimplify the multi-objective nature of synthesis design and rely on proxy datasets like patent routes rather than universally grounded criteria, limiting their practical utility in medicinal and process chemistry.

Method: An expert-augmented, data-driven scoring framework integrating machine learning with chemists' domain knowledge. Uses DeepSets-based model trained with tree edit distance between reference and machine-generated routes, then fine-tuned with expert evaluations to produce quantitative scores and qualitative categories (Good, Plausible, Bad).
