Result: The system achieves Spearman correlation coefficient of 0.78 and Pearson correlation of 0.77 for category assessment prediction, and 60.2% top-1 ranking accuracy for score prediction, substantially outperforming the previous baseline of 17.5%.

Conclusion: The integration of expert knowledge with machine learning creates an effective framework for both numerical and explainable route assessment, demonstrating significant improvements over existing methods and offering practical value for synthetic route selection in organic synthesis.

Abstract: Selecting efficient multi-step synthetic routes is a central challenge in organic synthesis, particularly in medicinal and process chemistry, where route choice directly impacts feasibility, cost, and development efficiency. Data-driven assessment systems often oversimplify the multi-objective nature of synthesis design and rely on proxy datasets, such as patent routes, rather than universally grounded criteria. To address this, we introduce an expert-augmented, data-driven scoring framework that integrates machine learning with chemists' domain knowledge for both numerical and explainable route assessment. A DeepSets-based model is trained using tree edit distance between reference and machine-generated routes, and then fine-tuned with expert evaluations to produce both quantitative scores and interpretable qualitative categories: Good, Plausible, and Bad. The resulting system achieves a Spearman correlation coefficient of 0.78 and a Pearson correlation of 0.77 for category assessment prediction, and 60.2% top-1 ranking accuracy for score prediction, substantially outperforming the previous baseline of 17.5%.

</details>


### [182] [When and How Long? The Readout-Mediator Angle in Temporal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29126)
*Shreyas Fadnavis,Praitayini Kanakaraj,Felix Wyss*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Linear probes can perfectly decode representations but be orthogonal to how models actually compute, making them unreliable for interpretability and safety monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current interpretability methods using linear probes assume they reveal how models use representations, but probes might learn directions irrelevant to actual computation, undermining their reliability for safety-critical applications.

Method: Used calendar-date duration reasoning in language models, comparing $\sin$/$\cos$ probes vs. Distributed Alignment Search (DAS) to find causal subspaces. Applied ablation studies, measured readout-mediator angles, reverse-engineered circuits, and used sparse autoencoder decomposition across multiple model scales.

Result: $\sin$/$\cos$ probes recover day-of-year perfectly but are orthogonal to causal computation (readout-mediator angle ~ random). DAS finds 4D causal subspace whose ablation collapses performance. Circuit analysis shows attention heads route month context via QK offsets, MLPs convert dates to durations downstream of probe-detected features.

Conclusion: Linear probes can be completely orthogonal to models' actual computational pathways, creating a fundamental failure mode for probe-based interpretability that undermines their use as safety monitors, since probes can confidently detect directions the model has abandoned.

Abstract: A linear probe can decode a representation almost perfectly and yet be completely irrelevant to how the model uses it. On calendar-date duration reasoning in language models, a $\sin$/$\cos$ probe recovers day-of-year from a layer's activations, yet ablating its direction has no effect on the model's answers -- while ablating a four-dimensional subspace found by Distributed Alignment Search (DAS) at the same layer collapses performance entirely. We measure the angle between these two subspaces -- the \emph{readout-mediator angle} -- and find it indistinguishable from the angle between two random subspaces (the Haar-uniform null), meaning the probe has learned a direction orthogonal to the model's actual computation. Reverse-engineering the circuit reveals why: attention heads route month-grained context through learned QK offsets at ${\pm}30$ and ${\pm}61$ days, and MLPs then convert \emph{when} (absolute date) into \emph{how long} (duration) -- all downstream of the causal subspace the probe never touches. Sparse-autoencoder decomposition confirms the split: probe-aligned and DAS-aligned features encode semantically disjoint concepts with negligible causal overlap. The dissociation replicates across four scales ($1.5$-$9\,$B) and two model families, with preliminary evidence on two further domains (spatial displacement, symbolic arithmetic), suggesting that readout-mediator orthogonality is a general failure mode of probe-based interpretability. This directly undermines proposals to deploy probes as runtime safety monitors: the probe can report high confidence on a direction the model has silently abandoned.

</details>


### [183] [Apertus LLM Family Expansion via Distillation and Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29128)
*Andrei Panferov,Davit Melikidze,Martin Jaggi,Dan Alistarh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Apertus-v1.1 is a distilled family of models (up to 4B parameters) created from Apertus 8B LLM using distillation and quantization to expand model families for different hardware constraints and budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With widespread LLM adoption across various applications (chatbots, data annotation), there's a need for models that satisfy diverse budget and hardware constraints. Current practice involves releasing model families of various sizes, requiring cost-effective methods to expand model families to new sizes and hardware formats.

Method: Using distillation and quantization techniques on the open-recipe Apertus 8B LLM, trained on 1.7T permissive license tokens to create Apertus-v1.1, a distilled family of models with up to 4B parameters.

Result: The approach demonstrates cost-efficiency and strong accuracy performance for covering large ranges of hardware and systems requirements, validating distillation and quantization as effective methods for expanding model families.

Conclusion: Distillation and quantization provide a cost-effective way to create diverse model families that can meet various hardware and budget constraints while maintaining strong performance, addressing the practical needs of widespread LLM deployment.

Abstract: The wide adoption of LLMs has led to their use in great variety of applications and scenarios, such as chatbot assistants and data annotation, creating the need for the models to satisfy certain budget and hardware constraints. This has led to the trend of LLMs being released in batches consisting of similar models of various sizes for the family of models to adhere to as wide of a range of constraints as possible. In this paper, we validate distillation and quantization as a cost-effective way to expand model families to new sizes and hardware formats. Based on the open-recipe Apertus 8B LLM, we produce Apertus-v1.1 - a distilled family of models with up to 4B parameters trained on 1.7T permissive license tokens. We demonstrate cost-efficiency and strong accuracy performance of our approach for covering large ranges of hardware and systems requirements.

</details>


### [184] [Optimal Gap-Dependent Regret for Private Stochastic Decision-Theoretic Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29148)
*Tommaso Cesari,Roberto Colomboni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a horizon-free pure differential privacy algorithm for stochastic decision-theoretic online learning that achieves optimal gap-dependent regret matching known lower bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve an open problem from COLT posed by Hu and Mehta: determining the optimal gap-dependent regret rate for stochastic decision-theoretic online learning under pure event-level differential privacy.

Method: The algorithm partitions time into blocks of exponentially increasing size, plays a single action throughout each block, and chooses the next action using an exponential mechanism applied to a data-independent random prefix of the previous block. The random prefix converts block regret into a sum of softmax selection errors, and a single entropy-potential argument controls privacy-dominated large-gap actions.

Result: The algorithm achieves explicit regret bound: Reg_T ≤ 1000·(log K/Δ_min + log K/ε) for every horizon T, matching the known lower bound up to a constant factor (1000 not optimized).

Conclusion: The paper solves the open problem by providing a horizon-free pure-DP algorithm with optimal gap-dependent regret, demonstrating that the lower bound is tight and achievable with a simple block-structured approach.

Abstract: We study stochastic decision-theoretic online learning with full information and event-level pure differential privacy. A COLT open problem of Hu and Mehta asks to determine the optimal gap-dependent regret rate for stochastic decision-theoretic online learning under pure event-level differential privacy. For $K$ actions, losses in $[0,1]$, and a unique best action separated from the second-best action by gap $Δ_{\min}$, the known lower bound is of order $
  \frac{\log K}{\min\{Δ_{\min},\varepsilon\}}, $
or equivalently, up to universal constants, of order \[
  \frac{\log K}{Δ_{\min}}+\frac{\log K}{\varepsilon}. \] We give a horizon-free pure-DP algorithm and prove the explicit regret bound \[
  \operatorname{Reg}_T
  \le
  1000 \cdot \left(\frac{\log K}{Δ_{\min}}+\frac{\log K}{\varepsilon}\right) \] for every horizon $T$. The numerical constant is not optimized. The algorithm partitions time into blocks of exponentially increasing size, plays a single action throughout each block, and chooses the next action by an exponential mechanism applied to a data-independent random prefix of the previous block. The random prefix converts block regret into a sum, over all prefix lengths, of softmax selection errors. A single entropy-potential argument controls all privacy-dominated large-gap actions at cost $\log K/\varepsilon$.

</details>


### [185] [Do Deep Networks Forget Initialization? A Forgetting-Time View of Practical Inductive Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29152)
*Mohua Das,Pierfrancesco Beneventano,Shibshankar Dey,Gareth H. McKinkey,Tomaso Poggio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Random initialization memory in neural networks persists through training depending on optimization choices: low-learning-rate SGD preserves initialization bias for thousands of epochs while Adam and larger learning rates erase it, revealing that practical inductive bias = architectural prior filtered by forgetting dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how much of the initial architectural bias from random initialization survives the training process, and to characterize the interaction between architectural priors and training dynamics in determining the final predictor's properties.

Method: Introduces "initialization memory" metric measuring dependence of validation-selected predictor on initialization scale. Performs controlled CIFAR-10 experiments on ResNets using different optimization regimes (SGD with various learning rates, Adam), tracks test accuracy variations across initialization scales, and analyzes forgetting dynamics across thousands of epochs.

Result: Low-learning-rate SGD preserves strong initialization memory: ResNet-9 with batch size 128 shows test accuracy variations of 26.5 percentage points across initialization scales despite ≥99.5% training accuracy, persisting over 5,000 epochs. Adam-family methods largely erase initialization dependence. Larger learning rates with L₂ norm control also erase memory. Forgetting occurs on timescales governed by regularization size.

Conclusion: The practical inductive bias of trained networks is not just the architectural prior but that prior filtered by the forgetting dynamics of the training pipeline. Regularizers that improve generalization are precisely those that erase initialization memory, connecting forgetting dynamics to generalization performance.

Abstract: Randomly initialized neural networks induce a prior over functions, but the predictor used in practice is produced only after training. We ask how much of this initial bias survives the training pipeline. To make the question measurable, we introduce initialization memory: the dependence of the validation-selected predictor on the scale of the random initialization. We perform controlled CIFAR-10 experiments on ResNets where initialization memory already sharply separates training regimes. Low-learning-rate SGD can interpolate while still remembering its initialization: on ResNet-9 with batch size $b=128$, test accuracy varies by $26.5$ percentage points across initialization scales despite $\ge99.5\%$ training accuracy. This is not undertraining: extending the same low-learning-rate regime to $5{,}000$ epochs leaves the spread essentially unchanged. In contrast, Adam-family methods largely erase the dependence. SGD can also be made to forget when larger learning rates are paired with explicit $L_2$ norm control. We interpret these findings in terms of the time scale of forgetting: gradient-flow-like dynamics can preserve initialization memory, whereas stochastic finite-step effects, explicit norm decay, and adaptive preconditioning erase it on scales governed by the size of explicit or implicit regularization. The practical inductive bias of a trained network is therefore not the architectural prior alone, but the architectural prior after being filtered by the forgetting dynamics of the training pipeline; and the same regularizers that improve generalization are precisely those that erase memory of initialization.

</details>


### [186] [Unveiling Multi-regime Patterns in SciML: Distinct Failure Modes and Regime-specific Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29153)
*Yuxin Wang,Yuanzhe Hu,Xiaokun Zhong,Xiaopeng Wang,Haiquan Lu,Tianyu Pang,Michael W. Mahoney,Yujun Yan,Pu Ren,Yaoqing Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Three distinct training regimes exist in scientific machine learning models, each requiring different optimization strategies, and conventional loss-landscape metrics can be misleading for diagnosing failure modes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural networks in scientific machine learning exhibit different behaviors across different hyperparameter settings, forming distinct "regimes" with qualitative differences. Understanding these regimes and their implications for model performance and robustness is crucial for improving SciML reliability.

Method: Developed a regime-aware diagnostic framework that jointly analyzes performance, training dynamics, and loss-landscape geometry across different SciML models, constraint enforcements, and optimizer designs.

Result: Identified three key findings: 1) Consistent three-regime structure emerges across diverse SciML models and settings, 2) Optimization effectiveness is regime-specific (no single method works well across all regimes), 3) SciML models exhibit fine-grained failure modes that challenge conventional loss-landscape metric interpretations.

Conclusion: The findings provide a unified, task-oblivious perspective on SciML failure modes and offer regime-aware guidance for improving model robustness, validated across physics-informed neural networks, neural operators, and neural ODEs on benchmark PDE/ODE problems.

Abstract: Neural networks trained under different hyperparameter settings can fall into distinct training "regimes," with consistent behavior within regimes and qualitative differences across regimes. In this paper, we study such multi-regime behavior in scientific machine learning (SciML) models through a regime-aware diagnostic framework that jointly analyzes performance, training dynamics, and loss-landscape geometry. We identify three key findings: (i) a consistent three-regime structure emerges across many standard SciML models, different constraint enforcements, and various optimizer designs; (ii) optimization effectiveness is regime-specific, with no single method performing well across all regimes; and (iii) SciML models can exhibit fine-grained failure modes that can challenge conventional interpretations of standard loss-landscape metrics. Our results provide an approach to establish a unified, task-oblivious perspective on failure modes in SciML and to inform regime-aware guidance for improving robustness. We validate these findings across widely-used SciML models, including physics-informed neural networks, neural operators, and neural ordinary differential equations, on benchmarks spanning representative ordinary and partial differential equations.

</details>


### [187] [RUBRIC-ARROW: Alternating Pointwise Rubric Reward Modeling for LLM Post-training in Non-verifiable Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29156)
*Haoxiang Jiang,Zihan Dong,Tianci Liu,Wanying Wang,Ran Xu,Tony Yu,Linjun Zhang,Haoyu Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RUBRIC-ARROW: An alternating framework that jointly trains a rubric generator and rubric-conditioned judge using only pairwise preference data, with improved scoring and reduced ties for LLM evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pointwise reward modeling struggles with absolute scoring in subjective settings, while existing rubric-based methods depend on frontier LLMs and suffer from ties caused by hard Boolean aggregation.

Method: Alternating framework that jointly trains a rubric generator and rubric-conditioned judge with RL using only pairwise preference data. Features probability-based scoring rule to reduce ties, phase-specific preference-based rewards, and alternating GRPO scheme to train pointwise evaluator.

Result: Extensive experiments show RUBRIC-ARROW achieves competitive reward-modeling accuracy and yields consistent gains for downstream policy post-training.

Conclusion: RUBRIC-ARROW presents an effective alternative to existing rubric-based evaluation methods that reduces ties and dependency on frontier LLMs while improving downstream performance.

Abstract: Pointwise reward modeling offers critical signals for LLM post-training, yet struggles with absolute scoring in subjective, non-verifiable settings. Rubric-based methods address this by decomposing evaluation into explicit criteria, but existing approaches typically depend on frontier LLMs and suffer from ties caused by hard Boolean aggregation. We present RUBRIC-ARROW, an alternating framework that jointly trains a rubric generator and a rubric-conditioned judge, with its RL stage using only pairwise preference data. Our method couples a probability-based scoring rule that reduces ties with phase-specific preference-based rewards and an alternating GRPO scheme that together train the pointwise evaluator. Extensive experiments show that RUBRIC-ARROW achieves competitive reward-modeling accuracy and yields consistent gains for downstream policy post-training.

</details>


### [188] [Parallax: Parameterized Local Linear Attention for Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29157)
*Yifei Zuo,Dhruv Pai,Zhichen Zeng,Alec Dewulf,Shuming Hu,Zhaoran Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Parallax is a scalable Local Linear Attention mechanism that upgrades standard attention to local linear estimation, achieving better performance than FlashAttention in LLM pretraining with improved perplexity and downstream task results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard attention mechanisms have remained structurally unchanged despite LLMs becoming central to AI. While Local Linear Attention (LLA) offers theoretical advantages with better bias-variance tradeoffs, it hasn't been scaled for LLM pretraining due to computational and numerical stability issues.

Method: Parallax parameterizes Local Linear Attention by eliminating the numerical solver in LLA and learning an extra query-like projector to probe the KV covariance. It includes a hardware-aware algorithm that increases arithmetic intensity over FlashAttention, shifting attention to a more compute-bound regime. The method is placed within a family of attention mechanisms characterized by bandwidth, probe construction, and affine structure.

Result: The prototype decode kernel matches or outperforms FlashAttention 2/3 across diverse batch sizes and context lengths. Pretraining at 0.6B and 1.7B scales shows consistent perplexity improvements throughout training that transfer to downstream benchmarks. Advantages persist under both parameter-matched and compute-matched controls, demonstrating Pareto improvement. A novel phenomenon called Muon was identified that unlocks Parallax's capacity.

Conclusion: Parallax successfully scales Local Linear Attention for LLM pretraining, achieving empirical improvements over standard attention. The work demonstrates the first empirical evidence of strong architecture-optimizer codesign for attention mechanisms, representing an important advancement in attention architecture research.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have become the central paradigm in artificial intelligence, yet the core computational primitive of attention has remained structurally unchanged. Local Linear Attention (LLA) is an attention mechanism derived from nonparametric statistics in the test-time regression framework. In contrast to prior research on efficient attention variants, LLA upgrades the local constant estimate in softmax attention to a local linear estimate, yielding provably superior bias-variance tradeoffs for associative memory. However, LLA has not been scaled in LLM pretraining due to computational and numerical stability concerns. We introduce Parallax, a parameterized Local Linear Attention that is scalable for LLMs. Parallax eliminates the numerical solver in LLA and learns an extra query-like projector that probes the KV covariance. We place Parallax within a family of attention mechanisms connected by the bandwidth, the probe construction and the affine structure. We propose a hardware-aware algorithm that increases the arithmetic intensity over FlashAttention, shifting attention into a more compute bound regime. Our prototype decode kernel matches or outperforms FlashAttention 2/3 across diverse batch sizes and context lengths. We pretrain Parallax at 0.6B and 1.7B scales and find consistent perplexity improvements throughout pretraining with gains that transfer to downstream benchmarks. The advantage persists under both parameter-matched and compute-matched controls, demonstrating a Pareto improvement. We perform careful pretraining ablations and identify a novel phenomenon whereby Muon unlocks the capacity of Parallax. To our knowledge, this is the first empirical demonstration of strong architecture-optimizer codesign for attention mechanisms in the architecture research literature.

</details>


### [189] [PROTOCOL: Late Interaction Retrieval for Protein Homolog Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29158)
*Gabrielle Cohn,Rohan Gumaste,Minh Hoang,Vihan Lakshman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProtoCol improves protein homology search in the "twilight zone" by using residue-level embeddings with ColBERT-style late interaction, outperforming existing methods on remote homology detection benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protein homology search becomes difficult in the "twilight zone" where global sequence similarity is weak and traditional alignment methods lose sensitivity. While protein language models offer promising representations, existing approaches that pool embeddings into single vectors may obscure important local structural information needed for remote homology detection.

Method: ProtoCol represents proteins as sets of residue embeddings using protein language models and employs ColBERT-style late interaction for comparison. It encodes proteins independently, keeps candidate representations pre-computable, and scores candidates using MaxSim over residue embeddings, enabling residue-level comparison for improved homology detection.

Result: On SCOPe superfamily and Pfam clan benchmarks, ProtoCol outperforms multiple baselines including sequence-composition methods, alignment-based approaches, pooled protein language model representations, and trained single-vector methods, demonstrating superior performance in remote homology search.

Conclusion: The success of ProtoCol supports late interaction as an effective retrieval layer for remote homology search, showing that residue-level comparison with contextual embeddings improves sensitivity in the twilight zone where traditional methods struggle.

Abstract: Protein homology search underlies function annotation, structure prediction, and evolutionary analysis, but remains challenging in the "twilight zone," where global sequence similarity is weak and classical alignment methods lose sensitivity. Protein language models provide context-aware representations that could improve alignment sensitivity in this regime. However, prior protein embedding-based retrieval pipelines often pool these representations into a single vector, potentially obscuring local motifs, domains, or conserved residues that reveal remote homology. We introduce ProtoCol, a model which represents proteins as sets of residue embeddings and uses ColBERT-style late interaction to test whether residue-level comparison improves homolog retrieval. ProtoCol encodes proteins independently, keeps candidate representations pre-computable, and scores candidates with MaxSim over residue embeddings. On SCOPe superfamily and Pfam clan benchmarks, ProtoCol outperforms sequence-composition, alignment-based, pooled PLM, and trained single-vector baselines, supporting late interaction as an effective retrieval layer for remote homology search.

</details>


### [190] [Evolutionary Refinement of Generative Graph Topologies: A Hybrid WGAN-GA Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29161)
*James Sargant,Seyedeh Ava Razi Razavi,Renata Dividino,Sheridan Houghten*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A GA-based refinement method that corrects residual structural deviations in GAN-generated graphs to better match real graph patterns


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GAN-based graph generators still exhibit noticeable deviations in structural properties like degree and spectral distributions compared to real graphs, indicating important structural properties aren't fully preserved.

Method: Apply Genetic Algorithm to refine edges of graphs produced by an existing GAN-based graph generator framework, guiding synthetic graphs toward closer agreement with real data while preserving diversity.

Result: GA refinement consistently lowers combined Maximum Mean Discrepancy compared to the base model, producing graphs that more closely match real structural patterns.

Conclusion: Evolutionary refinement is an effective and flexible way to correct residual structural deviations in GAN-based graph generators, improving their suitability for realistic graph synthesis and data augmentation.

Abstract: Generating realistic graph-structured data is challenging due to discrete connectivity, varying graph sizes, and class-specific structural patterns. Recent Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)-based graph generation methods improve edge modelling by learning connectivity and matching class-specific density distributions. However these models still exhibit noticeable deviations such as in degree and spectral distribution when compared to real graphs, indicating that important structural properties are not fully preserved. This work aims to reduce these deviations by refining the graphs produced by an existing GAN-based graph generator framework with a Genetic Algorithm (GA). In the GAN framework, the generator produces both node features and connectivity patterns, while a GNN-based critic evaluates graph realism and class consistency to ensure global structural and class alignment. Building on this foundation, we apply a GA to refine the edges of generated graphs. The refinement process guides synthetic graphs toward closer agreement with real data, while preserving diversity and novelty. Experimental results show that the GA refinement consistently lowers combined Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) compared to the base model, leading to graphs that more closely match real structural patterns. This demonstrates that evolutionary refinement is an effective and flexible way to correct residual structural deviations in GAN-based graph generators, improving their suitability for realistic graph synthesis and data augmentation.

</details>


### [191] [Probabilistic bias adjustment of seasonal forecasts using generative machine learning: A case study of Arctic sea ice predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29172)
*Parsa Gooya,Reinel Sospedra-Alfonso*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents an enhanced probabilistic post-processing framework using conditional Variational Autoencoders with a generator and CRPS loss to improve calibration, sharpness, and spatial resolution of seasonal Arctic sea ice forecasts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Seasonal climate predictions require large ensembles for uncertainty quantification, but raw forecast ensembles have biases, drift, and spatio-temporal errors that grow with lead time. Traditional post-processing methods and standard cVAEs produce blurry forecasts lacking fine-scale energy, limiting forecast utility for planning and risk management.

Method: Extended the cVAE framework by: 1) replacing the Gaussian parametrized decoder with a generator, 2) using Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) instead of Mean Square Error in the objective function, and 3) applying the method to higher resolution target datasets compared to raw forecasts.

Result: The enhanced framework produces bias-corrected forecasts with: better calibration, greater consistency with observational distribution, smaller errors than benchmark predictions, enhanced resolution of raw forecasts, improved sharpness, and better spectral power relative to standard cVAEs.

Conclusion: The proposed improvements to the cVAE-based post-processing framework successfully address limitations of standard approaches by generating well-calibrated, sharp, high-resolution seasonal sea ice predictions that better capture fine-scale features while maintaining probabilistic reliability.

Abstract: Seasonal climate predictions support planning and risk management by offering early information of the most likely-to-occur climate conditions in the coming months, and associated uncertainties. Ensemble forecasts enable this by simulating many plausible outcomes, allowing predictions to be expressed as usable probabilities. Large ensembles and high-resolution forecasts strengthen this guidance by better sampling uncertainty and capturing finer-scale processes but come with significant computational cost. Moreover, forecast ensembles drift and exhibit systematic biases and spatio-temporal errors that grow with lead time, requiring careful post-processing and calibration. A probabilistic post-processing framework based on conditional Variational Autoencoders (cVAEs) was developed at the Canadian Center for Climate Modeling and Analysis to generate large ensembles of bias adjusted seasonal predictions of Arctic sea ice. The generative model was designed to learn the observational distribution conditioned on the biased model prediction. This enables generation of arbitrarily large ensembles of well-calibrated, bias corrected forecasts with improved skill. Here, we extend this framework to address the loss of fine-scale energy and the characteristic blurriness in predictions, a known limitation of standard cVAEs. Specifically, we employ a generator in place of the Gaussian parametrized decoder in the cVAE and use Continuous Ranked Probability Score in the objective function instead of the Mean Square Error. We further use a higher resolution target dataset compared to the raw forecast. We show that the adjusted forecasts are better calibrated, more consistent with the observational distribution, and exhibit smaller errors than benchmark predictions, while also enhancing the resolution of the raw forecasts and improving sharpness and spectral power relative to the standard cVAE.

</details>


### [192] [TIMEGATE: Sustainable Time-Boxed Promotion Gates for Continual ML Adaptation Under Resource Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29183)
*Abhijit Chakrabroty,Suddhasvatta Das,Kevin A. Gary,Yash Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TIMEGATE is a policy layer that manages ML system adaptation by budgeting time, labeling, training, and evaluation resources, using a metric-availability signal to decide between partial vs. full evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As machine learning systems increasingly require continual adaptation, each re-training cycle consumes significant computational resources, annotation effort, and energy, creating a need for more efficient adaptation management.

Method: TIMEGATE introduces a policy layer that budgets four resources: time, labeling, training, and evaluation. It emits a metric-availability signal (M) that determines whether to perform partial or full evaluation during adaptation cycles.

Result: Experiments show: (1) labeling outperforms training by 2.3x on Adult dataset; (2) transfers to LLaMA-3.1-8B with QLoRA on SST-2 (accuracy improved from 0.80 to 0.96, M=1 in 35/36 runs); (3) M signal is informative but drops to 0.81 at tight thresholds; (4) 100-cycle simulation achieves 66% evaluation-compute savings without silent mis-promotions; (5) 10%-slice evaluation on LLaMA uses 89% less wall-clock time and energy.

Conclusion: TIMEGATE provides an effective framework for resource-efficient ML system adaptation, significantly reducing computational costs, energy consumption, and annotation effort while maintaining or improving model performance through intelligent resource budgeting and evaluation scheduling.

Abstract: As machine learning(ML) systems evolve to continual adaptation, each re-training cycle uses compute, annotation, and energy. We introduce TIMEGATE, a policy layer managing adaptation by budgeting time, labeling, training, and evaluation. TIMEGATE emits a metric-availability signal M for partial vs. full-evaluation decisions. We validate: (i) labeling outperforms training by 2.3x on Adult tabular; (ii) it transfers to LLaMA-3.1-8B + QLoRA on SST-2 (accuracy 0.80 to 0.96; M =1 in 35/36 runs); (iii) M is informative, 28-cell sensitivity shows M drops to 0.81 at tight thresholds; (iv) 100-cycle simulation achieves 66% evaluation-compute savings with no silent mis-promotions; (v) 10%-slice evaluation on LLaMA uses 89% less wall-clock and energy on a single H200 (ratios agree to 0.2%).

</details>


### [193] [Influence-Guided Symbolic Regression: Scientific Discovery via LLM-Driven Equation Search with Granular Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29184)
*Evgeny S. Saveliev,Samuel Holt,Nabeel Seedat,David L. Bentley,Jim Weatherall,Mihaela van der Schaar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IGSR introduces influence-guided symbolic regression that combines LLM term generation with influence score-based pruning, validated through MCTS search and demonstrated on diverse benchmarks including biological discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based symbolic regression methods are constrained by inefficient search strategies and coarse feedback signals (like global MSE) that fail to identify which equation components drive performance or cause errors, limiting their effectiveness for scientific discovery.

Method: IGSR frames equation discovery as an iterative two-step process: 1) LLM generates candidate basis functions for a linear model, 2) terms are evaluated using granular influence scores quantifying each term's marginal contribution to generalization accuracy, enabling influence-guided pruning. This is integrated into Monte Carlo Tree Search to navigate the combinatorial space while balancing exploration and exploitation.

Result: IGSR demonstrates effectiveness on diverse benchmarks including LLM-SRBench, pharmacological PKPD models, epidemiological simulation, and real-world genomic data. Notably, it identified a novel relationship between DNA methylation and RNA Polymerase II pausing in high-dimensional biological data, which was subsequently validated through wet-lab experimentation.

Conclusion: IGSR provides a more effective approach to symbolic regression by using fine-grained influence scores to guide equation discovery, enabling genuine scientific discovery as demonstrated by the biological case study where it identified a novel, experimentally validated relationship.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising avenue for scientific discovery, yet their application to symbolic regression is often constrained by inefficient search strategies and coarse feedback signals. Current methods typically guide LLMs using scalar metrics (e.g., global Mean Squared Error), which fail to identify which components of a proposed equation are driving performance or causing error. We introduce \textit{Influence-Guided Symbolic Regression} (IGSR), a method that frames equation discovery as an iterative two-step process combining diverse term generation with rigorous selection: an LLM generates candidate basis functions $ψ_j(\mathbf{x})$ for a linear model, which are then evaluated using granular influence scores $Δ_j$. These scores quantify each term's marginal contribution to generalization accuracy, enabling an influence-guided pruning process that systematically refines the model structure. Integrating this mechanism into a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) enables navigating the combinatorial search space while balancing exploration of novel functional forms with exploitation of high-influence components. We demonstrate IGSR's effectiveness on a diverse suite of benchmarks, including LLM-SRBench, pharmacological PKPD models, an epidemiological simulation, and real-world genomic data. Notably, we validate the framework's capacity for genuine discovery in a case study using a high-dimensional biological dataset, in which IGSR identified a novel relationship between DNA methylation and RNA Polymerase II pausing; a hypothesis that was subsequently supported via wet-lab experimentation.

</details>


### [194] [When RL Suppresses Its Own Vocabulary: Recovering Reasoning Diversity in Puzzle-to-Math Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29190)
*Mayug Maniparambil,Arjun Karuvally,Terrence Sejnowski,Fergal Reid*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR (Reinforcement Learning using Verifiable Rewards) trained on constraint-satisfaction puzzles transfers to math problems without seeing math in post-training data, with additional improvements from a novelty bonus that preserves exploratory reasoning primitives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to understand how and why reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards transfers across different domains, specifically from constraint-satisfaction puzzles to mathematics problems, and to analyze the emergence of this transfer through reasoning primitives.

Method: 1) Train 7B model using SFT and RL post-training only on constraint-satisfaction puzzles (no math). 2) Develop reasoning primitive-level framework with 9-class span classifier and motif extraction to analyze CoT traces. 3) Compare vanilla GSPO vs. novel method with novelty bonus using perplexity under reference model to reward diverse correct rollouts.

Result: 1) Puzzle SFT induces reasoning primitives (+7pp pass@32 on OlymMATH-Hard). 2) Vanilla GSPO composes primitives into longer compute-verify chains (+6pp). 3) Novelty bonus restores exploratory primitives (+7pp over vanilla GSPO). 4) End-to-end recipe improves hard-math capability from 16.0% to 36.0% without math in post-training.

Conclusion: RLVR can effectively transfer reasoning skills across domains when trained on constraint-satisfaction tasks, and preserving exploratory reasoning primitives through novelty bonuses is crucial for maintaining transfer capabilities during reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning using verifiable rewards (RLVR) improves LLM reasoning, but the conditions under which it transfers across domains -- and why it does so -- remain under-explored. We study cross-domain transfer in a 7B model whose SFT and RL post-training stages use only constraint-satisfaction puzzles, with no mathematics problems in the post-training data. To analyze how transfer emerges, we introduce a reasoning primitive-level framework that combines a 9-class span classifier with motif extraction, allowing us to segment chain-of-thought traces into primitive motifs and track their evolution across training stages and domains. We find that puzzle SFT induces a reasoning-primitive vocabulary, yielding a $+7$pp \texttt{pass@32} gain on OlymMATH-Hard. Vanilla GSPO then composes these primitives into longer compute-verify chains, adding a further $+6$pp. However, this RL stage also suppresses exploratory primitives such as \textit{hypothesize} and \textit{backtrack}. To address this, we introduce a novelty bonus that rewards diverse correct rollouts, using perplexity under the reference model as a signal. This restores recovery primitives during RL and adds a further $+7$pp \texttt{pass@32} relative to vanilla GSPO. Finally, the end-to-end recipe raises the hard-math capability ceiling from $16.0\%$ at the OLMo3-7B-Instruct-SFT base to $36.0\%$, without adding any mathematics problems during the SFT or RL stages.

</details>


### [195] [Stochastic Lifting for Generating Trajectories of Stochastic Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29194)
*Jules Berman,Tobias Blickhan,Benjamin Peherstorfer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Stochastic Lifting: A method that attaches random labels to state transitions during training to learn stochastic dynamics, enabling generation of diverse trajectories with single network evaluations per time step.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many stochastic physical systems evolve smoothly but stochastically over time, with distributions changing regularly across time steps. Traditional methods often collapse to mean predictions when learning from finite samples, failing to capture the inherent randomness in state transitions.

Method: Attach independent, high-dimensional random labels to each state transition in training data, then fit a transition map from current state + label to next state using standard regression loss. Labels serve as auxiliary coordinates to represent multiple plausible next states from similar current states.

Result: The method avoids collapse to mean predictions in finite-sample regime by using random labels as auxiliary coordinates. At inference, fresh labels are sampled at each time step and the learned map is rolled forward autoregressively, generating diverse trajectories with only one network evaluation per time step.

Conclusion: Stochastic Lifting provides an effective framework for learning and generating stochastic dynamics by leveraging auxiliary random coordinates, enabling efficient simulation of diverse trajectories while avoiding the mean collapse problem common in finite-sample learning scenarios.

Abstract: Many stochastic physical systems evolve smoothly over time in the sense that the distribution of states changes regularly across time steps. The transition from current state to the next state can often be modeled as the combination of a smooth map and an explicit source of randomness. Stochastic Lifting exploits this structure by attaching an independent, high-dimensional random label to each state transition in the training data and fitting a transition map from the current state and label to the next state using a standard regression loss. The labels act as auxiliary coordinates that let the model represent multiple plausible next states from similar current states, avoiding collapse to a mean prediction in the finite-sample size regime. At inference, fresh labels are sampled at each time step and the learned map is rolled forward autoregressively, generating diverse trajectories with a single network evaluation per time step.

</details>


### [196] [Auditing Training Data in Generative Music Models via Black-Box Membership Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29202)
*Yi Chen Liu,Jiawei Yu,Kexin Cao,Syed Irfan Ali Meerza,Trishika Movva,Jian Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a black-box membership inference method for generative music models that can determine if a music sample was used in training by analyzing semantic and structural alignment between the candidate sample and model's caption-conditioned generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from growing concerns about data provenance, consent, and training transparency in text-to-music generation models. These models are trained on large-scale corpora with little disclosure, leaving no practical way to verify if a particular audio sample was included in training.

Method: The method uses black-box membership inference where the key insight is that training membership induces stronger semantic and structural alignment between a candidate sample and the model's caption-conditioned generation. They query the target model with the associated caption, measure relationships in a learned feature space, construct paired examples from shadow models, and train a music auditor to classify membership based on alignment patterns.

Result: Across multiple state-of-the-art music generators, the method achieves up to 98.6% accuracy, with false-positive and false-negative rates as low as 1.9% and 1.0%, demonstrating reliable training-data auditing in realistic deployment scenarios.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that reliable training-data auditing for generative music models is feasible in fully black-box settings without access to model parameters or training metadata, addressing important concerns about data provenance and consent in music generation systems.

Abstract: Recent advances in text-to-music generation enable high-fidelity synthesis of structured musical audio, raising growing concerns about data provenance, consent, and training transparency. These models are typically trained on large-scale corpora with little disclosure, leaving no practical mechanism to verify whether a particular audio sample was included in training. In this paper, we investigate black-box membership inference for generative music models, aiming to determine whether a candidate music sample was used during training, given only query access to the deployed system. Our key insight is that training membership induces systematically stronger semantic and structural alignment between a candidate sample and the model's generation conditioned on its caption. We query the target model with the associated caption and measure the relationship between the candidate audio and the generated output in a learned feature space. To capture features that separate members from non-members, we construct paired examples consisting of each track and its caption-conditioned generation from shadow models, and train a music auditor to classify membership. The auditor captures alignment patterns characteristic of training membership and generalizes to unseen target models in a fully black-box setting without access to model parameters or training metadata. Across multiple state-of-the-art music generators, our method achieves up to 98.6% accuracy, with false-positive and false-negative rates as low as 1.9% and 1.0%, demonstrating that reliable training-data auditing is feasible in realistic deployment scenarios.

</details>


### [197] [Inferring the Size of Large Language Models From Popular Text Memorization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29223)
*Ivica Nikolic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A black-box method to estimate LLM parameter counts from text outputs alone by measuring memorization of widely-circulated texts, providing lower bounds on model size even for closed-source models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many LLM developers withhold parameter counts, leaving model size undisclosed despite being crucial for interpreting capabilities and costs. There's a need for methods to estimate model size without access to internal details.

Method: Black-box approach using memorization signals: measure how accurately models predict next words in widely-circulated texts (classical literature, religious texts, etc.) across varying fragment lengths. Aggregate signals into accuracy profile vectors, then apply two methods: 1) pairwise statistical test to determine which model is larger, and 2) PCA-based scaling-law estimator to map aggregated signal to parameter count.

Result: The methods produce accurate and reliable lower bounds on open-weight models. When applied to closed-weight models, they reveal industry scaling strategies: some show large parameter growth across generations, others operate under strict parameter ceilings, recovering internal product hierarchies.

Conclusion: Hidden design choices in LLM development can be systematically probed even under strict API limitations. The approach provides a practical way to infer model size from black-box access alone, addressing the information asymmetry in commercial LLM deployment.

Abstract: The parameter counts of the most widely used large language models (LLMs) are often withheld by their developers, leaving model size -- a primary reference point for interpreting capabilities and costs -- largely undisclosed. We propose a black-box method to infer conservative lower bounds on LLM size from generated text outputs alone, requiring nothing beyond the ability to submit text fragments and observe next-token predictions. Our approach is grounded in a key observation: popular, widely-circulated texts -- such as classical literature, religious texts, and foundational documents -- are present in virtually every large-scale pretraining corpus, and how accurately a model predicts the next word across text fragments of varying length is a reliable signal of how much it has memorized them, which in turn is fundamentally limited by its total parameter count. We aggregate this memorization signal across a diverse corpus of texts and fragment lengths into a single accuracy profile vector per model, and build two complementary inference methods on top of it: a pairwise statistical test that determines which of two models is larger, and a scaling-law estimator that extracts a one-dimensional latent index from these vectors via Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to map the aggregated signal to a parameter count. Validated on a broad set of open-weight models, both methods produce accurate and reliable lower bounds. When applied to popular closed-weight models, our framework recovers internal product hierarchies and reveals a clear divergence in industry scaling strategies: while some developers yield significantly higher bounds indicative of large generational parameter growth, others operate under strict parameter ceilings, demonstrating that hidden design choices can be systematically probed even under strict API limitations.

</details>


### [198] [Traditional machine learning vs. deep learning from dynamic graph representations of proteins' 3D folds in the task of protein structure classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29228)
*Aydin Wells,Francis A. Gatsi,Aaron Striegel,Tijana Milenković*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Traditional machine learning (handcrafted features + off-the-shelf classifier) and deep learning (automatic feature learning) on dynamic protein structure networks perform similarly in protein structure classification, but DL is significantly slower.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous work showed that dynamic protein structure networks (PSNs) outperform static PSNs, sequence features, and direct 3D structural features in protein structure classification. The authors want to determine if deep learning's automatic feature extraction can further improve performance over traditional machine learning's handcrafted features.

Method: The authors evaluate traditional ML (manual feature engineering + off-the-shelf classifier) versus deep learning (automatic feature learning from dynamic PSNs) on protein structure classification. They test on 72 datasets comprising ~44,000 CATH- or SCOPe-labeled dynamic protein structure networks.

Result: Traditional ML and DL achieve similar classification accuracy on most datasets - they are "close to tied" for a large majority. However, DL is significantly slower, being on average 10+ times slower than traditional ML approaches.

Conclusion: Deep learning doesn't provide clear accuracy advantages over traditional machine learning for dynamic PSN-based protein structure classification, while being much more computationally expensive. This study represents the first comparison of traditional ML vs DL in this specific task domain.

Abstract: Protein structure classification (PSC) uses supervised learning to predict a protein's CATH/SCOP(e) class from the protein's sequence or 3D structural feature(s). We already modeled 3D structures as (static) protein structure networks (PSNs), demonstrating the competitiveness of PSN-based features to sequence or direct (i.e. non-network) 3D structural features in the PSC task. More recently, we demonstrated the power of features extracted from dynamic PSNs over features extracted from static PSNs (and thus by transitivity over sequence and direct 3D structural features) in the same task. That dynamic PSN approach used traditional machine learning (ML), combining manual (pre-engineered) features with an off-the-shelf classifier. Here, we evaluate whether automatic deep learning (DL) from the dynamic PSNs yields improvements. Our evaluation on 72 datasets spanning ~44,000 CATH- or SCOPe-labeled dynamic PSNs reveals that in terms of PSC accuracy, traditional ML and DL are (close to) tied for a large majority of the datasets, while DL is on average 10+ times slower. We are the first to evaluate traditional ML vs. DL in the dynamic PSN-based PSC task.

</details>


### [199] [BlockBatch: Multi-Scale Consensus Decoding for Efficient Diffusion Language Model Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29233)
*Xiaoyou Wu,Cheng-Jhih Shih,Binfei Ji,Yong Liu,Yingyan,Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BlockBatch is a training-free inference framework that runs multiple block-size branches in parallel for diffusion language models, achieving 26.6% reduction in denoising steps and 1.33× speedup while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion language models face a granularity trade-off in block-wise inference: small blocks preserve local conditioning but require many steps, while large blocks offer parallelism but risk premature commitments and cache errors. Existing methods use single block sizes per request, missing opportunities from complementary block sizes.

Method: BlockBatch executes multiple block-size branches for the same request in a batched forward pass, coordinating them through confidence-gated token merging, leader-based synchronization, and periodic full-sequence refreshes to maintain global KV-cache consistency.

Result: Across 3 dLLMs and 4 datasets, BlockBatch reduces denoising non-feedforward evaluations by 26.6% on average and achieves 1.33× average end-to-end speedup over Fast-dLLM while preserving accuracy.

Conclusion: Block-size diversity represents a practical and previously underexplored axis for branch-parallel dLLM inference, offering significant efficiency gains without compromising accuracy.

Abstract: Diffusion language models (dLLMs) generate text by iteratively denoising multiple token positions in parallel, offering an attractive alternative to strictly autoregressive decoding. In practice, however, block-wise dLLM inference exposes a difficult granularity trade-off: small blocks preserve local conditioning but require many denoising steps, whereas large blocks expose more parallelism but can make premature commitments and accumulate cache error. Existing acceleration methods typically choose a single block size per request, leaving the complementarity among block sizes unused. We show that block size itself is a useful branching dimension. Different block sizes induce related but non-identical KV-cache trajectories: branches often share an initial prefix, bifurcate at semantically decisive positions, and later agree on syntactically lightweight tokens. Motivated by this structure, we propose BlockBatch, a training-free online inference framework that executes multiple block-size branches for the same request inside a batched forward pass. BlockBatch coordinates these branches through confidence-gated token merging, leader-based synchronization, and periodic full-sequence refreshes that re-anchor local block updates to a globally consistent KV state. Across 3 representative dLLMs and 4 datasets, BlockBatch reduces denoising NFEs by 26.6\% on average and achieves a 1.33$\times$ average end-to-end speedup over Fast-dLLM while preserving accuracy. These results identify block-size diversity as a practical and previously underexplored axis for branch-parallel dLLM inference.

</details>


### [200] [SigmaMedStat: Temporal Signal Modeling for ICU False Alarm Reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29236)
*Arunkumar Ramachandran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SigmaMedStat is a machine learning system using temporal chunking with EfficientNet-B0 and LSTM to filter ICU alarm signals, achieving 0.822 AUC and reducing clinically irrelevant alarm fatigue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Alarm fatigue in ICUs is a critical patient safety crisis where 350+ daily alarms per patient lead to 72-99% clinically irrelevant notifications, causing staff desensitization and increased risk of missed true emergencies.

Method: Temporal framework splitting 60-second recordings into six 10-second chunks, generating CWT scalograms per chunk, encoding with shared EfficientNet-B0, and processing feature sequences through a two-layer LSTM network for classification.

Result: Five-fold cross-validation shows mean AUC of 0.822 ± 0.016, significantly outperforming static baseline (AUC 0.641). Best performance on Ventricular Flutter (AUC 0.820), weakest on Asystole (AUC 0.722). Identified 65 false negatives and 85 high-confidence misclassifications.

Conclusion: Temporal modeling with chunking and multi-channel fusion effectively improves ICU alarm classification, demonstrating potential to reduce alarm fatigue by filtering non-actionable alarms while maintaining sensitivity to true emergencies.

Abstract: Alarm fatigue in intensive care units (ICUs) is a well documented patient safety crisis. Clinical monitors generate 350 or more alarms per patient per day, out of which 72-99% are clinically irrelevant. Staff desensitization to non-actionable alarms increases the risk of missed true emergencies. This paper presents SigmaMedStat, a machine learning system that evaluates the trustworthiness of physiological alarm signals before clinical action is taken. Four approaches were evaluated on the PhysioNet/Computing in Cardiology Challenge 2015 dataset of 498 four-channel ICU alarm recordings. Primary contribution is a temporal modeling framework that splits each 60 second recording into six consecutive 10-second chunks, and this in turn generates Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) scalograms per chunk, encodes each chunk with a shared EfficientNet-B0 encoder, and passes the resulting feature sequence to a two-layer Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. Five-fold stratified cross-validation yields a mean AUC of 0.822 +/- 0.016 (95% CI: [0.790,0.853]), compared to 0.641 for a static EfficientNet baseline trained on the full 60-second window. Ablation studies confirm that temporal chunking and multi-channel signal fusion both contribute independently to classification performance. Per-alarm type analysis reveals that Ventricular Flutter is the most accurately classified alarm type (AUC 0.820) while Asystole remains the hardest (AUC 0.722). Error analysis identifies 65 false negatives and 85 high-confidence misclassifications as the primary failure modes. All code and results are publicly available at https://github.com/Arun-K-Ram/sigmamedstat.

</details>


### [201] [KLAS: Using Similarity to Stitch Neural Networks for Improved Accuracy-Efficiency Tradeoffs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29259)
*Debopam Sanyal,Anantharaman Iyer,Alind Khare,Trisha Jain,Akshay Jajoo,Myungjin Lee,Clayton Kerce,Alexey Tumanov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KLAS is a novel stitch selection framework that uses KL divergence between intermediate representations to automatically select optimal stitching configurations between pretrained models, improving accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs without additional finetuning cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing stitching approaches for interpolating accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs often yield suboptimal results and lack generalizability because they rely on heuristics rather than systematically capturing the similarity between pretrained models being stitched. This limits their effectiveness in producing cost-effective model variants for various deployment targets.

Method: KLAS introduces a stitch selection framework that leverages KL divergence between intermediate representations of pretrained models to automatically identify promising stitch configurations. It efficiently searches through O(k²n²) possible binary stitches (for k models of depth n) to find optimal interpolation points along the accuracy-efficiency spectrum.

Result: KLAS improves the accuracy-efficiency curve of stitched models while using the same finetuning cost as baselines. Specifically, it achieves up to 1.21% higher ImageNet-1K top-1 accuracy at the same computational cost, or maintains accuracy with a 1.33× reduction in FLOPs.

Conclusion: The KL divergence between intermediate representations effectively captures model similarity for stitch selection, enabling automated and generalizable stitch configuration across model families. KLAS provides better accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs for flexible model deployment.

Abstract: Given the wide range of deployment targets, flexible model selection is essential for optimizing performance within a given compute budget. Recent work demonstrates that stitching pretrained models within a model family enables cost-effective interpolation of the accuracy-efficiency tradeoff space. Stitching transforms intermediate activations from one pretrained model into another, producing a new interpolated stitched network. Such networks provide a pool of deployment options along the accuracy-efficiency spectrum. However, existing stitching approaches often yield suboptimal tradeoffs and lack generalizability, as they primarily rely on heuristics to select stitch configurations. We argue that constructing improved accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs requires explicitly capturing and leveraging the similarity between pretrained models being stitched. To this end, we introduce KLAS, a novel stitch selection framework that automates and generalizes stitch selection across model families by leveraging KL divergence between intermediate representations. KLAS identifies the most promising binary stitches from the $O(k^2n^2)$ possibilities for $k$ pretrained models of depth $n$. Through comprehensive experiments, we demonstrate that KLAS improves the accuracy-efficiency curve of stitched models at the same finetuning cost as baselines. KLAS achieves up to $1.21\%$ higher ImageNet-1K top-1 accuracy at the same computational cost, or maintains accuracy with a $1.33\times$ reduction in FLOPs.

</details>


### [202] [Robust Frequency-Calibrated Virtual EEG Channel Generation from Four Frontal Electrodes for Wearable EEG Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29263)
*Minghao Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FAVC-Net generates 13 additional EEG channels from 4 frontal electrodes using frequency-calibrated virtual-channel network, achieving better spectral fidelity than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-channel wearable EEG (4 frontal electrodes) provides only sparse and spatially biased view of brain activity, limiting monitoring capabilities. Need to generate unmeasured channels for more comprehensive distributed scalp coverage.

Method: FAVC-Net combines shared multi-scale source encoding, source-state embeddings, target-conditioned signed source-block mixing, GATv2-based attention refinement, attention-consistent skip fusion, and weak Welch power spectral density calibration to generate 13 virtual EEG channels from 4 frontal electrodes (Fp1, Fp2, F7, F8).

Result: On PRED+CT dataset, achieved best joint waveform-spectral performance among neural and interpolation baselines. Reduced log-spectral distance by 30.09% and PSD KL divergence by 37.98% relative to strongest non-FAVC comparator. Maintained spectral fidelity under source perturbations and resisted spectral collapse.

Conclusion: Virtual EEG channel generation should be treated as a dual-domain augmentation problem. Generated posterior/parietal channels should be interpreted as frequency-calibrated representations from sparse frontal measurements rather than independent physical recordings.

Abstract: Low-channel wearable electroencephalography (EEG) is attractive for long-term monitoring, but four frontal electrodes provide only a sparse and spatially biased view of distributed scalp activity. We present FAVC-Net, a compact frequency-calibrated virtual-channel network that generates 13 unmeasured EEG channels from Fp1, Fp2, F7, and F8. The model combines shared multi-scale source encoding, source-state embeddings, target-conditioned signed source-block mixing, GATv2-based attention refinement, attention-consistent skip fusion, and weak Welch power spectral density calibration. Rather than treating sparse-to-dense EEG generation as a purely waveform-matching task, the framework jointly emphasizes amplitude fidelity, spectral allocation, channel-frequency texture, and robustness to corrupted wearable inputs. On the PRED+CT dataset, FAVC-Net achieved the best joint waveform-spectral operating point among neural and interpolation baselines. Its time-domain gains were modest, whereas log-spectral distance and PSD KL divergence were reduced by 30.09% and 37.98% relative to the strongest non-FAVC comparator. Under wearable-like source perturbations, the model preserved spectral fidelity and resisted spectral collapse. These results support virtual EEG channel generation as a dual-domain augmentation problem, while emphasizing that generated posterior and parietal channels should be interpreted as frequency-calibrated representations derived from sparse frontal measurements rather than as independent physical recordings.

</details>


### [203] [Causal Label Recovery in Payment Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29272)
*Gaurav Dhama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Sequential Triply Robust (STR) estimator that corrects for four systematic biases in fraud detection labels (authorization, reporting, delay, corruption), achieving the semiparametric efficiency bound while enabling training on fresh data rather than waiting months for chargebacks to mature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fraud detection models suffer from systematically biased labels due to three sequential gates (authorization declines, issuer reporting omissions, and delay in chargeback arrival) plus corruption from first-party misuse or issuer misclassification, creating a minimax lower bound on detection performance established in a companion paper.

Method: Develops Sequential Triply Robust (STR) estimator formalizing the observation pipeline as sequential missing-data problem with three propensity stages and corruption layer. Uses noise-rate-adjusted pseudo-labels for corruption correction, empirical Bayes shrinkage to stabilize weights for small issuers, plug-in variance estimator for confidence intervals, and Bernstein concentration inequality for finite-sample guarantees.

Result: STR achieves semiparametric efficiency bound (no estimator can have lower asymptotic variance), is sequentially triply robust (at each gate, consistency requires only propensity OR outcome model to be correct), provably dominates naive chargeback-based training in MSE for any sample size, and permits training on data days old rather than months old by deriving optimal training delay.

Conclusion: The STR estimator can achieve the minimax lower bound established in prior work, overcoming four systematic impairments in fraud detection labels while enabling substantially fresher model training and providing theoretical guarantees of superiority over conventional approaches.

Abstract: Fraud detection models in payment networks train on chargeback labels that are systematically biased. Every label must survive three sequential gates: authorization (declined transactions generate no labels), issuer reporting (unreported fraud is invisible), and delay (pending chargebacks are missing at training time). Labels that do arrive may be corrupted by first-party misuse or issuer misclassification. A companion paper [arXiv:2605.27557] proved that these four impairments impose a minimax lower bound on detection performance. This paper asks: can that bound be achieved?
  We formalize the observation pipeline as a sequential missing-data problem with three propensity stages and a corruption layer, and construct the Sequential Triply Robust (STR) estimator. The STR corrects for all four impairments simultaneously and achieves the semiparametric efficiency bound -- no estimator can have lower asymptotic variance. It is sequentially triply robust: at each gate, consistency requires only that either the propensity model or the outcome regression is correctly specified, not both. We provide corruption correction via noise-rate-adjusted pseudo-labels, empirical Bayes shrinkage to stabilize inverse-propensity weights for small issuers, a plug-in variance estimator yielding valid confidence intervals, and a Bernstein concentration inequality for finite-sample guarantees.
  On the operational side, we derive the optimal training delay -- the maturity window that minimizes the sum of label-quality loss and model staleness -- and prove that the STR permits training on data that is days old rather than months old, decoupling model freshness from the chargeback maturity cycle. The STR provably dominates naive chargeback-based training in mean squared error for any sample size.

</details>


### [204] [A Theoretical and Experimental Study of a Novel Adaptive Learning Algorithm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29273)
*Sakshi Kumari,Shyam Kumar M,Sushmitha P*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes C-Adam, a new adaptive optimizer variant that addresses convergence issues in existing methods like Adam and AMSGrad using a line of sight approach, with theoretical convergence proof and experimental validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive learning rate optimizers like Adam are widely used but don't guarantee convergence. While AMSGrad was introduced to address non-convergence issues, limitations still exist in current adaptive optimization methods that need to be addressed.

Method: The paper critically reviews popular adaptive optimization methods (Adam and AMSGrad), then proposes a new optimizer variant called C-Adam based on the line of sight approach. The method includes providing theoretical proof for convergence.

Result: C-Adam optimizer is developed and validated through numerous real-life numerical experiments, demonstrating improved performance compared to existing adaptive optimization methods.

Conclusion: The proposed C-Adam optimizer successfully addresses convergence limitations of existing adaptive methods while maintaining computational efficiency, providing a theoretically sound and practically effective optimization solution for machine learning applications.

Abstract: A crucial component of machine learning algorithms is minimizing loss functions with less computational cost and less oscillations. While adaptive learning rate-based optimizers have been widely used for real-world tasks, they do not guarantee convergence, which is why AMSGrad was later introduced to investigate the non-convergence behaviour of Adam. In this paper, popular adaptive optimization methods like Adam and AMSGrad are critically reviewed with an emphasis on their fundamental design concepts. To address limitations of the above mentioned optimizers, a new optimizer variant, C-Adam, is proposed based on the line of sight approach. A theoretical proof for convergence is also provided and the optimizer is validated through a number of real-life based numerical experiments.

</details>


### [205] [LoopFM: Learning frOm HistOrical RePresentations of Foundation Model for Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29280)
*Shali Jiang,Hua Zheng,Boyang Liu,Laming Chen,Kenny Lov,Chuanqi Xu,Lisang Ding,Qinghai Zhou,Can Cui,Xiaolong Liu,Xiaoyi Liu,Yasmine Badr,Xin Xu,Jiyan Yang,Ellie Dingqiao Wen,Gerard Jonathan Mugisha Akkerhuis,Chenxiao Guan,Rong Jin,Ruichao Qiu,Xian Chen,Shifu Xu,Zhehui Zhou,Ping Chen,Rui Yang,Haicheng Chen,Xiangge Meng,Song Zhou,Dharak Kharod,Shuyu Xu,Qiang Jin,Qiao Yang,Wankun Zhu,Qin Huang,Yuzhen Huang,Darren Liu,Parish Aggarwal,Hui Zhou,Erzhuo Wang,Shuo Chang,Xiaorui Gan,Wenlin Chen,Santanu Kolay,Huayu Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoopFM addresses the diminishing knowledge transfer in distillation by using FM's historical intermediate embeddings as structured input features for compact models, enabling high-bandwidth knowledge transfer without real-time FM inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional knowledge distillation suffers from diminishing transfer ratio because it only transfers a single scalar prediction from foundation models to vertical models. This single scalar cannot convey the rich intermediate knowledge that larger foundation models learn, creating a bottleneck in knowledge transfer efficiency.

Method: LoopFM structures foundation model intermediate embeddings as input features (like user history sequences) for downstream vertical models. This creates a high-bandwidth transfer channel without requiring real-time FM inference during serving or architectural coupling between FM and VM. The method includes a theoretical framework with gain decomposition and transfer-ratio analysis.

Result: On three public benchmarks: strong AUC improvements (e.g., 6%+ on TaobaoAd) and complementary knowledge transfer capability with KD. On industrial-scale systems (billions of examples, trillion-parameter FMs): approximately doubles the knowledge transfer ratio on top of KD, delivering +0.5% conversion improvement in Y1H1, and +1.03% and +1.22% conversion improvements from two individual launches in Y1H2.

Conclusion: LoopFM effectively overcomes the bottleneck of traditional knowledge distillation by enabling high-bandwidth knowledge transfer through structured intermediate embeddings, significantly improving knowledge transfer ratios and delivering substantial performance gains in both academic benchmarks and large-scale industrial applications.

Abstract: Knowledge distillation (KD) transfers a single scalar prediction from a large foundation model (FM) to compact vertical models (VMs), suffering from diminishing transfer ratio -- the fraction of FM improvement captured by the VM -- as a single scalar cannot convey the rich intermediate knowledge that larger FMs learn. To address this bottleneck, we propose LoopFM (Learning frOm HistOrical ReP*resentations of FM), a framework that opens a high-bandwidth transfer channel by structuring FM intermediate embeddings as input features (e.g., user history sequence) for downstream VMs, without requiring real-time FM inference at serving and architectural coupling between FM and VM. We provide a theoretical framework for LoopFM with a gain decomposition and transfer-ratio analysis. On three public benchmarks, LoopFM demonstrates strong AUC improvements (e.g., 6\%+ on TaobaoAd) and complementary knowledge transfer capability with KD. On industrial-scale systems (billions of examples, trillion-parameter FMs), LoopFM approximately doubles the knowledge transfer ratio on top of KD, delivering a +0.5\% conversion improvement in Y1H1, and a +1.03\% and +1.22\% conversion improvement from two individual launches respectively in Y1H2.

</details>


### [206] [Do Physics Foundation Models Learn Generalizable Physics? A Bias-Aware Benchmark Across Physical Regimes and Distribution Shifts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29283)
*Mengdi Chu,Yang Liu,Ayan Biswas,Han-Wei Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Physics foundation models aren't truly universal - they perform well only under specific conditions, and scaling or more data won't fix their fundamental biases across different physical regimes and distribution shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current physics foundation models claim general spatiotemporal forecasting ability, but evaluations collapse performance into single average scores under fixed training distributions. This makes it hard to determine if models actually learn generalizable physical dynamics or just perform well under specific settings.

Method: Constructed a comprehensive benchmark with 8 physical dynamics, 3 training-data mixtures, and 25 test regimes covering in-distribution, distribution-shift, and out-of-distribution settings. Evaluated 5 physics foundation model architectures with 4 variants each (scratch + three pretrained sizes), resulting in 60,000 measurements.

Result: Current physics foundation models behave as conditional rather than universal generalists - their generality depends on physical regime, temporal scale, initial conditions, pretraining, model size, and architecture. Improving training data only partially mitigates this limitation, and pretraining/scaling don't reliably remove ability biases.

Conclusion: Improving physics foundation models requires moving beyond scaling models or expanding data, toward learning mechanisms that better capture transferable physical knowledge across regimes, temporal scales, and distribution shifts.

Abstract: Recent physics foundation models claim general spatiotemporal forecasting ability, yet their evaluations often collapse performance into a single average score under a fixed training distribution. This makes it difficult to determine whether a model has learned generalizable physical dynamics or only performs well under particular settings. We construct a benchmark with 8 physical dynamics, 3 training-data mixtures, and 25 test regimes induced by dynamic-scale and initial-condition complexity shifts, covering in-distribution, distribution-shift, and out-of-distribution settings. We evaluate five physics foundation model architectures and four model variants per architecture (scratch and three pretrained sizes), resulting in 60,000 measurements.
