Our results show that current physics foundation models behave as conditional rather than universal generalists: their generality depends on the physical regime, temporal scale, initial-condition setting, pretraining, model size, and architecture. Improving the training data distribution only partially mitigates this limitation. Pretraining and scaling are also unable to reliably remove their ability biases. We argue that improving physics foundation models requires moving beyond scaling models or expanding data, toward learning mechanisms that better capture transferable physical knowledge across regimes, temporal scales, and distribution shifts.

</details>


### [207] [NeuroEdge: Real-Time Hand Gesture Recognition with High-Density EMG Using Deep Learning at the Edge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29326)
*Peter Chudinov,Zhenyu Lin,Jay Motamarry,Srihita Panati,Xiaorong Zhang,Zhuwei Qin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NeuroEdge is a real-time HD-EMG-based neural-machine interface system that performs gesture recognition entirely on resource-constrained microcontrollers, achieving 90% accuracy with 83ms latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While deep learning approaches show high accuracy for EMG-based gesture recognition, their deployment on embedded hardware remains challenging due to computational and memory constraints, creating a need for efficient edge solutions.

Method: System features two modules: HD-EMG StreamBridge (wireless interface from amplifier to ESP32) and EdgeDL Inference Engine (lightweight DL framework on Sony Spresense). Uses compact 1D CNN optimized for embedded inference, pipelined architecture with DMA and SPI burst communication for low-latency data transfer.

Result: Achieves 90% classification accuracy across seven hand gestures with total average latency of 83 ms using 192 channels of HD-EMG recorded from the forearm.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of deploying complex HD-EMG-based gesture recognition on microcontroller-based edge devices, bridging gap between high-resolution biosignal acquisition and deep learning-based embedded inference for next-generation NMIs.

Abstract: High-density electromyography (HD-EMG) has emerged as a powerful modality for decoding fine-grained neuromuscular activity, enabling real-time neural-machine interfaces (NMIs) for applications such as prosthetic control, rehabilitation, and augmented interaction. While deep learning approaches such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs)have demonstrated high classification accuracy for EMG-based gesture recognition, their deployment on embedded hardware remains a major challenge due to computational and memory constraints. This paper presents NeuroEdge, a real-time HD EMG-based NMI system that performs gesture recognition entirely on resource-constrained microcontrollers. The system features two custom-designed modules: the HD-EMG StreamBridge, a wireless communication interface that streams raw HD-EMG data from a Quattrocento amplifier to an ESP32 microcontroller; and the EdgeDL Inference Engine, a lightweight deep learning framework executing on a Sony Spresense microcontroller. A compact 1-dimensional CNN optimized for embedded inference processes, sliding windows of EMG data in real time. Data streaming and inference are pipelined and synchronized through an architecture that utilizes Direct Memory Access (DMA) for data transfer and Serial Peripheral Interface (SPI) burst communication between the ESP32 and Spresense, ensuring low-latency performance. Experimental results show that NeuroEdge achieves a real-time classification accuracy of 90% across seven hand gestures, with a total average latency of 83 ms using 192 channels of HD-EMG recorded from the forearm. Our system demonstrates the feasibility of deploying complex HD-EMG-based gesture recognition on microcontroller-based edge devices, bridging the gap between high-resolution biosignal acquisition and deep learning-based embedded inference for next-generation NMIs.

</details>


### [208] [Attention as In-Context Empirical Bayes: A Two-Stage View via Particle Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29351)
*Matthew Smart,Soumya Ganguly,Nilava Metya,Alexandre V. Morozov,Anirvan M. Sengupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Attention-only transformers under all-token corruption can be interpreted as two-stage empirical Bayes: depth refines context distribution via particle dynamics, while skip connections use noisy input as queries for posterior inference, revealing separate roles for depth and attention residuals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the statistical mechanisms behind attention-only transformers, particularly how they perform in-context learning and inference without explicit density modeling. The paper aims to provide a principled interpretation of attention mechanisms as empirical Bayesian inference.

Method: Studies minimal attention-only transformers under all-token corruption using a two-stage empirical Bayes framework. Stage 1 uses depth to refine the empirical distribution through particle dynamics, while Stage 2 uses long-range skip connections to carry noisy input as queries for posterior inference. Analyzes fixed kernel bandwidth and finite integration horizon without explicit noise schedules.

Result: Shows that effective denoising emerges without explicit noise schedules, with fixed kernel bandwidth and finite integration horizon sufficient, yielding a principled depth-noise relationship. Establishes posterior-mean recovery guarantee for well-behaved priors where empirical estimator converges to Bayes-optimal predictor asymptotically. Connects these dynamics to reverse-diffusion limits.

Conclusion: Provides a statistical interpretation of attention as in-context inference via sample-based posterior estimation, without explicit density modeling. Reveals distinct statistical roles for depth (refining context distribution) and attention residuals (carrying noisy queries), offering theoretical grounding for understanding transformer behavior.

Abstract: We study minimal attention-only transformers under all-token corruption and show they admit a two-stage empirical Bayes interpretation. A single attention step computes a kernel-weighted posterior mean with respect to the empirical distribution defined by the context. Depth refines this distribution through particle dynamics (Stage 1), while a long-range skip-connection carries the noisy input as a query for posterior inference (Stage 2), revealing distinct statistical roles for depth and attention residuals. The framework isolates a minimal setting in which the context itself induces a depth-dependent energy landscape governing in-context inference. We show that effective denoising can emerge without an explicit noise schedule: a fixed kernel bandwidth and finite integration horizon suffice, yielding a principled depth-noise relationship. We further establish a posterior-mean recovery guarantee for a class of well-behaved priors, where the empirical estimator converges to the Bayes-optimal predictor under asymptotic conditions. Connecting these dynamics to reverse-diffusion limits, our results provide a statistical interpretation of attention as in-context inference via sample-based posterior estimation, without explicit density modeling.

</details>


### [209] [Neural-Behavioral Representation of Natural Whole-body Movement in Monkeys](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29355)
*Jieshi He,Puzhe Li,Yanan Sui,Mu-ming Poo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Researchers developed a framework to decode natural whole-body movements in freely moving monkeys using large-scale epidural cortical signals and motion capture data, achieving realistic movement reconstruction without explicit physical constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous motor decoding studies were limited by focusing on constrained tasks and limited limb movements due to lack of diverse movement data and inaccessibility of large-scale neural representation of whole-body kinematics in primates.

Method: Combined large-scale epidural cortical signals from distributed sensory- and motor-related areas with synchronized multi-view motion capture using a custom data collection platform. Used an autoregressive encoder-decoder model to reconstruct whole-body kinematics and learn a compact behavior prior.

Result: The model decoded accurate and realistic whole-body movement conditioned on neural signals without explicit physical constraints, successfully reconstructing natural whole-body kinematics in freely moving monkeys.

Conclusion: This work provides a novel proof-of-concept approach for decoding natural whole-body movements in primates using large-scale intracranial neural activity, advancing our understanding of cortical representation of complex behaviors.

Abstract: Understanding how cortical activity represents natural whole-body behaviors in primates remains challenging. Limited by the diversity of movements and inaccessibility of large-scale neural representation of whole-body kinematics, previous motor decoding studies focused on constrained tasks and limited limb movements. Here, we present a neural-behavioral recording and modeling framework for freely moving monkeys, combining large-scale epidural cortical signals from distributed sensory- and motor-related areas with synchronized multi-view motion capture through a custom-made data collection platform. We reconstructed whole-body monkey kinematics and learned a compact behavior prior using an autoregressive encoder-decoder model. Conditioned on neural signals, the model decoded accurate and realistic whole-body movement without explicit physical constraints. Our results provide a novel proof-of-concept approach for decoding natural whole-body movements in primates using large-scale intracranial neural activity.

</details>


### [210] [Solving Integer Linear Programming with Parallel Tempering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29366)
*Kyuil Sim,Sanghyeok Choi,Jinkyoo Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A solver-free sampling-based optimization framework for Integer Linear Programming that directly explores discrete feasible regions without training or external solvers, achieving competitive performance with classical solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing learning-based approaches for ILP suffer from poor generalization to out-of-distribution instances and inherent dependence on external solvers. There's a need for methods that can directly solve ILPs without these limitations.

Method: Proposes a sampling-based optimization framework using Locally-Balanced Proposal to construct transition kernels without gradient approximation, combined with Parallel Tempering (temperature tempering + novel penalty tempering) to overcome highly multimodal energy landscapes in ILP.

Result: Outperforms SCIP across all four benchmarks, matches/exceeds Gurobi on two of four tasks within 200-second budget, substantially more robust to distribution shift than learning-based methods, and remains competitive with classical solvers on MIPLIB 2017 instances without problem-specific tuning.

Conclusion: The proposed solver-free, sampling-based framework provides an effective alternative to both classical solvers and learning-based methods for ILP, offering better generalization and eliminating dependence on external solvers while maintaining competitive performance.

Abstract: Integer Linear Programming (ILP) serves as a versatile framework for modeling a wide range of combinatorial optimization problems, typically addressed by sophisticated exact solvers or heuristics. While learning-based approaches have recently shown their effectiveness, they suffer from poor generalization to out-of-distribution instances and inherent dependence on external solvers. In this work, we propose a solver-free, sampling-based optimization framework for ILP that directly explores discrete feasible regions without training or external solvers. Exploiting the linear structure of ILP, we employ a Locally-Balanced Proposal to construct a transition kernel, thereby avoiding the gradient approximation. To overcome the highly multimodal nature of ILP energy landscapes, we integrate Parallel Tempering. In addition to standard temperature tempering, we introduce penalty tempering, which modulates constraint barriers while preserving the objective landscape over feasible solutions. Empirically, our method consistently outperforms SCIP across all four benchmarks, matches or exceeds Gurobi on two of four tasks within a 200-second budget, and is substantially more robust to distribution shift than learning-based methods. Furthermore, on MIPLIB 2017 instances, our framework remains competitive with classical solvers without any problem-specific tuning.

</details>


### [211] [Deep Adaptive Dimension Reduction for Bayesian Inference in Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29373)
*Yueyang Wang,Xili Wang,Kejun Tang,Xiaoliang Wan,Tao Zhou,Chao Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel deep adaptive dimension-reduction Bayesian inference framework combining Variational Flow with iterative prior updating and FNO surrogate fine-tuning achieves superior performance on high-dimensional PDE-governed inverse problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional PDE-governed inverse problems face three major challenges: complex non-Gaussian posterior distributions, expensive forward model evaluations, and misspecified prior information. Existing methods struggle with these issues simultaneously.

Method: 1) Variational Flow (VF) model integrating VAE-based nonlinear dimension reduction with dual normalizing flows for latent prior and encoder. 2) Iterative prior updating strategy that gradually shifts prior mean toward high-probability posterior regions. 3) Closed adaptive loop with adaptively fine-tuned Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) surrogate where VF and surrogate mutually refine each other.

Result: Numerical experiments on 100-dimensional Rosenbrock problem and three standard PDE-governed inverse problems show competitive or superior accuracy compared to MCMC, UKI, and SVGD baselines. Most pronounced advantages appear in challenging scenarios like high-noise observations and high-dimensional parameter spaces.

Conclusion: The proposed deep adaptive dimension-reduction Bayesian inference framework effectively addresses the core challenges of high-dimensional PDE inverse problems through integrated VF modeling, adaptive prior updating, and surrogate refinement, demonstrating strong empirical performance across diverse test cases.

Abstract: Solving high-dimensional PDE-governed inverse problems is often challenging due to complex non-Gaussian posterior distributions, expensive forward model evaluations, and misspecified prior information. To address these issues, we propose a deep adaptive dimension-reduction Bayesian inference framework based on the Variational Flow (VF) model. Since standard normalizing flows are restricted by bijective mappings and cannot directly reduce dimensions, VF overcomes this limitation by integrating VAE-based nonlinear dimension reduction with dual normalizing flows for the latent prior and encoder. This design provides a strictly higher evidence lower bound than VAE and allows more flexible approximation of complex posterior distributions. We further introduce an iterative prior updating strategy that gradually moves the prior mean toward high-probability posterior regions, avoiding manual prior tuning. These components form a closed adaptive loop together with an adaptively fine-tuned Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) surrogate: VF generates posterior-concentrated samples to refine the surrogate, while the updated surrogate further improves posterior inference. Numerical experiments on a 100-dimensional Rosenbrock problem and three standard PDE-governed inverse problems show that our method delivers competitive or superior accuracy compared with MCMC, UKI, and SVGD baselines across all tested configurations, with the most pronounced advantages emerging in challenging scenarios such as high-noise observations and high-dimensional parameter spaces.

</details>


### [212] [TRACER: Persistent Regularization for Robust Multimodal Finetuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29380)
*Hesam Asadollahzadeh,Feng Liu,Christopher Leckie,Sarah M. Erfani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRACER is a multimodal contrastive finetuning method that uses Weighted Moving Average teachers instead of standard EMA to prevent collapse and maintain out-of-distribution robustness while preserving pretrained knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard finetuning of pretrained multimodal models often causes catastrophic forgetting of out-of-distribution (OOD) knowledge. Existing approaches like EMA teachers suffer from collapse and fail to maintain regularizing forces.

Method: Developed theoretical framework for multimodal contrastive finetuning with closed-form solutions. Introduced Weighted Moving Average (WMA) teacher that maintains persistent regularization. TRACER combines contrastive learning with WMA-guided multi-perspective distillation.

Result: Extensive CLIP finetuning experiments show consistent OOD accuracy and calibration gains across three backbone architectures. TRACER demonstrates robustness to hyperparameter choices and outperforms existing regularization approaches.

Conclusion: TRACER addresses limitations of standard EMA teachers in robust finetuning, providing a principled approach that maintains pretrained knowledge while improving OOD robustness through WMA-based regularization.

Abstract: Mainstream strategies for finetuning pretrained multimodal models often degrade out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness, a phenomenon known as catastrophic forgetting. In this paper, we develop a theoretical framework for multimodal contrastive finetuning, yielding closed-form solutions and a geometric decomposition for each strategy. This framework shows that self-distillation is more effective than other regularization approaches to retain the knowledge of the pretrained model. Our analysis reveals a largely overlooked limitation: standard Exponential Moving Average (EMA) teachers, widely used in robust finetuning, suffer from collapse. To solve this, we prove that a Weighted Moving Average (WMA) teacher maintains a persistent regularizing force over finite horizons and yields bias-free convergence in the task subspace while preserving orthogonal knowledge. These insights motivate **TRACER** (**T**rajectory-**R**obust **A**nchoring for **C**ontrastive **E**ncoder **R**egularization), which combines contrastive learning with WMA-guided multi-perspective distillation. Extensive experiments on CLIP finetuning demonstrate consistent OOD accuracy and calibration gains across three backbone architectures, and comprehensive ablations confirm that TRACER is both principled and robust to hyperparameter choices. Code is available at [https://github.com/HesamAsad/TRACER](https://github.com/HesamAsad/TRACER).

</details>


### [213] [On the Optimizer Dependence of Neural Scaling Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29387)
*Vansh Ramani,Shourya Vir Jain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Optimizer choice systematically affects neural scaling law exponents α, with preconditioned optimizers yielding steeper scaling (larger α), especially for natural language-like spectral conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scaling exponent α in neural scaling laws L(N) ∝ N^{-α} is commonly treated as a fixed constant determined by architecture and data, but evidence suggests it may depend on the optimizer used.

Method: Controlled random-feature regression experiments (canonical theoretical framework) measuring α across five optimizer variants and six spectral conditions, with spectral diagnostic for predicting optimizer advantages.

Result: Preconditioned optimizers consistently yield steeper scaling (larger α), with α-shift increasing across spectral range, peaking near s=1.5. At natural language characteristic s≈1.0, natural gradient achieves α≈0.31 vs α≈0.12 for gradient descent (2.6× larger exponent).

Conclusion: Scaling-law forecasts should account for optimizer choice, and a spectral diagnostic can predict when advanced optimizers will pay off, though transfer to large-scale LLM training remains an open question.

Abstract: The scaling exponent $α$ in neural scaling laws $L(N) \propto N^{-α}$ is commonly treated as a fixed constant set by architecture and data. We present evidence that $α$ depends systematically on the optimizer. In controlled random-feature regression experiments -- the canonical theoretical framework for neural scaling -- we measure $α$ across five optimizer variants and six spectral conditions. Preconditioned optimizers consistently yield steeper scaling (larger $α$), with the $α$-shift increasing across most of the tested spectral range, peaking near $s = 1.5$, and remaining large at $s = 2.0$. At $s \approx 1.0$ (characteristic of natural language), the full natural gradient achieves $α\approx 0.31$ versus $α\approx 0.12$ for gradient descent -- a $2.6\times$ larger fitted exponent that, within the random-feature model, compounds with each model-size doubling. Whether and how this exponent shift transfers to large-scale LLM training -- where recent evidence suggests the advantage may attenuate with scale -- remains an important open question. Our results imply that scaling-law forecasts should account for optimizer choice, and we provide a spectral diagnostic predicting when advanced optimizers will pay off.

</details>


### [214] [GDSD: Reinforcement Learning as Guided Denoiser Self-Distillation for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29398)
*Xiaohang Tang,Keyue Jiang,Che Liu,Qifang Zhao,Xiaoxiao Xu,Sangwoong Yoon,Ilija Bogunovic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GDSD proposes a direct denoiser self-distillation method for diffusion LLMs that bypasses ELBO-based likelihood approximations in RL, achieving better performance and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL methods for diffusion LLMs use ELBO as a likelihood surrogate, which introduces training-inference mismatch bias that degrades performance despite being efficient.

Method: Guided Denoiser Self-Distillation (GDSD) directly distills dLLM denoisers from an advantage-guided self-teacher derived from the closed-form optimum of reverse-KL regularized RL, using a normalization-free objective that reduces RL to likelihood-free self-distillation.

Result: GDSD outperforms prior ELBO-based methods on planning, math, and coding benchmarks with LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B, achieving up to +19.6% test accuracy improvements with more stable training dynamics.

Conclusion: Direct denoiser self-distillation without ELBO likelihood surrogates provides a more stable and effective RL procedure for diffusion LLMs, with ELBO-based methods shown to be special cases with diagnosable pathologies.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) can be used to improve the policy (denoiser) of diffusion large language models (dLLMs), while being hindered by the intractability of the policy likelihood. A dominant and efficient family of methods replaces the likelihood in standard RL with its evidence lower bound (ELBO), estimated from randomly masked sequences. Despite being well aligned with pre-training, these approaches introduce bias through training--inference mismatch by using the ELBO as a likelihood surrogate, which can degrade performance. In this work, we propose Guided Denoiser Self-Distillation (GDSD) to directly distill the denoiser of dLLMs from an advantage-guided self-teacher, derived from the closed-form optimum of reverse-KL regularized RL. GDSD matches the dLLM's denoiser logits to the teacher's via a normalization-free objective, which reduces RL to likelihood-free self-distillation and thus bypasses the TIM biases. Recent ELBO-based methods emerge as instances of applying different distillation divergences, but with diagnosable pathologies that GDSD avoids. On planning, math, and coding benchmarks with LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B, GDSD consistently outperforms prior state-of-the-art ELBO-based methods with a more stable training reward dynamics, achieving test-accuracy improvements of up to $+19.6\%$. These results suggest that direct denoiser self-distillation, without relying on an ELBO likelihood surrogate, can provide a more stable and effective RL procedure for dLLMs. Code is available at https://github.com/GaryBall/GDSD.

</details>


### [215] [Rethinking Post-Training Recipes for Multimodal Time-Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29401)
*Haoxin Liu,Yichen Zhou,Rajat Sen,B. Aditya Prakash,Abhimanyu Das*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PostTime trains LLMs to revise numerical time-series forecasts using multimodal context, outperforming standalone models by combining TSFM priors with LLM reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TSFMs excel at numerical forecasting but cannot incorporate multimodal context (like text, images) that influences real-world time-series, creating a gap in comprehensive forecasting.

Method: Post-training recipe combining Supervised Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards, teaching LLMs to generate context-conditioned forecast interventions (revise, preserve, ignore) on TSFM priors.

Result: Significantly outperforms standalone TSFMs, LLM-only baselines, and existing multimodal forecasting approaches on TimesX benchmark using Gemma-3-4B LLM and TimesFM-2.5 TSFM.

Conclusion: Multimodal time-series forecasting approach combining LLMs with TSFM priors via structured post-training enables effective context-guided revision for improved forecasting accuracy.

Abstract: Time-Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) excel at zero-shot unimodal forecasting using numerical data, but unlike LLMs they cannot consume multimodal, non-numerical context that often shape real-world trajectories. In this work, we bridge this gap and argue for a multimodal time-series forecasting approach that post-trains LLMs to act as context-guided revisors over strong numerical TSFM priors. We introduce PostTime, a post-training recipe combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), along with a methodology to generate automated reasoning traces for forecast revisions. PostTime teaches an LLM to generate context-conditioned forecast interventions -- decisions to revise, preserve, or ignore the TSFM prior based on the multimodal context. We evaluate this approach on the TimesX multimodal forecasting benchmark using a Gemma-3-4B LLM and TimesFM-2.5 TSFM, and show that it significantly outperforms standalone TSFMs, LLM-only baselines, and existing multimodal forecasting approaches.

</details>


### [216] [Information-Directed Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29405)
*Keru Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Information-Directed Sampling (IDS) for offline-to-online learning, where actions balance regret against information gain conditioned on offline data, achieving better regret bounds than Thompson sampling in certain warm-start regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While offline data reduces uncertainty in decision-making, it doesn't eliminate the need for exploration - it merely changes what remains to be explored. The key challenge is formalizing and efficiently exploring this "residual uncertainty" after conditioning on offline datasets.

Method: Proposes Information-Directed Sampling (IDS) parameterized by η≥0, which selects actions by trading off instantaneous regret against information gain. Formally defines residual uncertainty as conditional mutual information I(χ;τ_{1:T}|D_N) between learning target χ and online trajectories given offline data.

Result: Proves offline-to-online Bayesian regret bounds for IDS through a ratio certificate. In Bayesian linear-reward models, vanilla IDS (η=0) achieves Õ(Hd min{√T, T√C†_{β,IDS₀}(N,T)/N}). Shows IDS outperforms Thompson sampling in warm-start regimes with informative probes.

Conclusion: IDS is particularly effective when offline data is informative but leaves biased or low-probability residual uncertainty that targeted online actions can resolve. This regime applies broadly to offline RL, offline black-box optimization, and Bayesian optimization problems.

Abstract: Decision-making from offline datasets typically warm-starts a policy or score model from fixed offline data and then refines it with limited online interaction. Offline data reduces uncertainty, but it does not remove the need for exploration; it changes what remains to be explored. We formalise this residual uncertainty by the conditional mutual information $I(χ;τ_{1:T}\mid\mathcal{D}_N)$ between a learning target $χ$ and the online trajectories after conditioning on the offline dataset. This view leads naturally to information-directed sampling (IDS), a family parameterised by $η\ge 0$ that selects actions by trading off instantaneous regret against information gain. We prove a generic offline-to-online Bayesian regret bound for IDS through a ratio certificate: any information-ratio bound satisfied by a reference Thompson-sampling policy over the same randomised policy class is inherited by IDS. In a known-dynamics Bayesian linear-reward model, the conditional mutual information has a log-determinant form, and vanilla IDS ($η=0$) satisfies $\widetilde O\!\left(Hd\min\left\{\sqrt T,\,T\sqrt{C^\dagger_{β,\mathrm{IDS}_0}(N,T)/N}\right\}\right),$ where the coverage coefficient is tied to the visitation distribution induced by vanilla IDS itself. We also identify a warm-start regime with a dominated but informative probe in which vanilla IDS selects the probe while Thompson sampling never does, giving a constant-factor Bayesian regret separation. Controlled bandit experiments and D4RL offline-to-online RL experiments validate this mechanism: IDS is most beneficial when offline data is informative but leaves biased or low-probability residual uncertainty that targeted online actions can resolve, a regime shared by offline RL, offline black-box optimization, and Bayesian optimization.

</details>


### [217] [The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly of Markov Boundary for Tabular Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29411)
*Shu Wan,Abhinav Gorantla,Huan Liu,K. Selçuk Candan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Despite theoretical benefits, using the Markov boundary for prediction is nuanced - oracle boundaries help but existing discovery methods fail to beat full feature sets in practice due to structural optimization, asymmetric error costs, and multiple alternative feature sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The Markov boundary theoretically identifies the minimal sufficient feature set for prediction, but modern regressors still use all features. The paper investigates whether this theoretical benefit translates to practical predictive gains on large-scale tabular data.

Method: Large-scale empirical evaluation on SCM3K (3,450 synthetic SCM tasks) with 40-1000 features across six SCM families, using six regressors. Compares oracle Markov boundaries with full feature sets, then evaluates practical causal discovery pipelines for boundary recovery.

Result: Oracle boundaries substantially improve prediction (especially with larger/sparser features), but practical discovery pipelines fail: they exhaust compute before reaching regimes where boundaries help most, rarely beat full feature sets, and suffer from three key issues.

Conclusion: The gap between theory and practice reveals that discovery optimizes structural recovery, false positives/negatives have asymmetric predictive costs, and many alternative feature sets exist. This informs future work on prediction-aligned feature selection and tabular models learning causal structure.

Abstract: Under standard graphical assumptions, the Markov boundary of a target variable is the smallest set of features that renders every other feature redundant. Once the boundary is observed, the target is conditionally independent of the rest of the table. This is a tempting object for tabular prediction, since it names exactly the columns a model should need. Yet modern regressors are still trained on the full feature set. We ask whether the Markov boundary is genuinely useful for prediction on SCM3K, a 3,450-task synthetic SCM benchmark with feature counts from 40 to 1000 and six SCM families, evaluated with six regressors. The answer is more nuanced than the theory suggests. Restricting a regressor to the oracle boundary often improves prediction substantially, and the improvement grows as the feature space becomes larger and sparser. But the natural pipeline of recovering the boundary with causal discovery and training on the recovered mask does not deliver. Existing estimators exhaust the compute budget before reaching the regime where the boundary helps most, and even where they run they rarely beat the full feature set. We trace this to three causes. Discovery optimizes structural recovery rather than prediction. False negatives and false positives carry sharply asymmetric predictive cost. The exact boundary is only one of many feature sets that beat all features. We then develop what these facts imply for prediction-aligned feature selection and for tabular models that learn to use causal structure.

</details>


### [218] [How Much Is a Dataset Worth? Scaling Laws, the Vendi Score, and Matrix Spectral Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29448)
*Jeff A. Bilmes,Gantavya Bhatt,Arnav M. Das*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper shows neural scaling laws and Vendi Score are submodular, introduces efficient optimization techniques for these objectives, and finds that facility location performs best for data appraisal across various regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data appraisal methods like neural scaling laws and Vendi Score are computationally expensive to optimize, limiting practical application on large datasets. The paper aims to make optimization feasible and compare different objectives for predicting training subset value.

Method: The authors prove neural-scaling-law objectives and Vendi Score are submodular, introduce secular-equation-based updates to avoid repeated eigendecompositions, compare multiple objectives (Vendi Score, DPPs, facility location, and new matrix spectral variants) across different training regimes, and analyze subset characteristics.

Result: Achieved 35,000x speedup making Vendi Score optimization feasible on ImageNet-scale datasets. Found facility location performs best overall. Discovered that pushing Vendi Score too high makes it a poor performance proxy. Uniform random subsets are remarkably concentrated in both scores and performance. Data value depends on more than just size, class balance, and budget.

Conclusion: The paper provides efficient optimization methods for submodular data appraisal objectives, enabling practical application on large datasets. Facility location emerges as the best-performing objective, while uniform random subsets show surprisingly consistent behavior. Data value is not solely determined by size, balance, or budget constraints.

Abstract: Neural scaling laws appraise data through dataset size, while the Vendi Score uses quantum entropy to measure dataset value. We show both that common neural-scaling-law objectives and the Vendi Score are submodular. We further show that the Vendi Score is a special case of a broader class of submodular objectives that we call matrix spectral functions. This also includes determinantal (DPP) objectives, as well as many others. We also introduce weakly matrix monotone functions and show how they lead to weakly submodular matrix spectral functions, yielding a broad family of practical objectives for data appraisal. We develop secular-equation-based updates that avoid repeated eigendecompositions during greedy optimization, reducing marginal-gain evaluation for $m$-dimensional embeddings by an $O(m)$ factor relative to oracle queries. This yields an average empirical speedup of about 35,000x, making direct optimization of the Vendi Score feasible on ImageNet-1K-scale datasets. Thus enabled, we compare how well several objectives predict the value of training subsets for held-out test performance under fixed-size, class-balanced, and fixed training-budget regimes, including the Vendi Score, DPPs, facility location, and three new matrix spectral variants. Across multiple datasets, facility location performs the best. Direct optimization also reveals that, while the Vendi Score is predictive over moderate score ranges, pushing the objective to higher values can make it a poor downstream performance proxy. We also find that uniformly at random fixed-size subsets, both unconstrained and class-balanced, are remarkably concentrated in both appraisal scores and held-out performance. Finally, we show that size, class balance, and training budget do not alone determine data value: even when controlling for these factors, performance ranges smoothly from good to bad.

</details>


### [219] [Forget Less, Generalize More: Unifying Temporal and Structural Adaptation for Dynamic Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29453)
*Qian Chang,Ciprian Doru Giurcaneanu,Runsong Jia,Xia Li,Guoping Hu,Xiufeng Cheng,Jinqing Yang,Mengjia Wu,Yi Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DSRD is a unified framework for dynamic graph representation learning that uses dual-scale retentive states with adaptive decay kernels to automatically balance temporal and structural dependencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dynamic graph learning methods use fixed temporal decay schemes and predetermined structural propagation depths, which limit their ability to generalize across graphs with diverse interaction frequencies and topological characteristics.

Method: DSRD introduces: 1) a retentive state with dual-scale adaptation that jointly models temporal dynamics and structural propagation in a single recurrent formulation, and 2) adaptive decay kernels with learnable time-sensitivity parameters that automatically balance short-term responsiveness and long-term retention.

Result: Theoretical analysis establishes equivalence between event-wise parallel aggregation and efficient recurrent state updates, with stability guarantees. Extensive experiments on 14 real-world benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance on link prediction and node classification tasks with strong generalization across transductive and inductive settings.

Conclusion: DSRD provides an effective unified framework for dynamic graph representation learning that addresses limitations of existing approaches through adaptive temporal-structural modeling and demonstrates superior performance across diverse real-world scenarios.

Abstract: Representation learning on dynamic graphs requires capturing complex dependencies that evolve across both time and structure. Existing approaches typically adopt fixed temporal decay schemes or predetermined structural propagation depths, limiting their ability to generalize across graphs with diverse interaction frequencies and topological characteristics. We propose Dual-Scale Retentive Dynamics (DSRD), a unified framework that maintains a retentive representation state encoding both temporal memory and structural context. DSRD introduces two key components: (i) a retentive state with dual-scale adaptation that jointly models temporal dynamics and structural propagation within a single recurrent formulation, and (ii) adaptive decay kernels with learnable time-sensitivity parameters that automatically balance short-term responsiveness and long-term retention based on the underlying interaction patterns. We provide theoretical analysis establishing the equivalence between event-wise parallel aggregation and efficient recurrent state updates, as well as stability and boundedness guarantees for the learned dynamics. Extensive experiments on 14 real-world benchmarks demonstrate that DSRD consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance on both link prediction and node classification tasks, with strong generalization across transductive and inductive settings.

</details>


### [220] [A Full-Pipeline Framework for Evaluating Membership Inference Attacks in Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29454)
*Ding Chen,Xinwen Cheng,Xuyang Zhong,Xinping Chen,Xiaolin Huang,Chen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive framework for evaluating Membership Inference Attacks across diverse ML contexts with standardized threat models and practical guidelines for privacy auditing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The field lacks a systematic framework for evaluating how different operational contexts affect MIA efficacy, risking deployment of algorithms that perform well on benchmarks but become statistically irrelevant in real-world settings with specific dataset nuances.

Method: Introduces an evaluation framework that systematically characterizes privacy risks across the entire ML pipeline (data, architectures, algorithms, post-training modules). Uses three complementary metrics: Balanced Accuracy for symmetric costs, TPR at low FPR/TNR at low FNR for asymmetric scenarios. Formalizes two standardized threat models and adapts existing attacks accordingly.

Result: Extensive empirical evaluations show MIA efficacy is highly sensitive to assumed threat models and chosen evaluation metrics. The framework enables systematic comparison and provides actionable insights.

Conclusion: Provides distilled guidelines and a ready-to-use auditing toolkit to empower practitioners to conduct better privacy assessments, bridging the gap between theoretical MIA research and practical privacy auditing needs.

Abstract: While Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) are the prevailing method for identifying training data, their application has expanded into privacy auditing and machine unlearning. Nevertheless, the field lacks a systematic framework for evaluating how different contexts affect MIA efficacy. Without such a characterization, practitioners risk deploying algorithms that perform well on benchmarks but become statistically irrelevant when faced with the nuances of specific, real-world datasets. To bridge this gap and provide actionable insights, we introduce a comprehensive evaluation framework that systematically characterizes privacy risks across the entire machine learning pipeline, spanning data, architectures, algorithms, and post-training modules. Designed to inherently capture diverse operational contexts, our framework rigorously evaluates state-of-the-art MIAs across a broad spectrum of training configurations. To account for varying misclassification costs in real-world deployments, we employ three complementary metrics: Balanced Accuracy for symmetric costs, alongside TPR at low FPR (or TNR at low FNR) for asymmetric scenarios where false alarms or missed detections are strictly penalized. Furthermore, recognizing that existing MIAs assume divergent adversary capabilities, we formalize two standardized threat models and adapt these attacks into corresponding variants to ensure an equitable benchmark. Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that the efficacy of specific MIA methodologies is highly sensitive to the assumed threat models and chosen evaluation metrics. Ultimately, we distill these findings into actionable guidelines and provide a ready-to-use auditing toolkit, empowering practitioners to conduct better privacy assessments.

</details>


### [221] [Honest Lying: Understanding Memory Confabulation in Reflexive Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29463)
*Prakhar Dixit,Sadia Kamal,Tim Oates*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reflexion-style agents often fail due to memory confabulation - they store confident but incorrect interpretations of tasks and persist in acting on them despite environmental resets, requiring new metrics and programmatic failure extraction to mitigate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper challenges the core assumption of Reflexion-style agents that agents can accurately self-diagnose their failures. The authors discovered that agents often develop persistent false beliefs about tasks that continue across multiple trials, even when environments reset correctly.

Method: 1) Introduced the Reflection Repetition Rate (RRR) metric to detect repeated reliance on incorrect reflective content by analyzing agent logs. 2) Identified frozen environments in ALFWorld (16 cases) and HumanEval (4 cases) where agents persistently fail. 3) Proposed a mitigation strategy that replaces open-ended self-diagnosis with programmatic extraction of trajectory-level failure signals.

Result: 1) Found systematic memory confabulation: agents stored confident but incorrect task interpretations. 2) In ALFWorld frozen environments, 0 of 121 reflections mentioned the correct target object. 3) The mitigation increased correct object mention from 0% to 86%, reduced RRR from 0.64 to 0.10, and solved 3 of 16 frozen ALFWorld environments.

Conclusion: Reflective memory can actually reinforce false beliefs rather than correct them in agent systems. The proposed programmatic failure signal extraction approach significantly improves agent performance by preventing memory confabulation and reducing reliance on incorrect self-diagnosis.

Abstract: Reflexion-style agents rely on self-generated reflections as memory, implicitly assuming that agents can accurately diagnose their own failures.We show that this assumption can fail systematically: across ALFWorld and HumanEval, agents store confident but incorrect interpretations of the task and continue acting on them across trials,even though the environment resets to the correct task each time. We call this failure mode memory confabulation and introduce the Reflection Repetition Rate (RRR), a log-based metric that detects repeated reliance on incorrect reflective content.Using RRR, we identify 16 frozen environments in ALFWorld, where 0 of 121 reflections mention the correct target object, and 4 analogous cases in HumanEval. Our mitigation replaces open-ended self-diagnosis with programmatic extraction of trajectory-level failure signals, increasing correct object mention from 0% to 86%, reducing RRR from 0.64 to 0.10, and solving 3 of 16 frozen ALFWorld environments, suggesting that reflective memory can reinforce false beliefs rather than correct them.

</details>


### [222] [Composing Non-Conjugate Factor Graphs with Closed-Form Variational Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29467)
*Mykola Lukashchuk,Kyrylo Yemets,Wouter M. Kouw,Dmitry Bagaev,İsmail Şenöz,Jeff Beck,Bert de Vries*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Stackable probabilistic building blocks enable closed-form variational inference through five factor-graph primitives, allowing deep architectures that preserve tractability and can represent arbitrary decision trees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep probabilistic architectures typically break closed-form inference, requiring approximate methods. The paper aims to preserve closed-form inference while allowing composition into deeper models.

Method: Identifies five factor-graph primitives (bilinear factor, exponential link, Gamma prior, Gaussian likelihood, equality node) that preserve closed-form variational message passing. Shows these primitives maintain Gaussian messages for Gaussian variables and Gamma messages for precision variables, with tractability preserved through moment-generating functions.

Result: Demonstrates composition at increasing depth from static ensembles to routing architectures. Shows stacking routing layers can encode arbitrary decision trees, establishing universal function approximation with closed-form inference. Applied to ensemble time-series forecasting, yields Bayesian mixture of experts with inferred rather than learned gating functions.

Conclusion: Closed-form variational inference can be preserved in deep probabilistic architectures through carefully designed factor-graph primitives, enabling Bayesian mixture models with calibrated uncertainty and tractable inference across multiple benchmark datasets.

Abstract: Stacking probabilistic building blocks into deeper architectures typically breaks closed-form inference. We show that closed-form inference can be preserved. We identify five factor-graph primitives: a bilinear factor, an exponential link, a Gamma prior, a Gaussian likelihood, and an equality node, and prove that any model composed from them admits closed-form variational message passing. The construction works because each primitive preserves a small set of message families: under mean-field factorization, messages on Gaussian variables remain Gaussian and messages on precision variables remain Gamma, while the only non-conjugate interface, the exponential link, remains tractable through the Gaussian moment-generating function and the sufficient statistics of the Gamma family. We demonstrate composition at increasing depth, from static ensembles through input-dependent gating to split-branch routing, and show that stacking routing layers encodes arbitrary decision trees, establishing universal function approximation with closed-form inference. Applied to ensemble time-series forecasting, the framework yields a Bayesian mixture of experts in which gating functions are inferred rather than learned, providing calibrated uncertainty over expert selection across five benchmark datasets.

</details>


### [223] [Access Sets Matter: Budgeting Expert Reads for Scalable Weight-Space Model Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29489)
*Yuanyi Wang,Yanggan Gu,Su Lu,Yifan Yang,Zhaoyi Yan,Congkai Xie,Jianmin Wu,Hongxia Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MergePipe is a budget-aware execution layer that reduces I/O costs for large language model merging by treating it as an expert access-set problem with bounded error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When merging LLM checkpoints at scale, the limiting resource is often reading expert weights. Current weight-space model merging approaches don't consider I/O budgets, leading to inefficient expert weight access patterns.

Method: MergePipe casts LLM merging as an expert access-set problem: it indexes parameter blocks, builds deterministic access plans under explicit I/O budgets, and executes budgeted merges with replayable manifests. The approach is budget-sound and for additive operators, bounds error by the norm of omitted deltas.

Result: MergePipe reduces expert-read I/O by up to 10x and achieves up to 11x speedups. Budget sweeps show only O(10^-3) parameter deviation from full-read merges with no monotonic degradation on downstream benchmarks for Qwen and Llama models.

Conclusion: MergePipe provides an effective execution layer for budget-aware LLM merging that significantly reduces I/O costs while maintaining merge quality, making large-scale model merging more practical and efficient.

Abstract: Weight-space model merging is usually formulated as an algebraic operation on checkpoints, yet at LLM scale the limiting resource is often the set of expert weights that must be read. We introduce MergePipe, a budget-aware execution layer that casts LLM merging as an \emph{expert access-set} problem: given a merge operator and a checkpoint family in a shared weight coordinate system, choose which expert delta blocks to access under an explicit I/O budget. MergePipe indexes parameter blocks, builds deterministic access plans, and executes the induced budgeted merge with replayable manifests. The plan is budget-sound by construction and recovers the full-read merge at full budget; for fixed-coefficient additive operators, the omitted-update error is bounded by the norm of omitted deltas. Across Qwen and Llama merging workloads, MergePipe reduces expert-read I/O by up to an order of magnitude and achieves up to $11\times$ speedups. Representative budget sweeps show $O(10^{-3})$ parameter deviation from full-read merges and no monotonic degradation on downstream benchmarks.

</details>


### [224] [Gradient Perturbation: Learning to Perturb Gradients for Adaptive Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29494)
*Hua Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LPG (Learning to Perturb Gradients) is a novel method that adaptively perturbs logit-level gradients at class level to achieve category-aware training, outperforming existing methods across various classification tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While forward chain perturbations (feature, logit, label) have been extensively studied, gradient perturbation in the backward chain has received little systematic investigation despite its importance in deep neural network training.

Method: Proposes a unified framework for gradient perturbation showing existing methods (SAM, gradient clipping, gradient noise) as specific forms. Introduces LPG that adaptively perturbs logit-level gradients at class level based on a hypothesis: amplifying gradient norm enhances learning (positive augmentation) while dampening it suppresses overfitting (negative augmentation).

Result: LPG consistently outperforms existing methods on balanced classification, long-tail classification, and noisy label learning tasks. Can be combined with existing methods as a plug-in module. Theoretical connections established between gradient perturbation bounds and generalization guarantees via PAC-Bayesian analysis.

Conclusion: Gradient perturbation represents an underexplored dimension of training regularization that can significantly improve model performance across various classification scenarios when properly structured and adaptively applied at class level.

Abstract: Deep neural network training involves both forward propagation (from features through logits to loss) and backward propagation (from loss through gradients to parameter updates). While perturbations along the forward chain, including feature perturbation, logit perturbation, and label perturbation, have been extensively studied, the backward chain's gradient perturbation has received little systematic investigation. In this paper, we establish a unified framework for gradient perturbation, revealing that existing methods such as Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM), gradient clipping, and gradient noise injection can all be interpreted as imposing specific forms of gradient perturbation. Analogous to the recently proposed Logit Perturbation Learning (LPL), we conjecture that amplifying the gradient norm for a class acts as positive augmentation (enhancing learning), while dampening it acts as negative augmentation (suppressing overfitting). Based on these observations, we propose Learning to Perturb Gradients (LPG), which adaptively perturbs logit-level gradients at the class level to achieve category-aware training. We also establish theoretical connections between gradient perturbation bounds and generalization guarantees via PAC-Bayesian analysis. Experiments on balanced classification, long-tail classification, and noisy label learning demonstrate that LPG consistently outperforms existing methods and can be combined with them as a plug-in module.

</details>


### [225] [On-Policy Replay for Continual Supervised Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29495)
*Yan Chen,Taojie Zhu,Meng Zhang,Xin Chen,Jiaqi Huang,Dongyang Xu,Yizhi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: On-Policy Replay (OPR) reduces catastrophic forgetting in continual fine-tuning by filtering model generations on historical prompts via task reward and replaying high-quality examples as standard SFT data, outperforming baseline methods without extra losses or teacher models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual supervised fine-tuning of LLMs suffers from catastrophic forgetting of earlier capabilities. While recent work shows on-policy signals reduce forgetting better than off-policy supervision, existing methods require teacher models, extra forward passes, and suffer from schedule sensitivity and stylistic drift.

Method: OPR routes on-policy signal through training data source instead of new objectives. It rolls out the most recent checkpoint on a small budget of historical prompts, filters generations by task reward, and replays the surviving (prompt, model response) pairs as ordinary SFT examples. No teacher model, auxiliary loss, or on-the-fly distillation is needed.

Result: OPR consistently reduces forgetting across three 7-8B instruction-tuned backbones on TRACE benchmark. On Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with Sequential SFT BWT of -13.93, OPR lifts BWT to -0.65 (10% replay budget) and -2.29 (1% budget) - a 46% reduction in |BWT| over tuned Vanilla Replay baseline, with 42-46% reductions across all backbones.

Conclusion: OPR effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting in continual SFT through on-policy replay, showing that the active ingredient is the on-policy distribution rather than response quality alone. The method provides a simpler, more effective alternative to teacher-based distillation approaches.

Abstract: Continual supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is the de facto recipe for adapting large language models (LLMs) to a stream of downstream tasks, but it suffers from catastrophic forgetting of earlier capabilities. Recent work shows that on-policy signals -- training on the model's own outputs -- reduce forgetting more reliably than off-policy supervision. Existing on-policy methods route this signal through a new training objective (e.g., self-distillation losses with a teacher copy), inheriting an extra forward pass, schedule sensitivity, and stylistic drift from the teacher.We instead route the on-policy signal through the training data source. Our method, On-Policy Replay (OPR), rolls out the most recent checkpoint on a small budget of historical prompts, filters the generations by a task reward, and replays the surviving (prompt, model response) pairs as ordinary SFT examples. There is no teacher, no auxiliary loss, and no on-the-fly distillation. Across three 7--8B instruction-tuned backbones (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Qwen3-8B, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct) on the TRACE continual-learning benchmark, OPR consistently reduces forgetting; on the sharpest stress test (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Sequential SFT BWT -13.93), OPR lifts BWT to -0.65 at a 10% replay budget and to -2.29 at a 1% budget -- a 46% reduction in |BWT| over a tuned Vanilla Replay baseline, with 42--46% reductions observed across all three backbones. We give a KL-shrinkage interpretation that places OPR and prior on-policy distillation methods on a single axis, and we present a counterintuitive finding that explains why Vanilla Replay is already a strong baseline: low-score replay is uniformly worse than Vanilla Replay, demonstrating that the active ingredient in OPR is the on-policy distribution, not the response quality alone.Our code is available at https://github.com/Yancey2024/OnPolicyReplay.

</details>


### [226] [Convex Basins in Single-Index Model Loss Landscapes: Applications to Robust Recovery under Strong Adversarial Corruption](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29497)
*Santanu Das,Sagnik Chatterjee,Jatin Batra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First robust algorithm for learning Gaussian Single Index Models with non-monotonic link functions under heavy-tailed noise and adversarial corruption, achieving near-linear sample/time complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior robust recovery work doesn't handle generic asymmetric non-monotonic link functions like GeLU and Swish, which are important primitives in modern neural architectures. No existing guarantees exist for this broad family of nonlinear SIMs.

Method: Develop new structural understanding of Gaussian squared-loss landscape under contamination. Prove existence of dimension-independent constant-radius convex basin around ground truth. Use robust spectral initialization plus principled warm start for robust gradient descent.

Result: Achieves final estimation error of O(σ√ε) in Õ(nd) time with Õ(d) samples, where ε is contamination fraction. First robust recovery guarantees for broad family of nonlinear SIMs with non-monotonic link functions.

Conclusion: Closes important gap in robust learning theory by providing first algorithm for non-monotonic SIMs under adversarial corruption, enabling robust recovery for neural architecture primitives with practical efficiency.

Abstract: We study the problem of robustly learning Gaussian Single Index Models (SIMs) in the presence of heavy-tailed noise and a constant fraction of adversarially corrupted covariates and responses. Prior work on robust recovery has considered settings such as linear regression (Pensia et al., JASA 2024), strictly monotonic link functions (Awasthi et al., NeurIPS 2022), and phase retrieval (Buna and Rebeschini, AISTATS 2025). However, these techniques do not extend to generic asymmetric non-monotonic link functions such as \textsc{GeLU} and \textsc{Swish}, which arise naturally as scalar primitives in modern gated neural architectures. We close this gap by giving the first robust recovery algorithm with near-linear sample and time complexity for generic non-monotonic link functions, thereby establishing the first robust recovery guarantees for a broad family of nonlinear SIMs for which \textit{no guarantees were previously known}. Our central contribution is a new structural understanding of the Gaussian squared-loss landscape under adversarial contamination. Crucially, we prove that for a broad class of nonlinear non-monotonic SIMs, a dimension-independent, constant-radius convex basin exists around the ground truth and is efficiently reachable via robust spectral initialization even under adversarial contamination. Prior works fail to establish both guarantees simultaneously, thereby either breaking down under adversarial contamination or failing to handle generic non-monotonic link functions. Together, these structural insights yield a principled warm start for robust gradient descent that provably converges to a final estimation error of $O(σ\sqrtε)$ in $\tilde{O}(nd)$ time with $\tilde{O}(d)$ samples, where $ε$ is the contamination fraction.

</details>


### [227] [Quotient DAGs for Off-Policy Evaluation:Forward-Flow Importance Sampling and Exact Slate Propensities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29500)
*Ziwen Xie,Shaowen Xiang,Hongyu He,Dianbo Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Forward-DP enables efficient exact propensity estimation for unordered slate recommendations by merging equivalent generation histories in a quotient-DAG framework, avoiding factorial enumeration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard importance sampling for off-policy evaluation creates nuisance variance and computational bottlenecks when evaluation targets ignore details of the generation process (e.g., unordered vs ordered slates in recommender systems). Exact unordered slate propensities require factorial enumeration, making practical evaluation difficult.

Method: Introduces a quotient-DAG view that merges histories equivalent for evaluation and assigns weights using target-to-behavior forward-flow ratios on the merged graph. For slate recommendation with set-sufficient next-item interface, develops Forward-DP - a subset-DAG dynamic program that computes exact unordered propensities without factorial enumeration.

Result: The method provides a practical propensity primitive that enables efficient propensity-based evaluation and model selection for context-dependent autoregressive slate loggers, avoiding the computational gap of exact enumeration.

Conclusion: The quotient-DAG framework and Forward-DP algorithm successfully address the computational challenges of off-policy evaluation for unordered slate recommendations, enabling practical propensity-based methods without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Off-policy evaluation estimates how a target policy would perform using data collected by a different behavior policy, which is crucial when online testing is costly or risky, such as in recommendation or healthcare. Standard importance sampling reweights each logged trajectory, but it can treat details of the generation process as meaningful even when the evaluation target ignores them: for example, an autoregressive slate recommender may generate an ordered sequence of items while the reward and downstream estimator depend only on the unordered slate. This creates nuisance variance and a computational gap, since exact unordered slate propensities require summing over all generation orders. We introduce a quotient-DAG view that merges histories equivalent for evaluation and assigns weights using target-to-behavior forward-flow ratios on the merged graph. For slate recommendation under a set-sufficient next-item interface, this yields Forward-DP, a subset-DAG dynamic program that computes exact unordered propensities without factorial enumeration. The resulting propensity primitive enables practical propensity-based evaluation and model selection for context-dependent autoregressive slate loggers.

</details>


### [228] [K-FinHallu: A Hallucination Detection Benchmark for Multi-Turn RAG in Korean Finance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29523)
*Eunbyeol Cho,Yunseung Lee,Mirae Kim,Jeewon Yang,Youngjun Kwak,Edward Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: K-FinHallu: First benchmark for multi-turn Korean financial RAG hallucination detection with hierarchical taxonomy including justified abstention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM hallucinations remain critical barrier for financial automation deployment. Existing benchmarks focus on single-turn, English tasks, ignoring multi-turn dynamics and Korean financial domain linguistic-regulatory nuances.

Method: Construct multi-turn dialogues from authentic Korean financial documents, inject hallucinations using hierarchical taxonomy based on context answerability that explicitly accounts for justified abstention. Benchmark frontier and open-source LLMs as hallucination detectors.

Result: Even strongest models struggle with fine-grained financial diagnostics and refusal behavior. Fine-tuning 8B model yields performance competitive with frontier LLMs, but justified abstention remains weakest axis across all models.

Conclusion: Need for specialized benchmarks addressing multi-turn interactions and linguistic-regulatory nuances in non-English financial domains. Justified abstention detection remains challenging open problem.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have advanced financial automation through Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), yet hallucinations remain a critical barrier to deployment in high-stakes environments. Existing benchmarks focus on single-turn, English-centric tasks, leaving the multi-turn dynamics and linguistic-regulatory nuances of the Korean financial domain unaddressed. We introduce K-FinHallu, the first benchmark for hallucination detection in multi-turn Korean financial RAG. We construct multi-turn dialogues from authentic Korean financial documents and inject hallucinations under a proposed hierarchical taxonomy based on context answerability that explicitly accounts for justified abstention. Benchmarking frontier and open-source LLMs as hallucination detectors, we find that even the strongest models struggle with fine-grained financial diagnostics and refusal behavior. While fine-tuning an 8B model on our training split yields performance competitive with frontier LLMs, justified abstention remains the weakest axis across all evaluated models.

</details>


### [229] [Learning to Perturb Hidden Representations for Generalizable Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29525)
*Hua Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes LPA (Learning to Perturb Activations), a novel framework for adaptive hidden activation perturbations that learns class-level perturbations, outperforming existing methods in various classification tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While input, logit, and label perturbations have been extensively studied, hidden activations (which constitute the bulk of neural network computation) lack unified perturbation analysis. Existing methods like Dropout and Manifold Mixup use class-agnostic or random strategies for activation perturbation.

Method: The authors establish a unified framework for hidden activation perturbation and propose LPA, which adaptively perturbs activations at a selected hidden layer with class-level perturbations learned via Projected Gradient Descent (PGD). They provide theoretical analysis connecting activation perturbation to flat minima and perturbation amplification through layers.

Result: LPA consistently outperforms existing methods in experiments on balanced classification, long-tail classification, and domain generalization tasks. It also provides complementary benefits when combined with logit perturbation methods like LPL.

Conclusion: The paper presents a comprehensive framework for understanding and applying hidden activation perturbations, demonstrating that learned class-level perturbations at appropriate layers can significantly improve model performance across diverse tasks, while also providing theoretical insights into the mechanisms behind these improvements.

Abstract: Deep neural networks process data through a cascade of representations: input features, hidden activations, logits, and loss. While perturbations at the input, logit, and label levels have been systematically studied, the intermediate hidden activations, which constitute the bulk of the network's computation, have received no unified perturbation analysis. In this paper, we establish a unified framework for hidden activation perturbation, revealing that Dropout, Manifold Mixup, adversarial feature perturbation, and related methods all impose specific forms of activation perturbation but with class-agnostic or random strategies. We conjecture that expansive perturbation (increasing activation norm) acts as positive augmentation, while contractive perturbation (decreasing activation norm) acts as negative augmentation, and that the perturbation layer determines whether the effect resembles input-level augmentation (shallow layers) or logit-level manipulation (deep layers). We propose Learning to Perturb Activations (LPA), which adaptively perturbs activations at a selected hidden layer with class-level perturbations learned via PGD. We further provide theoretical analysis connecting activation perturbation to flat minima and perturbation amplification through layers. Experiments on balanced classification, long-tail classification, and domain generalization demonstrate that LPA consistently outperforms existing methods and provides complementary benefits to logit perturbation methods such as LPL.

</details>


### [230] [AsymVLM: Asymmetric Token Pruning for Efficient Vision-Language Model Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29535)
*Yilin Feng,Ahmed Burak Gulhan,Mahmut Taylan Kandemir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AsymVLM is an asymmetric compression method for Vision-Language Models that applies aggressive pruning to vision tokens during prefill and selective eviction to text tokens during decoding, achieving up to 54% FLOPs savings while maintaining competitive accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs process thousands of redundant visual tokens alongside few text tokens, but existing compression methods treat both modalities uniformly despite their fundamentally different properties. Vision tokens are spatially redundant and dominate prefill, while text tokens are causally dependent and accumulate during decoding.

Method: AsymVLM uses: 1) Aggressive pruning of vision tokens before prefill with a learned importance scorer and per-sample adaptive budgeting; 2) Temporal threshold-based eviction of text tokens only when they exceed a fixed budget during decoding.

Result: Achieves highest FLOPs savings (up to 54%) among state-of-the-art methods while outperforming existing approaches by 2-3% on document/chart understanding tasks. Maintains competitive accuracy on holistic benchmarks and substantially outperforms standard LLM cache compression in text-dominated scenarios.

Conclusion: Asymmetric compression tailored to modality-specific properties (vision vs. text) is effective for VLMs, achieving significant computational savings while maintaining or improving accuracy across different task types where visual information characteristics vary.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) process thousands of visual tokens per image alongside comparatively few text tokens, yet existing compression methods treat both modalities uniformly. We observe that the two modalities have fundamentally different properties: vision tokens are spatially redundant and dominate prefill, while text tokens are causally dependent and accumulate during decoding. Based on this asymmetry, we propose and empirically evaluate AsymVLM, which applies aggressive pruning to vision tokens before prefill using a learned importance scorer with per-sample adaptive budgeting, and temporal threshold-based eviction to text tokens only when they exceed a fixed budget. Our experiments indicate that AsymVLM achieves the highest FLOPs savings (up to 54%) among state-of-the-art methods while outperforming existing approaches by 2--3% on document and chart understanding tasks where visual information is spatially localized and query-specific, and maintaining competitive accuracy on holistic benchmarks. In text-dominated scenarios, our eviction strategy substantially outperforms standard LLM cache compression methods by adapting to the short-context nature of VLM.

</details>


### [231] [SCOPE: A Lightweight-training LLM Framework for Air Traffic Control Readback Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29543)
*Qihan Deng,Minghua Zhang,Yang Yang,Zhenyu Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCOPE is a lightweight LLM framework for ATC readback monitoring that achieves superior accuracy (91.05% open-set detection, 96.63% anomaly correction) with low latency by combining plug-in open-set classification with in-context learning on frozen LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ATC readback anomalies are involved in 80% of aviation incidents, a problem worsened by increasing traffic and cognitive workload. Traditional rule-based and ML approaches fail to generalize across variable ATC phraseology, while existing LLM methods face deployment and computational barriers.

Method: Proposes SCOPE framework: couples a plug-in open-set classifier with carefully designed in-context learning on top of a frozen LLM, enabling lightweight training without full model fine-tuning.

Result: On semi-synthetic communication data, SCOPE achieves 91.05% accuracy in open-set detection and corrects 96.63% of anomalous readbacks, outperforming baselines while providing explanations and meeting low-latency requirements for operational use.

Conclusion: SCOPE demonstrates potential as a practical, interpretable, and controllable pathway for ATC readback monitoring, addressing both accuracy and deployment efficiency challenges.

Abstract: Pilot readback of Air Traffic Control (ATC) voice instructions is a primary safeguard against miscommunication in air transportation. However, readback anomalies remain implicated in approximately 80% of aviation incidents. This vulnerability is further exacerbated by rising traffic volume and elevated cognitive workload, thereby motivating automated readback monitoring by machine. Traditional rule-based and machine learning approaches struggle to generalize across the highly variable and evolving phraseology of air traffic controller-pilot communications. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have opened a new avenue through their strong reasoning and generalization capabilities, existing approaches still face deployment and computational barriers in practice. In this work, we propose Semantic reasoning for Communication via Open-set Plug-in with Examples (SCOPE), a novel lightweight-training LLM framework that advances both the efficiency and accuracy of machine-based ATC readback monitoring. The core idea is to couple a plug-in open-set classifier with a carefully designed in-context learning mechanism on top of a frozen LLM. Extensive experiments on the semi-synthetic communication dataset show that SCOPE attains superior accuracy while delivering the low-latency response required for operational environments. Under a few-shot setting, SCOPE achieves 91.05% accuracy in open-set detection and corrects 96.63% of anomalous readbacks, thereby outperforming the strongest available baselines while providing explanations for its decisions. These findings demonstrate the potential of our framework as a practical pathway toward interpretable and controllable ATC readback monitoring.

</details>


### [232] [Singularity-aware Optimization via Randomized Geometric Probing: Towards Stable Non-smooth Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29547)
*Ruoran Xu,Borong She,Xiaobo Jin,Qiufeng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: S-Adam is a new optimizer that stabilizes training on non-smooth loss landscapes by detecting geometric instability and adaptively damping step sizes to prevent gradient chattering, outperforming existing methods on challenging tasks like quantization-aware training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern deep learning architectures use non-smooth components like ReLU and quantization operators, violating the smooth landscape assumption. Adaptive optimizers like Adam suffer from gradient chattering (violent oscillations) in these regimes due to conflicting signals in the Clarke subdifferential, leading to poor convergence and generalization.

Method: Introduces Singularity-aware Adam (S-Adam) with a Local Geometric Instability (LGI) metric that efficiently estimates the Clarke subdifferential diameter using randomized directional derivatives. S-Adam uses an adaptive damping mechanism exp(-λρ) that modulates step sizes based on local instability - slowing updates in high-instability regions while maintaining fast convergence in smooth areas.

Result: Provides rigorous convergence analysis proving S-Adam converges almost surely to (δ,ε)-Clarke stationary points at optimal O(1/√T) rate. Empirical results show S-Adam outperforms AdamW and Prox-SGD, achieving up to 6% accuracy gain on CIFAR-100 and 3% on TinyImageNet in quantization-aware training and high-noise small-batch learning, while effectively mitigating gradient oscillations.

Conclusion: S-Adam successfully addresses the fundamental problem of gradient chattering in non-smooth optimization by incorporating geometric awareness into the adaptive optimization framework, offering both theoretical guarantees and practical performance improvements on challenging real-world deep learning tasks.

Abstract: Deep learning optimization relies heavily on the assumption of smooth loss landscapes, a condition systematically violated by modern architectures due to non-smooth components such as ReLU activations and quantization operators.
