Motivation: LLMs need context-dependent criteria (expert preferences, organizational conventions) that are often tacit and difficult to elicit directly for effective writing and review support.

Method: Infer rubrics from accumulated inline comments on artifacts, then iteratively refine them by observing comment-wise mismatches between rubric-conditioned predictions and reference comments.

Result: Evaluation in real-world review settings and controlled settings with reference rubrics shows inline comments can be distilled into reusable rubrics that support comment prediction, rubric understanding, and automatic artifact revision.

Conclusion: Accumulated inline comments can be effectively transformed into reusable natural-language rubrics that enhance LLM-based writing and review support by capturing previously tacit contextual criteria.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for writing and review support, but their usefulness depends on context-dependent criteria, such as expert preferences or organization-specific conventions, that are often tacit, undocumented, and difficult to elicit directly. We propose a problem setting for learning reusable natural-language rubrics from accumulated inline comments on artifacts such as human-written or LLM-generated drafts. Our method infers rubrics from these comments and iteratively refines them by observing comment-wise mismatches between rubric-conditioned predictions and reference comments. We evaluate the proposed method in real-world review settings and in controlled settings with reference rubrics. These results show that inline comments can be distilled into reusable rubrics that support comment prediction, rubric understanding, and automatic artifact revision.

</details>


### [260] [ESPO: Early-Stopping Proximal Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29860)
*Zihang Li,Rui Zhou,Yingcheng Shi,Wenhan Yu,Zhewen Tan,Zixiang Liu,Zeming Li,Binhua Li,Yongbin Li,Tong Yang,Jieping Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ESPO improves RL training by early termination of failed reasoning trajectories, reducing compute waste and improving advantage estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RL algorithms waste compute by forcing models to continue generating after early reasoning errors, polluting advantage estimates with post-failure noise.

Method: ESPO detects trajectory failure on-the-fly by computing surrogate regret using only existing logits, terminating rollouts when cumulative regret significantly exceeds estimates. Truncated trajectories become absorbing failure states with terminal reward.

Result: ESPO outperforms PPO on AIME~2024 (46.28% vs 45.25%), AMC~2023 (85.83% vs 82.94%), and MATH-500 (87.42% vs 85.43%), while saving >20% rollout tokens.

Conclusion: Early-stopping during RL training improves mathematical reasoning performance while significantly reducing computational waste from failed trajectories.

Abstract: When a large language model under reinforcement learning commits a wrong reasoning step early in a trajectory, standard algorithms force it to keep generating until the maximum horizon, spending compute on tokens that never receive positive reward and polluting advantage estimates with post-failure noise. We propose ESPO (Early-Stopping Proximal Policy Optimization), which detects trajectory failure on-the-fly and terminates rollouts early. At each generation step, ESPO computes a surrogate regret using only the logits already computed during sampling, and terminates when the smoothed cumulative regret significantly exceeds its estimated values. Truncated trajectories are treated as absorbing failure states with a terminal reward, concentrating negative temporal-difference (TD) errors near the detected failure step without any additional reward model or human annotation. On DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B trained for mathematical reasoning, ESPO surpasses PPO on AIME~2024 (46.28% vs. 45.25%), AMC~2023 (85.83% vs. 82.94%), and MATH-500 (87.42% vs. 85.43%), while saving more than 20% rollout tokens cumulatively.

</details>


### [261] [STAP: A Shuffle-Tokenized App Predictor with Ultra Long Context for Vocabulary-Free Mobile App Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29863)
*Chengyu Fan,Hang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STAP is a Transformer-based model for predicting next mobile app launches that uses random virtual indices instead of fixed vocabularies, enabling cross-dataset zero-shot prediction and cold start capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mobile app prediction models have limitations: they rely on fixed app vocabularies that prevent generalization across different app ecosystems, and they often depend on user-specific knowledge which creates deployment challenges in cold start scenarios.

Method: STAP eliminates fixed vocabularies by replacing true app identities with randomly reassigned virtual indices through a shuffle mechanism. To compensate for lost semantic information, it processes behavioral sequences using an ultra-long context design. The model includes theoretical analysis showing convergence to correct distributions with sufficiently long contexts, and features a deployment strategy for maintaining long contexts during continuous inference with acceptable latency.

Result: STAP achieves strong cross-dataset zero-shot prediction accuracy across datasets from different continents - a setting where fixed-vocabulary methods are inherently inapplicable. Its cold start performance within each dataset remains competitive with leading models.

Conclusion: STAP overcomes key limitations of existing app prediction models by removing fixed vocabulary dependencies, enabling cross-ecosystem generalization and better cold start performance, with practical deployment strategies for real-world applications.

Abstract: Predicting the next mobile application a user will launch is essential for intelligent device resource management and proactive assistance. Existing models rely on fixed app vocabularies, which prevents them from generalizing across different app ecosystems. Many also depend on user-specific knowledge, which complicates deployment in cold start scenarios. We propose STAP, a Transformer-based model that eliminates the need for a fixed vocabulary. STAP replaces true app identities with randomly reassigned virtual indices via a shuffle mechanism, and compensates for discarded semantic information by processing behavioral sequences with an ultra-long context design. A theoretical analysis shows that, given a sufficiently long context, the predicted distribution converges to the correct one despite the anonymity of the mapping. Experiments on two datasets from different continents demonstrate that STAP achieves strong cross-dataset zero-shot prediction accuracy -- a setting where all existing fixed-vocabulary methods are inherently inapplicable -- while its cold start performance within each dataset remains competitive with leading models. Furthermore, we introduce a deployment strategy that enables the model to retain a sufficiently long context during continuous inference while keeping latency within acceptable bounds.

</details>


### [262] [Open Problem: Separating Geometric and Algorithmic Compression via Cayley-Table Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29885)
*Dongsung Huh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep learning lacks algorithmic bias for discrete algebraic rules; proposes Cayley-table completion as testbed to develop complexity-minimizing priors for exact discrete recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep learning excels at continuous tasks but fails at extrapolating exact algorithmic/discrete algebraic rules, indicating a missing inductive bias toward algorithmic complexity minimization. Need to develop methods that can autonomously discover discrete algebraic axioms.

Method: Proposes Cayley-table completion as canonical testbed (discrete analog to matrix completion). Suggests operator-valued tensor factorizations with flatness priors as potential approach, analogous to how matrix factorization with weight decay yields low-rank bias. Challenges community to develop formal recovery bounds and generalize flatness priors for discrete algorithmic discovery.

Result: Abstract presents a theoretical proposal and open problem - no empirical results reported. Recent findings suggest operator-valued tensor factorizations with flatness priors yield implicit algorithmic bias toward exact discrete associativity, offering promising direction.

Conclusion: Deep learning needs new inductive biases for discrete algorithmic generalization. Cayley-table completion serves as ideal testbed. The community should work on formal recovery bounds and extend continuous flatness priors to autonomously discover discrete algorithmic axioms without combinatorial search.

Abstract: Modern statistical learning theory and deep learning characterize generalization primarily in terms of continuous capacity control (e.g., norm-based regularization, margin maximization, low-rank bias). While highly successful in continuous domains, deep learning consistently fails to extrapolate exact algorithmic or discrete algebraic rules, reflecting a missing inductive bias toward algorithmic complexity minimization. We propose the Cayley-table completion as the canonical testbed for this missing bias, serving as the discrete algebraic counterpart to matrix completion. Just as matrix factorization combined with weight decay yields an implicit geometric bias toward low linear rank, recent results demonstrate that operator-valued tensor factorizations paired with a flatness prior yield an implicit algorithmic bias toward exact discrete associativity. We pose the open problem of establishing formal exact recovery bounds for Cayley-table completion, and challenge the community to generalize continuous flatness priors to autonomously discover broader discrete algorithmic axioms without combinatorial search.

</details>


### [263] [LaRA: Layer-wise Representation Analysis for Detecting Data Contamination in RL Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29888)
*Minju Gwak,Minseo Kwak,Dongseok Lee,Guijin Son,Alan Ritter,Jaehyung Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LaRA: A layer-wise representation analysis framework for detecting data contamination in RL post-trained LLMs using geometric metrics that outperform existing output-level methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL post-training improves reasoning in LLMs, but data contamination undermines generalization and evaluation reliability. Existing detection methods relying on output-level signals (likelihood/entropy) become unreliable for RL-trained models since RL shapes behavior through trajectory-level rewards rather than token likelihoods.

Method: LaRA introduces three complementary metrics measuring perturbation sensitivity, directional collapse, and local representation rigidity under controlled perturbations. The framework analyzes progressive geometric deviations across layers and develops a detection protocol that aggregates representation-level deviations across layers and metrics.

Result: Contamination produces progressive geometric deviations across layers: amplified perturbation sensitivity, stronger directional collapse, and enhanced local rigidity. Experiments on RL-trained reasoning models show that LaRA's protocol outperforms existing output-level baselines for contamination detection.

Conclusion: LaRA provides an effective representation-level framework for detecting data contamination in RL post-trained LLMs, addressing limitations of output-level methods and enabling more reliable assessment of generalization in RL training processes.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training has shown to improve reasoning in large language models (LLMs). However, there has been little exploration on the problem of data contamination in RL post-training, potentially undermining generalization and evaluation reliability of the training process itself. Existing detection methods primarily rely on output-level signals such as likelihood or entropy, which become unreliable for RL-trained models since RL shapes behavior through trajectory-level rewards rather than token likelihoods. We propose LaRA, a layer-wise representation analysis framework for detecting contamination in RL post-trained LLMs. LaRA introduces three complementary metrics, measuring perturbation sensitivity, directional collapse, and local representation rigidity under controlled perturbations. We find that contamination produces progressive geometric deviations across layers, including amplified perturbation sensitivity, stronger directional collapse, and enhanced local rigidity. Based on our findings, we also develop a contamination detection protocol that aggregates representation-level deviations across layers and metrics. Experiments on RL-trained reasoning models show that our protocol outperforms existing output-level baselines for contamination detection.

</details>


### [264] [OVA-IB: One vs All Information Bottleneck for Multi-Modal Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29900)
*Tianchao Li,Shujian Yu,Xinrui Zu,Zhaolong Wei,Jeremy Gummeson,Jack C. P. Cheng,Robert Jenssen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OVA-IB: An Information Bottleneck framework for multi-modal alignment using One-vs-All contrastive learning that optimizes both sufficiency and minimality principles for arbitrary-modality alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing contrastive learning methods mainly focus on aligning pairs of modalities, but multi-modal alignment (beyond two modalities) lacks principled approaches. Pairwise CLIP-style losses don't capture higher-order dependencies, while recent beyond-pairwise methods lack clear criteria for what each modality should preserve vs. compress relative to others.

Method: Proposes OVA-IB (One-vs-All Information Bottleneck) framework that views each modality relative to remaining modalities. Uses Information Bottleneck principle: sufficiency preserves information predictable from other modalities, minimality compresses modality-specific information. Optimizes tractable One-vs-All contrastive lower bound for sufficiency with parameter-free geometry-aware projection score, and tractable upper-bound regularizer for minimality.

Result: Demonstrates strong and robust performance on classification, regression, modality-agnostic evaluation, and cross-modal retrieval benchmarks, showing effectiveness for arbitrary-modality alignment tasks.

Conclusion: OVA-IB provides a principled Information Bottleneck framework for multi-modal alignment that explicitly models both sufficiency and minimality, addressing limitations of pairwise methods and enabling effective alignment across arbitrary numbers of modalities.

Abstract: Contrastive learning is effective for aligning paired views or modalities, but alignment beyond two modalities remains non-trivial and comparatively underexplored. Pairwise CLIP-style losses decompose multi-modal alignment into independent two-way comparisons and therefore do not explicitly model higher-order dependencies among multiple modalities. Recent beyond-pairwise objectives approach this problem from statistical or geometric perspectives, but arbitrary-modality alignment still lacks a principled criterion for defining what each modality should preserve and compress relative to the others. We revisit arbitrary-modality alignment through the Information Bottleneck principle. In multi-modal learning, sufficiency should preserve information predictable from the remaining modalities, while minimality should compress modality-specific information not supported by them. This naturally leads to a One-vs-All view, where each modality is characterized with respect to the remaining modalities. We propose OVA-IB, an Information Bottleneck framework for arbitrary-modality alignment. OVA-IB optimizes a tractable One-vs-All contrastive lower bound for sufficiency connected to a Dual Total Correlation-style objective, uses a parameter-free geometry-aware projection score, and derives a tractable upper-bound regularizer for minimality by bounding each representation's dependence on its own input with representation distributions induced by the remaining modalities. Experiments on classification, regression, modality-agnostic evaluation, and cross-modal retrieval benchmarks demonstrate strong and robust performance.

</details>


### [265] [Plan, Don't Pose: Long Composite Motion Generation with Text-Aligned BFM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29906)
*Nikolay Shvetsov,Maksim Bobrin,Nazar Buzun,Dmitry V. Dylov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Text2BFM: A novel text-to-motion framework that uses pretrained Behavioral Foundation Models as executable motion priors, operating in latent policy space with a text-aligned variational behavioral bottleneck for efficient, robust generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing text-to-motion methods force a single model to handle semantic interpretation, long-horizon structure, and low-level physical realization, making them costly and unreliable for long, compositional, or semantically dense prompts.

Method: Text2BFM aligns natural language with pretrained BFMs in latent policy space, using a text-aligned variational behavioral bottleneck to compress BFM policy-latent sequences into compact motion representations compatible with language. Generation happens in this compact behavioral manifold with a lightweight conditional generator, then decoded into policy latents to drive the frozen BFM.

Result: The framework achieves efficient, robust T2M generation and strong performance on long, compositional textual descriptions by decoupling semantic planning from motion execution.

Conclusion: Text2BFM is the first framework that aligns natural language with pretrained BFMs for T2M generation without heavy end-to-end motion generators, offering a more efficient and reliable approach for complex text-to-motion tasks.

Abstract: Text-to-motion (T2M) generation has broad applications in character animation, virtual avatars, and human-robot interaction. Existing methods typically generate pose trajectories or motion tokens directly from language, forcing a single model to handle semantic interpretation, long-horizon structure, and low-level physical realization. This coupling makes them costly and often unreliable for long, compositional, or semantically dense prompts. We propose Text2BFM, the first framework that aligns natural language with pretrained Behavioral Foundation Models (BFMs) for T2M generation without relying on heavy end-to-end motion generators. Text2BFM operates in the latent policy space of a frozen BFM, using it as an executable motion prior. A text-aligned variational behavioral bottleneck compresses BFM policy-latent sequences into compact motion representations that are compatible with language and preserve long-horizon behavioral structure. Generation is performed in this compact behavioral manifold with a lightweight conditional generator, and the resulting latent encoded behaviors are decoded into policy latents that drive the pretrained frozen BFM. By decoupling semantic planning from motion execution, Text2BFM achieves efficient, robust T2M generation and strong performance on long, compositional textual descriptions.

</details>


### [266] [Reducing Experimental Testing in Space Propulsion Film Cooling Analyses by Pixelwise Generative Image Interpolation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29911)
*Adam T. Müller,Philipp J. Teuffel,Konstantin Manassis,Nicolaj C. Stache*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A machine learning method using lightweight neural networks with positional encoding for image regression from sparse measurements, applied to film cooling studies to reduce physical testing requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the need for extensive physical testing in propulsion system development, particularly for film cooling studies which traditionally require numerous experimental measurements. The goal is to enable efficient optimization of coolant injector configurations with reduced testing resources.

Method: Employs a lightweight feed-forward neural network with positional encoding to generate images conditioned by input parameters. The method takes sparse experimental measurements as input and produces complete images. A knowledge-informed extension is proposed for local adaptability of generated images.

Result: Achieves high image similarity with RMSE < 8% and SSIM > 93% when validated on real and synthetic data. Maintains accuracy with a 30% reduction in measurements. The approach enables efficient optimization of coolant injector configurations.

Conclusion: The proposed machine learning approach significantly reduces required tests while preserving high-quality data, enabling efficient optimization in film cooling studies with potential applications extending beyond aerospace engineering.

Abstract: We propose a machine learning approach for image regression from sparse experimental measurements. We show the application of the proposed method on film cooling studies in propulsion system development, aiming to reduce the need for extensive physical testing. Our method employs a lightweight feed-forward neural network with positional encoding to generate images conditioned by input parameters. Validated on real and synthetic data, it achieves high image similarity (RMSE < 8 %, SSIM > 93 %) while maintaining accuracy with a 30 \% reduction of measurements. We further propose a knowledge-informed extension for local adaptability of the generated images. This approach significantly reduces required tests while preserving high-quality data, enabling efficient optimization of coolant injector configurations with applications beyond aerospace.

</details>


### [267] [Midpoint Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29920)
*Daniil Shlenskii,Nikita Gushchin,Lev Novitskiy,Dmitry V. Dylov,Alexander Korotin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new framework called Midpoint Generative Models (MGM) for training one-step generative models using symmetries in flow matching with linear interpolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for theoretically grounded one-step generative models that can achieve competitive performance while maintaining principled foundations, addressing limitations in existing one-step methods.

Method: Based on symmetry of Flow Matching with linear interpolation: when endpoint distributions coincide, drift field vanishes at midpoint time t=1/2. Introduces Midpoint Divergence as distribution discrepancy measure, extends with randomly flipped interpolations and symmetric stochastic interpolants, and derives variational formulation for training one-step generators.

Result: MGM provides an effective and theoretically grounded approach achieving competitive performance against existing one-step generative modeling methods.

Conclusion: MGM offers a principled framework for training one-step generative models that is both effective in practice and grounded in mathematical theory, representing a promising direction for efficient generative modeling.

Abstract: We introduce Midpoint Generative Models (MGM), a principled framework for training one-step generative models. MGM is based on a simple symmetry of Flow Matching with linear interpolation: when the two endpoint distributions coincide, the corresponding drift field vanishes at the midpoint time, $t=1/2$. We show that the norm of this field defines a valid discrepancy between distributions, which we call the Midpoint Divergence. We extend this discrepancy beyond the midpoint by introducing randomly flipped interpolations and further generalize it by replacing deterministic linear Flow Matching interpolations with symmetric stochastic interpolants, yielding a generalized Midpoint Divergence. Finally, we derive a variational formulation of our generalized divergence, yielding a tractable objective for training a one-step generator. The resulting MGM algorithm offers an effective and theoretically grounded approach to generative modeling, achieving competitive performance against existing one-step generative modeling methods.

</details>


### [268] [A Triple-Modal Contrastive Learning Framework with Sequence, Graph, and 3D Features for Drug-Target Interaction Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29926)
*Le Xu,Xi Zhang,Dan Luo,Ting Wang,Xuan Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TriMod-DTI is a triple-modal contrastive learning framework that integrates 1D sequences, 2D graphs, and 3D structures for drug-target interaction prediction, outperforming existing methods by leveraging complementary multimodal features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current drug-target interaction prediction methods are limited by using only single or dual modalities (sequences or graphs), ignoring important 3D structural information, which creates a gap in capturing comprehensive molecular features for accurate DTI prediction.

Method: Proposes TriMod-DTI with: 1) Feature Extractor capturing drug and target features across 1D sequences, 2D graphs, and 3D structures; 2) Triple-modal contrastive learning strategy aligning different modal representations in latent space using cross-modal positive/negative sample pairs to enhance discriminative ability.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on three benchmark datasets. Ablation studies confirm contributions of each modality. Case studies demonstrate practical potential for DTI prediction and drug discovery applications.

Conclusion: TriMod-DTI effectively integrates triple-modal information (1D, 2D, 3D) for comprehensive drug and protein representations, demonstrating superior performance and practical utility in drug-target interaction prediction through multimodal fusion and contrastive learning.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of drug-target interactions (DTI) is critical for drug discovery. Existing methods often rely on single-modal representations (e.g., sequences or graphs) or combine only two modalities, overlooking 3D structural features. To address this challenge, we propose TriMod-DTI, a triple-modal contrastive learning framework that incorporates 1D sequences, 2D graphs, and 3D structures of drugs and proteins, obtaining the universal and complementary feature representations for DTI prediction. We design a Feature Extractor to capture drug and target features across the three modalities, thereby enriching their representations. We further propose a triple-modal contrastive learning strategy to align different modal representations of the same drug or protein in the latent space. By constructing cross-modal positive and negative sample pairs, this approach enhances the model's discriminative ability. Experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that TriMod-DTI outperforms state-of-the-art methods. The ablation studies validate the contributions of each modality. Moreover, case studies highlight its practical potential for DTI prediction and drug discovery.

</details>


### [269] [Treatment-Conditioned Diffusion for Forecasting Neurodegenerative Disease Progression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29932)
*Danylo Boiko,Viktoriia Mishkurova*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A diffusion model framework for predicting future brain states in Parkinson's disease using treatment-conditioned neuroimaging that preserves anatomical details better than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current systems for forecasting neurodegenerative disease progression produce scalar clinical scores that ignore longitudinal neuroimaging data, while traditional generative approaches lose anatomical details and blur subtle progression patterns, limiting their clinical utility for personalized therapeutic planning.

Method: A novel treatment-conditioned diffusion framework that predicts high-fidelity future brain states by conditioning on patients' screening DaTscan images and levodopa equivalent daily dose over one year. Uses a Transformer-based encoder for non-linear, time-dependent pharmacological dynamics and optimizes generation with a multi-weight region-of-interest mask focusing on biologically critical areas.

Result: The framework maintains sharp anatomical boundaries and significantly improves clinical fidelity compared to baseline methods, achieving 14.0% lower MSE, 7.2% lower MAE, and 4.9% higher SSIM in experimental evaluations.

Conclusion: The proposed treatment-conditioned diffusion framework represents a significant advancement in neurodegenerative disease forecasting by preserving anatomical details and capturing subtle progression patterns, enabling more effective long-term planning and personalized therapeutic interventions for Parkinson's disease.

Abstract: Forecasting the progression of neurodegenerative diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, is essential for effective long-term planning and personalized therapeutic intervention. Existing systems typically produce scalar clinical scores that ignore the rich structure of longitudinal neuroimaging, while traditional generative approaches suffer from a loss of anatomical details and blurring subtle progression patterns. To address this, we introduce a novel treatment-conditioned diffusion framework that predicts high-fidelity future brain states by conditioning the generative process on patients' screening DaTscan images and levodopa equivalent daily dose over one year. The pipeline uses a Transformer-based encoder to represent non-linear, time-dependent pharmacological dynamics and optimizes generation through a multi-weight region-of-interest mask that focuses on biologically critical areas. Experimental evaluation shows that our framework maintains sharp anatomical boundaries and significantly improves clinical fidelity relative to the baseline, achieving 14.0% lower MSE, 7.2% lower MAE, and 4.9% higher SSIM.

</details>


### [270] [CLUBench: A Clustering Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29933)
*Feng Xiao,Dazhi Fu,Chris Ding,Jicong Fan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CLUBench is a comprehensive clustering benchmark evaluating 24 diverse algorithms on 131 datasets across tabular, text, and image data, providing systematic insights into algorithm performance, pretrained embeddings, and practical deployment guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite extensive clustering research, there's a lack of large-scale empirical evaluation that jointly considers conventional algorithms, deep learning methods, and foundation model-based approaches, limiting guidance for practical algorithm selection and deployment.

Method: Introduces CLUBench benchmark with 24 algorithms of diverse principles evaluated on 131 datasets across tabular, text, and image data, conducting 178,815 experiments. Analyzes hyperparameter tuning impact, data type effects, pretrained embeddings, LLM-based clustering, algorithm similarity, and low-rank performance matrix structures.

Result: Key findings: 1) Deep clustering methods show no significant advantage over top conventional algorithms (KMeans, SpeClu) on average; 2) Pretrained embeddings combined with conventional algorithms are effective for image/text clustering; 3) Clustering remains challenging even with foundation models. Low-rank structure in performance matrices enables efficient approximation for practical applications.

Conclusion: CLUBench provides comprehensive empirical insights for clustering research and practice, demonstrating that conventional algorithms remain competitive, pretrained embeddings enhance performance efficiently, and low-rank structures in performance matrices enable practical model selection and evaluation.

Abstract: Clustering is a fundamental problem in data science with a long-standing research history, yielding numerous insightful algorithms. Despite this progress, a systematic and large-scale empirical evaluation that jointly considers conventional algorithms, deep learning-based methods, and recent foundation model-based clustering remains largely absent, leading to limited guidance on algorithm selection and deployment. To address this gap, we introduce CLUBench, a comprehensive clustering benchmark comprising 24 algorithms of diverse principles evaluated on 131 datasets across tabular, text, and image data, involving 178,815 experiments. Importantly, our analyses of (i) the impact of hyperparameter tuning,(ii) the impact of data types and characteristics,(iii) the impact of pretrained embeddings,(iv) large language model-based clustering,(v) the similarity of algorithms, and (vi) the low-rank structures of performance matrices, yield meaningful insights and promising pathways for clustering research. For instance, our study reveals that: 1) All evaluated deep clustering methods do not exhibit a significant advantage compared with the top-performing conventional clustering algorithms (e.g., KMeans, SpeClu) in terms of average performance; 2) For image and text clustering tasks, combining pretrained embeddings with conventional clustering algorithms (e.g., KMeans, SpeClu) offers effective and efficient clustering; 3) Clustering remains a challenging and nontrivial problem, even in the era of increasingly dominant foundation models. Moreover, we propose to use the low-rank structure in cross-model performance matrices to efficiently approximate the overall performance evaluation in practical applications. We further demonstrate the feasibility of model selection based on the performance matrices across all hyperparameter configurations.

</details>


### [271] [From Short Histories to Long Futures: Horizon-Aware Graph Neural Networks for Long Horizon Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29952)
*Zesheng Liu,Maryam Rahnemoonfar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-horizon graph neural network emulator for long-range ice sheet prediction that directly predicts future states at multiple lead times simultaneously, improving stability and accuracy over traditional autoregressive approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-range geophysical prediction is challenging due to nonlinear dynamics, computational costs of full-physics simulations, and error accumulation in autoregressive surrogates. Current neural network emulators often drift or become unstable when rolled out over decades.

Method: Proposes a multi-horizon graph neural network where the physical domain is represented as a graph (nodes=spatial locations, edges=local interactions). The model learns to predict future evolution (ice thickness and velocities) at multiple lead times from a single current state using a shared graph backbone with separate output branches. Uses state increment prediction for stability and employs coarse-to-fine rollout strategy during inference.

Result: Experiments on multi-decadal Pine Island Glacier simulations show higher long-range accuracy and improved stability compared to both initial-state baselines and standard single-step autoregressive rollouts.

Conclusion: The multi-horizon graph neural network approach produces a more reliable emulator for downstream climate and sea-level studies by reducing drift, avoiding redundant computation, and maintaining stability over long forecast horizons.

Abstract: Accurate long-range prediction of geophysical systems is difficult due to strongly nonlinear dynamics, the high computational cost of full-physics simulations, and the error accumulation that arise when one-step autoregressive surrogates are rolled out over decades. Deep neural network can serve as efficient emulators, but most are trained only for next-step prediction and often drift or become unstable as the forecast horizon grows. We propose a multi-horizon graph neural network emulator that learns state-to-state transitions from a single current time to multiple future lead times within one unified model. The physical domain is represented as a graph, where nodes correspond to spatial locations with time-varying geophysical attributes and edges encode local spatial interactions. Given the current graph state, the model predicts the future evolution of key fields, ice thickness and ice velocities at all nodes, using a shared graph backbone with separate output branches for each target variable. To improve stability, the network predicts state increments relative to the current state, which are then added back to reconstruct future states. Training jointly optimizes all lead times with a unified regression objective, and inference uses a coarse-to-fine rollout that advances with larger jumps and selectively refines with shorter jumps to reduce drift and avoid redundant computation. Experiments on multi-decadal Pine Island Glacier simulations show that our approach achieves higher long-range accuracy and improved stability than both (i) an initial-state baseline that predicts each future time directly from the starting state and (ii) a standard single-step autoregressive rollout, producing a more reliable emulator for downstream climate and sea-level studies.

</details>


### [272] [A Fully Convolutional Approach to Denoising Structural Dynamics Data from X-Ray Photon Correlation Spectroscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29975)
*Nisar Nellikunnummel,Andi Barbour,Lutz Wiegart,Tatiana Konstantinova,Anthony DeGennaro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FC-DAE (Fully Convolutional Denoising Autoencoder) denoises 2-time correlation functions in XPCS, handles variable input sizes, preserves correlation structures, and works well in low signal-to-noise conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for effective denoising of X-ray photon correlation spectroscopy (XPCS) data under low signal-to-noise conditions, particularly in photon-limited and low-dose measurement scenarios where conventional methods may fail to preserve intricate dynamical features.

Method: Developed a fully convolutional denoising autoencoder (FC-DAE) that accepts arbitrary input dimensions while preserving correlation structures. Trained on experimentally derived C₂ data from NSLS-II beamlines with data augmentation to expand dataset diversity and reduce overfitting.

Result: FC-DAE successfully recovers intricate dynamical features in low signal-to-noise conditions while maintaining structural fidelity. Quantitative metrics demonstrate robust denoising performance with high computational efficiency, enabling recovery of XPCS dynamics under challenging measurement conditions.

Conclusion: The FC-DAE provides a computationally efficient and reliable denoising solution for XPCS data that outperforms conventional methods in preserving dynamical features under low signal-to-noise conditions, with potential applications in photon-limited experimental scenarios.

Abstract: We present a fully convolutional denoising autoencoder (FC-DAE) for denoising two-time intensity-intensity correlation functions ($C_2$) in X-ray photon correlation spectroscopy (XPCS). Unlike conventional denoising autoencoders that are typically restricted to fixed input sizes, the FC-DAE accepts inputs of arbitrary dimensions while preserving correlation structures across diverse dynamical regimes. The model is trained using experimentally derived $C_2$ data collected at NSLS-II beamlines, with data augmentation applied to expand the diversity of the dataset and reduce overfitting. The FC-DAE successfully recovers intricate dynamical features in low signal-to-noise conditions while maintaining structural fidelity. To assess reconstruction reliability, we employ quantitative metrics to evaluate structural fidelity and identify potential model-induced bias. Our results demonstrate that the FC-DAE provides robust denoising performance with high computational efficiency, enabling recovery of XPCS dynamics under photon-limited and low-dose measurement conditions.

</details>


### [273] [Improving Adversarial Robustness of Attribution via Implicit Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29983)
*Amir Mehrpanah,Matteo Gamba,Hossein Azizpour*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SGD learning dynamics can implicitly provide attribution robustness without explicit regularization, but this fails for softmax attention due to entropy constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches for adversarial robustness of attributions rely on computationally expensive explicit regularization, and there's a need for more efficient mechanisms for reliable explainability in deep learning.

Method: Theoretical analysis connecting parameter-space and input-space curvature to show attribution robustness arises implicitly from standard SGD learning dynamics. Experimental validation across architectures, datasets, and attribution methods with negligible computational overhead.

Result: Standard SGD learning dynamics provides implicit attribution robustness (validated experimentally), but this robustness does not transfer to attention-based attribution under softmax normalization due to inherent entropy constraints. Replacing softmax attention with kernel-based attention restores robustness gains in transformers.

Conclusion: Learning dynamics serves as a principled and practical mechanism for robust explainability, but attention-based attribution under softmax normalization has fundamental limitations that can be overcome with kernel-based attention.

Abstract: The adversarial robustness of attributions is a fundamental requirement for reliable explainability in deep learning, yet existing approaches typically rely on computationally expensive explicit regularization. In this work, we show that attribution robustness can arise implicitly from the learning dynamics of standard stochastic gradient descent. We theoretically motivate this effect through connections between parameter-space and input-space curvature, and validate it across architectures, datasets, and attribution methods, with negligible computational overhead. In contrast, we prove that such robustness gains often does not transfer to attention-based attribution under softmax normalization, due to inherent entropy constraints, and we validate this limitation experimentally. Finally, we show that replacing softmax attention with kernel-based attention restores the robustness gains in transformer models. Our results highlight learning dynamics as a principled and practical mechanism for robust explainability, and reveal fundamental limitations of attention-based attribution under normalization.

</details>


### [274] [MIC: Maximizing Informational Capacity in Adaptive Representations via Isotropic Subspace Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29987)
*Dang Hong Nguyen,Nhi Ngoc-Yen Nguyen,Huy-Hieu Pham*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MIC is a framework that optimizes multi-granular embeddings through isotropic subspace alignment, addressing redundancy and spectral collapse via Soft Collapse and Spectral Isotropy Regularizations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-scale representation learning with nested subspaces suffers from dimensional redundancy and spectral collapse, particularly in high-compression scenarios where maintaining informational capacity is critical.

Method: Introduces MIC framework with two key components: Soft Collapse Regularization (SCR) to mitigate redundancy between prefix and residual subspaces via cross-correlation penalties, and Spectral Isotropy Regularization (SIR) to ensure hyper-spherical uniformity in low-dimensional prefixes. These are unified through a self-distillation objective.

Result: MIC significantly outperforms standard baselines, particularly in high-compression scenarios where maintaining informational capacity is most critical.

Conclusion: MIC successfully addresses redundancy and collapse issues in multi-scale representations by optimizing the geometric landscape through isotropic subspace alignment, generating semantically dense embeddings with high discriminative power.

Abstract: Although multi-scales representation learning enables elastic-dimension embeddings, nested subspaces often suffer from dimensional redundancy and spectral collapse. To address this, we introduce MIC, a framework that optimizes the geometric landscape of multi-granular embeddings through isotropic subspace alignment. MIC employs Soft Collapse Regularization (SCR) to mitigate redundancy between prefix and residual subspaces via cross-correlation penalties, alongside Spectral Isotropy Regularization (SIR) to ensure hyper-spherical uniformity in low-dimensional prefixes. By unifying these strategies through a self-distillation objective, MIC generates semantically dense representations that maintain high discriminative power. Our experiments demonstrate that MIC significantly outperforms standard baselines, particularly in high-compression scenarios where maintaining informational capacity is most critical.

</details>


### [275] [Test Time Training for Supervised Causal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30015)
*Zizhen Deng,Jiaru Zhang,Rui Ding,Huang Bojun,Jinzhuo Wang,Qiang Fu,Shi Han,Dongmei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TTT-SCL overcomes generalization limitations of Supervised Causal Learning by dynamically generating test-aligned training sets, demonstrating superior performance across various datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Supervised Causal Learning (SCL) faces significant limitations in real-world applications due to poor out-of-distribution generalization, including performance gaps between synthetic and real data, fragility to distribution shifts, and failure in compositional generalization.

Method: TTT-SCL dynamically generates training sets explicitly aligned with specific test instances, leveraging connections to score-based methods and designing an efficient module based on classic scoring functions to create instance-specific training data.

Result: TTT-SCL significantly outperforms existing SCL and traditional causal discovery methods across synthetic benchmarks, pseudo-real, and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: Test-Time Training for Supervised Causal Learning (TTT-SCL) is an effective framework that addresses the generalization challenges of traditional SCL methods and outperforms existing approaches across diverse datasets, demonstrating its potential for real-world causal discovery applications.

Abstract: Supervised Causal Learning (SCL) has shown promise in causal discovery by framing it as a supervised learning problem. However, it suffers from significant out-of-distribution generalization challenges. We reveal three limitations of previous SCL practices: a significant performance gap between synthetic benchmarks and real-world data, fragility to distribution shifts, and failure in compositional generalization, collectively questioning its real-world applicability. To address this, we propose Test-Time Training for Supervised Causal Learning (TTT-SCL), a novel framework that dynamically generates training sets explicitly aligned with any specific test instance. We demonstrate the correlation between TTT-SCL and score-based methods, and design an efficient module for generating training sets based on the classic scoring function. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks, pseudo-real and real-world datasets demonstrate that TTT-SCL significantly outperforms existing SCL and traditional causal discovery methods.

</details>


### [276] [Alignment-Guided Score Matching for Text-to-Image Alignment in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30038)
*Jaa-Yeon Lee,Yeobin Hong,Taesung Kwon,Jong Chul Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight, reward-free post-training method improves text-image alignment in diffusion models by integrating contrastive alignment guidance into the score-matching objective, mitigating over-counting and repetition issues, and achieving >35% improvement in counting accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models generate highly realistic images but often struggle with precise text-image alignment. Recent post-training methods rely on external rewards or human preference signals, which heavily depend on reward quality and do not directly address alignment within the diffusion process itself. Reward-free approaches like SoftREPA improve alignment but their contrastive formulation can excessively penalize negative pairs, leading to characteristic failure cases such as over-counting and repetition.

Method: A lightweight, reward-free post-training method that refines soft tokens by integrating contrastive alignment guidance directly into the score-matching objective of diffusion models, assigning alignment directions at the score level.

Result: Experiments show that the method matches SoftREPA while substantially improving its failure cases, achieving over 35% improvement in counting accuracy on the GenEval benchmark. The method is seamlessly applicable to existing diffusion backbones (SD1.5, SDXL, and SD3) and is complementary to existing RL-based diffusion post-training methods.

Conclusion: The proposed approach mitigates the limitations of contrastive formulations, yielding more coherent and semantically faithful generations.

Abstract: Diffusion models generate highly realistic images but often struggle with precise text-image alignment. While recent post-training methods improve alignment using external rewards or human preference signals, their performance heavily depends on reward quality and does not directly address alignment within the diffusion process itself. Recent reward-free approaches such as SoftREPA demonstrate that optimizing soft text tokens via contrastive learning can effectively improve text-image representation alignment, outperforming standard parameter-efficient fine-tuning baselines. However, the contrastive formulation can excessively penalize negative pairs, which manifests as characteristic failure cases such as over-counting and repetition. To address this issue, we propose a lightweight, reward-free post-training method that refines soft tokens by integrating contrastive alignment guidance directly into the score-matching objective of diffusion models. By assigning alignment directions at the score level, our approach mitigates these limitations and yields more coherent and semantically faithful generations. Experiments show that our method matches SoftREPA while substantially improving its failure cases, achieving over 35% improvement in counting accuracy on the GenEval benchmark. Our method is seamlessly applicable to existing diffusion backbones (SD1.5, SDXL, and SD3), and is complementary to existing RL-based diffusion post-training methods. Project page: https://jaayeon.github.io/AGSM

</details>


### [277] [Masked Diffusion Modeling for Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30046)
*Lixing Zhang,Yuchen Liang,Liyan Xie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MaskDiff-AD: A masked diffusion model approach for anomaly detection that learns to reconstruct randomly masked discrete data without reverse-time sampling, achieving state-of-the-art performance on categorical, mixed-type, and text data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Anomaly detection for categorical, mixed-type, and discrete sequence data remains challenging and underexplored, despite being central to safety-critical applications. Existing methods struggle with these data types that don't fit well into continuous space representations.

Method: MaskDiff-AD uses masked diffusion models trained only on nominal data, constructing anomaly scores from the difficulty of reconstructing randomly masked coordinates. It operates directly on discrete state spaces without reverse-time sampling, with a non-parametric variant also developed.

Result: Experiments on 14 categorical/mixed-type tabular datasets and 4 text anomaly detection datasets show MaskDiff-AD achieves competitive performance, achieving the best overall average rank and outperforming all 12 tabular baseline methods.

Conclusion: MaskDiff-AD provides an effective forward-only approach for anomaly detection on discrete data types, with theoretical error guarantees and strong empirical performance across multiple domains.

Abstract: Anomaly detection aims to identify samples that deviate from the nominal data distribution and is central to many safety-critical applications. However, developing effective anomaly detection methods for categorical, mixed-type, and discrete sequence data remains challenging and relatively underexplored. Masked diffusion models provide a natural way to model such data by learning to recover masked values from the remaining visible context. In this paper, we propose Masked Diffusion for Anomaly Detection (MaskDiff-AD), a forward-only method based on masked diffusion models trained only on nominal data. Given a test sample, MaskDiff-AD constructs anomaly scores from the difficulty of reconstructing randomly masked coordinates, yielding a content-sensitive score that operates directly on discrete state spaces while avoiding reverse-time sampling. We also develop a non-parametric variant of MaskDiff-AD and provide theoretical guarantees by characterizing Type-I and Type-II errors under a fixed detection threshold. Experiments on fourteen categorical and mixed-type tabular datasets from ADBench and UADAD, as well as four text anomaly detection datasets from NLP-ADBench, show that MaskDiff-AD achieves competitive performance against classical, diffusion-based, and recent tabular/text anomaly detection baselines. Notably, MaskDiff-AD achieves the best overall average rank, outperforming all twelve tabular baseline methods.

</details>


### [278] [Ridge Regression from Poisson Resetting: A Renewal Perspective on Spectral Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30059)
*Petar Jolakoski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Stochastic resetting from non-equilibrium physics is connected to ridge regularization in machine learning, showing that Poisson resetting of gradient flow produces the exact ridge estimator, while non-exponential reset laws generate alternative spectral filters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to bridge the gap between stochastic resetting concepts from non-equilibrium statistical physics and ridge regularization techniques in statistical learning, exploring how different resetting mechanisms can produce various regularization effects.

Method: The authors analyze continuous-time gradient flow with isotropic resetting on quadratic objectives. They study Poisson resetting (exponential inter-reset times) and extend to general renewal reset laws. They also examine an additive Ornstein-Uhlenbeck extension with constant diffusion as a stylized SGD approximation.

Result: 1) Poisson resetting at rate r produces stationary mean identical to ridge estimator with penalty λ=r via Laplace-transform relationship. 2) Exponential reset time distribution is unique renewal law that reproduces scalar ridge in every eigendirection. 3) Non-exponential renewal laws generate alternative spectral filters. 4) In the OU extension with additive noise, equality holds only at mean level due to nonzero stationary covariance from accumulated noise and reset-timing variance.

Conclusion: Stochastic resetting provides a physical interpretation and generalization of ridge regularization, where different resetting mechanisms correspond to different spectral filtering approaches. The exponential reset distribution uniquely reproduces ridge regularization, while other renewal laws offer alternative regularization schemes that can differ predictively from standard ridge.

Abstract: We connect stochastic resetting from non-equilibrium statistical physics with ridge regularization in statistical learning. For linear gradient flow, resetting to the origin at rate $r$ produces stationary mean $(X^\top X+rI)^{-1}X^\top y$, exactly the ridge estimator with penalty $λ=r$. This uses the known Laplace-transform relationship between ridge regression and exponential-time averaging of gradient flow, with the exponential time now interpreted as the stationary age associated with Poisson resetting. We then extend this identity to general renewal reset laws: the exponential reset time distribution is the unique renewal law whose stationary mean reproduces scalar ridge in every eigendirection as an exact filter identity for every positive curvature, while non-exponential renewal laws generate alternative spectral filters. At the fluctuation level, we study a separate additive Ornstein-Uhlenbeck extension with constant diffusion, interpreted as a stylized SGD approximation. In this setting, the equality holds only at the level of the mean, since the reset process has a nonzero stationary covariance from accumulated OU noise and reset-timing variance, whereas deterministic ridge is a fixed estimator with the same center. Stylized experiments compare the deterministic renewal-induced filters directly and illustrate when filters induced by non-exponential reset-time laws can differ predictively from ridge. The results for the stationary mean and the induced spectral filters are established for continuous-time gradient flow with isotropic resetting on quadratic objectives; the covariance and risk formulas additionally assume additive noise with state-independent covariance.

</details>


### [279] [A Predictive Law for On-Policy Self-Distillation From World Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30070)
*Tommy He,Jerome Sieber,Matteo Saponati*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPSD performance can be predicted before training using initial performance gaps, enabling principled incorporation of world feedback in RL post-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the reliability of OPSD compared to established methods like GRPO and find ways to incorporate richer world feedback into scalable RL post-training.

Method: Analyzed correlations between initial student-self-teacher performance gaps and final performance improvements across various contexts and model families.

Result: Discovered a strikingly consistent linear correlation between initial performance gaps and final improvement in OPSD that holds across contexts, model families, and scales.

Conclusion: The paper's findings demonstrate that OPSD performance can be reliably predicted before training based on initial performance gaps, offering a principled method to incorporate world feedback into post-training pipelines.

Abstract: Moving beyond simple scalar rewards toward richer world feedback is a natural path to more scalable RL post-training. On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) is a promising recent approach that uses arbitrary feedback as learning signal, yet its reliability compared to established methods, such as GRPO, remains unclear. We identify a strikingly consistent linear correlation between the initial student-self-teacher performance gap and the final performance improvement in OPSD. This relationship holds across context types and model families, providing a powerful predictive law for anticipating the outcome of an OPSD configuration without running the full training procedure. Interestingly, we show that this linear predictability holds with model scale, suggesting a potential basis for new empirical scaling laws on larger models with stronger in-context learning capabilities. In essence, our findings show that OPSD performance can be predicted and tuned before training, offering a principled way to incorporate world feedback as a first-class component of the post-training pipeline.

</details>


### [280] [Q-ANCHOR: Federated Quantum Learning with ZNE-guided Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30075)
*Hoang M. Ngo,Quan Nguyen,Wanli Xing,My T. Thai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Q-ANCHOR tackles quantum hardware bias and client drift in Quantum Federated Learning through zero-noise extrapolation and stateful client correction, outperforming standard FedAvg.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantum Federated Learning faces a severe double-drift phenomenon when deployed on practical hardware: the global model suffers from client drift due to non-IID data and hardware bias from noisy quantum gradient estimates. Standard FedAvg aggregation cannot correct these issues, leading to persistent error floors.

Method: The authors mathematically analyze FedAvg convergence under realistic quantum hardware conditions, then propose Q-ANCHOR - a quantum-aware federated aggregation architecture that uses zero-noise extrapolation to anchor server updates and applies stateful client correction to suppress client drift and hardware bias.

Result: Convergence analysis proves Q-ANCHOR mitigates client drift while reducing hardware-bias floor. Experimental results demonstrate Q-ANCHOR achieves significantly more stable training than conventional FL baselines.

Conclusion: The authors have proposed Q-ANCHOR to overcome both client drift and hardware bias issues in Quantum Federated Learning, with both theoretical convergence guarantees and experimental validation showing improved stability and performance.

Abstract: Quantum Federated Learning (QFL) offers a promising framework to train quantum models across distributed clients while keeping data strictly local. Due to its simplicity and low communication overhead, Federated Averaging (FedAvg) is the standard aggregation choice in QFL literature. However, deploying QFL on practical hardware exposes a severe double-drift phenomenon: the global model is simultaneously derailed by client drift from non-IID data and hardware bias from noisy quantum gradient estimates. In this work, we first analyze the convergence of FedAvg under these realistic conditions, mathematically demonstrating that quantum hardware bias creates a persistent error floor that standard averaging cannot correct. To overcome this limitation, we propose Q-ANCHOR, a quantum-aware federated aggregation architecture that anchors server updates with zero-noise extrapolation while applying stateful client correction to suppress both client drift and hardware-induced bias. Our convergence theory proves that Q-ANCHOR successfully mitigates classical client drift while actively reducing the hardware-bias floor. Experimental results demonstrate that Q-ANCHOR achieves significantly more stable training than conventional FL baselines.

</details>


### [281] [Distributionally Robust Set Representation Learning Under Inference-Time Element Corruption](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30089)
*Yankai Chen,Hanrong Zhang,Bowei He,Philip S. Yu,Xue,Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SW-DRSO: A distributionally robust optimization framework for set representation learning that handles inference-time element corruption through a barycentric adversary approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard set representation learning methods perform well on curated data but fail when deployed models encounter element-level degradations like outliers or missing components at inference time, which can distort set representations and degrade performance.

Method: Proposes SW-DRSO (distributionally robust optimization framework for sets) that optimizes a tractable surrogate of worst-case expected loss over plausible inference-time variations. Introduces a barycentric adversary that approximates the intractable search over corrupted sets through differentiable training-time optimization over simplex weights.

Result: Extensive experiments across four tasks demonstrate that SW-DRSO effectively enhances robustness against corruption while maintaining high overall performance.

Conclusion: SW-DRSO provides a practical distributionally robust optimization approach for set representation learning that addresses real-world deployment challenges of element corruption, offering improved robustness without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: Standard Set Representation Learning methods typically excel on curated data but often overlook the challenge of inference-time element corruption. This refers to scenarios where deployed models encounter element-level degradations, such as outliers or missing components, that may distort set representation and degrade performance. We propose SW-DRSO, a distributionally robust optimization framework tailored for sets. Rather than minimizing loss solely on observed training data, SW-DRSO optimizes a tractable surrogate of the worst-case expected loss over a family of plausible inference-time variations. We introduce a barycentric adversary that approximates the intractable search over corrupted sets by a differentiable training-time optimization over simplex weights. Extensive experiments across four tasks demonstrate that SW-DRSO effectively enhances robustness against corruption while maintaining high overall performance.

</details>


### [282] [Chess-World-Model: A 10M-Game Benchmark for Exact State Tracking from Chess Move Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30100)
*Benjamin Walker,Terry Lyons*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Chess-World-Model, a large-scale benchmark for state tracking built from real chess games, showing recurrent models outperform Transformers and out-of-distribution testing reveals limitations hidden by model scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing state-tracking benchmarks are often synthetic or language-based, limiting their value as tests of structured state updates in realistic domains. There is a need for a practical large-scale benchmark that can properly evaluate state tracking abilities in realistic settings.

Method: The paper introduces Chess-World-Model benchmark built from 10 million real chess games, with two splits: held-out real-game split and out-of-distribution split from uniformly random legal play. Four models are benchmarked under matched interface and training protocol: causal Transformer, block-diagonal SLiCE, Mamba-3, and Gated DeltaNet with negative eigenvalues.

Result: Recurrent models strongly outperform Transformers at 3 and 8 million parameters. Real-game performance saturates above 18 million parameters, but the random-uniform split remains discriminative up to 40 million, exposing failures otherwise hidden by scale. Ablations show that less expressive state-transition mechanisms reduce performance on the out-of-distribution split for all three recurrent models.

Conclusion: Chess-World-Model is established as a practical large-scale benchmark for state tracking that exposes failures that model scale would otherwise conceal, with recurrent models outperforming Transformers and out-of-distribution testing revealing important limitations.

Abstract: World models require state tracking, which is the ability to maintain a correct latent state across action sequences. Existing benchmarks are often synthetic or language-based, limiting their value as tests of structured state updates in realistic domains. We introduce Chess-World-Model, a large-scale state-tracking benchmark built from 10 million real chess games, where models predict the exact board state reached after a sequence of legal moves. Alongside a held-out real-game split, we include an out-of-distribution split from uniformly random legal play, which tests whether models learn the transition rules rather than shortcuts from common human positions. Prior theoretical and empirical work has shown that Transformers struggle to state-track, while input-dependent linear RNNs require expressive state-transition matrices to do so. We therefore benchmark a causal Transformer, block-diagonal SLiCE, Mamba-3, and Gated DeltaNet with negative eigenvalues under a matched interface and training protocol. The recurrent models strongly outperform the Transformer at 3 and 8 million parameters. Real-game performance saturates above 18 million parameters, but the random-uniform split remains discriminative up to 40 million, exposing failures otherwise hidden by scale. Additionally, ablations show that less expressive state-transition mechanisms reduce performance on the out-of-distribution split for all three recurrent models. Together, these results establish Chess-World-Model as a practical large-scale benchmark for state tracking that exposes failures model scale would otherwise conceal.

</details>


### [283] [Convergence Theory for Iterative LLM-Based Neural Architecture Search: A Parametric Cross-Entropy Framework with Closed-Form Proxy Reliability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30103)
*Santosh Premi Adhikari,Radu Timofte,Dmitry Ignatov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-based Neural Architecture Search (NAS) is modeled as a parametric Cross-Entropy method, with convergence theory developed showing monotonic improvement, geometric convergence rates, and analysis of generation strategies and proxy reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models are increasingly used as generators in iterative neural architecture search, but there's no formal convergence theory for this class of algorithms, leaving performance guarantees and reliability unclear.

Method: Models iterative LLM-NAS as a parametric Cross-Entropy method over executable programs, analyzes convergence properties, compares delta-based vs full-code generation strategies, introduces MinHash-Jaccard novelty filter, and derives proxy reliability formulas.

Result: Proves six theoretical results: (1) equivalence to CE update, (2) monotonic quality improvement, (3) geometric convergence, (4) delta-based generation superiority, (5) novelty filter prevents collapse, (6) proxy reliability closed-form. Experimental validation on 22 cycles with 3,300 architectures confirms predictions.

Conclusion: LLM-NAS can be formally analyzed as parametric Cross-Entropy method with proven convergence guarantees, providing theoretical foundation for design choices and explaining empirically observed proxy reliability limitations.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as generators in iterative neural architecture search (NAS), yet no formal convergence theory exists for this class of algorithms. We model iterative LLM-NAS as a parametric Cross-Entropy (CE) method over executable programs and prove six results: (1) iterative LLM fine-tuning on elite architectures is equivalent to the CE update restricted to the LLM parametric family; (2) expected architecture quality is monotonically non-decreasing across cycles; (3) elite-set probability converges to a fixed point at a geometric rate C_t >= 1-(1-rho_0)^t; (4) delta-based generation achieves a strictly higher valid-generation rate than full-code generation under a first-order Markov token-error model; (5) the MinHash-Jaccard novelty filter prevents mode collapse; (6) proxy reliability admits the closed-form rho_S = (6/pi) arcsin(rho_P(SNR)/2), yielding the practical diagnostic sigma^2_arch >> sigma^2_noise as a necessary condition for trustworthy proxy-based rankings. Testing against a 22-cycle, three-LLM, six-dataset experiment with 3,300 generated architectures confirms two predictions quantitatively, two at direction-of-effect level, and explains the proxy-reliability ceiling effect previously reported empirically but left unexplained.

</details>


### [284] [Striding Across Reynolds Numbers: Representation Geometry in Neural PDE Generalisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30112)
*Jianing Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural PDE solvers struggle with cross-Reynolds generalization, but matching states in source-trained latent spaces and borrowing source-regime dynamics can achieve decent transfer performance without target-regime data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-Reynolds generalization in neural PDE solvers remains poorly characterized, and current methods show high error rates (46.68% relative L2 error) under Reynolds-number shifts. The paper investigates whether representation geometry is a key organizing variable for transfer learning across different Reynolds regimes.

Method: The authors propose ConvAE-Relay, which matches states in a source-trained convolutional autoencoder latent space and borrows dynamics from a source-regime database without target-regime fitting. They conduct ablation studies, oracle experiments, and compare with a U-Net with multi-scale skip connections to understand representation effects.

Result: ConvAE-Relay achieves 38.34±0.07% error using only source-regime data, outperforming baselines. Oracle experiments show source-regime dynamics remain transferable (cosine similarity ~0.84) when matching stays on-manifold. Autoregressive drift is identified as the primary bottleneck (~12 percentage points). The U-Net achieves 34.72±0.60% error, supporting that local, multi-scale representations organize cross-Reynolds transfer.

Conclusion: Representation geometry significantly organizes cross-Reynolds transfer in neural PDE solvers. Local, multi-scale representations appear effective for generalization, and source-regime dynamics remain transferable when state matching stays on-manifold, with autoregressive drift being the main performance bottleneck.

Abstract: Cross-Reynolds generalisation in neural PDE solvers remains poorly characterised. On the canonical forced 2D Navier-Stokes benchmark, a trained Fourier Neural Operator reaches 46.68% relative L2 error under a 10x Reynolds-number shift, yet zero-forward-model retrieval baselines already improve to 41-42%. This suggests representation geometry as a major organising variable among the tested methods. We test this hypothesis through ConvAE-Relay, which matches states in a source-trained convolutional autoencoder latent space and borrows dynamics from a source-regime database, achieving 38.34+/-0.07% using only a source-regime database and no target-regime fitting, labels, or database entries. A 2x2 ablation isolates matching quality as dominant over the update rule. Oracle experiments confirm that source-regime dynamics directions remain transferable (cosine similarity ~0.84) when matching stays on-manifold; autoregressive drift is the primary bottleneck (~12 percentage points). From the learned-prediction side, a U-Net with multi-scale skip connections achieves 34.72+/-0.60%, consistent with the retrieval-side finding that local, multi-scale representations organise cross-Reynolds transfer among tested methods. All claims are scoped to this benchmark.

</details>


### [285] [Evolving Features vs Evolving Entire Trees with GP for Interpretable Survival Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30119)
*Thalea Schlender,Peter A. N. Bosman,Tanja Alderliesten*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evolutionary approaches (genetic programming and joint evolution) improve predictive performance of shallow survival trees while maintaining interpretability by optimizing feature sets and tree structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional survival trees face two key challenges: 1) they need to grow large to capture complex relationships, threatening interpretability; 2) greedy tree induction approaches may overlook globally optimal splits, limiting predictive performance. Shallow survival trees specifically need expressive feature combinations for competitive accuracy.

Method: 1) Use genetic programming to multi-objectively evolve inherently inspectable feature sets, studying their interaction with different tree induction strategies. 2) Introduce an evolutionary approach that jointly optimizes both the survival tree structure and the non-linear split logic (not just feature combinations).

Result: 1) Evolutionary feature construction improves predictive performance across different tree induction strategies on two real-world datasets and two different survival tree depths. 2) Full joint evolution (optimizing both structure and split logic) has the highest overall potential to propose multiple inherently inspectable shallow survival trees with good performance.

Conclusion: Evolutionary approaches effectively address the trade-off between accuracy and interpretability in survival analysis. Joint optimization of tree structure and split logic through evolutionary methods can produce high-performing yet interpretable shallow survival trees, making them more practical for medical applications where both prediction accuracy and model explainability are crucial.

Abstract: Survival analysis concerns the task of predicting the time until an event occurs. Often used in the medical field, survival analysis deals with incomplete (i.e., censored) data, for instance, from patients who did not experience the event during the duration of the study. For practical use, both accuracy and interpretability are important.
  Survival trees are easy-to-follow survival models that split the patient cohort recursively into discrete patient groups. Whilst survival trees can capture complex relationships, they typically need to grow large, threatening interpretability. Moreover, survival trees are often built using greedy approaches that may overlook globally optimal split combinations, limiting predictive performance.
  Shallow survival trees require expressive, higher-order feature combinations to achieve competitive accuracy. We therefore use genetic programming to multi-objectively evolve inherently inspectable feature sets and study how they interact with different tree induction strategies. We further introduce an evolutionary approach that jointly optimises the survival tree structure and the non-linear split logic.
  Our findings demonstrate that evolutionary feature construction improves predictive performance across different tree induction strategies on two real-world datasets and two different survival tree depths. Full joint evolution has the overall highest potential to propose multiple inherently inspectable shallow survival trees of good performance.

</details>


### [286] [Beyond MSE: Improving Precipitation Nowcasting with Multi-Quantile Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30122)
*Gijs van Nieuwkoop,Siamak Mehrkanoon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multi-quantile training improves precipitation nowcasting over standard MSE/MAE losses, reducing MSE by 8.6% while providing risk-sensitive heavy rainfall predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard pointwise losses like MSE and MAE in deep-learning precipitation nowcasting lead to overly smooth forecasts and poor representation of heavy rainfall, limiting their usefulness for operational forecasting.

Method: Reformulated training as multi-quantile regression problem using SmaAt-UNet architecture, comparing MSE, MAE, and multi-quantile pinball-loss training on radar precipitation nowcasting over the Netherlands.

Result: Multi-quantile training improved central deterministic forecast performance (8.6% MSE reduction vs MSE-trained model) and produced useful upper-quantile outputs for risk-sensitive heavy precipitation prediction.

Conclusion: Quantile regression provides a simple alternative to standard pointwise losses without requiring new architectures or generative sampling procedures, enhancing both central forecasts and risk-sensitive predictions.

Abstract: Deep-learning precipitation nowcasting models are often optimized using pointwise losses such as mean squared error or mean absolute error, which can lead to overly smooth forecasts and poor representation of heavy rainfall.
